Formally, an embedding layer maintains a weight matrix \( E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \), where \( |V| \) is the vocabulary size and \( d \) is the embedding dimension. Given a token index \( i \), the embedding is simply the \( i \)-th row:

\[ e_i = E[i, :] \in \mathbb{R}^d \]

This is mathematically equivalent to multiplying a one-hot vector by \( E \): \( e_i = \text{onehot}(i)^T E \), but implementations use direct indexing (a gather operation) for efficiency, avoiding the \( O(|V| \cdot d) \) matrix multiply.

Gradient flow and sparsity. During backpropagation, gradients flow only to the rows corresponding to tokens present in the current mini-batch. The gradient of the loss \( \mathcal{L} \) with respect to the embedding matrix is extremely sparse: \( \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial E[i,:]} \neq 0 \) only for indices \( i \) appearing in the batch. This sparsity motivates specialized optimizers — for example, adaptive methods like Adam naturally handle the varying update frequencies across rows.

Initialization. Common strategies include small random Gaussian \( \mathcal{N}(0, 0.01) \), uniform \( U(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}) \), or initialization from pre-trained embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText) for transfer learning.

Role in Transformers. Embedding layers are the entry point of all Transformer-based models. Token embeddings are summed with positional encodings (sinusoidal or learned) before entering the first self-attention layer. In the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), the embedding weights are scaled by \( \sqrt{d_{\text{model}}} \) to maintain variance relative to the positional encoding. Modern LLMs commonly use weight tying (Press & Wolf, 2017): the input embedding matrix \( E \) is shared with the output projection layer, so \( \text{logits} = h \cdot E^T \), reducing parameters by \( |V| \times d \) and often improving perplexity.

Model \( |V| \) \( d \) Embedding params
BERT-base 30,522 768 ~23M
GPT-2 50,257 768–1600 39M–80M
LLaMA-2 (7B) 32,000 4,096 ~131M
Python 
import numpy as np

class Embedding:
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim):
        # Xavier-style init
        bound = 1.0 / np.sqrt(embed_dim)
        self.W = np.random.uniform(-bound, bound, (vocab_size, embed_dim))

    def forward(self, token_ids):
        """token_ids: array of ints, shape (seq_len,)"""
        return self.W[token_ids]  # shape: (seq_len, embed_dim)

    def project(self, hidden):
        """Weight-tied output projection: logits = hidden @ E^T"""
        return hidden @ self.W.T  # shape: (seq_len, vocab_size)

title: Embedding vector
summary: A dense, low-dimensional, continuous vector representing a discrete entity (word, token, item, or node) in a learned latent space where geometric proximity encodes semantic or functional similarity.

papers.citation: 2003 · Bengio et al. · A Neural Probabilistic Language Model

sections.body:
A dense, low-dimensional, continuous vector representing a discrete entity (word, token, item, or node) in a learned latent space where geometric proximity encodes semantic or functional similarity.

An embedding vector \( e \in \mathbb{R}^d \) is the output of an embedding layer for a specific input. The central insight is that the geometry of the embedding space captures meaningful relationships: semantically similar entities are mapped to nearby points, and directions in the space can encode relational structure. The classic demonstration is Word2Vec's analogy arithmetic:

\[ \vec{\text{king}} - \vec{\text{man}} + \vec{\text{woman}} \approx \vec{\text{queen}} \]

This works because the vector offset \( \vec{\text{king}} - \vec{\text{man}} \) approximates a "royalty" direction, which transfers across genders. Such linear regularities emerge from the distributional training objective without explicit supervision.

Similarity measurement. The most common metric for comparing embedding vectors is cosine similarity: \( \text{sim}(a, b) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|} \), which captures directional alignment independent of magnitude. Euclidean distance \( \|a - b\|_2 \) is used when magnitude is meaningful (e.g., frequency-weighted embeddings). In retrieval systems, approximate nearest-neighbor algorithms (FAISS, ScaNN, HNSW) enable efficient similarity search over millions of vectors.

Dimensionality. Embedding dimension \( d \) is a critical hyperparameter balancing expressiveness against computational cost:

Setting Typical \( d \) Examples
Static word embeddings 50–300 Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText
Contextual embeddings 768–12288 BERT (768), GPT-4 (12288)
Recommender systems 32–256 User/item embeddings in collaborative filtering

Lower dimensions risk information loss; higher dimensions increase compute and memory cost and risk overfitting on small corpora. The Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma provides a theoretical lower bound: \( O(\log N / \epsilon^2) \) dimensions suffice to preserve pairwise distances among \( N \) points within a \( (1 \pm \epsilon) \) factor.

Embeddings extend far beyond NLP: recommender systems embed users and items into shared spaces, graph neural networks embed nodes, and multimodal models (CLIP) embed images and text into aligned vector spaces for cross-modal retrieval.

title: Word embedding
summary: A mapping from words in a vocabulary to dense, real-valued vectors in a continuous space, where distances and directions encode linguistic relationships such as synonymy, analogy, and syntactic role.

papers.citation: 2003 · Bengio et al. · A Neural Probabilistic Language Model

sections.body:
A mapping from words in a vocabulary to dense, real-valued vectors in a continuous space, where distances and directions encode linguistic relationships such as synonymy, analogy, and syntactic role.

Word embeddings assign each word \( w \in V \) a vector \( e_w \in \mathbb{R}^d \), replacing sparse one-hot representations (\( \mathbb{R}^{|V|} \), where \( |V| \) can exceed 100k) with dense, low-dimensional alternatives (typically \( d \in [50, 300] \)). The foundational hypothesis is the distributional hypothesis (Firth, 1957: "You shall know a word by the company it keeps"): words appearing in similar contexts have similar meanings, and this similarity should be reflected geometrically.

Two major families emerged in the 2010s:

Approach Method Objective
Prediction-based Word2Vec (2013), FastText (2017) Predict context words from center word (or vice versa) via shallow neural network
Count-based GloVe (2014) Factorize global word co-occurrence matrix \( X \) via weighted least squares: \( \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij})(w_i^T \tilde{w}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij})^2 \)

Levy and Goldberg (2014) proved that Word2Vec's Skip-gram with negative sampling (SGNS) implicitly factorizes a shifted PMI matrix, unifying the two approaches theoretically: both recover a low-rank approximation to word co-occurrence statistics.

These are static embeddings — each word type has a single fixed vector regardless of context. The word "bank" receives the same representation whether it refers to a financial institution or a river bank. This polysemy limitation was addressed by contextual embeddings (ELMo, 2018; BERT, 2019; GPT), where representations are functions of the entire input sequence. Static embeddings remain valuable for low-resource languages, fast retrieval baselines, and interpretability studies.

title: Word2Vec
summary: A family of shallow neural network models that learn word embeddings by predicting either a target word from its context (CBOW) or the context from a target word (Skip-gram), trained efficiently on billions of words using negative sampli…

papers.citation: 2013 · Tomas Mikolov, Kai Chen, Greg Corrado, Jeffrey Dean · Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space

sections.body:
A family of shallow neural network models that learn word embeddings by predicting either a target word from its context (CBOW) or the context from a target word (Skip-gram), trained efficiently on billions of words using negative sampling or hierarchical softmax.

Word2Vec was a landmark contribution that made high-quality word embeddings practical at scale. It introduced two architectures:

  • CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words): predicts the center word \( w_t \) from the average of surrounding context word vectors within a fixed window: \( P(w_t \mid w_{t-c}, \ldots, w_{t+c}) \). Faster to train, slightly better for frequent words.
  • Skip-gram: predicts each context word from the center word: \( P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) \) for \( j \in [-c, c], j \neq 0 \). Slower but produces better representations for rare words and small datasets.

The Skip-gram objective over a corpus of words \( w_1, \ldots, w_T \) is:

\[ J = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{\substack{-c \leq j \leq c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) \]

where the softmax probability is:

\[ P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp({v'_{w_O}}^\top v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w \in V} \exp({v'_w}^\top v_{w_I})} \]

Computing the full softmax over \( |V| \) (often 100k–1M) is prohibitively expensive at \( O(|V| \cdot d) \) per update. Two approximations make training tractable:

  • Negative sampling (SGNS): replace the softmax with a binary classification objective — distinguish true (center, context) pairs from \( k \) randomly sampled negatives drawn from \( P_n(w) \propto f(w)^{3/4} \). Typical \( k \): 5–20 for small datasets, 2–5 for large corpora.
  • Hierarchical softmax: organize the vocabulary as a Huffman tree, reducing the output computation from \( O(|V|) \) to \( O(\log |V|) \).

Additional training tricks include subsampling frequent words (discarding tokens like "the" with probability \( P(\text{discard}) = 1 - \sqrt{t / f(w)} \), where \( t \approx 10^{-5} \)) and dynamic context windows (sampling \( c' \sim \text{Uniform}(1, c) \) per training instance).

The resulting vectors famously exhibit linear algebraic structure encoding semantic analogies: \( \vec{\text{king}} - \vec{\text{man}} + \vec{\text{woman}} \approx \vec{\text{queen}} \). Levy and Goldberg (2014) later showed that SGNS implicitly factorizes a shifted pointwise mutual information (PMI) matrix, connecting it theoretically to classical count-based distributional semantics (LSA, GloVe).

title: Skip-gram
summary: A Word2Vec training objective that learns word embeddings by predicting surrounding context words given a center word, inverting the typical language modeling direction and producing high-quality representations especially for rare words.

papers.citation: 2013 · Mikolov, Sutskever, Chen, Corrado, Dean · Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality

sections.body:
A Word2Vec training objective that learns word embeddings by predicting surrounding context words given a center word, inverting the typical language modeling direction and producing high-quality representations especially for rare words.

Given a center word \( w_t \) and a context window of size \( c \), Skip-gram maximizes:

\[ J = \sum_{\substack{-c \leq j \leq c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) \]

Each word maintains two learned vectors: an input embedding \( v_w \in \mathbb{R}^d \) (when it serves as center) and an output embedding \( v'_w \in \mathbb{R}^d \) (when it serves as context). In practice, only the input embeddings \( v_w \) are used after training, though averaging \( v_w \) and \( v'_w \) sometimes improves quality.

Negative sampling (SGNS). The expensive full-vocabulary softmax is replaced by a binary classification objective. For a true (center, context) pair \( (w_I, w_O) \), the loss becomes:

\[ \mathcal{L} = \log \sigma({v'_{w_O}}^\top v_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)} \left[\log \sigma(-{v'_{w_i}}^\top v_{w_I})\right] \]

where \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid function and \( P_n(w) \propto f(w)^{3/4} \) is a smoothed unigram distribution. The \( 3/4 \) exponent was found empirically to work well: it upweights rare words relative to the raw frequency distribution. Typical values: \( k = 5\text{--}20 \) negatives for small datasets, \( k = 2\text{--}5 \) for large corpora.

Theoretical connection. Levy and Goldberg (2014) proved that SGNS, with sufficient training data and embedding dimension, implicitly factorizes a shifted pointwise mutual information (PMI) matrix:

\[ v_w \cdot v'_c \approx \text{PMI}(w, c) - \log k \]

This result unified Skip-gram with classical count-based distributional semantics methods (LSA, PPMI+SVD), showing they optimize closely related objectives. It also explains why GloVe — which explicitly factorizes a co-occurrence matrix — produces similar embeddings.

Derivations

Starting from the softmax: \( P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp({v'_{w_O}}^\top v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w} \exp({v'_w}^\top v_{w_I})} \). The partition function is intractable for large \( |V| \). Negative sampling replaces this with noise-contrastive estimation: model the problem as distinguishing data from noise. The probability that a pair \( (w, c) \) came from data rather than noise: \( P(D=1 \mid w, c) = \sigma(v'_c \cdot v_w) \). The full SGNS loss per positive pair is the binary cross-entropy summing over 1 positive + \( k \) negative samples.

title: FastText
summary: An extension of Word2Vec's Skip-gram that represents each word as a bag of character n-grams, enabling meaningful embeddings for out-of-vocabulary words and morphologically rich languages.

papers.citation: 2017 · Piotr Bojanowski, Edouard Grave, Armand Joulin, Tomas Mikolov · Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information

sections.body:
An extension of Word2Vec's Skip-gram that represents each word as a bag of character n-grams, enabling meaningful embeddings for out-of-vocabulary words and morphologically rich languages.

In standard Word2Vec, each word is an atomic unit — unseen words have no representation, and morphological relationships (e.g., "teach" / "teacher" / "teaching") are not explicitly modeled. FastText decomposes word \( w \) into its set of character n-grams \( \mathcal{G}_w \) (typically \( n \in [3, 6] \)), plus a special whole-word token \( \langle w \rangle \). The word's embedding is the sum of its constituent n-gram vectors:

\[ v_w = \sum_{g \in \mathcal{G}_w} z_g \]

where \( z_g \in \mathbb{R}^d \) is the embedding for n-gram \( g \). For example, "where" with \( n = 3 \) and boundary markers < > yields: {<wh, whe, her, ere, re>, <where>}. The Skip-gram scoring function becomes:

\[ s(w, c) = \sum_{g \in \mathcal{G}_w} z_g^\top v'_c \]

This is plugged into the same negative sampling objective as Word2Vec SGNS.

Hashing trick. The number of possible character n-grams is very large, so FastText hashes n-grams into a fixed number of buckets (default: 2M), keeping the embedding matrix at a manageable size \( \mathbb{R}^{B \times d} \) regardless of vocabulary. Collisions are rare enough to have minimal impact on embedding quality.

sections.title: Key advantages
sections.body:
• OOV handling: entirely unseen words can be embedded by summing their constituent n-grams — critical for web text, user-generated content, and agglutinative languages (Turkish, Finnish).
  • Morphological regularities: words sharing stems or affixes naturally share n-gram vectors, so "teach," "teacher," and "teaching" receive related embeddings without supervision.
  • Rare word quality: rare words benefit from shared n-gram statistics with frequent words, partially alleviating the data sparsity problem.

FastText also includes a highly efficient text classification model (supervised mode) that averages n-gram embeddings and passes them through a linear classifier, competitive with deep models at orders-of-magnitude less compute.

title: Semantic similarity
summary: A measure of how closely two linguistic units (words, sentences, or documents) relate in meaning, typically quantified by distance or angle in an embedding space using metrics such as cosine similarity.

papers.citation: 1957 · J.R. Firth (distributional hypothesis) · Modern operationalization: Mikolov et al. (2013), Reimers & Gurevych (2019)

sections.body:
A measure of how closely two linguistic units (words, sentences, or documents) relate in meaning, typically quantified by distance or angle in an embedding space using metrics such as cosine similarity.

Semantic similarity is operationalized by embedding texts into a shared vector space and computing a distance metric. The dominant choice is cosine similarity:

\[ \text{sim}(a, b) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\| \, \|b\|} \in [-1, 1] \]

Other metrics include Euclidean distance \( \|a - b\|_2 \) (sensitive to magnitude) and raw dot product \( a \cdot b \) (conflates magnitude with direction, but used in attention mechanisms and some retrieval systems for efficiency).

Similarity vs. relatedness. A persistent and important distinction: similarity means belonging to the same category or being substitutable ("coffee" / "tea" — both hot beverages), while relatedness means any semantic association ("coffee" / "mug" — associated but not substitutable). SimLex-999 was specifically designed to measure similarity (not relatedness), unlike earlier benchmarks like WordSim-353 which conflated the two. Most embedding models trained with distributional objectives capture relatedness better than strict similarity.

sections.title: Evaluation benchmarks
sections.body:
Level Benchmark Metric
Word SimLex-999, WordSim-353, MEN Spearman \( \rho \) between model scores and human ratings
Sentence STS Benchmark (STS-B) Pearson / Spearman correlation with human scores (0–5 scale)
Document MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) Suite of retrieval, clustering, classification tasks

Modern approaches. Sentence-level semantic similarity improved dramatically with contrastive learning objectives. Sentence-BERT (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019) fine-tunes BERT with a siamese architecture, and SimCSE (Gao et al., 2021) achieves strong results using simple dropout-based augmentation as positive pairs. These models produce embedding spaces where cosine similarity aligns tightly with human similarity judgments, enabling efficient retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines.

title: Cosine similarity
summary: A similarity metric that measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, capturing directional alignment independently of magnitude. It is the standard metric for comparing embeddings in NLP and information retrieval.

papers.citation: 1880s · Various (inner product geometry) · Popularized in IR by Salton & McGill (1983)

sections.body:
A similarity metric that measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, capturing directional alignment independently of magnitude. It is the standard metric for comparing embeddings in NLP and information retrieval.

For two vectors \( a, b \in \mathbb{R}^d \):

\[ \cos(a, b) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\| \, \|b\|} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^d a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^d a_i^2} \; \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^d b_i^2}} \]

The result lies in \( [-1, 1] \): \( +1 \) indicates identical direction, \( 0 \) indicates orthogonality (no linear relationship), and \( -1 \) indicates opposite direction. When vectors are L2-normalized (unit vectors on the hypersphere), cosine similarity reduces to the dot product: \( \cos(\hat{a}, \hat{b}) = \hat{a} \cdot \hat{b} \), and cosine distance equals \( 1 - \cos(a,b) \).

Why magnitude invariance matters. Cosine similarity is preferred over Euclidean distance in NLP because vector magnitude often encodes irrelevant information — word frequency, document length, or arbitrary scaling. A document repeated twice should not appear more similar to a query simply because its TF-IDF vector has larger norm. By normalizing away magnitude, cosine focuses purely on the "direction" of meaning in the embedding space.

Relationship to attention. Scaled dot-product attention in Transformers computes:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V \]

The \( QK^\top \) term is an unnormalized dot-product similarity (not cosine, since queries and keys are not L2-normalized). The \( \sqrt{d_k} \) scaling prevents dot products from growing large in high dimensions, which would push softmax into saturated regions with near-zero gradients. Some recent architectures (e.g., QK-normalization in ViT-22B) do apply L2-normalization to queries and keys, making attention weights true cosine similarities.

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• For retrieval at scale, pre-normalizing all vectors to unit length converts cosine similarity to a dot product, enabling faster MIPS (maximum inner product search) with libraries like FAISS.
  • Cosine similarity is undefined for zero vectors — implementations must handle this edge case.
  • In high-dimensional spaces, random vectors tend to be nearly orthogonal (cosine ~ 0), so meaningful similarities are typically in a narrow positive range.
Python 
import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(a, b):
    """Cosine similarity between vectors a and b."""
    dot = np.dot(a, b)
    norms = np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)
    if norms == 0:
        return 0.0
    return dot / norms

# Batch version: cosine similarity matrix
def cosine_sim_matrix(X, Y):
    """X: (n, d), Y: (m, d) -> (n, m) similarity matrix."""
    X_norm = X / np.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    Y_norm = Y / np.linalg.norm(Y, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    return X_norm @ Y_norm.T

title: Bag of words
summary: A text representation that discards word order and grammar, encoding a document solely as an unordered multiset of its word frequencies — the simplest and historically most influential document representation in NLP and information retri…

papers.citation: 1954 · Zellig Harris (distributional analysis) · Formalized in IR by Salton, Wong, Yang (1975)

sections.body:
A text representation that discards word order and grammar, encoding a document solely as an unordered multiset of its word frequencies — the simplest and historically most influential document representation in NLP and information retrieval.

Given a vocabulary \( V = \{w_1, \ldots, w_{|V|}\} \), a document \( d \) is represented as a sparse vector \( x \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|} \). Three common weighting schemes:

Scheme Formula Notes
Raw count \( x_i = \text{count}(w_i, d) \) Simplest; biased toward long documents
Binary \( x_i = \mathbb{1}[w_i \in d] \) Presence/absence only; used in some Naive Bayes variants
TF-IDF \( x_i = \text{tf}(w_i, d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(w_i)} \) Downweights ubiquitous terms; standard in retrieval

The sentence "the cat sat on the mat" becomes a sparse vector: the=2, cat=1, sat=1, on=1, mat=1 (with all other entries zero). Despite the extreme simplicity — "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" are identical under BoW — the representation proved remarkably effective throughout the 1990s–2000s for:

  • Document classification: Naive Bayes, logistic regression, and SVMs over BoW features were the dominant paradigm. Multinomial Naive Bayes models \( P(w \mid c) \) directly from word counts per class.
  • Topic modeling: LDA treats each document as a mixture of topics, each topic as a distribution over words — essentially a probabilistic decomposition of the BoW representation.
  • Information retrieval: TF-IDF BoW vectors with cosine similarity formed the basis of vector space models (Salton et al., 1975).

BM25 (Robertson et al., 1995), the most successful BoW retrieval function, extends TF-IDF with saturation and length normalization:

\[ \text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{w \in q} \text{IDF}(w) \cdot \frac{f(w, d) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(w, d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|d|}{\text{avgdl}}\right)} \]

where \( k_1 \approx 1.2 \) controls term frequency saturation and \( b \approx 0.75 \) controls length normalization. BM25 remains a competitive baseline even against neural dense retrieval systems and is the standard first-stage ranker in modern search pipelines.

BoW was gradually superseded by word embeddings and then contextual representations for tasks requiring semantic understanding, but its virtues — interpretability, computational efficiency, and no training data requirement — keep it relevant. Hybrid retrieval systems often combine BM25 sparse scores with dense embedding scores for best results.

title: Document-term matrix
summary: A matrix where each row represents a document, each column represents a term from the vocabulary, and each entry records the frequency or weight of that term in that document — the foundational data structure of the vector space model in…

papers.citation: 1975 · Gerard Salton et al. · A Vector Space Model for Automatic Indexing

sections.body:
A matrix where each row represents a document, each column represents a term from the vocabulary, and each entry records the frequency or weight of that term in that document — the foundational data structure of the vector space model in information retrieval.

For a corpus of \( N \) documents and a vocabulary of \( |V| \) terms, the document-term matrix \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times |V|} \) has entries \( A_{ij} \) representing the importance of term \( j \) in document \( i \). Raw entries may be term counts, binary indicators, or TF-IDF weights. A concrete example with three documents and four terms:

Document cat dog fish pet
d₁: "the cat is a pet" 1 0 0 1
d₂: "the dog is a pet" 0 1 0 1
d₃: "the fish" 0 0 1 0

Stop words ("the", "is", "a") are often removed before constructing the matrix. In practice, the matrix is extremely sparse — a typical English document of 500 words touches only a tiny fraction of a 100,000-word vocabulary, so \( > 99\% \) of entries are zero. Sparse storage formats like CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) exploit this to keep memory manageable.

Key operations on the document-term matrix connect to major ML techniques:

  • Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA): applies truncated SVD to discover latent topic dimensions: \( A \approx U_k \Sigma_k V_k^T \), where \( k \) is the number of retained dimensions (typically 100–300). The rows of \( U_k \Sigma_k \) become dense document representations.
  • Cosine similarity: measures document similarity as \( \cos(\theta) = \frac{a_i \cdot a_j}{\|a_i\| \|a_j\|} \), where \( a_i, a_j \) are row vectors from \( A \). Unlike Euclidean distance, cosine similarity is invariant to document length.
  • Topic models (LDA): treat \( A \) as generated from document-topic and topic-word distributions, providing a probabilistic alternative to SVD-based decomposition.
  • Inverted index: the transpose \( A^T \) — the term-document matrix — is the conceptual basis of the inverted index used in search engines, mapping each term to its list of containing documents.

The document-term matrix is the central data structure of classical information retrieval. Modern dense retrieval methods (using BERT or other Transformer encoders to produce document embeddings) can be viewed as replacing the sparse rows of \( A \) with learned dense vectors, trading interpretability for semantic generalization.

title: TF-IDF
summary: A term-weighting scheme that scores a word's importance in a document relative to a corpus by multiplying its local frequency (TF) by its global rarity (IDF).

papers.citation: 1972 · Karen Spärck Jones · A Statistical Interpretation of Term Specificity and Its Application in Retrieval

sections.body:
A term-weighting scheme that scores a word's importance in a document relative to a corpus by multiplying its local frequency (TF) by its global rarity (IDF).

For term \( t \) in document \( d \) from corpus \( D \):

\[ \text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) = \text{TF}(t, d) \times \text{IDF}(t, D) \]

Common formulations:

Component Formula Intuition
Term Frequency \( \text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \) How often \( t \) appears in \( d \), normalized
Inverse Document Frequency \( \text{IDF}(t, D) = \log \frac{|D|}{|\{d \in D : t \in d\}|} \) Down-weights terms appearing in many documents

Words like "the" and "is" have high TF but near-zero IDF, correctly receiving low scores. Domain-specific terms have moderate TF but high IDF, receiving appropriately high scores. TF-IDF remains the backbone of BM25 (the standard sparse retrieval algorithm) and is still a competitive baseline for document classification, keyword extraction, and search ranking.

Python 
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter

def tfidf(docs):
    """Compute TF-IDF for a list of tokenized documents."""
    N = len(docs)
    vocab = sorted(set(w for d in docs for w in d))
    word2idx = {w: i for i, w in enumerate(vocab)}
    matrix = np.zeros((N, len(vocab)))
    df = Counter(w for d in docs for w in set(d))
    for i, doc in enumerate(docs):
        tf = Counter(doc)
        for w, count in tf.items():
            idf = np.log(N / df[w])
            matrix[i, word2idx[w]] = (count / len(doc)) * idf
    return matrix, vocab

title: Sparsity
summary: A property of vectors, matrices, or models where the majority of elements are exactly zero, enabling both computational efficiency and interpretable representations.

papers.citation: 1996 · Tibshirani (Lasso) · Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso

sections.body:
A property of vectors, matrices, or models where the majority of elements are exactly zero, enabling both computational efficiency and interpretable representations.

A vector \( x \in \mathbb{R}^n \) is \( k \)-sparse if it has at most \( k \) non-zero entries, with \( k \ll n \). The \( \ell_0 \) "norm" counts non-zero entries: \( \|x\|_0 = |\{i : x_i \neq 0\}| \). Since \( \ell_0 \) minimization is NP-hard, the \( \ell_1 \) norm \( \|x\|_1 = \sum_i |x_i| \) serves as its convex relaxation — the tightest convex surrogate that still promotes sparsity.

Sparsity appears at multiple levels in ML:

Context Example Mechanism & Benefit
Data representation One-hot vectors, BoW, TF-IDF Naturally sparse; memory-efficient via CSR/COO storage
Regularization (Lasso) \( \mathcal{L} + \lambda \|w\|_1 \) Drives weights to exactly zero → automatic feature selection
Model architecture Mixture of Experts (top-\( k \) routing) Activates \( k \) of \( N \) experts per token → scales parameters without proportional FLOPs
Activations ReLU: \( \max(0, x) \) Zeroes negative pre-activations → sparse hidden representations
Pruning Magnitude pruning, lottery tickets Zeroes small weights post-training → smaller, faster models

Why \( \ell_1 \) induces sparsity but \( \ell_2 \) does not: geometrically, the \( \ell_1 \) ball has corners aligned with the coordinate axes. When the loss contour meets the constraint boundary, it is most likely to touch at a corner where one or more coordinates are exactly zero. The \( \ell_2 \) ball is smooth, so contact points generically have all-nonzero coordinates.

The contrast between sparse and dense representations is a recurring theme in ML: sparse vectors (e.g., TF-IDF) are high-dimensional and interpretable but cannot capture synonymy; dense vectors (e.g., word embeddings) are low-dimensional and capture semantics but are opaque. Modern retrieval systems often combine both in hybrid search. In the interpretability literature, sparse autoencoders are trained to decompose dense Transformer activations into sparse, monosemantic features.

title: Dense vector
summary: A vector in which most or all elements are non-zero, typically low-dimensional and learned, as opposed to a sparse high-dimensional representation. Dense vectors are the standard output of neural embedding models and the native represent…

papers.citation: 2003 · Bengio et al. · A Neural Probabilistic Language Model

sections.body:
A vector in which most or all elements are non-zero, typically low-dimensional and learned, as opposed to a sparse high-dimensional representation. Dense vectors are the standard output of neural embedding models and the native representation inside Transformers.

A word embedding \( e \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \) (e.g., \( d = 300 \) for Word2Vec, \( d = 768 \) for BERT-base) will typically have every component carrying a non-zero floating-point value. This contrasts sharply with sparse representations like one-hot vectors or TF-IDF, where \( > 99\% \) of entries are zero.

sections.title: How dense vectors are produced
sections.body:
• Static embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe): trained via shallow objectives — skip-gram maximizes \( \sum_{(w,c)} \log \sigma(e_w \cdot e_c) \), where \( w \) is the target word and \( c \) is a context word. Each word gets a single fixed vector.
  • Contextual embeddings (BERT, GPT): produced by Transformer encoder/decoder layers. The same word receives different vectors depending on context, since the representation is a function of the entire input sequence via self-attention.
  • Sentence/document embeddings: obtained by pooling token-level vectors (mean pooling, [CLS] token) or via contrastive training (e.g., Sentence-BERT).

sections.title: Key properties and operations
sections.body:
• Semantic generalization: similar concepts are nearby — \( \cos(e_{\text{king}}, e_{\text{queen}}) \) is high — enabling generalization to unseen inputs.
  • Similarity metrics: cosine similarity \( \frac{u \cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|} \) is standard. For normalized vectors, cosine similarity equals the dot product, so approximate nearest-neighbor libraries (FAISS, ScaNN) can use inner product search.
  • Fixed dimensionality: representation size is independent of vocabulary — a 300-dim embedding works whether \( |V| = 10{,}000 \) or \( 100{,}000 \).
  • Arithmetic structure: well-trained embeddings exhibit linear analogies: \( e_{\text{king}} - e_{\text{man}} + e_{\text{woman}} \approx e_{\text{queen}} \).

The fundamental trade-off against sparse vectors is interpretability: individual dimensions of a dense vector rarely correspond to identifiable features. Recent work on sparse autoencoders aims to decompose dense Transformer representations into interpretable sparse features, bridging the two paradigms.

title: Sparse vector
summary: A vector in which the vast majority of elements are exactly zero, typically high-dimensional with each dimension corresponding to a discrete feature or vocabulary term. Sparse vectors are the native representation of classical informatio…

papers.citation: 1975 · Salton et al. · A Vector Space Model for Automatic Indexing

sections.body:
A vector in which the vast majority of elements are exactly zero, typically high-dimensional with each dimension corresponding to a discrete feature or vocabulary term. Sparse vectors are the native representation of classical information retrieval and count-based NLP.

Sparse vectors arise naturally when representing categorical or count-based data. A one-hot encoding of a word from a vocabulary of size \( |V| = 50{,}000 \) produces a vector with exactly 1 non-zero entry and 49,999 zeros. A TF-IDF document vector may have a few hundred non-zero entries out of tens of thousands of dimensions.

Storage formats exploit sparsity by recording only non-zero entries:

Format Storage Best for
CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) Row pointers + column indices + values Row slicing, matrix-vector products (document-term matrices)
CSC (Compressed Sparse Column) Column pointers + row indices + values Column slicing (term lookups across documents)
COO (Coordinate) (row, col, value) triples Incremental construction, format conversion

A CSR matrix with \( \text{nnz} \) non-zero entries uses \( O(\text{nnz}) \) memory versus \( O(N \times |V|) \) for a dense matrix — often a reduction of 100x or more.

Sparse retrieval. In search engines, the document-term matrix's transpose yields an inverted index: for each term, a posting list records which documents contain it and with what weight. At query time, only documents containing at least one query term are scored, making search sublinear in corpus size. BM25 — the standard sparse retrieval algorithm — computes:

\[ \text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d} \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \cdot (1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|d|}{\text{avgdl}})} \]

where \( f_{t,d} \) is term frequency, \( k_1 \) and \( b \) are tuning parameters, and avgdl is average document length.

Sparse vectors cannot capture synonymy ("car" and "automobile" are orthogonal), which motivated the development of dense embeddings. Modern hybrid search systems combine both: sparse matching for exact keyword recall and dense matching for semantic generalization.

title: One-hot encoding
summary: A representation of a categorical variable as a binary vector with exactly one element set to 1 (indicating the active category) and all others set to 0. It is the starting point for most neural approaches to categorical data.

papers.citation: 1960s · Various (information theory and digital encoding literature)

sections.body:
A representation of a categorical variable as a binary vector with exactly one element set to 1 (indicating the active category) and all others set to 0. It is the starting point for most neural approaches to categorical data.

For a variable with \( K \) categories, the one-hot encoding of category \( k \) is the standard basis vector \( e_k \in \mathbb{R}^K \), where \( (e_k)_i = \mathbb{1}[i = k] \). For a vocabulary of words \( V = \{\text{cat}, \text{dog}, \text{fish}\} \):

\[ \text{cat} = [1, 0, 0], \quad \text{dog} = [0, 1, 0], \quad \text{fish} = [0, 0, 1] \]

sections.title: Key mathematical properties
sections.body:
• Equidistant: all pairs are equidistant — \( \|e_i - e_j\|_2^2 = 2 \) for \( i \neq j \) — so no notion of similarity is encoded.
  • Orthogonal: \( e_i^T e_j = 0 \) for \( i \neq j \), meaning dot-product similarity between any two distinct categories is zero.
  • Dimensionality = cardinality: the vector dimension equals the number of categories, which becomes prohibitive for large vocabularies (\( |V| > 100{,}000 \) in NLP).

Connection to embedding layers. Multiplying a one-hot vector by an embedding matrix \( E \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times d} \) selects the corresponding row:

\[ e_k^T E = E[k, :] \in \mathbb{R}^d \]

This is why embedding layers in PyTorch (nn.Embedding) and TensorFlow (tf.keras.layers.Embedding) are implemented as lookup tables rather than matrix multiplications — the one-hot product is equivalent to indexing row \( k \). The embedding layer thus replaces the sparse, semantics-free one-hot space with a dense, learned representation where similar items can cluster together.

Limitations and alternatives. For high-cardinality categorical features (e.g., user IDs, ZIP codes), one-hot encoding creates extremely wide feature vectors. Alternatives include hashing tricks (map categories to a fixed-size vector via hash functions, accepting collisions) and learned embeddings that compress the space to \( d \ll K \) dimensions.

title: Tokenization
summary: The process of segmenting raw text into discrete units (tokens) that serve as the atomic inputs to a language model. Tokenization determines the vocabulary, sequence length, and the model's ability to handle unseen words.

papers.citation: 2016 · Sennrich, Haddow, Birch (BPE for NMT) · Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units

sections.body:
The process of segmenting raw text into discrete units (tokens) that serve as the atomic inputs to a language model. Tokenization determines the vocabulary, sequence length, and the model's ability to handle unseen words.

Tokenization is the first step in any NLP pipeline. The choice of granularity involves a fundamental trade-off between vocabulary size and sequence length:

Granularity Example ("unhappiness") Trade-off
Character u, n, h, a, p, p, i, n, e, s, s Tiny vocab (~256), very long sequences → burdens \( O(n^2) \) attention
Subword (BPE) un, happi, ness Balanced vocab (32K–100K) / length, handles OOV gracefully
Word unhappiness Enormous vocab, OOV problem for rare/morphological variants

Modern LLMs universally use subword tokenization — BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram (SentencePiece) — as a compromise. The tokenizer is trained on the corpus before model training and remains fixed thereafter. Training a BPE tokenizer proceeds by:

  1. Initializing the vocabulary with all individual characters (or bytes).
  2. Counting all adjacent symbol pairs in the training corpus.
  3. Merging the most frequent pair into a new symbol and adding it to the vocabulary.
  4. Repeating until the desired vocabulary size is reached (e.g., 32,000 merges).

sections.title: Practical impact on model performance
sections.body:
• Fertility — the average number of tokens per word — varies across languages and domains. English typically averages ~1.3 tokens/word, while morphologically rich languages or non-Latin scripts may average 3–5x higher fertility, consuming more of the context window and degrading performance.
  • Code and math: tokenizers trained mainly on natural language often fragment code tokens (e.g., splitting != into two tokens), wasting model capacity.
  • Whitespace handling: GPT-style tokenizers prepend spaces to tokens ("Ġthe"), while SentencePiece uses a special character (▁) to mark word boundaries.

Tokenization is not a neutral preprocessing step — it shapes what the model can learn. Two models with identical architectures but different tokenizers can exhibit substantially different performance on the same task.

title: Token
summary: The atomic unit of text processed by a language model — a discrete symbol from a fixed vocabulary, which may represent a word, subword, character, or byte. Tokens are the fundamental unit of cost, context, and generation in modern LLMs.

papers.citation: 1990s · Various (tokenization literature)

sections.body:
The atomic unit of text processed by a language model — a discrete symbol from a fixed vocabulary, which may represent a word, subword, character, or byte. Tokens are the fundamental unit of cost, context, and generation in modern LLMs.

After tokenization, raw text is converted into a sequence of integer token IDs. Each ID indexes into the model's vocabulary and maps to an embedding vector via the embedding layer:

\[ \text{token ID } k \;\xrightarrow{\; E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \;}\; e_k = E[k, :] \in \mathbb{R}^d \]

For GPT-style models with BPE tokenization, a token typically corresponds to ~4 characters or ~0.75 words of English text. A rough but useful heuristic: 1 token ≈ 0.75 English words, or equivalently, 100 tokens ≈ 75 words.

sections.title: Tokens as the unit of computation
sections.body:
Dimension Role of the Token Example
Context window Maximum sequence length measured in tokens GPT-4: 128K tokens; Claude: 200K tokens
Pricing API costs charged per input + output token $X per million input tokens
Generation Autoregressive models produce one token per forward pass Each pass computes \( P(w_t | w_{1:t-1}) <="" anki-mathjax=""> over |V| \) tokens
Attention cost Self-attention scales as \( O(n^2) \) in sequence length Doubling tokens quadruples attention FLOPs

Special tokens serve structural roles beyond content:

  • <BOS> / <EOS> — beginning/end of sequence, used to signal generation boundaries
  • <PAD> — padding token for batching sequences of unequal length; masked out in attention
  • [SEP] — separates segments in BERT-style models (e.g., question from passage)
  • [CLS] — classification token in BERT; its final hidden state is used as the sequence representation
  • <UNK> — unknown/out-of-vocabulary; rare in subword systems since any string can be decomposed to bytes

The token is not merely a preprocessing artifact — it is the grain at which the model reasons, attends, and generates. Architectural innovations like KV-caching, speculative decoding, and sparse attention all operate at the token level.

title: Subword
summary: A linguistic unit smaller than a word but larger than a character, used as the tokenization granularity in modern language models to balance vocabulary size against sequence length. Subword tokenization is universally adopted in Transfor…

papers.citation: 2016 · Sennrich, Haddow, Birch · Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units

sections.body:
A linguistic unit smaller than a word but larger than a character, used as the tokenization granularity in modern language models to balance vocabulary size against sequence length. Subword tokenization is universally adopted in Transformers.

Subword tokenization decomposes words into frequently occurring fragments. For example, "tokenization" might split into ["token", "ization"], while common words like "the" remain whole tokens. This approach resolves the core tension in tokenizer design:

  • Word-level tokenizers cannot handle unseen words (OOV problem) and require enormous vocabularies for morphologically rich languages.
  • Character-level tokenizers produce extremely long sequences, burdening attention's \( O(n^2) \) cost and making it harder to learn long-range dependencies.
  • Subword tokenizers adaptively split rare words into known components while keeping common words intact — a data-driven compression of the input.

The three dominant subword algorithms:

Algorithm Strategy Used by
BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding) Bottom-up: iteratively merge most frequent adjacent pairs GPT-2/3/4, LLaMA, Claude
WordPiece Bottom-up: merge pairs that maximize language model likelihood BERT, DistilBERT
Unigram LM Top-down: start with large vocab, iteratively prune tokens with least impact on corpus likelihood T5, mBART (via SentencePiece)

sections.title: BPE training algorithm in detail
sections.body:
1. Initialize vocabulary with all individual bytes (or characters).
  2. Count frequencies of all adjacent symbol pairs across the corpus.
  3. Merge the most frequent pair into a new symbol; add to vocabulary.
  4. Repeat steps 2–3 for \( M \) merges (e.g., \( M = 32{,}000 \)), yielding a vocabulary of size \( 256 + M \) for byte-level BPE.

At inference time, BPE applies the learned merges greedily in priority order. WordPiece instead uses a longest-match-first strategy, prefixing continuation tokens with ## (e.g., "playing" → ["play", "##ing"]). Unigram selects the tokenization that maximizes the product of unigram probabilities: \( \arg\max_{(x_1, \ldots, x_m)} \sum_i \log p(x_i) \), solved efficiently with the Viterbi algorithm.

SentencePiece (Kudo & Richardson, 2018) is a language-agnostic library that implements both BPE and Unigram. It treats the input as a raw byte stream rather than pre-tokenized words, making it suitable for languages without whitespace boundaries (Chinese, Japanese).

Python 
from collections import Counter

def train_bpe(corpus_tokens, num_merges):
    """Train a simple BPE tokenizer."""
    # Represent each word as a tuple of characters + end-of-word marker
    vocab = Counter()
    for word, freq in corpus_tokens.items():
        vocab[tuple(word) + ('',)] = freq
    
    merges = []
    for _ in range(num_merges):
        pairs = Counter()
        for word, freq in vocab.items():
            for i in range(len(word) - 1):
                pairs[(word[i], word[i+1])] += freq
        if not pairs:
            break
        best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)
        merges.append(best)
        new_vocab = {}
        for word, freq in vocab.items():
            new_word = []
            i = 0
            while i < len(word):
                if i < len(word)-1 and (word[i], word[i+1]) == best:
                    new_word.append(word[i] + word[i+1])
                    i += 2
                else:
                    new_word.append(word[i])
                    i += 1
            new_vocab[tuple(new_word)] = freq
        vocab = new_vocab
    return merges, vocab

title: Vocabulary
summary: The fixed, finite set of all tokens that a language model can recognize and generate, defined during tokenizer training and remaining immutable during model training and inference.

papers.citation: 1990s · Various (statistical NLP literature)

sections.body:
The fixed, finite set of all tokens that a language model can recognize and generate, defined during tokenizer training and remaining immutable during model training and inference.

The vocabulary \( V \) determines the discrete output space of the model. At each generation step, the model produces a probability distribution over \( |V| \) tokens via a softmax over logits:

\[ P(w_t | w_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{head}} \, h_t) \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|} \]

where \( W_{\text{head}} \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \) is the language model head (often tied with the embedding matrix \( E \)) and \( h_t \in \mathbb{R}^d \) is the hidden state at position \( t \).

sections.title: Vocabulary sizes across major models
sections.body:
Model \( |V| \) Tokenizer
GPT-2 50,257 Byte-level BPE
BERT 30,522 WordPiece
LLaMA 2 32,000 SentencePiece BPE
GPT-4 ~100,000 cl100k_base BPE

sections.title: Design trade-offs of vocabulary size
sections.body:
• Larger \( |V| \) → shorter sequences: fewer tokens per text, reducing attention cost (\( O(n^2) \)) and improving throughput. But the embedding matrix \( E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \) and the softmax computation both grow linearly with \( |V| \).
  • Smaller \( |V| \) → longer sequences: more tokens per text, increasing compute but allowing a smaller embedding table and faster softmax.
  • Embedding weight tying: many models tie \( W_{\text{head}} = E^T \), so the embedding matrix does double duty. This reduces total parameters by \( |V| \times d \) but couples input and output representations.

Multilinguality. The vocabulary determines how efficiently different languages are encoded. A tokenizer trained predominantly on English may fragment Chinese or Arabic text into individual bytes, producing sequences 3–6x longer than English for the same semantic content. This "tokenization tax" means the model consumes more context window and more compute for non-English text. Multilingual models (mT5, BLOOM) address this by training tokenizers on balanced multilingual corpora.

Vocabulary extension. After model training, adding new tokens (e.g., for a domain-specific fine-tune) requires expanding the embedding matrix and the LM head, initializing new rows randomly or by averaging related token embeddings. This is how models are adapted for specialized vocabularies (code, chemistry, legal) without full retraining.

title: Corpus
summary: A large, structured collection of text used for training or evaluating language models and NLP systems. The quality, size, and composition of the training corpus fundamentally determine what the model learns and how it behaves.

papers.citation: 1961 · W. Nelson Francis, Henry Kučera (Brown Corpus)

sections.body:
A large, structured collection of text used for training or evaluating language models and NLP systems. The quality, size, and composition of the training corpus fundamentally determine what the model learns and how it behaves.

The corpus (plural: corpora) is the raw material from which statistical and neural language models learn. Before a model sees any text, the corpus must be assembled, cleaned, and tokenized — each step introducing design choices that propagate into model behavior.

Key dimensions of corpus design

Dimension Description Example
Scale Token count; modern LLMs train on trillions LLaMA 2: ~2T tokens
Diversity Mix of domains (web, books, code, papers) The Pile: 22 sub-corpora
Curation Deduplication, toxicity filtering, quality scoring C4: heuristic quality filters on Common Crawl
Data mixture Relative proportion of each source — a critical hyperparameter Chinchilla-optimal ratios

Scaling laws and corpus size. Hoffmann et al. (2022) showed that for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale roughly equally — doubling parameters requires doubling data. This means corpus size is not just "more is better" but must be balanced against model capacity. Training a 70B model on only 300B tokens wastes compute; the Chinchilla-optimal recipe calls for ~1.4T tokens at that scale.

Curation pipeline. A typical preprocessing pipeline for web-scraped corpora includes: (1) language identification, (2) deduplication via MinHash or exact n-gram matching — Penedo et al. found ~30% of Common Crawl is near-duplicate, (3) heuristic quality filters (line length, symbol ratios, perplexity scoring against a clean reference model), and (4) domain-specific filtering for toxic or personally identifiable content.

sections.title: Notable corpora in the history of NLP
sections.body:
• Brown Corpus (1961): first digital English corpus, 1M words, 500 texts across 15 genres
  • Penn Treebank (1993): ~1M words of Wall Street Journal text with syntactic parse annotations — the standard benchmark for n-gram and early neural LMs
  • Wikipedia dumps: ~4B words of encyclopedic text, used in nearly every LLM pretraining mix
  • Common Crawl: petabytes of raw web text; the basis for C4, OSCAR, and most large-scale corpora
  • The Pile (2020): 800GB diverse text explicitly designed for LLM training, including Arxiv, GitHub, PubMed, and StackExchange
  • RedPajama (2023): open reproduction of LLaMA's training data, ~1.2T tokens

Relationship to tokenization. The corpus and tokenizer are co-dependent: BPE and SentencePiece vocabularies are learned from the training corpus itself, so the token distribution reflects corpus composition. A corpus heavy on code will produce a tokenizer with many code-specific merges; switching corpora without retraining the tokenizer degrades efficiency.

title: N-gram
summary: A contiguous sequence of \( n \) tokens from a text, used as the fundamental unit in classical statistical language models and many NLP features.

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
A contiguous sequence of \( n \) tokens from a text, used as the fundamental unit in classical statistical language models and many NLP features.

For a sequence of tokens \( w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T \), the n-grams are all subsequences of length \( n \). Common sizes have specific names:

\( n \) Name Example ("the cat sat")
1 Unigram the, cat, sat
2 Bigram (the, cat), (cat, sat)
3 Trigram (the, cat, sat)

In an n-gram language model, the probability of the next word is conditioned on the preceding \( n-1 \) words via the Markov assumption:

\[ P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_{t-1}) \]

Probabilities are estimated by counting: \( P(w_t \mid w_{t-2}, w_{t-1}) = \frac{C(w_{t-2}, w_{t-1}, w_t)}{C(w_{t-2}, w_{t-1})} \). The fundamental limitation is data sparsity — most n-grams for \( n \geq 3 \) are never observed in any finite corpus, requiring smoothing techniques (Laplace, Kneser-Ney). Neural language models eliminated this bottleneck by operating in continuous embedding space rather than discrete count space.

Python 
from collections import Counter

def extract_ngrams(tokens, n):
    return [tuple(tokens[i:i+n]) for i in range(len(tokens) - n + 1)]

def ngram_probs(tokens, n):
    ngrams = Counter(extract_ngrams(tokens, n))
    context = Counter(extract_ngrams(tokens, n - 1))
    probs = {}
    for ng, count in ngrams.items():
        probs[ng] = count / context[ng[:-1]]
    return probs

title: Count-based language model
summary: A language model that estimates the probability of a word sequence by counting n-gram occurrences in a corpus and applying smoothing to handle unseen sequences. Count-based models dominated NLP from the 1980s through the early 2010s.

papers.citation: 1995 · Reinhard Kneser, Hermann Ney · Improved backing-off for M-gram language modeling

sections.body:
A language model that estimates the probability of a word sequence by counting n-gram occurrences in a corpus and applying smoothing to handle unseen sequences. Count-based models dominated NLP from the 1980s through the early 2010s.

The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) for an n-gram probability is:

\[ P_{\text{MLE}}(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_{t-1}) = \frac{C(w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_t)}{C(w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_{t-1})} \]

where \( C(\cdot) \) denotes the raw count in the training corpus. This assigns zero probability to any n-gram not observed during training — a fatal flaw because a single unseen bigram makes the entire sequence probability zero, and perplexity becomes infinite.

Smoothing techniques

Smoothing redistributes probability mass from observed events to unseen events:

Method Mechanism Formula (bigram case)
Add-one (Laplace) Add 1 to every count \( \frac{C(w_{t-1}, w_t) + 1}{C(w_{t-1}) + V} \)
Add-\( k \) Add \( k < 1 \) to every count \( \frac{C(w_{t-1}, w_t) + k}{C(w_{t-1}) + kV} \)
Good-Turing Re-estimate counts using frequency of frequencies \( N_c \) \( c^* = (c+1) \frac{N_{c+1}}{N_c} \)
Kneser-Ney Absolute discounting + novel back-off distribution \( \frac{\max(C(w_{t-1}, w_t) - d, 0)}{C(w_{t-1})} + \lambda \, P_{\text{KN}}(w_t) \)

Kneser-Ney smoothing (1995) is widely regarded as the best n-gram smoothing method. Its key insight is that the back-off (lower-order) distribution should not use the raw unigram frequency but rather the continuation probability — how many distinct contexts a word appears in:

\[ P_{\text{KN}}(w) = \frac{|\{w' : C(w', w) > 0\}|}{|\{(w', w'') : C(w', w'') > 0\}|} \]

This captures the intuition that a word like "Francisco" has a high raw count but appears in very few contexts (mostly after "San"), so it should have low back-off probability. The discount \( d \) is typically estimated via the formula \( d = \frac{n_1}{n_1 + 2n_2} \) where \( n_1, n_2 \) are the counts of n-grams occurring exactly once or twice.

Interpolation vs. back-off. Interpolation (Jelinek-Mercer) always mixes higher- and lower-order estimates: \( P_{\text{interp}} = \lambda_n P_n + (1 - \lambda_n) P_{n-1} \). Back-off (Katz) uses the lower-order model only when the higher-order count is zero. Modified Kneser-Ney uses interpolation and is the standard in practice.

Limitations and the transition to neural models. Even with sophisticated smoothing, count-based models cannot generalize to semantically similar but lexically distinct contexts — "cat sat on the mat" tells a trigram model nothing about "dog sat on the rug." Neural language models (Bengio et al., 2003) addressed this by mapping words to continuous embeddings where similar words share similar representations, eliminating the sparsity bottleneck entirely.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Why MLE assigns zero to unseen n-grams: If \( C(w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_t) = 0 \), then \( P_{\text{MLE}}(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1:t-1}) = \frac{0}{C(w_{t-n+1:t-1})} = 0 \). For the full sequence probability \( P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_t P(w_t \mid \text{context}) \), a single zero factor makes the entire product zero and the log-likelihood \( -\infty \), so perplexity is undefined.

Laplace smoothing derivation: Adding a pseudocount of 1 to each of the \( V \) possible continuations gives \( P_{\text{Lap}}(w_t \mid w_{t-1}) = \frac{C(w_{t-1}, w_t) + 1}{\sum_{w} (C(w_{t-1}, w) + 1)} = \frac{C(w_{t-1}, w_t) + 1}{C(w_{t-1}) + V} \). This over-smooths dramatically for large \( V \) — with \( V = 50{,}000 \), even frequent bigrams lose most of their probability mass to unseen events.

title: Language model
summary: A probabilistic model that assigns a probability to any sequence of tokens, capturing the statistical structure of natural language. Language models are the foundation of modern NLP — the same framework underlies n-gram models, LSTMs, an…

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
A probabilistic model that assigns a probability to any sequence of tokens, capturing the statistical structure of natural language. Language models are the foundation of modern NLP — the same framework underlies n-gram models, LSTMs, and large Transformer-based LLMs.

Formally, a language model defines a probability distribution over sequences \( w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T \). By the chain rule of probability, this joint distribution factorizes exactly as:

\[ P(w_1, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) \]

Different model families approximate the conditional distributions differently:

Type Conditioning Era Example
N-gram \( P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1:t-1}) \) 1980s–2000s Kneser-Ney trigram
Neural (feedforward) \( P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1:t-1}; \theta) \) 2003 Bengio NNLM
Recurrent \( P(w_t \mid h_t; \theta) \), \( h_t = f(h_{t-1}, w_{t-1}) \) 2010–2017 LSTM-LM
Autoregressive Transformer \( P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}; \theta) \) via causal attention 2018–present GPT, LLaMA, Claude
Masked \( P(w_t \mid w_{\backslash t}; \theta) \) 2019–present BERT, RoBERTa

Training objective. Nearly all language models are trained by maximum likelihood estimation — equivalently, minimizing the negative log-likelihood (NLL) or cross-entropy loss:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid \text{context}; \theta) \]

The standard intrinsic evaluation metric is perplexity: \( \text{PPL} = \exp(\mathcal{L}) \), which measures how many tokens the model is effectively choosing between at each step.

The scaling hypothesis. The surprising finding of the scaling era (Kaplan et al., 2020) is that the simple objective of next-token prediction, applied at sufficient scale in parameters, data, and compute, produces models with emergent capabilities: in-context learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, and broad world knowledge. This was not predicted by the classical NLP community and has made "language model" nearly synonymous with "foundation model" in modern usage.

Historical context. Shannon (1948) first formalized language as a stochastic process and estimated English entropy via n-gram statistics. Jelinek and colleagues at IBM (1970s–80s) built the first practical LMs for speech recognition. Bengio et al. (2003) introduced neural language models with learned embeddings. The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) eliminated the sequential bottleneck of RNNs, enabling the massive parallelism that made scaling to billions of parameters feasible.

title: Autoregressive language model
summary: A language model that generates text left-to-right by predicting each token conditioned on all preceding tokens, with no access to future context. All GPT-family models, LLaMA, and Claude are autoregressive.

papers.citation: 2018 · Alec Radford et al. (GPT-1) · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training

sections.body:
A language model that generates text left-to-right by predicting each token conditioned on all preceding tokens, with no access to future context. All GPT-family models, LLaMA, and Claude are autoregressive.

An autoregressive model factorizes the joint probability using the chain rule in the forward (left-to-right) direction:

\[ P(w_1, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}; \theta) \]

This is enforced architecturally via causal masking in the self-attention mechanism. The attention mask \( M \) is a lower-triangular matrix:

\[ M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases} \]

so that after softmax, position \( i \) attends only to positions \( \leq i \). This ensures that the model's output at position \( t \) is a function only of \( w_{1:t} \), preserving the autoregressive property.

Training. The training objective is next-token prediction (cross-entropy loss over the vocabulary at each position). Because every token in the sequence provides a training signal, autoregressive models are highly data-efficient compared to masked LMs which only predict ~15% of tokens. The loss is:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}; \theta) \]

Inference. Generation proceeds token by token: the model computes \( P(\cdot \mid w_{1:t-1}) \), samples or greedily selects \( w_t \), appends it to the sequence, and repeats. This sequential process means generating \( T \) tokens requires \( T \) forward passes. The KV-cache optimization avoids recomputing attention for previous positions, reducing each step from \( O(T) \) to \( O(1) \) in new computation, but memory grows linearly with sequence length.

Decoding strategies control how tokens are selected from the predicted distribution:

Strategy Mechanism
Greedy \( w_t = \arg\max P(\cdot \mid w_{1:t-1}) \)
Temperature sampling \( P'(w) \propto P(w)^{1/\tau} \); \( \tau < 1 \) sharpens, \( \tau > 1 \) flattens
Top-\( k \) Sample from the \( k \) highest-probability tokens only
Nucleus (top-\( p \)) Sample from smallest set whose cumulative probability \( \geq p \)

Advantages over masked LMs: Autoregressive models naturally support open-ended generation, can be evaluated via perplexity on any text, and scale more predictably. The key limitation is that generation is inherently sequential, and the model cannot "look ahead" — it must commit to each token before seeing what comes next, which can lead to locally optimal but globally suboptimal sequences.

title: Masked language model
summary: A language model trained to predict randomly masked tokens in a sequence using bidirectional context — both the left and right surrounding tokens. Introduced by BERT (2019), MLMs produce rich contextual representations that excel at unde…

papers.citation: 2019 · Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova · BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

sections.body:
A language model trained to predict randomly masked tokens in a sequence using bidirectional context — both the left and right surrounding tokens. Introduced by BERT (2019), MLMs produce rich contextual representations that excel at understanding tasks.

The masked language model (MLM) objective randomly replaces a fraction (typically 15%) of input tokens with a special [MASK] token and trains the model to reconstruct them:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{t \in \mathcal{M}} \log P(w_t \mid w_{\backslash \mathcal{M}}; \theta) \]

where \( \mathcal{M} \) is the set of masked positions and \( w_{\backslash \mathcal{M}} \) denotes all unmasked tokens. Because the model attends to context in both directions (no causal mask), each representation at position \( t \) encodes information from the entire sequence — a strictly richer signal than the left-only context of autoregressive models.

BERT's masking strategy. Of the 15% selected tokens: 80% are replaced with [MASK], 10% with a random token, and 10% kept unchanged. This design mitigates the train/test mismatch since [MASK] never appears at inference time. The 10% random replacement forces the model to maintain a distributional representation at every position, not just masked ones.

Architecture. BERT uses a standard Transformer encoder (no decoder) with bidirectional self-attention. BERT-base has 12 layers, 768 hidden dimensions, and 12 attention heads (110M parameters); BERT-large has 24 layers, 1024 hidden dimensions, and 16 heads (340M parameters). It also uses a second pretraining objective, next sentence prediction (NSP), which was later shown to be unnecessary — RoBERTa (2019) dropped NSP, trained longer on more data, and consistently outperformed BERT.

sections.title: Comparison with autoregressive models
sections.body:
Property MLM (BERT) Autoregressive (GPT)
Context Bidirectional Left-to-right only
Training signal ~15% of tokens 100% of tokens
Generation Requires iterative re-masking Natural (token-by-token)
Best for Classification, NER, extractive QA Open-ended generation, dialogue

Downstream use. MLMs are typically fine-tuned by adding a task-specific head (linear layer) on top of the [CLS] token representation or the per-token representations. The pretrained bidirectional representations transfer remarkably well — BERT achieved state-of-the-art on 11 NLP benchmarks upon release, demonstrating that unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised fine-tuning (the "pretrain-finetune" paradigm) was superior to task-specific architectures.

title: Causal language model
summary: A language model that predicts each token using only the tokens that precede it, enforcing a strict left-to-right causal ordering — synonymous with "autoregressive language model." The term "causal" refers to the causal mask applied in s…

papers.citation: 2018 · Alec Radford et al. · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training

sections.body:
A language model that predicts each token using only the tokens that precede it, enforcing a strict left-to-right causal ordering — synonymous with "autoregressive language model." The term "causal" refers to the causal mask applied in self-attention.

The term "causal" comes from the causal (lower-triangular) mask applied to the self-attention scores before softmax. For a sequence of length \( T \), the mask matrix is:

\[ M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases} \]

Adding \( M \) to the raw attention logits before softmax ensures that \( \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V \), so position \( i \) attends only to positions \( \leq i \). This enforces the autoregressive property: the model's output at position \( t \) depends only on \( w_{1:t} \), matching the chain rule factorization \( P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \).

sections.title: Causal vs. masked language models — an architectural distinction
sections.body:
Property Causal LM (GPT) Masked LM (BERT)
Attention pattern Unidirectional (lower-triangular mask) Bidirectional (no mask)
Training signal Every token is predicted (100%) Only ~15% masked tokens
Generation Natural token-by-token sampling Requires iterative re-masking
Density estimation Exact log-likelihood via chain rule No tractable likelihood
Dominant use Open-ended generation, LLMs Classification, NER, extractive QA

Why "causal" matters beyond naming. The causal mask is what makes these models valid density estimators — they define a proper probability distribution over sequences because the chain rule factorization is exact. This enables principled evaluation via perplexity and log-likelihood, which masked LMs cannot compute tractably. It also means causal LMs can be used for importance sampling, sequence scoring, and other probabilistic inference tasks.

Prefix LM variant. Some models (e.g., T5 encoder, UL2) use a prefix language model that allows bidirectional attention over a prefix portion of the input and causal attention over the rest. This hybrid approach gives richer representations for conditioning context while retaining autoregressive generation for the output.

Causal LMs have become the dominant paradigm for general-purpose AI, as the same architecture and objective support both understanding (via prompting) and generation — a unification that masked LMs cannot achieve.

title: Chat language model
summary: A language model fine-tuned to follow instructions and engage in multi-turn dialogue, typically produced by applying supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference-based alignment (RLHF/DPO) to a pretrained base model. ChatGPT (2022) popula…

papers.citation: 2022 · OpenAI (ChatGPT / InstructGPT) · Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

sections.body:
A language model fine-tuned to follow instructions and engage in multi-turn dialogue, typically produced by applying supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference-based alignment (RLHF/DPO) to a pretrained base model. ChatGPT (2022) popularized this paradigm.

A chat model extends a base causal LM through a multi-stage post-training pipeline:

  1. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): Train on curated (instruction, response) pairs to teach the model to follow prompts rather than merely complete text. Datasets typically contain 10K–100K high-quality examples written by human annotators.
  2. Reward model training: Train a separate model to predict human preferences given pairs of responses to the same prompt. The reward model \( R(x, y) \) scores response \( y \) to prompt \( x \).
  3. Preference alignment: Optimize the policy (chat model) against the reward model using one of:
    • RLHF (PPO): maximize \( \mathbb{E}[R(x, y)] - \beta \, D_{\text{KL}}(\pi_\theta \| \pi_{\text{ref}}) \), where the KL penalty prevents the model from drifting too far from the SFT policy
    • DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): reparameterizes RLHF to eliminate the reward model, directly optimizing: \( \mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l \mid x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l \mid x)}\right) \) where \( y_w, y_l \) are the preferred and dispreferred responses

Chat template / prompt format. Chat models use a structured prompting format that distinguishes roles within the token stream — typically system, user, and assistant turns, delimited by special tokens. Examples:

Format Delimiters Used by
ChatML <|im_start|>role ... <|im_end|> OpenAI models
Llama format [INST] ... [/INST] LLaMA 2 Chat
Alpaca ### Instruction: / ### Response: Stanford Alpaca

This format is baked into the fine-tuning data so the model learns to condition its behavior on role boundaries. Misformatting the template at inference time (e.g., omitting special tokens) can severely degrade performance.

Base vs. chat model. The base model and chat model share identical architectures and parameter counts — the difference is entirely in the training data distribution of the post-training stages. This means chat models sacrifice some raw completion ability (they may refuse to simply continue a passage) in exchange for instruction-following, safety behaviors, and conversational coherence. The "alignment tax" — the performance cost of alignment — is generally small on benchmarks but noticeable in unconventional prompting scenarios.

title: Perplexity
summary: The standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models, measuring how "surprised" the model is by a held-out test sequence — lower perplexity indicates a better model.

papers.citation: 1977 · Frederick Jelinek et al. (IBM speech recognition group)

sections.body:
The standard intrinsic evaluation metric for language models, measuring how "surprised" the model is by a held-out test sequence — lower perplexity indicates a better model.

Perplexity is defined as the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood per token:

\[ \text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})\right) \]

Equivalently, \( \text{PPL} = 2^{H(P, \hat{P})} \) when using base-2 logarithms, where \( H(P, \hat{P}) \) is the cross-entropy between the true data distribution \( P \) and the model's distribution \( \hat{P} \). The connection to information theory is direct: cross-entropy measures the average number of bits needed to encode data from \( P \) using the code designed for \( \hat{P} \), and perplexity is the effective vocabulary size that coding cost implies.

Intuition. A perplexity of \( k \) means the model is as uncertain as if it were choosing uniformly among \( k \) tokens at each step. A perfect model on deterministic text achieves PPL = 1 (no uncertainty). A uniform distribution over a vocabulary of size \( V \) gives PPL = \( V \).

sections.title: Historical benchmarks on Penn Treebank
sections.body:
Model PPL Year
Kneser-Ney 5-gram ~141 1990s
LSTM (Zaremba et al.) ~58 2014
AWD-LSTM (Merity et al.) ~52 2018
Transformer-XL ~18 2019

sections.title: Important caveats and limitations
sections.body:
• Tokenization dependence: PPL is only comparable across models using the same vocabulary and tokenizer. Different tokenizers produce different token counts \( T \), making raw PPL values incomparable. A model using BPE with 50K merges will report different PPL than one using 32K merges on the same text, even if they assign identical probabilities to the raw character sequence.
  • Bits-per-character (BPC): To enable cross-tokenizer comparison, BPC normalizes by character count: \( \text{BPC} = -\frac{1}{C} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log_2 P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \) where \( C \) is the total character count. This is tokenizer-invariant.
  • PPL vs. downstream performance: Lower perplexity generally correlates with better downstream task performance, but the correlation is imperfect — a model can achieve low PPL by excelling at predicting function words while struggling with factual content.
  • Masked LMs: Perplexity is not directly defined for masked language models like BERT, which do not define a proper autoregressive distribution over sequences. Pseudo-perplexity approximations exist but are not standard.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

From cross-entropy to perplexity. The cross-entropy of a model \( \hat{P} \) with respect to the true distribution \( P \) over sequences is:

\[ H(P, \hat{P}) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log \hat{P}(x) \]

In practice, we approximate this with the empirical cross-entropy over a test corpus of \( T \) tokens:

\[ \hat{H} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log_2 P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \]

Perplexity is then \( \text{PPL} = 2^{\hat{H}} \). Using natural logs: \( \text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ln P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})\right) \). The two are equivalent since \( 2^{x / \ln 2} = e^x \).

title: Log-likelihood
summary: The natural logarithm of the likelihood function, converting the product of probabilities into a sum for numerical stability and algebraic convenience in optimization. Maximizing the log-likelihood is the most common training objective i…

papers.citation: 1922 · R.A. Fisher · On the Mathematical Foundations of Theoretical Statistics

sections.body:
The natural logarithm of the likelihood function, converting the product of probabilities into a sum for numerical stability and algebraic convenience in optimization. Maximizing the log-likelihood is the most common training objective in machine learning.

Given a dataset \( \mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\} \) assumed i.i.d. from some distribution, and a model with parameters \( \theta \), the likelihood is:

\[ L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta) \]

The log-likelihood is:

\[ \ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta) \]

The logarithm is strictly monotonically increasing, so \( \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta) = \arg\max_\theta L(\theta) \). The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) is \( \hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta) \).

sections.title: Why the log transform is essential
sections.body:
• Numerical stability: Multiplying many small probabilities causes floating-point underflow. For \( N = 1000 \) data points each with probability 0.01, the likelihood is \( 10^{-2000} \) — far below the smallest representable float64 (\( \approx 10^{-308} \)). The log-likelihood is simply \( -2000 \log 10 \approx -4605 \), perfectly representable.
  • Gradient tractability: Sums have simpler derivatives than products. The gradient of the log-likelihood is \( \nabla_\theta \ell = \sum_{i=1}^N \nabla_\theta \log P(x_i \mid \theta) \), which decomposes into per-example contributions — essential for stochastic gradient descent.
  • Connection to information theory: The average negative log-likelihood equals the cross-entropy: \( -\frac{1}{N} \ell(\theta) \to H(P_{\text{data}}, P_\theta) \) as \( N \to \infty \). Minimizing NLL is equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence \( D_{\text{KL}}(P_{\text{data}} \| P_\theta) \) plus a constant.

In language modeling: For a sequence \( w_1, \ldots, w_T \), the log-likelihood under an autoregressive model is:

\[ \ell(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}; \theta) \]
