Training minimizes the negative log-likelihood (NLL), and the per-token NLL exponentiated gives perplexity: \( \text{PPL} = \exp(-\ell / T) \).

Regularized variants. In Bayesian and penalized maximum likelihood, a log-prior or penalty is added: \( \ell_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \ell(\theta) + \log P(\theta) \). For a Gaussian prior \( P(\theta) \propto \exp(-\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\theta\|^2) \), this recovers L2 regularization (weight decay). MAP estimation maximizes this regularized objective.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

MLE for a Gaussian: Given \( x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \):

\[ \ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2 \]

Setting \( \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = 0 \): \( \hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i \) (the sample mean).

Setting \( \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = 0 \): \( \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{\mu})^2 \) (the biased sample variance).

NLL equals cross-entropy: As \( N \to \infty \), \( -\frac{1}{N}\ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log P_\theta(x_i) \to \mathbb{E}_{P_{\text{data}}}[-\log P_\theta(x)] = H(P_{\text{data}}, P_\theta) \). Since \( H(P_{\text{data}}, P_\theta) = H(P_{\text{data}}) + D_{\text{KL}}(P_{\text{data}} \| P_\theta) \) and the entropy \( H(P_{\text{data}}) \) is constant w.r.t. \( \theta \), minimizing NLL minimizes the KL divergence.

title: Negative log-likelihood
summary: The negation of the log-likelihood, used as a loss function so that maximizing likelihood is recast as a minimization problem compatible with gradient descent — the dominant training objective across deep learning.

papers.citation: 1922 · R.A. Fisher

sections.body:
The negation of the log-likelihood, used as a loss function so that maximizing likelihood is recast as a minimization problem compatible with gradient descent — the dominant training objective across deep learning.

Given a dataset \( \mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\} \) drawn i.i.d. from an unknown distribution, and a parametric model \( P_\theta \), the NLL loss is:

\[ \text{NLL}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i) \]

For supervised classification, where the model outputs a probability vector \( \hat{y} = \text{softmax}(z) \) and the true label is class \( c \), the per-sample NLL reduces to:

\[ \ell = -\log \hat{y}_c = -\log P_\theta(y = c \mid x) \]

which is exactly the categorical cross-entropy loss with one-hot targets. The connection to information theory is direct: minimizing NLL is equivalent to minimizing the cross-entropy \( H(p_{\text{data}}, p_\theta) \), which in turn equals \( H(p_{\text{data}}) + D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \| p_\theta) \). Since the data entropy is constant w.r.t. \( \theta \), NLL minimization is equivalent to KL divergence minimization.

NLL in language modeling. For autoregressive language models (including Transformers like GPT), the training objective is the average per-token NLL over a sequence \( w_1, \ldots, w_T \):

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \]

Perplexity — the standard evaluation metric for language models — is the exponentiated average NLL: \( \text{PPL} = \exp(\mathcal{L}) \). A perplexity of \( k \) means the model is on average as uncertain as a uniform distribution over \( k \) tokens.

Numerical considerations. In practice, NLL is computed via log_softmax rather than taking \( \log(\text{softmax}(z)) \) separately, because the latter is numerically unstable: for large logits, softmax saturates and the log of a near-zero value underflows. The log-sum-exp trick avoids this: \( \log \text{softmax}(z_c) = z_c - \log \sum_k e^{z_k} \).

Gradient. The gradient of per-sample NLL w.r.t. the logits \( z \) has the elegant form \( \nabla_z \ell = \hat{y} - y \), where \( y \) is the one-hot target — making it cheap to compute and well-behaved early in training.

title: Maximum likelihood estimate (MLE)
summary: A parameter estimation method that selects the parameter values under which the observed data is most probable, providing the theoretical foundation for most neural network training objectives.

papers.citation: 1922 · R.A. Fisher · On the Mathematical Foundations of Theoretical Statistics

sections.body:
A parameter estimation method that selects the parameter values under which the observed data is most probable, providing the theoretical foundation for most neural network training objectives.

Given observations \( \mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\} \) assumed to be i.i.d. from a distribution \( P(x \mid \theta) \), the MLE is:

\[ \hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x_i \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid \theta) \]

The log transformation converts the product into a sum (more numerically stable and analytically tractable) and preserves the argmax since log is monotonic.

sections.title: Key statistical properties
sections.body:
• Consistency: \( \hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \xrightarrow{p} \theta^* \) as \( N \to \infty \) — the estimate converges to the true parameter
  • Asymptotic efficiency: achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound — no unbiased estimator has lower variance for large \( N \)
  • Asymptotic normality: \( \sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} - \theta^*) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, I(\theta^*)^{-1}) \) where \( I(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial \theta^2} \log P(x \mid \theta)\right] \) is the Fisher information
  • Invariance: if \( \hat{\theta} \) is the MLE of \( \theta \), then \( g(\hat{\theta}) \) is the MLE of \( g(\theta) \) for any function \( g \)

MLE and deep learning. Training a neural network with cross-entropy loss is precisely MLE: minimizing NLL is maximizing the likelihood of the training data under the model. This holds for classifiers (categorical cross-entropy), language models (per-token NLL), and regression with Gaussian noise assumptions (MSE loss corresponds to MLE under \( P(y \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(f_\theta(x), \sigma^2) \)).

Limitations and extensions. MLE has no built-in regularization and can overfit on finite data, motivating several extensions:

Method Modification Effect
MAP estimation \( \arg\max_\theta P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta) \) Gaussian prior \( \Rightarrow \) L2 regularization
Full Bayesian Integrate over \( P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \) Uncertainty estimates, better calibration
Penalized MLE \( \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta) - \lambda \Omega(\theta) \) Laplace prior \( \Rightarrow \) L1 / sparsity

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

MLE for Gaussian mean: Given \( x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \) with known \( \sigma^2 \):

\[ \ell(\mu) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \mu)^2 \]

Setting \( \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \mu) = 0 \) gives:

\[ \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N x_i = \bar{x} \]

the sample mean — confirming that MLE recovers the intuitive estimator for Gaussian data.

title: Laplace smoothing
summary: A simple smoothing technique that adds a small constant to every count in a probability estimate, ensuring no event has zero probability — the earliest and most intuitive form of Bayesian regularization for discrete distributions.

papers.citation: 1814 · Pierre-Simon Laplace · Essai philosophique sur les probabilités

sections.body:
A simple smoothing technique that adds a small constant to every count in a probability estimate, ensuring no event has zero probability — the earliest and most intuitive form of Bayesian regularization for discrete distributions.

For a categorical distribution with \( K \) outcomes observed \( N \) times with counts \( C_1, \ldots, C_K \), the MLE probability is \( \hat{p}_k = C_k / N \). Laplace smoothing (add-\( \alpha \)) modifies this to:

\[ \hat{p}_k = \frac{C_k + \alpha}{N + \alpha K} \]

where \( \alpha > 0 \) is the smoothing parameter. With \( \alpha = 1 \) (add-one smoothing), this is the MAP estimate under a symmetric Dirichlet prior \( \text{Dir}(\alpha, \ldots, \alpha) \). The denominator \( N + \alpha K \) ensures normalization: \( \sum_k \hat{p}_k = 1 \).

Bayesian interpretation. The Dirichlet distribution is the conjugate prior for the categorical/multinomial likelihood. The posterior is \( \text{Dir}(C_1 + \alpha, \ldots, C_K + \alpha) \), and Laplace smoothing returns its mode (MAP estimate). The hyperparameter \( \alpha \) controls the strength of the uniform prior: \( \alpha \to 0 \) recovers MLE, while \( \alpha \to \infty \) forces a uniform distribution regardless of the data.

Limitations in language modeling. For n-gram models with vocabulary \( |V| \), Laplace smoothing is considered crude because it steals too much probability mass from observed events and redistributes it uniformly across \( |V|^n \) possible n-grams — most of which will never occur in natural language. Concrete example: in a bigram model with \( |V| = 50{,}000 \), add-one smoothing divides mass among 2.5 billion bigrams, dramatically underestimating observed bigram probabilities.

sections.title: Comparison with better smoothing methods
sections.body:
Method Idea Advantage over Laplace
Good-Turing Re-estimate counts using frequency of frequencies Data-driven discount, no fixed \( \alpha \)
Kneser-Ney Absolute discounting + continuation probability Context-aware backoff distribution
Interpolation Weighted mix of n-gram orders Always uses all available information

Despite these limitations, Laplace smoothing remains the standard choice for Naive Bayes classifiers, where the feature space is typically small enough that the uniform redistribution is acceptable, and its Bayesian interpretation as a Dirichlet prior is a pedagogical advantage.

title: Conditional probability
summary: The probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred, forming the mathematical basis of Bayesian inference and all autoregressive sequence models.

papers.citation: 1763 · Thomas Bayes · An Essay towards Solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances

sections.body:
The probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred, forming the mathematical basis of Bayesian inference and all autoregressive sequence models.

The conditional probability of event ( A ) given event ( B ) is defined as:

[ P(A mid B) = rac{P(A cap B)}{P(B)}, quad P(B) > 0 ]

This definition directly yields the product rule: ( P(A cap B) = P(A mid B), P(B) ), and by repeated application, the chain rule of probability:

[ P(x_1, ldots, x_n) = prod_{t=1}^{n} P(x_t mid x_1, ldots, x_{t-1}) ]

This factorization is exact and assumption-free — it holds for any joint distribution. It is the mathematical justification for autoregressive language modeling, where a Transformer generates tokens left-to-right: ( P(w_{1:T}) = prod_{t=1}^{T} P_ heta(w_t mid w_{1:t-1}) ).

Bayes' theorem follows directly by applying the definition symmetrically:

\[ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\, P(A)}{P(B)} \]

This "inversion" of conditioning is the foundation of Bayesian inference: given a prior \( P(\theta) \) and likelihood \( P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \), we obtain the posterior \( P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\, P(\theta) \). The denominator \( P(\mathcal{D}) = \int P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)\, d\theta \) is the marginal likelihood (model evidence), often intractable for complex models.

Independence and conditional independence. Events \( A \) and \( B \) are independent iff \( P(A \mid B) = P(A) \), meaning observing \( B \) provides no information about \( A \). Conditional independence — \( P(A \mid B, C) = P(A \mid C) \) — is the structural assumption behind graphical models, Naive Bayes (features independent given class), and Markov chains (future independent of past given present).

Discriminative vs. generative models differ in which conditional they model:

Approach Models Examples
Discriminative \( P(y \mid x) \) directly Logistic regression, BERT classifier
Generative \( P(x, y) = P(x \mid y)\, P(y) \) Naive Bayes, GPT, VAEs

title: Probability distribution
summary: A mathematical function that assigns probabilities to all possible outcomes of a random variable, fully describing the uncertainty in its value — the central object in statistical machine learning, since models learn to approximate or sa…

papers.citation: 1700s · Various (Bernoulli, de Moivre, Gauss, Kolmogorov)

sections.body:
A mathematical function that assigns probabilities to all possible outcomes of a random variable, fully describing the uncertainty in its value — the central object in statistical machine learning, since models learn to approximate or sample from distributions over data.

A probability distribution \( P \) over a random variable \( X \) must satisfy Kolmogorov's axioms: (1) \( P(x) \geq 0 \) for all outcomes \( x \), and (2) the total probability equals one. The two major types differ in how this normalization is expressed:

Type Described by Normalization ML example
Discrete PMF: \( P(X = x) \) \( \sum_x P(x) = 1 \) Softmax output over vocabulary
Continuous PDF: \( f(x) \) \( \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1 \) Gaussian prior on weights

sections.title: Key distributions and their roles in ML
sections.body:
Distribution Formula Parameters Where it appears
Bernoulli \( P(x) = p^x(1-p)^{1-x} \) \( p \in [0,1] \) Binary classification (sigmoid output)
Categorical \( P(x=k) = p_k \) \( \mathbf{p} \in \Delta^{K-1} \) Multi-class softmax, token prediction
Gaussian \( f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \) \( \mu, \sigma^2 \) Weight init, VAE latent prior, noise model
Uniform \( f(x) = \frac{1}{b-a} \) for \( x \in [a,b] \) \( a, b \) Random init, dropout masks, data augmentation

Moments and summary statistics. Distributions are characterized by their moments: the mean \( \mu = \mathbb{E}[X] \) (first moment), variance \( \sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[(X - \mu)^2] \) (second central moment), skewness (asymmetry), and kurtosis (tail heaviness). For Gaussian distributions, the first two moments fully determine the distribution — a property exploited by the reparameterization trick in VAEs: \( z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon \), where \( \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \).

Measuring distance between distributions is central to training: the KL divergence \( D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = \sum_x p(x) \log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \) measures how much extra information is needed to encode samples from \( p \) using \( q \). Minimizing cross-entropy \( H(p, q) = H(p) + D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) \) with respect to \( q \) is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence, which is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation. Other distances include the Jensen-Shannon divergence (used in GANs) and the Wasserstein distance (used in WGANs).

The probability simplex. For a categorical distribution over \( K \) outcomes, the vector of probabilities \( \mathbf{p} = (p_1, \ldots, p_K) \) lies on the \( (K-1) \)-dimensional probability simplex \( \Delta^{K-1} = \{ \mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^K : p_k \geq 0, \sum_k p_k = 1 \} \). The softmax function maps arbitrary logits \( z \in \mathbb{R}^K \) onto this simplex, and temperature scaling \( \text{softmax}(z / \tau) \) controls the sharpness of the resulting distribution — lower \( \tau \) concentrates mass on the argmax, higher \( \tau \) approaches uniform.

Generative modeling as distribution learning. The goal of most generative models is to learn a parameterized distribution ( P_ heta(x) ) that approximates the true data distribution ( P_{ ext{data}}(x) ). Different model families parameterize this differently: autoregressive models factor the joint via the chain rule ( P_ heta(x) = prod_t P_ heta(x_t mid x_{1:t-1}) ), VAEs introduce a latent variable ( z ) so that ( P_ heta(x) = int P_ heta(x mid z) P(z) , dz ), normalizing flows apply invertible transformations to a base distribution, and diffusion models learn to reverse a noise process.

title: Discrete probability distribution
summary: A probability distribution over a countable set of outcomes, described by a probability mass function (PMF) that assigns a non-negative probability to each outcome summing to one — the distribution family behind every classification outp…

papers.citation: 1713 · Jacob Bernoulli · Ars Conjectandi

sections.body:
A probability distribution over a countable set of outcomes, described by a probability mass function (PMF) that assigns a non-negative probability to each outcome summing to one — the distribution family behind every classification output and token prediction in deep learning.

A discrete random variable \( X \) taking values in \( \{x_1, x_2, \ldots\} \) is characterized by its PMF \( P(X = x_k) = p_k \) where \( p_k \geq 0 \) and \( \sum_k p_k = 1 \). Its expected value and variance are:

\[ \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_k x_k \, p_k, \qquad \text{Var}(X) = \sum_k (x_k - \mathbb{E}[X])^2 \, p_k \]

sections.title: Key discrete distributions in ML
sections.body:
Distribution PMF Mean / Variance ML usage
Bernoulli(\( p \)) \( p^x (1-p)^{1-x} \) \( p \) / \( p(1-p) \) Binary classification, dropout masks
Categorical(\( \mathbf{p} \)) \( P(x = k) = p_k \) — Softmax over \( K \) classes, token prediction
Multinomial(\( n, \mathbf{p} \)) \( \frac{n!}{\prod_k x_k!} \prod_k p_k^{x_k} \) \( np_k \) / \( np_k(1-p_k) \) Bag-of-words, topic models
Poisson(\( \lambda \)) \( \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!} \) \( \lambda \) / \( \lambda \) Count data, event rate modeling
Geometric(\( p \)) \( (1-p)^{k-1} p \) \( 1/p \) / \( (1-p)/p^2 \) Number of trials until first success

The probability simplex. For a categorical distribution over \( K \) outcomes, the parameter vector \( \mathbf{p} = (p_1, \ldots, p_K) \) lies on the \( (K-1) \)-dimensional simplex \( \Delta^{K-1} \). The softmax function \( \text{softmax}(z)_k = e^{z_k} / \sum_j e^{z_j} \) maps unconstrained logits \( z \in \mathbb{R}^K \) onto this simplex, ensuring valid probability outputs.

Entropy of discrete distributions. The Shannon entropy \( H(X) = -\sum_k p_k \log p_k \) quantifies the uncertainty in a discrete distribution. A deterministic distribution (all mass on one outcome) has entropy 0; the uniform distribution over \( K \) outcomes has maximum entropy \( \log K \). In language modeling, perplexity \( = \exp(H) \) converts per-token entropy into an interpretable measure: how many tokens the model is "choosing between" on average.

Sampling from discrete distributions is essential for inference in language models. Common strategies include:

  • Greedy decoding: always pick \( \arg\max_k p_k \) — deterministic but repetitive
  • Temperature sampling: sample from \( \text{softmax}(z / \tau) \) — \( \tau < 1 \) sharpens, \( \tau > 1 \) flattens
  • Top-k sampling: zero out all but the \( k \) highest-probability tokens, then renormalize and sample
  • Nucleus (top-p) sampling: keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds \( p \), then renormalize — adapts the number of candidates to the entropy of the distribution

Conjugate priors. In Bayesian inference, discrete distributions have natural conjugate priors: the Beta distribution is conjugate to the Bernoulli/Binomial, and the Dirichlet distribution is conjugate to the Categorical/Multinomial. This means the posterior has the same functional form as the prior, enabling closed-form Bayesian updates — the mathematical basis of Laplace smoothing.

title: Backoff (N-gram backoff)
summary: A smoothing strategy for n-gram language models that estimates the probability of an unseen n-gram by "backing off" to a shorter, lower-order n-gram that has been observed — a key technique for handling data sparsity in pre-neural langua…

papers.citation: 1987 · Slava Katz · Estimation of Probabilities from Sparse Data for the Language Model Component of a Speech Recognizer

sections.body:
A smoothing strategy for n-gram language models that estimates the probability of an unseen n-gram by "backing off" to a shorter, lower-order n-gram that has been observed — a key technique for handling data sparsity in pre-neural language modeling.

The fundamental problem: in a corpus of even billions of words, most possible n-grams for \( n \geq 3 \) are never observed, yet they may be perfectly valid. An unsmoothed model assigns zero probability to these, making it useless for any downstream task that requires non-zero probabilities (speech recognition, machine translation).

Katz backoff formalizes the intuition of falling back to shorter contexts:

\[ P_{\text{bo}}(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1:t-1}) = \begin{cases} d_{C} \cdot \dfrac{C(w_{t-n+1:t})}{C(w_{t-n+1:t-1})} & \text{if } C(w_{t-n+1:t}) > 0 \\[8pt] \alpha(w_{t-n+1:t-1}) \cdot P_{\text{bo}}(w_t \mid w_{t-n+2:t-1}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

where \( C(\cdot) \) is the count, \( d_C \) is a discount factor derived from Good-Turing estimation that reduces the probability of observed n-grams to free up mass for unseen ones, and \( \alpha(w_{t-n+1:t-1}) \) is a normalization constant ensuring the distribution sums to 1. The recursion bottoms out at the unigram level (or a uniform distribution).

Concrete example. Suppose we have a trigram model and encounter "the cat purrs" for the first time. Katz backoff proceeds:

  1. Look up \( C(\text{the, cat, purrs}) \) — count is 0, so back off
  2. Look up \( C(\text{cat, purrs}) \) — suppose count is 3, so use discounted bigram: \( P_{\text{bo}}(\text{purrs} \mid \text{cat}) = d_3 \cdot 3 / C(\text{cat, *}) \)

sections.title: Backoff vs. interpolation
sections.body:
Strategy Formula When lower-order is used
Backoff Use highest-order n-gram with nonzero count; fall back only if count is 0 Only when higher-order is missing
Interpolation \( P(w_t \mid w_{t-2:t-1}) = \lambda_3 P_3 + \lambda_2 P_2 + \lambda_1 P_1 \) with \( \sum \lambda = 1 \) Always — all orders contribute

Kneser-Ney smoothing is the gold standard for n-gram smoothing and uses absolute discounting (subtract a fixed \( d \approx 0.75 \) from each count) with a modified backoff distribution. The key insight: the lower-order distribution should not be the raw unigram probability but the continuation probability — how many distinct contexts a word appears in. For example, "Francisco" has high unigram count but appears almost exclusively after "San", so its continuation probability is low, correctly reducing its backoff weight.

\[ P_{\text{KN}}(w \mid w_{t-1}) = \frac{\max(C(w_{t-1}, w) - d, 0)}{C(w_{t-1})} + \lambda(w_{t-1}) \cdot P_{\text{continuation}}(w) \]

where \( P_{\text{continuation}}(w) = \frac{|\{v : C(v, w) > 0\}|}{|\{(v', w') : C(v', w') > 0\}|} \).

Historical context. Backoff and interpolation were the dominant techniques in statistical NLP from the late 1980s through the 2010s. Neural language models (RNNs, Transformers) implicitly handle unseen n-grams through continuous representations, eliminating the need for explicit smoothing — though understanding these techniques remains essential for grasping why neural LMs were such a breakthrough.

title: Zipf's law
summary: An empirical law stating that the frequency of a word in a corpus is approximately inversely proportional to its rank in the frequency table, producing a characteristic long-tailed distribution that profoundly shapes the design of NLP sy…

papers.citation: 1935 · George Kingsley Zipf · The Psycho-Biology of Language

sections.body:
An empirical law stating that the frequency of a word in a corpus is approximately inversely proportional to its rank in the frequency table, producing a characteristic long-tailed distribution that profoundly shapes the design of NLP systems.

If words are sorted by decreasing frequency, the frequency of the word at rank \( r \) is approximately:

\[ f(r) \propto \frac{1}{r^\alpha}, \quad \alpha \approx 1 \]

Equivalently, \( \log f(r) = -\alpha \log r + C \), so a log-log plot of frequency vs. rank is approximately linear with slope \( -\alpha \). Mandelbrot generalized this to \( f(r) \propto \frac{1}{(r + \beta)^\alpha} \), which better fits the head of the distribution (the few most frequent words).

Concrete numbers. In a typical English corpus:

  • The top 10 words (~0.01% of vocabulary) account for ~25% of all tokens
  • The top 100 words (~0.1%) account for ~50% of all tokens
  • Over 50% of word types appear only once (hapax legomena)

This extreme skew means that even very large corpora leave most of the vocabulary poorly estimated, a challenge known as the long tail problem.

sections.title: Implications for NLP system design
sections.body:
Component How Zipf's law affects it
Tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) Frequent words get single tokens; rare words are split into subword units — directly motivated by the frequency distribution
Vocabulary size Diminishing returns from larger vocabularies because new entries are increasingly rare — GPT-2 uses 50,257 BPE tokens as a practical compromise
N-gram smoothing Most n-grams have zero counts; smoothing (Kneser-Ney, backoff) is essential to handle the vast unseen tail
Word embeddings Rare words have few training contexts, leading to poor embeddings — subword approaches (fastText, BPE) mitigate this
Softmax computation Full softmax over large vocabularies is expensive; techniques like adaptive softmax exploit frequency classes for efficiency

Zipf's law and information theory. There is a connection between Zipf's law and optimal coding: if a language were optimally encoded (Shannon's source coding theorem), the code length for a symbol of probability \( p \) would be \( -\log_2 p \) bits. Under Zipf's law, this means common words are short and rare words are long — which is approximately true of natural language and is explicitly implemented by BPE tokenization.

Beyond language. Zipf's law (more generally, power-law distributions) appears in many domains: city populations, website traffic, gene expression frequencies, and the degree distribution of scale-free networks. In ML, understanding power laws is essential for designing systems that handle skewed data gracefully.

title: Cross-entropy
summary: An information-theoretic measure of the average number of bits needed to encode events from a true distribution \( p \) using an approximate distribution \( q \) — the standard training loss for classification and language modeling, and…

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
An information-theoretic measure of the average number of bits needed to encode events from a true distribution \( p \) using an approximate distribution \( q \) — the standard training loss for classification and language modeling, and the single most important loss function in deep learning.

For discrete distributions \( p \) and \( q \) over outcomes \( x \):

\[ H(p, q) = -\sum_{x} p(x) \log q(x) \]

For continuous distributions, the sum becomes an integral. Cross-entropy decomposes as:

\[ H(p, q) = H(p) + D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) \]

where \( H(p) = -\sum_x p(x) \log p(x) \) is the entropy of the true distribution and \( D_{\text{KL}}(p \| q) = \sum_x p(x) \log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \geq 0 \) is the KL divergence. Since \( H(p) \) is constant with respect to model parameters, minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing KL divergence, which is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation.

Multi-class classification. With one-hot true labels \( y \) (all zeros except \( y_c = 1 \) for the correct class \( c \)), the cross-entropy simplifies dramatically:

\[ H(y, \hat{y}) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k = -\log \hat{y}_c \]

where \( \hat{y} = \text{softmax}(z) \) and \( z \) are the logits. This is the negative log-likelihood of the correct class.

Gradient. The gradient of the cross-entropy loss with respect to the logits has a remarkably clean form:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k \]

For the correct class \( c \), this is \( \hat{y}_c - 1 \) (pushing the logit up); for all other classes, it is \( \hat{y}_k \) (pushing their logits down). The gradient magnitude is proportional to the prediction error, providing natural adaptive learning — confident correct predictions produce small gradients while mistakes produce large ones.

Cross-entropy in language modeling. For autoregressive models (GPT, etc.), the training loss is the average per-token cross-entropy over a sequence:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \]

Perplexity is the exponentiated cross-entropy: \( \text{PPL} = \exp(\mathcal{L}) \). A perplexity of \( k \) means the model is on average as uncertain as a uniform distribution over \( k \) tokens.

Numerical stability. Computing \( -\log(\text{softmax}(z)_c) \) naively is dangerous: for large logits, softmax saturates and the log of a near-zero probability underflows. The standard solution combines the operations:

\[ -\log \text{softmax}(z)_c = -z_c + \log \sum_k e^{z_k} \]

which is computed stably via the log-sum-exp trick: \( \log \sum_k e^{z_k} = m + \log \sum_k e^{z_k - m} \) where \( m = \max_k z_k \). This is why PyTorch's nn.CrossEntropyLoss takes raw logits, not softmax probabilities.

sections.title: Variants and extensions
sections.body:
Variant Modification Use case
Binary CE \( -[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})] \) Binary / multi-label classification
Label smoothing Replace one-hot \( y \) with \( (1-\epsilon) y + \epsilon / K \) Prevents overconfidence, used in Transformers
Focal loss \( -(1-\hat{y}_c)^\gamma \log \hat{y}_c \) Class imbalance (object detection)
Knowledge distillation CE between student and teacher soft outputs Model compression

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

The MLE objective is \( \hat{\theta} = \arg\max_\theta \sum_{i=1}^N \log P_\theta(x_i) \). Dividing by \( N \) and taking the limit:

\[ \hat{\theta} = \arg\max_\theta \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \log P_\theta(x_i) \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \arg\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}[\log P_\theta(x)] \]

\[ = \arg\min_\theta \left( -\mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}[\log P_\theta(x)] \right) = \arg\min_\theta H(p_{\text{data}}, P_\theta) \]

which is exactly the cross-entropy between the data distribution and the model.

title: Binary cross-entropy
summary: The cross-entropy loss specialized for binary classification, measuring the divergence between a Bernoulli ground truth and the model's predicted probability — also the standard loss for multi-label classification where each label is an…

papers.citation: 1948 · Claude Shannon · A Mathematical Theory of Communication

sections.body:
The cross-entropy loss specialized for binary classification, measuring the divergence between a Bernoulli ground truth and the model's predicted probability — also the standard loss for multi-label classification where each label is an independent binary decision.

For a single example with true label \( y \in \{0, 1\} \) and predicted probability \( \hat{y} = \sigma(z) = P(y=1 \mid x) \in (0, 1) \), where \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid function and \( z \) is the logit:

\[ \text{BCE} = -[y \log \hat{y} + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})] \]

Over a dataset of \( N \) examples:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} [y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i)] \]

This is the negative log-likelihood of a Bernoulli distribution: if \( y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\hat{y}) \), then \( \log P(y \mid \hat{y}) = y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1-\hat{y}) \), making BCE minimization equivalent to MLE.

Gradient. The gradient with respect to the logit \( z \) has the same elegant form as categorical cross-entropy:

\[ \frac{\partial \text{BCE}}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y = \sigma(z) - y \]

This means the gradient is simply the prediction error. When \( y = 1 \), the gradient is \( \sigma(z) - 1 < 0 \), pushing \( z \) up (increasing predicted probability). When \( y = 0 \), the gradient is \( \sigma(z) > 0 \), pushing \( z \) down. The magnitude is largest when the prediction is most wrong.

Connection to logistic regression. Logistic regression is precisely a linear model \( z = w^\top x + b \) trained with BCE loss. The decision boundary is the hyperplane \( w^\top x + b = 0 \). BCE + sigmoid is to binary classification what categorical CE + softmax is to multi-class classification.

sections.title: Multi-class vs. multi-label
sections.body:
Setting Output activation Loss Constraint
Multi-class (one label) Softmax Categorical CE Probabilities sum to 1
Multi-label (many labels) Per-class sigmoid BCE per class Each class independent

Numerical stability. Computing \( \log(\sigma(z)) \) directly is numerically dangerous for large negative \( z \) (where \( \sigma(z) \approx 0 \)). The stable formulation combines the sigmoid and log:

\[ \text{BCE}(z, y) = \max(z, 0) - yz + \log(1 + e^{-|z|}) \]

This is why PyTorch's nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss (which takes raw logits) is preferred over nn.BCELoss (which takes probabilities).

Variants. Focal loss modifies BCE with a modulating factor \( (1-\hat{y})^\gamma \) for positive examples, down-weighting easy examples and focusing on hard ones — introduced for object detection where background examples vastly outnumber foreground. Weighted BCE applies class-specific weights to handle imbalanced datasets.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Let \( \hat{y} = \sigma(z) \), so \( \frac{d\hat{y}}{dz} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) \).

\[ \frac{\partial \text{BCE}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \text{BCE}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{d\hat{y}}{dz} = \left(-\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right) \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) \]

\[ = -y(1-\hat{y}) + (1-y)\hat{y} = \hat{y} - y \]

Python 
import numpy as np

def binary_cross_entropy(y_true, y_pred, eps=1e-7):
    """BCE from probabilities (numerically naive)."""
    y_pred = np.clip(y_pred, eps, 1 - eps)
    return -np.mean(y_true * np.log(y_pred) + (1 - y_true) * np.log(1 - y_pred))

def bce_with_logits(y_true, logits):
    """BCE from logits (numerically stable)."""
    return np.mean(np.maximum(logits, 0) - y_true * logits + np.log(1 + np.exp(-np.abs(logits))))

title: Logit
summary: The raw, unnormalized output of a neural network's final linear layer before any activation function (sigmoid or softmax) is applied. In statistics, the logit function is the log-odds transform — the inverse of the sigmoid.

papers.citation: 1944 · Joseph Berkson (coined "logit" for log-odds in bioassay)

sections.body:
The raw, unnormalized output of a neural network's final linear layer before any activation function (sigmoid or softmax) is applied. In statistics, the logit function is the log-odds transform — the inverse of the sigmoid.

In the strict statistical sense, the logit function maps a probability \( p \in (0, 1) \) to the real line via the log-odds:

\[ \text{logit}(p) = \log \frac{p}{1 - p} = \sigma^{-1}(p) \]

This is the canonical link function for the Bernoulli distribution in the framework of generalised linear models (GLMs). The inverse relationship with the sigmoid is central: \( \sigma(\text{logit}(p)) = p \) and \( \text{logit}(\sigma(z)) = z \).

In deep learning, "logits" refers more broadly to the pre-activation output vector \( \mathbf{z} = W\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^K \) of the final layer. These are converted to probabilities via:

  • Sigmoid: \( \hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \) for binary or multi-label classification
  • Softmax: \( \hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \) for multi-class classification

Numerical stability. Working with logits rather than probabilities is numerically preferable. Loss functions like cross_entropy_with_logits combine the softmax and log operations into a single numerically stable computation using the log-sum-exp trick:

\[ \log \text{softmax}(z_k) = z_k - \log \sum_{j} e^{z_j} = z_k - \left( m + \log \sum_{j} e^{z_j - m} \right) \]

where \( m = \max_j z_j \). This avoids overflow from large exponentials and catastrophic cancellation from computing \( \log(\text{softmax}(z)) \) in two separate steps.

Knowledge distillation. Logits play a key role in knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), where a student network is trained to match the softened output distribution of a teacher network. The teacher's logits are divided by a temperature \( T > 1 \) before softmax, producing softer probability distributions that reveal inter-class similarities (dark knowledge):

\[ q_k = \frac{e^{z_k / T}}{\sum_j e^{z_j / T}} \]

As \( T \to \infty \), the distribution approaches uniform; as \( T \to 0 \), it approaches a one-hot vector at the argmax.

title: Classification
summary: A supervised learning task where the model assigns each input to one of a finite set of discrete categories (classes), learning a decision boundary that partitions the feature space.

papers.citation: 1936 · R.A. Fisher (Linear Discriminant Analysis)

sections.body:
A supervised learning task where the model assigns each input to one of a finite set of discrete categories (classes), learning a decision boundary that partitions the feature space.

Given input features \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \) and a discrete label \( y \in \{1, \ldots, K\} \), a classifier learns a function \( f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \{1, \ldots, K\} \). In neural networks, this is typically implemented as a mapping to a probability distribution over classes:

\[ P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = \text{softmax}(W\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{b})_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \]

where \( \mathbf{z} = W\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{b} \) are the logits and \( \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) \) is the learned representation. Training minimises the cross-entropy loss: \( \mathcal{L} = -\log P(y = c \mid \mathbf{x}) \), where \( c \) is the ground-truth class.

Classification tasks are categorized by the number of classes and label structure:

Variant Classes Output Loss
Binary \( K = 2 \) Sigmoid Binary CE
Multi-class \( K > 2 \), one label Softmax Categorical CE
Multi-label \( K > 2 \), multiple labels Per-class sigmoid Binary CE per class

Decision boundaries. The decision boundary is the surface in feature space where the predicted class changes. Linear classifiers (logistic regression, linear SVM) produce hyperplane boundaries; neural networks with non-linear activations produce arbitrarily complex, non-linear boundaries — this is a consequence of the universal approximation theorem.

The Bayes optimal classifier assigns each \( \mathbf{x} \) to the class with the highest posterior probability: \( f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) \). It achieves the lowest possible error rate (the Bayes error), which serves as the theoretical lower bound for any classifier on the same problem. In practice, we never know the true posterior, so all classifiers are approximations.

Evaluation depends on the setting: accuracy suffices for balanced classes, but imbalanced problems require precision, recall, F1, or area under the ROC/PR curve. Calibration — whether predicted probabilities match empirical frequencies — matters when downstream decisions depend on confidence scores (e.g., medical triage, autonomous driving).

title: Binary classification
summary: A classification task with exactly two possible outcome classes, where the model outputs a single probability score indicating the likelihood of the positive class.

papers.citation: 1936 · R.A. Fisher

sections.body:
A classification task with exactly two possible outcome classes, where the model outputs a single probability score indicating the likelihood of the positive class.

The model outputs \( \hat{y} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b) \in (0, 1) \), interpreted as \( P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \). A decision threshold \( \tau \) (typically 0.5) converts this to a hard prediction: \( \hat{c} = \mathbb{1}[\hat{y} > \tau] \). Training minimises the binary cross-entropy loss:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{y}_i)\bigr] \]

This loss is the negative log-likelihood under a Bernoulli model, so minimising it is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation.

Evaluation metrics. Accuracy is misleading under class imbalance — a dataset with 99% negatives yields 99% accuracy from a trivial all-negative classifier. The confusion matrix provides a complete picture:

Metric Formula Optimise when
Precision \( \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \) False positives are costly (spam filter)
Recall \( \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \) False negatives are costly (cancer screening)
F1 \( 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \) Need a single balanced score
AUC-ROC \( \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(t)\, d(\text{FPR}(t)) \) Threshold-independent ranking quality

ROC and PR curves. The ROC curve plots TPR (recall) vs. FPR across all thresholds \( \tau \in [0, 1] \). AUC-ROC = 1.0 is perfect; 0.5 is random. Under severe class imbalance, the precision-recall curve (and its AUC, or average precision) is more informative because it is not inflated by the large number of true negatives that dominate the FPR denominator.

Calibration. A model is calibrated if, among all instances assigned probability \( p \), roughly a fraction \( p \) are truly positive. Platt scaling (fitting a sigmoid on the logits using a held-out set) and isotonic regression are common post-hoc calibration methods. Calibration matters in settings where the predicted probability drives downstream decisions — e.g., setting a threshold for medical referral.

Examples include spam detection, medical diagnosis, sentiment analysis (positive/negative), toxicity detection in LLM outputs, and click-through-rate prediction in recommendation systems.

title: Multiclass classification
summary: A classification task where each input is assigned to exactly one of \( K > 2 \) mutually exclusive classes, with the model outputting a probability distribution over all classes via softmax.

papers.citation: 1936 · R.A. Fisher

sections.body:
A classification task where each input is assigned to exactly one of \( K > 2 \) mutually exclusive classes, with the model outputting a probability distribution over all classes via softmax.

The model's output layer has \( K \) units producing logits \( \mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^K \), and a softmax activation ensures the outputs form a valid probability distribution:

\[ P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z})_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^K e^{z_j}}, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) = 1 \]

The predicted class is \( \hat{c} = \arg\max_k P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}) \), and training minimises categorical cross-entropy:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k = -\log \hat{y}_c \]

where \( y_k \) is the one-hot encoded label and \( c \) is the true class index. The second equality holds because the one-hot vector zeroes out all terms except the true class.

Classical reduction strategies. Inherently binary algorithms (e.g., SVM, logistic regression) handle multiclass problems via reduction:

  • One-vs-Rest (OvR): train \( K \) binary classifiers, each distinguishing one class from all others. Prediction: pick the class with the highest confidence score.
  • One-vs-One (OvO): train \( \binom{K}{2} \) classifiers for all class pairs. Prediction: majority vote. Scales quadratically but each classifier trains on a smaller subset.

Neural networks handle multiclass classification natively through the softmax output layer, making reductions unnecessary.

Evaluation. Top-1 accuracy is the standard metric: what fraction of predictions match the true label. Top-5 accuracy — whether the true class appears in the model's top 5 predictions — became standard with ImageNet (\( K = 1{,}000 \)), where fine-grained class distinctions make exact prediction difficult. The confusion matrix \( C \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K} \), where \( C_{ij} \) counts examples of true class \( i \) predicted as class \( j \), reveals systematic misclassification patterns.

Label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016) replaces the hard one-hot target with a soft distribution \( y_k' = (1 - \epsilon)\, y_k + \epsilon / K \), preventing the model from becoming overconfident by penalising logits that grow too large. This is standard in modern Transformer training (e.g., the original Transformer paper uses \( \epsilon = 0.1 \)).

Canonical benchmarks: MNIST (\( K = 10 \)), CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet (\( K = 1{,}000 \)). Next-token prediction in language models is multiclass classification with \( K = |V| \) (vocabulary size, often 32k–128k).

title: Regression
summary: A supervised learning task where the model predicts a continuous numerical output rather than a discrete class label. The standard loss is mean squared error, which corresponds to maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise.

papers.citation: 1805 · Adrien-Marie Legendre (method of least squares) · Carl Friedrich Gauss (normal equations, 1809)

sections.body:
A supervised learning task where the model predicts a continuous numerical output rather than a discrete class label. The standard loss is mean squared error, which corresponds to maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise.

Given input features \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \), a regression model learns a function \( f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R} \) (or \( \mathbb{R}^m \) for multi-output regression) that maps inputs to continuous targets. The output layer typically uses an identity activation (no non-linearity).

Mean squared error is the standard regression loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta))^2 \]

Probabilistic interpretation. Under the assumption that targets are corrupted by Gaussian noise \( y = f(\mathbf{x}) + \varepsilon, \; \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \), the negative log-likelihood is proportional to MSE:

\[ -\log p(y \mid \mathbf{x}, \theta) = \frac{(y - f(\mathbf{x};\theta))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \text{const} \]

So minimising MSE is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under Gaussian noise. This connection breaks when the noise is non-Gaussian — e.g., heavy-tailed or skewed distributions call for alternative losses.

sections.title: Alternative loss functions
sections.body:
Loss Formula Properties
MSE (L2) \( (y - \hat{y})^2 \) Smooth, penalises large errors quadratically, sensitive to outliers
MAE (L1) \( |y - \hat{y}| \) Robust to outliers, predicts the conditional median, non-smooth at zero
Huber \( \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}\delta^2 & |e| \le \delta \\ \delta(|e| - \frac{1}{2}\delta) & |e| > \delta \end{cases} \) Quadratic near zero, linear far away — combines MSE smoothness with MAE robustness
Quantile \( \rho_\tau(e) = e(\tau - \mathbb{1}[e < 0]) \) Predicts the \( \tau \)-th percentile of the conditional distribution

Evaluation metrics. Beyond MSE/MAE, the coefficient of determination \( R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2} \) measures the fraction of variance explained by the model. \( R^2 = 1 \) is perfect prediction; \( R^2 = 0 \) matches the mean baseline; negative values indicate the model is worse than predicting the mean.

Bias-variance decomposition. For MSE, the expected test error decomposes as \( \mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2 \), where \( \sigma^2 \) is irreducible noise. This decomposition motivates regularisation: trading a small increase in bias for a larger reduction in variance.

Examples include predicting housing prices, stock returns, molecular properties, reward model scores in RLHF, and continuous control outputs in reinforcement learning.

title: Linear Regression
summary: A supervised learning method that models the relationship between a dependent variable \( y \) and one or more independent variables \( \mathbf{x} \) by fitting a linear function. It minimises the sum of squared residuals (ordinary least…

papers.citation: 1805 · Adrien-Marie Legendre (least squares method) · Carl Friedrich Gauss (normal equations, 1809)

sections.body:
A supervised learning method that models the relationship between a dependent variable \( y \) and one or more independent variables \( \mathbf{x} \) by fitting a linear function. It minimises the sum of squared residuals (ordinary least squares) and admits a closed-form solution.

Given a dataset \( \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n} \) with \( \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d \), linear regression assumes:

\[ y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \]

The ordinary least squares (OLS) objective minimises the residual sum of squares:

\[ \hat{\mathbf{w}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{w}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i)^2 = \arg\min_{\mathbf{w}} \| \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} \|_2^2 \]

Setting the gradient to zero yields the normal equation:

\[ \hat{\mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \]

This closed-form solution exists when \( \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \) is invertible. When it is not (collinear features, \( d > n \)), regularisation (ridge/lasso) or the pseudo-inverse is used.

VariantPenaltyEffect
OLSNoneUnbiased, high variance if \( d \) large
Ridge (L2)\( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 \)Shrinks coefficients, handles collinearity
Lasso (L1)\( \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_1 \)Sparse solutions, feature selection

Under the Gauss–Markov theorem, OLS is the best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE) when errors are homoscedastic and uncorrelated.

Derivations

Starting from the cost \( J(\mathbf{w}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) \):

\[ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{X}^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) = 0 \] \[ \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \] \[ \hat{\mathbf{w}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \]

The second-order condition \( \nabla^2 J = 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} \succeq 0 \) confirms this is a minimum.

Python 
import numpy as np

def linear_regression(X, y):
    """OLS via the normal equation."""
    w = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
    return w

# With regularisation (ridge)
def ridge_regression(X, y, lam=1.0):
    d = X.shape[1]
    w = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X + lam * np.eye(d), X.T @ y)
    return w

title: Logistic Regression
summary: A linear model for binary classification that outputs calibrated probabilities by passing a linear combination of features through the logistic (sigmoid) function. Despite the name, it is a classification method, not a regression method.

papers.citation: 1958 · David Cox · The Regression Analysis of Binary Sequences

sections.body:
A linear model for binary classification that outputs calibrated probabilities by passing a linear combination of features through the logistic (sigmoid) function. Despite the name, it is a classification method, not a regression method.

Logistic regression models the probability that an input \( \mathbf{x} \) belongs to class 1:

\[ P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)}} \]

The model is trained by maximising the log-likelihood, which is equivalent to minimising the binary cross-entropy loss:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\bigr] \]

Unlike linear regression, there is no closed-form solution. Optimisation uses iterative methods — typically gradient descent or Newton's method (iteratively reweighted least squares, IRLS).

The decision boundary is a hyperplane \( \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0 \) in feature space. Points on one side are classified as 1, points on the other as 0.

PropertyLinear RegressionLogistic Regression
OutputContinuous valueProbability in [0, 1]
LossMSEBinary cross-entropy
Decision boundaryN/ALinear hyperplane
SolutionClosed-formIterative (no closed form)

Logistic regression extends to multiclass settings via the softmax function (multinomial logistic regression). It remains one of the most widely used baselines in both ML research and production.

Derivations

The gradient of the cross-entropy loss with respect to \( \mathbf{w} \):

\[ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\mathbf{x}_i \]

where \( \hat{p}_i = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \). This has the same form as the linear regression gradient but with \( \hat{p}_i \) replacing the linear prediction — a consequence of the canonical link function in generalised linear models.

title: Accuracy
summary: The fraction of predictions that a classifier gets correct — the simplest evaluation metric for classification, but often misleading on imbalanced datasets where it rewards trivial majority-class predictors.

papers.citation: Fundamental concept in statistical classification and information retrieval.

sections.body:
The fraction of predictions that a classifier gets correct — the simplest evaluation metric for classification, but often misleading on imbalanced datasets where it rewards trivial majority-class predictors.

\[ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\text{correct predictions}}{\text{total predictions}} \]

where TP = true positives, TN = true negatives, FP = false positives, FN = false negatives. Accuracy is equivalent to \( 1 - \text{error rate} \) and corresponds to the 0-1 loss: \( \mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1} = \mathbb{1}[\hat{y} \ne y] \).

The class imbalance trap. On a dataset with 99% negative examples, a classifier that always predicts "negative" achieves 99% accuracy while being entirely useless. This is why accuracy is only a reliable metric when classes are roughly balanced and misclassification costs are symmetric. In imbalanced settings, precision, recall, F1, or area under the PR curve are far more informative.

sections.title: Variants of accuracy
sections.body:
VariantDefinitionUse case
Top-1 accuracyStandard accuracy (argmax prediction matches label)Default for most classification tasks
Top-5 accuracyTrue class is within the 5 highest-probability predictionsImageNet (\( K = 1{,}000 \)) and large-scale classification
Balanced accuracy\( \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Recall}_k \) (macro-average of per-class recall)Imbalanced datasets — corrects for class frequency bias
Exact-match accuracyFull predicted sequence must exactly match gold answerQA benchmarks, multi-label settings, structured prediction

Relationship to loss functions. Accuracy is not directly optimisable by gradient descent because the 0-1 loss is non-differentiable. Cross-entropy serves as a differentiable surrogate: minimising cross-entropy generally improves accuracy, but the two can diverge — a model may achieve lower cross-entropy by improving calibration on already-correct predictions without changing any decisions. This is why validation accuracy (not training loss) is the standard criterion for model selection and early stopping.

Statistical significance. With \( N \) test examples and observed accuracy \( \hat{p} \), the 95% confidence interval is approximately \( \hat{p} \pm 1.96\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/N} \) (Wald interval). For comparing two models, McNemar's test is more appropriate than comparing raw accuracy scores because it accounts for the correlation between predictions on the same test set.

title: Precision
summary: The fraction of positive predictions that are actually positive — answering "Of everything the model flagged as positive, how much was correct?" High precision means few false positives.

papers.citation: Fundamental concept in information retrieval and classification evaluation. Formalised in the Cranfield experiments (1960s, Cyril Cleverdon).

sections.body:
The fraction of positive predictions that are actually positive — answering "Of everything the model flagged as positive, how much was correct?" High precision means few false positives.

\[ \text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} \]

Precision is the metric to optimise when the cost of a false positive is high: spam detection (users hate losing real email), legal document review (flagging irrelevant documents wastes expensive lawyer time), or content recommendation (showing irrelevant items erodes user trust).

Precision-recall trade-off. Increasing the decision threshold \( \tau \) makes the model more conservative — it predicts positive only when highly confident. This raises precision (fewer false positives) but lowers recall (more false negatives). The precision-recall (PR) curve plots this trade-off across all thresholds and is more informative than any single operating point. The area under the PR curve (AUPRC, or average precision) summarises overall performance in a single number.

Precision at K (P@K) measures precision among only the top \( K \) predictions ranked by model confidence. This is the standard metric in information retrieval and ranking tasks: if a search engine returns 10 results, P@10 is the fraction that are relevant. Average Precision (AP) integrates precision across all recall levels:

\[ \text{AP} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} P(k) \cdot \Delta R(k) \]

where \( P(k) \) is precision at the \( k \)-th threshold and \( \Delta R(k) \) is the change in recall. Mean Average Precision (mAP) averages AP over all queries or classes and is the primary metric for object detection (PASCAL VOC, COCO).

Multiclass extensions. In multiclass settings, precision is computed per class and then averaged:

AveragingMethodWhen to use
Macro\( \frac{1}{K}\sum_k \text{Precision}_k \)All classes equally important regardless of frequency
MicroGlobal \( \frac{\sum_k \text{TP}_k}{\sum_k (\text{TP}_k + \text{FP}_k)} \)Overall performance weighted by class frequency
Weighted\( \sum_k \frac{n_k}{N} \cdot \text{Precision}_k \)Compromise — accounts for imbalance without ignoring rare classes

Common pitfall. Precision is undefined when there are no positive predictions (\( \text{TP} + \text{FP} = 0 \)). Libraries like scikit-learn default to 0.0 in this case with a warning. This can silently distort macro-averaged precision when some classes are never predicted.

title: Recall
summary: The fraction of actual positives that the model correctly identifies — answering "Of everything that was truly positive, how much did the model catch?" Also called sensitivity or true positive rate. High recall means few false negatives.

papers.citation: Fundamental concept in information retrieval and classification evaluation. Formalised in the Cranfield experiments (1960s, Cyril Cleverdon).

sections.body:
The fraction of actual positives that the model correctly identifies — answering "Of everything that was truly positive, how much did the model catch?" Also called sensitivity or true positive rate. High recall means few false negatives.

\[ \text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \]

Recall is the metric to optimise when the cost of missing a positive is high: cancer screening (missing a tumour is far worse than a false alarm), fraud detection (letting fraud through is expensive), or safety-critical systems (failing to detect a hazard can be catastrophic).

Aliases across fields. The same quantity appears under different names depending on the domain:

NameFieldContext
RecallML / IRClassification, information retrieval
SensitivityMedicineDiagnostic test evaluation
True Positive Rate (TPR)StatisticsROC analysis — the y-axis of the ROC curve
Hit rate / detection rateSignal processingRadar, anomaly detection

Relationship to other metrics. Recall and precision are complementary: improving one typically degrades the other (the precision-recall trade-off). The F1 score is their harmonic mean: \( F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \). More generally, the \( F_\beta \) score weights recall \( \beta^2 \) times as much as precision:

\[ F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R} \]

For \( \beta = 2 \), recall is weighted 4x more than precision — useful in medical screening. For \( \beta = 0.5 \), precision is weighted higher — useful in spam filtering.

Recall in the ROC framework. The ROC curve plots recall (TPR) against the false positive rate \( \text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} \) = \( 1 - \text{Specificity} \). The complement of recall, the false negative rate (miss rate), is \( \text{FNR} = 1 - \text{Recall} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \).

Recall at K (R@K) in information retrieval measures what fraction of all relevant documents appear in the top \( K \) results. This is a standard metric for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems and dense passage retrieval, where the retriever must surface relevant passages within a fixed context window for the language model.

title: F1 Score
summary: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single metric that balances both. It ranges from 0 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect precision and recall. F1 is the standard metric for imbalanced classification tasks where accuracy…

papers.citation: 1979 · C. J. van Rijsbergen · Information Retrieval (2nd ed.)

sections.body:
The harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single metric that balances both. It ranges from 0 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect precision and recall. F1 is the standard metric for imbalanced classification tasks where accuracy is misleading.

In binary classification, predictions fall into four categories: true positives (TP), false positives (FP), true negatives (TN), and false negatives (FN). From these:

\[ \text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}, \qquad \text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} \]

The F1 score combines both into a single number:

\[ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2\,\text{TP}}{2\,\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} \]

The harmonic mean is used rather than the arithmetic mean because it penalises extreme imbalances: if either precision or recall is near zero, the F1 score is also near zero. For example, a model with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.01 achieves an arithmetic mean of 0.505 but an F1 of only 0.0198 — correctly reflecting its poor utility.

Why not accuracy? In a dataset with 99% negatives and 1% positives, a trivial "always predict negative" classifier achieves 99% accuracy but 0% recall and 0% F1. F1 is informative precisely because it ignores TN, focusing on the model's ability to find and correctly identify positive instances.

The generalised form is the F-beta score, which weights recall \( \beta^2 \) times as much as precision:

\[ F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \]
Variant\(\beta\)EmphasisUse case
F0.50.5Precision-weightedSpam filtering (minimise false positives)
F11Equal weightGeneral default for imbalanced tasks
F22Recall-weightedMedical screening (minimise false negatives)

sections.title: Multiclass extensions
sections.body:
• Macro-averaged F1 — compute F1 per class, then take the unweighted mean. Treats all classes equally regardless of support.
  • Micro-averaged F1 — aggregate TP, FP, FN globally across all classes, then compute F1. Equivalent to accuracy in multiclass single-label settings.
  • Weighted F1 — macro-average weighted by class support (number of true instances per class).

F1 is widely used in NLP tasks (NER, relation extraction, question answering) and information retrieval, where it originated. For ranking tasks, precision@k and mean average precision (MAP) are often preferred.

Derivations

F1 as the harmonic mean of precision \( P \) and recall \( R \):

\[ \frac{1}{F_1} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{P + R}{2PR} \] \[ F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P + R} \]

Substituting \( P = \text{TP}/(\text{TP}+\text{FP}) \) and \( R = \text{TP}/(\text{TP}+\text{FN}) \):

\[ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}} \cdot \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}}{\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}} + \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}}} = \frac{2\,\text{TP}}{2\,\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}} \]

title: ROUGE
summary: Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation — a family of metrics for evaluating automatic summarisation and text generation by measuring n-gram overlap between a candidate and reference text. Unlike BLEU (which is precision-orient…

papers.citation: 2004 · Chin-Yew Lin · ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries

sections.body:
Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation — a family of metrics for evaluating automatic summarisation and text generation by measuring n-gram overlap between a candidate and reference text. Unlike BLEU (which is precision-oriented), ROUGE emphasises recall.

ROUGE was developed by Chin-Yew Lin at USC/ISI and became the standard evaluation metric for summarisation tasks, adopted by the Document Understanding Conference (DUC) and later TAC. The key variants are:

VariantWhat it measures
ROUGE-NN-gram recall between candidate and reference
ROUGE-1Unigram overlap — captures content adequacy
ROUGE-2Bigram overlap — captures fluency and local word order
ROUGE-LLongest common subsequence (LCS)-based F-measure
ROUGE-SSkip-bigram co-occurrence — allows arbitrary gaps between word pairs

ROUGE-N computes recall over n-grams:

\[ \text{ROUGE-N} = \frac{\sum_{s \in \text{ref}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in s} \text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{gram}_n)}{\sum_{s \in \text{ref}} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in s} \text{Count}(\text{gram}_n)} \]

where \(\text{Count}_{\text{match}}(\text{gram}_n)\) is the number of n-grams in the reference that also appear in the candidate. The denominator normalises by total reference n-grams, making this a recall measure.

ROUGE-L uses the longest common subsequence, which captures sentence-level word order without requiring contiguous matches:

\[ R_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{m}, \quad P_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{n}, \quad F_{\text{lcs}} = \frac{(1+\beta^2) R_{\text{lcs}} P_{\text{lcs}}}{R_{\text{lcs}} + \beta^2 P_{\text{lcs}}} \]

where \( m \) and \( n \) are the lengths of reference and candidate, respectively, and \( \beta \) is typically set to favour recall (\( \beta = 1.2 \) in the original implementation).

ROUGE vs BLEU: BLEU uses precision with a brevity penalty (penalising short candidates), while ROUGE uses recall (penalising candidates that miss reference content). BLEU was designed for machine translation; ROUGE for summarisation. In practice, modern NLG evaluation often reports both, along with ROUGE-L and sometimes BERTScore or METEOR.

Limitations: ROUGE captures only surface-level lexical overlap and cannot recognise paraphrases or semantic equivalence. A summary that restates ideas in different words scores poorly. This has driven interest in model-based metrics like BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020), which computes cosine similarity between contextual BERT embeddings of candidate and reference tokens.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
summary: The longest common subsequence (LCS) of two sequences is the longest sequence of elements that appear in both, in the same relative order, but not necessarily contiguously. It is a classic dynamic programming problem and the basis for RO…

papers.citation: 1974 · Daniel Hirschberg · A Linear Space Algorithm for Computing Maximal Common Subsequences

sections.body:
The longest common subsequence (LCS) of two sequences is the longest sequence of elements that appear in both, in the same relative order, but not necessarily contiguously. It is a classic dynamic programming problem and the basis for ROUGE-L and diff algorithms.

Given sequences \( X = (x_1, \ldots, x_m) \) and \( Y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n) \), the LCS length is computed via the recurrence:

\[ L(i,j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i=0 \text{ or } j=0 \\ L(i-1,j-1) + 1 & \text{if } x_i = y_j \\ \max(L(i-1,j),\; L(i,j-1)) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

The time and space complexity is \( O(mn) \). The LCS can be recovered by tracing back through the DP table.

sections.title: Applications in ML/NLP
sections.body:
• ROUGE-L uses LCS to evaluate summarisation quality without requiring contiguous n-gram matches
  • Diff tools (e.g., Unix diff) use LCS to compute minimal edit scripts between files
  • Sequence alignment in bioinformatics is a generalisation of LCS with gap penalties

LCS differs from the longest common substring (which requires contiguity). For example, LCS("ABCDE", "ACE") = "ACE" (length 3), while the longest common substring is "A" (length 1).

Python 
def lcs_length(X, Y):
    m, n = len(X), len(Y)
    dp = [[0] * (n + 1) for _ in range(m + 1)]
    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        for j in range(1, n + 1):
            if X[i-1] == Y[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1
            else:
                dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
    return dp[m][n]

title: Edit Distance
summary: The minimum number of single-character operations (insertions, deletions, substitutions) required to transform one string into another. Also called Levenshtein distance, it is a fundamental metric for measuring string similarity in NLP a…

papers.citation: 1966 · Vladimir Levenshtein · Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions and Reversals

sections.body:
The minimum number of single-character operations (insertions, deletions, substitutions) required to transform one string into another. Also called Levenshtein distance, it is a fundamental metric for measuring string similarity in NLP and information retrieval.

Given strings \( a \) of length \( m \) and \( b \) of length \( n \), the Levenshtein distance is defined by the recurrence:

\[ d(i,j) = \begin{cases} j & \text{if } i = 0 \\ i & \text{if } j = 0 \\ d(i-1,j-1) & \text{if } a_i = b_j \\ 1 + \min\bigl(d(i-1,j),\; d(i,j-1),\; d(i-1,j-1)\bigr) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

The three operations in the \(\min\) correspond to deletion (removing \(a_i\)), insertion (inserting \(b_j\)), and substitution (replacing \(a_i\) with \(b_j\)), respectively. Time and space complexity: \( O(mn) \). Space can be reduced to \( O(\min(m,n)) \) since only two rows of the DP table are needed at any time.

Concrete example: edit_distance("kitten", "sitting") = 3:

  1. kitten → sitten (substitute k → s)
  2. sitten → sittin (substitute e → i)
  3. sittin → sitting (insert g)

sections.title: Applications in ML/NLP
sections.body:
• Spelling correction — rank candidate corrections by edit distance to the misspelled word (e.g., Norvig's spell checker)
  • Word error rate (WER) in speech recognition is a normalised edit distance at the word level: \( \text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \) where \(S\), \(D\), \(I\) are substitutions, deletions, insertions and \(N\) is the number of reference words
  • Character error rate (CER) in OCR is character-level edit distance normalised by reference length
  • Approximate string matching for fuzzy search and entity deduplication
VariantAllowed operationsNotes
LevenshteinInsert, delete, substituteAll operations cost 1
Damerau-Levenshtein+ transposition of adjacent charsCaptures common typos (e.g., "teh" → "the")
HammingSubstitution onlyEqual-length strings only
Weighted edit distanceInsert, delete, substituteNon-uniform costs (e.g., keyboard proximity)

Edit distance is closely related to sequence alignment in bioinformatics (Needleman-Wunsch and Smith-Waterman algorithms), which generalise the cost model to allow position-dependent gap penalties and substitution matrices (e.g., BLOSUM for amino acids).

Python 
def edit_distance(a, b):
    m, n = len(a), len(b)
    dp = [[0] * (n + 1) for _ in range(m + 1)]
    for i in range(m + 1):
        dp[i][0] = i
    for j in range(n + 1):
        dp[0][j] = j
    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        for j in range(1, n + 1):
            if a[i-1] == b[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]
            else:
                dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1])
    return dp[m][n]

title: Perceptron
summary: The simplest neural network: a single linear unit followed by a step function that outputs a binary decision. The perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge on linearly separable data, but it cannot learn non-linear decision…

papers.citation: 1958 · Frank Rosenblatt · The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain

sections.body:
The simplest neural network: a single linear unit followed by a step function that outputs a binary decision. The perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge on linearly separable data, but it cannot learn non-linear decision boundaries — a limitation famously demonstrated by Minsky and Papert.

The perceptron computes:

\[ \hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

The perceptron learning rule updates weights only on misclassified examples:

\[ \mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta\,(y_i - \hat{y}_i)\,\mathbf{x}_i \]

where \( \eta \) is the learning rate. The Perceptron Convergence Theorem guarantees that if the data is linearly separable, the algorithm converges in a finite number of steps.

Rosenblatt's perceptron (1958) generated enormous excitement as one of the first learning machines. Minsky and Papert's 1969 book Perceptrons showed that a single-layer perceptron cannot compute XOR or any non-linearly-separable function, contributing to the first "AI winter." This limitation was later overcome by multilayer perceptrons trained with backpropagation.

PropertySingle PerceptronMLP
Decision boundaryLinear hyperplaneNon-linear (universal approx.)
ActivationStep functionReLU, sigmoid, etc.
TrainingPerceptron ruleBackpropagation
Python 
import numpy as np

def perceptron_train(X, y, lr=1.0, max_iter=1000):
    w = np.zeros(X.shape[1])
    b = 0.0
    for _ in range(max_iter):
        errors = 0
        for xi, yi in zip(X, y):
            pred = 1 if (w @ xi + b) >= 0 else 0
            if pred != yi:
                w += lr * (yi - pred) * xi
                b += lr * (yi - pred)
                errors += 1
        if errors == 0:
            break
    return w, b

title: Decision Tree
summary: A supervised learning model that recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits, forming a tree structure where each leaf assigns a class label (classification) or a continuous value (regression). Decision trees are i…

papers.citation: 1984 · Leo Breiman, Jerome Friedman, Richard Olshen, Charles Stone · Classification and Regression Trees (CART)

sections.body:
A supervised learning model that recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits, forming a tree structure where each leaf assigns a class label (classification) or a continuous value (regression). Decision trees are interpretable but prone to overfitting.

At each internal node, the algorithm selects the feature and threshold that best separate the data according to an impurity criterion. For classification:

CriterionFormulaUsed by
Gini impurity\( G = 1 - \sum_{k} p_k^2 \)CART
Entropy\( H = -\sum_{k} p_k \log_2 p_k \)ID3, C4.5
MSE\( \frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 \)Regression trees

where \( p_k \) is the proportion of samples belonging to class \( k \) in the node. The split that maximises information gain (reduction in impurity) is chosen greedily:

\[ \text{Gain}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{values}(A)} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} H(S_v) \]

where \( S \) is the parent set and \( S_v \) is the subset where feature \( A \) takes value \( v \). Finding the globally optimal tree is NP-hard, so all practical algorithms use greedy, top-down heuristics.

Regularisation and pruning: Without constraints, a tree can perfectly memorise the training set (zero training error). Key regularisation strategies:

  • Pre-pruning — limit max depth, require minimum samples per leaf/split, or set a minimum impurity decrease
  • Post-pruning (cost-complexity pruning) — grow a full tree, then remove branches that do not improve validation performance. CART uses minimal cost-complexity pruning with parameter \( \alpha \): \( R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha |T| \), where \( R(T) \) is the misclassification rate and \( |T| \) is the number of leaves

sections.title: Key historical algorithms
sections.body:
• ID3 (Quinlan, 1986) — uses entropy/information gain, categorical features only
  • C4.5 (Quinlan, 1993) — extends ID3 with gain ratio (corrects information gain's bias toward many-valued features), handles continuous features and missing values
  • CART (Breiman et al., 1984) — binary splits only, supports regression, introduced cost-complexity pruning

Decision trees are the building blocks of powerful ensemble methods: random forests (Breiman, 2001) and gradient boosted trees (Friedman, 2001). While a single deep tree overfits, ensembles of shallow trees achieve state-of-the-art performance on tabular data.

Python 
import numpy as np

def gini(y):
    """Gini impurity of a label array."""
    classes, counts = np.unique(y, return_counts=True)
    probs = counts / len(y)
    return 1 - np.sum(probs ** 2)

def best_split(X, y):
    """Find the best feature and threshold (greedy, Gini)."""
    best_gain, best_feat, best_thresh = -1, None, None
    parent_gini = gini(y)
    for feat in range(X.shape[1]):
        thresholds = np.unique(X[:, feat])
        for t in thresholds:
            left = y[X[:, feat] <= t]
            right = y[X[:, feat] > t]
            if len(left) == 0 or len(right) == 0:
                continue
            w_l, w_r = len(left) / len(y), len(right) / len(y)
            gain = parent_gini - w_l * gini(left) - w_r * gini(right)
            if gain > best_gain:
                best_gain, best_feat, best_thresh = gain, feat, t
    return best_feat, best_thresh

title: Random Forest
summary: An ensemble method that trains many decision trees on random bootstrap samples of the data, with random feature subsets at each split, and aggregates their predictions by majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression). This red…

papers.citation: 2001 · Leo Breiman · Random Forests

sections.body:
An ensemble method that trains many decision trees on random bootstrap samples of the data, with random feature subsets at each split, and aggregates their predictions by majority vote (classification) or averaging (regression). This reduces variance while maintaining low bias.

The random forest algorithm combines two sources of randomness:

  1. Bagging (bootstrap aggregating): each tree is trained on a bootstrap sample (sampling with replacement) of size \( n \) from the original dataset.
  2. Feature subsampling: at each split, only a random subset of \( m \) features (out of \( d \) total) is considered. Typical defaults: \( m = \lfloor\sqrt{d}\rfloor \) for classification, \( m = \lfloor d/3 \rfloor \) for regression.

The feature subsampling decorrelates the trees, which is crucial. Averaging \( B \) correlated estimators with variance \( \sigma^2 \) and pairwise correlation \( \rho \) gives:

\[ \text{Var}_{\text{avg}} = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^2 \]

The second term vanishes as \( B \to \infty \), but the first term \( \rho\sigma^2 \) is irreducible. Reducing \( \rho \) via feature subsampling directly shrinks this floor. This is why random forests outperform simple bagging: bagged trees on the same features remain correlated (they all split on the strongest feature first).
