Out-of-bag (OOB) error: Each bootstrap sample excludes roughly \( 1 - (1 - 1/n)^n \approx 36.8\% \) of the data. These out-of-bag samples provide a free internal validation estimate without needing a separate holdout set. The OOB error converges to the leave-one-out cross-validation error as \( B \to \infty \).

Feature importance can be estimated two ways:

  • Impurity-based — sum the decrease in Gini/entropy from each feature across all trees. Fast but biased toward high-cardinality features.
  • Permutation importance — measure the increase in OOB error when a feature's values are randomly shuffled. More reliable but slower.
HyperparameterEffectTypical default
Number of trees \( B \)More trees → lower variance, diminishing returns100-500
Features per split \( m \)Lower → less correlation, possibly higher bias\(\sqrt{d}\) (clf), \(d/3\) (reg)
Max depthDeeper → lower bias but more overfitting per treeNone (grow fully)

Random forests vs gradient boosting: Both are tree ensembles, but they differ fundamentally. Random forests build trees independently in parallel (variance reduction via averaging), while gradient boosting builds trees sequentially, each correcting the errors of the previous ensemble (bias reduction via boosting). Gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) generally achieves higher accuracy with tuning, but random forests are more robust to hyperparameter choices and easier to parallelise.

title: Support Vector Machine (SVM)
summary: A classifier that finds the maximum-margin hyperplane separating two classes. The decision boundary is determined by a small subset of training points called support vectors. SVMs can learn non-linear boundaries via the kernel trick.

papers.citation: 1995 · Corinna Cortes, Vladimir Vapnik · Support-Vector Networks

sections.body:
A classifier that finds the maximum-margin hyperplane separating two classes. The decision boundary is determined by a small subset of training points called support vectors. SVMs can learn non-linear boundaries via the kernel trick.

For linearly separable data, the hard-margin SVM solves:

\[ \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 \;\; \forall i \]

The margin is \( 2/\|\mathbf{w}\| \), and maximising it is equivalent to minimising \( \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \). In practice, data is rarely separable, so the soft-margin formulation introduces slack variables \( \xi_i \geq 0 \):

\[ \min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \;\; \xi_i \geq 0 \]

The hyperparameter \( C \) controls the trade-off between margin width and training error. Large \( C \) → narrow margin, fewer misclassifications (risk of overfitting). Small \( C \) → wide margin, more tolerant of errors (risk of underfitting).

The dual formulation (via Lagrange multipliers \( \alpha_i \)) reveals the kernel trick:

\[ \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \]

Since the data appears only through inner products \( \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \), replacing these with a kernel function \( K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \) enables non-linear boundaries without explicitly computing the feature map.

KernelFormulaUse case
Linear\( K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} \)Text classification, high-\(d\) sparse data
Polynomial\( K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + c)^d \)NLP (degree 2-3 common)
RBF (Gaussian)\( K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp(-\gamma\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}\|^2) \)General non-linear; infinite-dim feature space

Support vectors are the training points with \( \alpha_i > 0 \). By the KKT conditions, only points on or within the margin have non-zero \( \alpha_i \). The decision function depends only on these points: \( f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in \text{SV}} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right) \). This sparsity is both a computational advantage and a form of compression.

SVMs dominated ML in the 2000s, particularly for text classification (linear SVM) and image recognition (RBF kernel on HOG features). Training is \( O(n^2) \) to \( O(n^3) \) due to quadratic programming, which limits scalability. The SMO algorithm (Platt, 1998) made training practical by decomposing the QP into small sub-problems. SVMs have largely been superseded by deep learning for high-dimensional tasks, but linear SVMs remain competitive for sparse, high-dimensional data.

Derivations

The Lagrangian for the hard-margin primal is:

\[ \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i\bigl[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1\bigr] \]

Setting \(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{w} = 0\):

\[ \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \]

Setting \(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b = 0\):

\[ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0 \]

Substituting back yields the dual:

\[ \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \]

title: Kernel Methods
summary: A family of algorithms that operate in high-dimensional (possibly infinite) feature spaces by using kernel functions to compute inner products without explicitly constructing the feature map. This is the "kernel trick" — it makes non-lin…

papers.citation: 1964 · Mark Aizerman, Emmanuel Braverman, Lev Rozonoer (potential functions) · Popularised by Vapnik (1990s)

sections.body:
A family of algorithms that operate in high-dimensional (possibly infinite) feature spaces by using kernel functions to compute inner products without explicitly constructing the feature map. This is the "kernel trick" — it makes non-linear methods computationally tractable.

A kernel function \( K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R} \) implicitly computes the inner product in a feature space \( \mathcal{H} \):

\[ K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{z}) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}} \]

where \( \phi: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{H} \) is a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature map. By Mercer's theorem, any continuous, symmetric, positive semi-definite function is a valid kernel corresponding to some feature space.

Concrete example: The polynomial kernel \( K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{z})^2 \) for \( \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \) implicitly maps to: \[ \phi(x_1, x_2) = (x_1^2,\; x_2^2,\; \sqrt{2}\,x_1 x_2) \] Computing \( K \) costs \( O(d) \) (a dot product), while explicitly computing \( \phi \) and taking the inner product in feature space costs \( O(d^2) \). For the RBF kernel, \( \phi \) is infinite-dimensional, so the kernel trick is not merely efficient — it is the only option.

The kernel trick works because many algorithms (SVM, PCA, ridge regression) depend on the data only through pairwise inner products. Replacing \( \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \) with \( K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \) "kernelises" the algorithm.

sections.title: Examples of kernelised algorithms
sections.body:
• Kernel SVM — non-linear classification via the dual formulation
  • Kernel PCA — non-linear dimensionality reduction by eigendecomposition of the kernel matrix
  • Gaussian processes — non-parametric Bayesian regression where the kernel defines the prior covariance
  • Kernel ridge regression — closed-form: \( \boldsymbol{\alpha} = (\mathbf{K} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{y} \)

The representer theorem guarantees that the optimal solution of a regularised risk minimisation problem in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) can be written as a linear combination of kernel evaluations at the training points:

\[ f^*(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) \]

This means we only need \( n \) parameters regardless of the (possibly infinite) dimensionality of \( \mathcal{H} \).

Scalability: Kernel methods require constructing and operating on the \( n \times n \) Gram matrix \( \mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \), giving \( O(n^2) \) storage and \( O(n^3) \) for inversion. This limits applicability to large datasets. Random Fourier features (Rahimi and Recht, 2007) approximate shift-invariant kernels using random projections: \( K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \approx \hat{\phi}(\mathbf{x})^\top \hat{\phi}(\mathbf{z}) \) where \( \hat{\phi} \in \mathbb{R}^D \) with \( D \ll n \), enabling linear-time algorithms.

title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
summary: An unsupervised method that finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance in the data, projecting it onto a lower-dimensional subspace while preserving as much variance as possible. PCA is the most widely used linear dimensionality red…

papers.citation: 1901 · Karl Pearson · On Lines and Planes of Closest Fit to Systems of Points in Space

sections.body:
An unsupervised method that finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance in the data, projecting it onto a lower-dimensional subspace while preserving as much variance as possible. PCA is the most widely used linear dimensionality reduction technique.

Given centred data \( \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \), PCA finds the directions (principal components) that maximise projected variance. The first principal component solves:

\[ \mathbf{v}_1 = \arg\max_{\|\mathbf{v}\|=1} \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{v} \]

where \( \mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} \) is the covariance matrix. By the method of Lagrange multipliers, the solution satisfies \( \mathbf{S}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v} \) — i.e., the principal components are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, sorted by descending eigenvalue. The top \( k \) eigenvectors form the projection matrix \( \mathbf{V}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \).

Equivalently, PCA can be computed via the singular value decomposition (SVD) of \( \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^\top \). The columns of \( \mathbf{V} \) are the principal components, and the projected data is \( \mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{V}_k = \mathbf{U}_k\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k \). The SVD approach is numerically more stable than explicit eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix.

Dual interpretation: PCA simultaneously (1) maximises variance of the projection and (2) minimises reconstruction error \( \|\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{V}_k\mathbf{V}_k^\top\|_F^2 \). These two objectives yield the same solution.

The explained variance ratio for component \( j \) is \( \lambda_j / \sum_i \lambda_i \), indicating what fraction of total variance that component captures. A common heuristic: choose \( k \) to retain 95% of total variance.

PropertyPCAAlternative
LinearityLinearKernel PCA (non-linear)
ObjectiveMax variance / min reconstruction errort-SNE/UMAP preserve local structure
Complexity\( O(\min(nd^2, n^2d)) \) via SVDRandomised PCA: \( O(ndk) \)
LimitationsLinear; sensitive to scaling; captures variance, not class structureLDA uses class labels

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• Standardise features before PCA (zero mean, unit variance) — otherwise high-variance features dominate
  • Whitening: projecting onto principal components and dividing by \(\sqrt{\lambda_j}\) yields uncorrelated, unit-variance features — useful as preprocessing for downstream models
  • Randomised PCA (Halko et al., 2011) uses random projections to approximate the top \( k \) components in \( O(ndk) \) time, making PCA practical for very large datasets
Derivations

Maximise variance subject to unit norm constraint:

\[ \max_{\mathbf{v}} \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{v} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{v} = 1 \]

Lagrangian: \( \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{S} \mathbf{v} - \lambda(\mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{v} - 1) \)

Setting \( \nabla_\mathbf{v} \mathcal{L} = 0 \):

\[ 2\mathbf{S}\mathbf{v} - 2\lambda\mathbf{v} = 0 \implies \mathbf{S}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v} \]

So \(\mathbf{v}\) must be an eigenvector of \(\mathbf{S}\). The projected variance is \(\mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{S}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\), so choosing the largest eigenvalue maximises variance.

Python 
import numpy as np

def pca(X, k):
    """Project X onto its top-k principal components."""
    X_centered = X - X.mean(axis=0)
    U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(X_centered, full_matrices=False)
    components = Vt[:k]          # top-k right singular vectors
    projected = X_centered @ components.T
    explained_var = (S[:k] ** 2) / (S ** 2).sum()
    return projected, components, explained_var

title: Dimensionality Reduction
summary: The process of reducing the number of features (dimensions) in a dataset while preserving as much relevant structure as possible. Dimensionality reduction combats the curse of dimensionality, reduces computational cost, removes collinear…

papers.citation: 1901 · Pearson · On Lines and Planes of Closest Fit to Systems of Points in Space
papers.note: Foundational paper for principal component analysis.

papers.citation: 1933 · Hotelling · Analysis of a Complex of Statistical Variables into Principal Components
papers.note: Formal development of PCA in modern multivariate statistics.

papers.citation: 2008 · van der Maaten and Hinton · Visualizing Data using t-SNE
2018 · McInnes et al. · UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction

sections.body:
The process of reducing the number of features (dimensions) in a dataset while preserving as much relevant structure as possible. Dimensionality reduction combats the curse of dimensionality, reduces computational cost, removes collinear features, and can improve generalisation by discarding noise dimensions.

The curse of dimensionality motivates the entire field: in high dimensions, distances between points concentrate (become nearly equal), volumes grow exponentially, and data becomes hopelessly sparse. Covering a \( d \)-dimensional unit cube with spacing 0.1 requires \( 10^d \) points — manageable for \( d = 3 \) (1,000 points) but astronomical for \( d = 100 \).
Linear methods project data onto a lower-dimensional subspace:

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the orthogonal directions of maximum variance. Given centred data \( \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \), PCA solves the eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix:

\[ \mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^\top \]

where \( \mathbf{U} \) contains the eigenvectors (principal components) and \( \boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d) \) the eigenvalues sorted in descending order. Projecting onto the top \( k \) eigenvectors gives the optimal rank-\( k \) approximation in the least-squares sense (Eckart–Young theorem). The fraction of variance retained is \( \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i \).

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is the supervised counterpart — it maximises the ratio of between-class to within-class scatter:

\[ \mathbf{w}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}} \]

where \( \mathbf{S}_B \) and \( \mathbf{S}_W \) are the between-class and within-class scatter matrices. LDA projects to at most \( C - 1 \) dimensions for \( C \) classes.

Random projections exploit the Johnson–Lindenstrauss lemma: for any \( \epsilon > 0 \), \( n \) points in \( \mathbb{R}^d \) can be projected to \( k = O(\epsilon^{-2} \log n) \) dimensions while preserving all pairwise distances within a factor of \( 1 \pm \epsilon \).

Non-linear methods (manifold learning) capture curved structures:

  • t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008) — minimises KL divergence between pairwise similarity distributions in high and low dimensions; excels at visualisation but is non-parametric and non-convex
  • UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018) — similar motivation using fuzzy simplicial sets; faster than t-SNE and better at preserving global structure
  • Autoencoders — neural networks with a bottleneck layer; learn a non-linear encoding \( f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^k \) and decoding \( g: \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^d \) minimising reconstruction error \( \| \mathbf{x} - g(f(\mathbf{x})) \|^2 \)
  • Isomap (Tenenbaum et al., 2000) — applies MDS to geodesic distances estimated via a nearest-neighbour graph
MethodTypeParametric?Best for
PCALinearYesGeneral preprocessing, denoising, whitening
LDALinearYesSupervised classification with Gaussian classes
t-SNENon-linearNo2D/3D visualisation of clusters
UMAPNon-linearOptionalVisualisation + downstream tasks
AutoencoderNon-linearYesLearned representations, generative models

In modern deep learning, explicit dimensionality reduction as a preprocessing step is less common — networks learn their own representations internally. However, PCA remains widely used for data exploration, and t-SNE/UMAP are standard tools for visualising embedding spaces (e.g., plotting word2vec or BERT embeddings in 2D).

title: Transformer
summary: A neural network architecture based entirely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. The Transformer processes all positions in parallel via self-attention and has become the dominant architecture for NLP, v…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A neural network architecture based entirely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. The Transformer processes all positions in parallel via self-attention and has become the dominant architecture for NLP, vision, protein folding, and increasingly all of deep learning.

The original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) uses an encoder-decoder structure, designed for sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation. Each encoder layer contains:

  1. Multi-head self-attention
  2. Position-wise feed-forward network (two linear layers with ReLU: \( \text{FFN}(x) = W_2 \max(0, W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 \), with inner dimension \( d_{ff} = 4d_{\text{model}} \))

Each sub-layer has a residual connection and layer normalisation: \( \text{output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x)) \). The decoder adds a third sub-layer: cross-attention over the encoder output, with causal masking in the self-attention to prevent attending to future tokens.

The self-attention mechanism computes:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \]

where \( Q = XW^Q \), \( K = XW^K \), \( V = XW^V \) are linear projections with \( W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k} \), \( W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v} \). The \( \sqrt{d_k} \) scaling prevents dot products from growing with dimension, avoiding softmax saturation.

Multi-head attention runs \( h \) parallel attention heads with separate projections, then concatenates and projects:

\[ \text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)W^O \]

where each \( \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V) \). The original model uses \( h = 8 \) heads with \( d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h = 64 \). Multiple heads allow the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions simultaneously.

Since attention is permutation-equivariant (it has no notion of position), positional encodings are added to the input embeddings. The original paper used sinusoidal encodings:

\[ PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \]

These were chosen because they allow the model to learn relative positions via linear projections. Modern variants use rotary embeddings (RoPE), ALiBi, or learned encodings.

Parameter count: For a model with \( L \) layers, \( d \)-dimensional embeddings, and vocabulary size \( |V| \):

  • Attention: \( 4d^2 \) per layer (Q, K, V, output projections)
  • FFN: \( 8d^2 \) per layer (two matrices of size \( d \times 4d \))
  • Embeddings: \( |V| \cdot d \)
  • Total \( \approx 12Ld^2 + |V|d \)
VariantArchitectureExamples
Encoder-onlyBidirectional self-attentionBERT, RoBERTa
Decoder-onlyCausal self-attentionGPT, LLaMA, Claude
Encoder-decoderFull original architectureT5, BART, original Transformer

The Transformer's \( O(n^2) \) attention cost with respect to sequence length has motivated efficient variants (FlashAttention, sparse attention, linear attention), but the quadratic mechanism remains standard for most production models. The paper's title — "Attention Is All You Need" — proved prophetic: the architecture's simplicity, parallelisability, and scalability made it the foundation of the large language model era.

title: Decoder Block
summary: A single processing layer within the decoder stack of a Transformer. In a decoder-only model (e.g., GPT, LLaMA), each decoder block consists of masked self-attention, a feed-forward network, residual connections, and normalisation layers.

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A single processing layer within the decoder stack of a Transformer. In a decoder-only model (e.g., GPT, LLaMA), each decoder block consists of masked self-attention, a feed-forward network, residual connections, and normalisation layers.

A standard decoder block processes a sequence of hidden states \( \mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \) through two sub-layers:

  1. Masked multi-head self-attention: Each token attends only to itself and preceding tokens (causal mask), preventing information leakage from future positions during autoregressive generation. The causal mask is a lower-triangular matrix applied to the score matrix before softmax, setting future positions to \( -\infty \).
  2. Position-wise feed-forward network (FFN): Two linear transformations with a non-linearity: \[ \text{FFN}(\mathbf{x}) = W_2\,\sigma(W_1\mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 \] where \( W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_{ff}} \), \( W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{ff} \times d} \), and \( d_{ff} = 4d \) by convention. The FFN accounts for roughly two-thirds of the parameters in each block.

Each sub-layer is wrapped with a residual connection and normalisation. Modern architectures use pre-norm (normalise before the sub-layer) rather than the original post-norm, as it improves training stability:

\[ \mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H} + \text{Attn}(\text{Norm}(\mathbf{H})) \] \[ \mathbf{H}'' = \mathbf{H}' + \text{FFN}(\text{Norm}(\mathbf{H}')) \]

In encoder-decoder Transformers, the decoder block has a third sub-layer: cross-attention over the encoder output, inserted between self-attention and the FFN.

Modern variants introduce several modifications to the original design:

  • RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm — removes the mean-centering step, reducing computation with no loss in quality (used in LLaMA, Gemma)
  • SwiGLU / GeGLU activation in the FFN — replaces the ReLU with a gated linear unit: \( \text{SwiGLU}(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{x} W_1 \odot \text{Swish}(\mathbf{x} W_3)) W_2 \), adding a third weight matrix but improving performance (used in LLaMA, PaLM)
  • Grouped-query attention (GQA) — shares key-value heads across query heads to reduce KV-cache memory during inference (used in LLaMA 2 70B, Mistral)
  • Rotary position embeddings (RoPE) — encodes relative position directly into the attention computation rather than adding positional embeddings to the input

A full Transformer decoder stacks \( L \) such blocks (e.g., GPT-3 uses 96 layers, LLaMA-70B uses 80). The final block's output is projected to vocabulary logits via an unembedding matrix \( W_U \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times |V|} \) for next-token prediction.

title: Decoder-only Transformer
summary: A Transformer architecture that uses only the decoder stack with causal (masked) self-attention, trained to predict the next token autoregressively. This is the architecture behind GPT, LLaMA, Claude, and most modern large language models.

papers.citation: 2018 · Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, Sutskever · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training (GPT-1)

sections.body:
A Transformer architecture that uses only the decoder stack with causal (masked) self-attention, trained to predict the next token autoregressively. This is the architecture behind GPT, LLaMA, Claude, and most modern large language models.

The decoder-only design removes the encoder and cross-attention entirely, leaving a stack of \( L \) identical blocks each containing:

  1. Causal self-attention — each position attends only to positions \( \leq t \), enforced by a lower-triangular mask
  2. Feed-forward network — typically with \( d_{ff} = 4d \) inner dimension

Both sub-layers use residual connections and normalisation (pre-norm in modern variants).

Training uses the causal language modelling (CLM) objective — maximum likelihood over next-token prediction:

\[ \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}; \theta) \]

Every prefix of the input serves as both context and target simultaneously, making training efficient — a single forward pass over a sequence of length \( T \) produces \( T \) training signals (teacher forcing). This is more sample-efficient than masked language modelling (BERT), which only predicts ~15% of tokens per pass.

Why decoder-only won: Several factors drove the dominance of this architecture:

  • Simplicity — one unified architecture, no encoder-decoder split, fewer design decisions
  • Scalability — no encoder bottleneck; the entire model is a single homogeneous stack that scales predictably (Kaplan et al., 2020 scaling laws)
  • Emergent versatility — despite seeing only left context during training, decoder-only models perform well on understanding tasks through in-context learning, eliminating the need for task-specific architectures
  • KV caching — during autoregressive inference, the key-value tensors from previous positions can be cached and reused, so each new token requires only a single-row attention computation rather than reprocessing the full sequence
ArchitectureAttentionTraining signalStrengths
Encoder-only (BERT)Bidirectional~15% masked tokensClassification, NLU
Encoder-decoder (T5)Bidirectional + causalCorrupted spansSeq2seq, translation
Decoder-only (GPT)Causal only100% of tokensGeneration, in-context learning, scalability

Key models in the lineage: GPT-1 (117M params, 2018) demonstrated generative pre-training; GPT-2 (1.5B, 2019) showed zero-shot task transfer; GPT-3 (175B, 2020) demonstrated in-context learning at scale; subsequent models (PaLM, LLaMA, Claude) refined the architecture with improvements like RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, and GQA but retained the core decoder-only structure.

title: Self-Attention
summary: A mechanism where each element in a sequence computes a weighted sum over all other elements, with weights determined by pairwise compatibility scores. Self-attention allows every position to directly attend to every other position, capt…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A mechanism where each element in a sequence computes a weighted sum over all other elements, with weights determined by pairwise compatibility scores. Self-attention allows every position to directly attend to every other position, capturing long-range dependencies in \( O(1) \) sequential operations.

Given an input sequence \( \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \), self-attention projects it into queries, keys, and values using learned weight matrices:

\[ Q = XW^Q, \quad K = XW^K, \quad V = XW^V \]

where \( W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k} \) and \( W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v} \). The attention output is:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \]

Each row of the output is a weighted combination of value vectors, where the weights reflect how much each query "matches" each key. The \( \sqrt{d_k} \) scaling prevents dot products from growing with dimension, which would push the softmax into near-one-hot distributions and kill gradients.

Intuition via information retrieval: Self-attention can be understood through a retrieval analogy. Each token broadcasts a query ("what am I looking for?"), a key ("what do I contain?"), and a value ("what do I provide if selected?"). The dot product \( \mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j \) measures relevance, and the softmax converts scores into a probability distribution over which values to aggregate.

Self-attention differs from cross-attention in that Q, K, and V all come from the same sequence. This allows the model to build contextualised representations where each token's representation depends on the entire sequence context.

Computational cost: \( O(n^2 d) \) time and \( O(n^2) \) memory for the attention matrix, where \( n \) is sequence length. This quadratic scaling is the primary bottleneck for long sequences and has motivated efficient variants:

  • FlashAttention — fuses the attention computation to avoid materialising the \( n \times n \) matrix, achieving exact attention with reduced memory
  • Sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird) — restricts the attention pattern to local windows plus global tokens
  • Linear attention — replaces softmax with kernel approximations to achieve \( O(n) \) complexity

Connection to other mechanisms: Unlike RNNs (which compress history into a fixed-size hidden state, creating an information bottleneck) or CNNs (which have limited receptive fields that grow logarithmically with depth), self-attention provides direct \( O(1) \) path length between any two positions. Self-attention can also be viewed as a form of associative memory or a soft dictionary lookup — it has connections to Hopfield networks, where the softmax attention rule corresponds to the update rule of modern continuous Hopfield networks (Ramsauer et al., 2020).

title: Attention Score
summary: The raw compatibility value between a query and a key, computed before normalisation. In scaled dot-product attention, the attention score is the dot product of the query and key vectors, divided by \( \sqrt{d_k} \), measuring how releva…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
The raw compatibility value between a query and a key, computed before normalisation. In scaled dot-product attention, the attention score is the dot product of the query and key vectors, divided by \( \sqrt{d_k} \), measuring how relevant a key position is to a given query.

For a query vector \( \mathbf{q}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k} \) and key vector \( \mathbf{k}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k} \), the attention score is:

\[ e_{ij} = \frac{\mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} \]

The full score matrix for a sequence of length \( n \) is \( \mathbf{E} = \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \), requiring \( O(n^2 d_k) \) operations to compute. These raw scores are then transformed into attention weights via softmax (and optionally masking).

Geometric interpretation: The dot product \( \mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j = \|\mathbf{q}_i\| \|\mathbf{k}_j\| \cos\theta \) measures both the alignment (angle) and magnitude of the query and key vectors. Unlike cosine similarity, which normalises by magnitude, dot-product scores allow the model to modulate attention strength through vector norms — a property the model can learn to exploit.

Alternative scoring functions have been proposed in the attention literature:

Scoring functionFormulaOriginProperties
Dot-product\( \mathbf{q}^\top \mathbf{k} \)Luong (2015)Fast, no extra params
Scaled dot-product\( \mathbf{q}^\top \mathbf{k} / \sqrt{d_k} \)Vaswani (2017)Variance-normalised
Additive (concat)\( \mathbf{v}^\top \tanh(W_1\mathbf{q} + W_2\mathbf{k}) \)Bahdanau (2015)More expressive, slower
Multiplicative (bilinear)\( \mathbf{q}^\top W \mathbf{k} \)Luong (2015)Learned interaction matrix

Vaswani et al. found that scaled dot-product and additive attention perform similarly in quality, but dot-product attention is significantly faster in practice because it can be implemented as a single matrix multiplication \( QK^\top \), leveraging highly optimised BLAS/GPU kernels. The scaling factor \( \sqrt{d_k} \) is critical: without it, for large \( d_k \), the variance of the dot products grows as \( d_k \), pushing the softmax into near-one-hot outputs with vanishing gradients.

Relationship to the attention pipeline: Attention scores are the first stage in a three-step process: (1) compute scores \( e_{ij} \), (2) optionally mask and normalise via softmax to get attention weights \( \alpha_{ij} \), (3) compute weighted sum of value vectors to produce the output.

title: What is a scaled attention score?
summary: An attention score divided by \( \sqrt{d_k} \) (the square root of the key/query dimension) to prevent the dot products from growing too large, which would push the softmax into extremely peaked distributions and cause vanishing gradients.

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
An attention score divided by \( \sqrt{d_k} \) (the square root of the key/query dimension) to prevent the dot products from growing too large, which would push the softmax into extremely peaked distributions and cause vanishing gradients.

\[ \text{score}(i,j) = \frac{\mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} \]

Why scale? If \( \mathbf{q} \) and \( \mathbf{k} \) have independent components with zero mean and unit variance, their dot product has:

\[ \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{q}^\top \mathbf{k}] = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(\mathbf{q}^\top \mathbf{k}) = d_k \]

As \( d_k \) grows (e.g., \( d_k = 64 \) per head in the original Transformer), the dot products become large in magnitude. This pushes the softmax into saturation:

  • The softmax output becomes near-one-hot — the model makes hard attention decisions rather than smooth, differentiable weightings
  • Gradients through the softmax become vanishingly small: for a peaked softmax output \( \alpha_i \approx 1 \), the Jacobian entries \( \alpha_i(1 - \alpha_i) \approx 0 \)
  • Learning stalls because the attention pattern is effectively frozen

Dividing by \( \sqrt{d_k} \) normalises the variance back to \( O(1) \), keeping the softmax in a regime where gradients flow well and the model can smoothly distribute attention across relevant positions.

Comparison with additive attention: Bahdanau-style additive attention \( \mathbf{v}^\top \tanh(W_1 \mathbf{q} + W_2 \mathbf{k}) \) does not suffer from this scaling issue because the \(\tanh\) saturates at \(\pm 1\) regardless of dimension. Vaswani et al. showed that scaled dot-product attention matches additive attention in quality while being faster and more memory-efficient (leveraging optimised matrix multiplication).

Practical note: The scaling factor is typically built into the attention kernel and is not a tunable hyperparameter. Some implementations (e.g., in multi-query attention or alternative architectures) have experimented with learned temperature parameters \( \tau \) replacing \( \sqrt{d_k} \), but the original fixed scaling remains standard.

title: Masked Attention Score
summary: An attention score that has been modified by a mask to prevent certain positions from being attended to. In causal (autoregressive) models, future positions are masked to \( -\infty \) before softmax, ensuring each token can only attend…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
An attention score that has been modified by a mask to prevent certain positions from being attended to. In causal (autoregressive) models, future positions are masked to \( -\infty \) before softmax, ensuring each token can only attend to itself and preceding tokens.

The masking is applied to the score matrix before the softmax:

\[ \text{MaskedScore}_{ij} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases} \]

After softmax, positions masked with \( -\infty \) receive zero weight since \( e^{-\infty} = 0 \). This is mathematically equivalent to restricting the normalisation denominator to only the unmasked positions:

\[ \alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k \leq i} \exp(e_{ik})} \quad \text{for } j \leq i, \qquad \alpha_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{for } j > i \]

sections.title: Types of masks used in Transformer models
sections.body:
• Causal mask — lower-triangular matrix; prevents attending to future tokens. Essential for autoregressive generation so the model cannot "cheat" by looking ahead. Used in all decoder-only models (GPT, LLaMA, Claude).
  • Padding mask — masks out padding tokens in variable-length batches so they contribute neither as keys nor queries. Without padding masks, padding tokens would absorb and dilute attention weight.
  • Prefix mask — allows bidirectional attention over a prefix (prompt) while maintaining causal attention over the generated continuation. Used in prefix-tuning (Li & Liang, 2021) and some infilling models.
  • Sliding window mask — restricts attention to a local window of \( w \) tokens, reducing complexity from \( O(n^2) \) to \( O(nw) \). Used in Mistral and Longformer.

In practice, the mask is implemented as a boolean or binary tensor. The masked score is computed as \( \hat{e}_{ij} = e_{ij} + (1 - M_{ij}) \cdot c \), where \( c \) is a large negative constant (typically \(-10^9\) or -float('inf')). Using \(-10^9\) instead of true \(-\infty\) avoids NaN issues when an entire row is masked in mixed-precision (fp16) training.

title: Attention Weights
summary: The normalised probability distribution over positions that determines how much each value vector contributes to a given position's output. Attention weights are obtained by applying softmax to the (optionally masked and scaled) attentio…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
The normalised probability distribution over positions that determines how much each value vector contributes to a given position's output. Attention weights are obtained by applying softmax to the (optionally masked and scaled) attention scores.

Given scaled attention scores \( e_{ij} \), the attention weight from position \( i \) to position \( j \) is:

\[ \alpha_{ij} = \text{softmax}_j(e_{ij}) = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(e_{ik})} \]

The output for position \( i \) is then the weighted combination of value vectors:

\[ \mathbf{o}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{ij} \mathbf{v}_j \]

In matrix form: \( \mathbf{A} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \) and \( O = AV \).

sections.title: Key properties
sections.body:
• Non-negative and sum to 1 across the key dimension — forming a valid probability distribution per query position
  • Differentiable — the softmax and weighted sum are smooth, allowing gradient-based training. The Jacobian of softmax is \( \frac{\partial \alpha_i}{\partial e_j} = \alpha_i(\delta_{ij} - \alpha_j) \)
  • Temperature sensitivity — multiplying scores by a temperature \( \tau \) (or equivalently changing the scaling factor) controls the sharpness: low temperature produces peaked weights (hard attention), high temperature produces uniform weights

Interpretability: Attention weight matrices are commonly visualised as heatmaps to understand which tokens a position attends to. However, interpreting these patterns requires caution — Jain & Wallace (2019) showed that attention weights often do not reliably indicate feature importance, and different heads can produce similar outputs with very different attention patterns. Attention weights reflect the model's information routing, not necessarily the causal importance of input tokens.

Multi-head specialisation: In multi-head attention, each head produces its own \( n \times n \) weight matrix, often specialising in different linguistic patterns. Clark et al. (2019) found that BERT heads learn interpretable patterns: some attend to the previous/next token, some to syntactic dependents, and one head in each layer broadly attends to the [SEP] token. The full attention weight tensor for all \( h \) heads across \( L \) layers uses \( O(Lhn^2) \) memory — this is why FlashAttention avoids materialising it, instead computing attention output tile-by-tile in SRAM.

title: Attention Mask
summary: A binary or boolean tensor applied to the attention score matrix to control which positions can attend to which. Masks enforce structural constraints like causality (no peeking at future tokens) and padding exclusion (ignore filler token…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A binary or boolean tensor applied to the attention score matrix to control which positions can attend to which. Masks enforce structural constraints like causality (no peeking at future tokens) and padding exclusion (ignore filler tokens in batched sequences).

The mask \( \mathbf{M} \in \{0, 1\}^{n \times n} \) is applied by setting masked positions to \( -\infty \) before softmax:

\[ \hat{e}_{ij} = e_{ij} + (1 - M_{ij}) \cdot (-\infty) \]

After softmax, masked positions receive zero weight since \( \exp(-\infty) = 0 \). The mask shapes the information flow graph of the model — it determines which tokens can influence which other tokens.

sections.title: Common mask types
sections.body:
MaskShapePurposeUsed by
CausalLower-triangularAutoregressive generationGPT, LLaMA, Claude
PaddingRow/column maskVariable-length batchingAll batched models
PrefixBidirectional prefix + causal restPrefix-tuning, infillingT5, UL2, prefix LMs
Sliding windowBand diagonal (\( w \)-wide)Efficient long-context attentionLongformer, Mistral
Global + localSparse structured patternCombining local and global attentionBigBird, ETC

Implementation details: In Hugging Face Transformers, the attention_mask argument typically refers to the padding mask (1 = attend, 0 = ignore), while causal masking is handled internally by the model class. The two masks are combined before being applied. For causal models, the library constructs a lower-triangular mask and adds it to the user-supplied padding mask.

Broadcasting: Masks are typically shaped as \( (B, 1, 1, n) \) for padding masks (broadcast across heads and query positions) or \( (1, 1, n, n) \) for causal masks (broadcast across batch and heads). This avoids redundant memory allocation.

Connection to graph neural networks: The attention mask can be viewed as an adjacency matrix defining a directed graph — self-attention with a causal mask is equivalent to message passing on a directed acyclic graph where each node receives messages only from itself and its predecessors. Sparse masks like those in Longformer explicitly design this graph structure to balance expressiveness against computational cost.

title: Causal Mask
summary: A lower-triangular binary mask applied inside scaled dot-product attention to prevent each position from attending to future positions, enforcing the autoregressive property: position \( t \) can only attend to positions \( 1, \ldots, t \).

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A lower-triangular binary mask applied inside scaled dot-product attention to prevent each position from attending to future positions, enforcing the autoregressive property: position \( t \) can only attend to positions \( 1, \ldots, t \).

The causal mask \( \mathbf{M} \in \{0, -\infty\}^{n \times n} \) is defined as:

\[ M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } j \leq i \\ -\infty & \text{if } j > i \end{cases} \]

It is applied additively to the attention logits before the softmax:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V \]

Because \( e^{-\infty} = 0 \), the masked positions receive zero attention weight after softmax. In practice, \( -\infty \) is replaced by a large negative float (e.g., \(-10^{9}\) or -float('inf') in PyTorch).

Why it matters for training: During training with teacher forcing, the entire target sequence is fed in at once. The causal mask allows parallel computation of all next-token predictions in a single forward pass — position \( t \) predicts token \( t+1 \) using only tokens \( 1, \ldots, t \). Without the mask, every position would see the full sequence, including the token it is supposed to predict, making the task trivial and the model useless at generation time.

During inference, tokens are generated one at a time, so the autoregressive property is naturally satisfied. However, when using a KV cache, the mask is still applied to ensure cached keys from future positions (if any exist from padding) are not attended to.

sections.title: Variants and related masks
sections.body:
• Padding mask — masks out padding tokens in variable-length batches (applied in both encoder and decoder)
  • Encoder-decoder mask — in cross-attention, the decoder queries attend to all encoder positions (no causal constraint)
  • Prefix LM mask — a hybrid where a prefix is fully visible (bidirectional) and the rest is causal; used in T5 and UL2
  • Sliding-window causal mask — restricts attention to a fixed window of recent tokens (used in Mistral)

The original paper calls it the "subsequent mask." It is also known as the "look-ahead mask" in some frameworks (e.g., TensorFlow tutorials).

title: Multi-Head Attention
summary: An extension of self-attention that runs \( h \) parallel attention operations ("heads"), each with its own learned projections, then concatenates and linearly projects the results. This allows the model to jointly attend to information…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
An extension of self-attention that runs \( h \) parallel attention operations ("heads"), each with its own learned projections, then concatenates and linearly projects the results. This allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions.

\[ \text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O \]

where each head computes:

\[ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q,\; KW_i^K,\; VW_i^V) \]

with projection matrices \( W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k} \), \( W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v} \), and output projection \( W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d} \). Typically \( d_k = d_v = d/h \), so the total computation is similar to single-head attention with full dimensionality.

Why multiple heads? A single attention head computes one set of weights — a single "view" of the relationships in the sequence. Multiple heads allow the model to capture different types of relationships simultaneously:

  • One head might attend to the syntactic head of a phrase
  • Another might track coreference
  • Another might attend to the previous token (local context)
ModelHeads \( h \)\( d_{\text{model}} \)\( d_k \)
Transformer (base)851264
GPT-39612288128
LLaMA-70B648192128

title: Attention Head
summary: A single instance of the scaled dot-product attention mechanism within multi-head attention, operating on a lower-dimensional subspace of the full model dimension. Each head learns its own query, key, and value projections, enabling it t…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A single instance of the scaled dot-product attention mechanism within multi-head attention, operating on a lower-dimensional subspace of the full model dimension. Each head learns its own query, key, and value projections, enabling it to specialise in capturing different types of relationships.

Head \( i \) computes:

\[ \text{head}_i = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{(XW_i^Q)(XW_i^K)^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)(XW_i^V) \]

where \( W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k} \) and \( W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v} \), with \( d_k = d_v = d/h \) typically.

Head specialisation: Empirical studies (e.g., Clark et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019) have found that individual heads often specialise:

  • Positional heads — attend to fixed relative positions (e.g., previous token, first token)
  • Syntactic heads — track dependency relations (subject→verb, noun→modifier)
  • Rare-pattern heads — activate for specific semantic or lexical patterns
  • Redundant heads — some heads can be pruned with minimal performance loss

The discovery that many heads are redundant motivated grouped-query attention (GQA), which shares key-value projections across groups of heads to reduce KV-cache memory during inference.

title: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)
summary: A variant of multi-head attention that shares key and value projections across groups of query heads, reducing KV-cache memory during inference while retaining most of multi-head attention's quality. GQA interpolates between multi-head a…

papers.citation: 2023 · Joshua Ainslie et al. · GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints

sections.body:
A variant of multi-head attention that shares key and value projections across groups of query heads, reducing KV-cache memory during inference while retaining most of multi-head attention's quality. GQA interpolates between multi-head attention (MHA) and multi-query attention (MQA).

In standard MHA with \( h \) heads, there are \( h \) separate Q, K, V projections each. In GQA with \( g \) groups:

  • \( h \) query heads (unchanged)
  • \( g \) key-value heads, each shared by \( h/g \) query heads
MethodKV headsKV cache sizeQuality
MHA\( h \)\( O(h \cdot n \cdot d_k) \)Highest
GQA\( g \) (1 < g < h)\( O(g \cdot n \cdot d_k) \)Near-MHA
MQA1\( O(n \cdot d_k) \)Slightly lower

The primary motivation is inference efficiency: during autoregressive generation, the KV cache stores key and value tensors for all previous tokens. With MHA, this grows as \( O(Lhn d_k) \) across layers, which becomes a memory bottleneck for long sequences. GQA reduces this by a factor of \( h/g \).

GQA is used in LLaMA 2 (70B), Mistral, and many other modern LLMs. It can be obtained from a pre-trained MHA model via "uptraining" — mean-pooling adjacent KV heads and fine-tuning briefly.

title: Query, Key, Value (QKV)
summary: The three linear projections of the input in attention mechanisms. The query asks "what am I looking for?", the key advertises "what do I contain?", and the value provides "what information do I give if selected?" Attention computes comp…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
The three linear projections of the input in attention mechanisms. The query asks "what am I looking for?", the key advertises "what do I contain?", and the value provides "what information do I give if selected?" Attention computes compatibility between queries and keys to weight the aggregation of values.

Given input \( \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \), the projections are:

\[ Q = XW^Q, \quad K = XW^K, \quad V = XW^V \]

where \( W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k} \) and \( W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_v} \).

The naming comes from an analogy to information retrieval:

ComponentRetrieval analogyRole in attention
Query \( Q \)Search queryWhat this position is looking for
Key \( K \)Index/tag of a documentWhat this position advertises
Value \( V \)Content of the documentInformation to retrieve if matched

The attention score \( \mathbf{q}_i^\top \mathbf{k}_j / \sqrt{d_k} \) measures compatibility between query \( i \) and key \( j \). After softmax normalisation, these scores weight the value vectors to produce the output.

In self-attention, Q, K, V all come from the same input. In cross-attention, Q comes from one sequence (e.g., decoder) while K, V come from another (e.g., encoder output).

Crucially, the separate projections allow the model to learn different representations for "what to look for" versus "what to advertise" versus "what to return" — a single input token plays all three roles simultaneously for different interactions.

title: Projection Matrix
summary: A learned linear transformation that maps input vectors into a different subspace. In Transformers, projection matrices create the Q, K, V representations from the input and project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model di…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need (projection matrices for Q/K/V mappings)
2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
A learned linear transformation that maps input vectors into a different subspace. In Transformers, projection matrices create the Q, K, V representations from the input and project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

Given an input \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \), a projection matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d'} \) maps it to a new space:

\[ \mathbf{z} = \mathbf{x}\,W \]

The key projection matrices in multi-head attention are:

MatrixShapePurpose
\( W_i^Q \)\( d \times d_k \)Project input to query subspace for head \( i \)
\( W_i^K \)\( d \times d_k \)Project input to key subspace for head \( i \)
\( W_i^V \)\( d \times d_v \)Project input to value subspace for head \( i \)
\( W^O \)\( hd_v \times d \)Project concatenated heads back to model dim

Parameter count: For a single multi-head attention layer with \( h \) heads and \( d_k = d_v = d/h \):

\[ \text{Parameters} = \underbrace{3 \cdot d \cdot d}_{W^Q, W^K, W^V} + \underbrace{d \cdot d}_{W^O} = 4d^2 \]

This is independent of the number of heads \( h \), because splitting into heads merely partitions the columns of a single large matrix.

Terminology note: In mathematics, a projection matrix satisfies \( P^2 = P \) (idempotent). The "projection matrices" in Transformers are not projections in this strict sense — they are general linear maps. The term is used loosely to mean "a matrix that maps to a (typically lower-dimensional) subspace."

Beyond attention, projection matrices appear throughout deep learning:

  • Classification head — projects final hidden states to logits: \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times |\mathcal{V}|} \)
  • Embedding layer — projects token IDs to dense vectors (often weight-tied with the classification head)
  • LoRA — adds low-rank pairs \( BA \) where \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \), \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d} \), with \( r \ll d \)
  • Down/up projections in FFN — \( W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_{ff}} \) and \( W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{ff} \times d} \)

title: Position-wise MLP
summary: A two-layer feed-forward network applied independently and identically to each position in the sequence. It is the second sub-layer in every Transformer block, acting as a per-token non-linear transformation after attention has mixed inf…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need
2020 · Shazeer · GLU Variants Improve Transformer

sections.body:
A two-layer feed-forward network applied independently and identically to each position in the sequence. It is the second sub-layer in every Transformer block, acting as a per-token non-linear transformation after attention has mixed information across positions.

The standard formulation:

\[ \text{FFN}(\mathbf{x}) = W_2\,\sigma(W_1\mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 \]

where \( W_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_{ff}} \), \( W_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{ff} \times d} \), and the inner dimension \( d_{ff} \) is typically \( 4d \). The activation \( \sigma \) was originally ReLU; modern models use SwiGLU or GeGLU:

\[ \text{SwiGLU}(\mathbf{x}) = (\text{Swish}(W_1\mathbf{x}) \odot W_3\mathbf{x})\,W_2 \]

which introduces a gating mechanism and a third weight matrix, often with \( d_{ff} = \frac{8}{3}d \) to keep the parameter count comparable.

"Position-wise" means the same weights are applied to every position independently — there is no interaction between positions in this layer. Cross-position mixing happens only in the attention sub-layer. This separation is a key design principle of the Transformer.

The FFN accounts for roughly two-thirds of a Transformer's parameters (the attention projections account for the other third). Research suggests FFN layers act as key-value memories, storing factual knowledge that can be retrieved via the input activations.

title: Residual Connection (Skip Connection)
summary: A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer only needs to learn a residual function \( F(\mathbf{x}) \) rather than the full mapping. Residual connections enable training of very deep networks by providing g…

papers.citation: 2015 · Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, Jian Sun · Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

sections.body:
A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer only needs to learn a residual function \( F(\mathbf{x}) \) rather than the full mapping. Residual connections enable training of very deep networks by providing gradient highways that mitigate the vanishing gradient problem.

\[ \mathbf{y} = F(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{x} \]

where \( F \) is the transformation applied by the layer (e.g., attention or FFN sub-layer). The gradient flows through both \( F \) and the identity shortcut:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + I \]

The identity term \( I \) ensures that gradients can flow unimpeded through the skip path, even if \( \partial F / \partial \mathbf{x} \) is small. This is why networks with hundreds of layers (ResNets, deep Transformers) can be trained effectively.

Residual connections in Transformers: Every sub-layer (attention, FFN) is wrapped with a residual connection:

\[ \mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{x} + \text{SubLayer}(\text{Norm}(\mathbf{x})) \quad \text{(pre-norm)} \]

Originally introduced for convolutional networks (ResNet), residual connections have become universal in deep learning. They also facilitate ensemble-like behaviour — Veit et al. (2016) showed that residual networks can be viewed as implicit ensembles of shallower networks.

VariantFormulaUsed in
Post-norm\( \text{Norm}(\mathbf{x} + F(\mathbf{x})) \)Original Transformer
Pre-norm\( \mathbf{x} + F(\text{Norm}(\mathbf{x})) \)GPT-2+, LLaMA, most modern LLMs

title: Root Mean Square Normalization (RMSNorm)
summary: A simplified layer normalisation that normalises activations by their root mean square only, omitting the mean-centering step. RMSNorm is computationally cheaper than LayerNorm while achieving comparable performance, and has become the d…

papers.citation: 2019 · Biao Zhang, Rico Sennrich · Root Mean Square Layer Normalization

sections.body: A simplified layer normalisation that normalises activations by their root mean square only, omitting the mean-centering step. RMSNorm is computationally cheaper than LayerNorm while achieving comparable performance, and has become the default normalisation in modern LLMs.

LayerNorm (Ba et al., 2016) normalises by both mean and variance:

\[ \text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x}) = \gamma \odot \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta \]

RMSNorm drops the mean subtraction and bias, normalising only by the RMS:

\[ \text{RMSNorm}(\mathbf{x}) = \gamma \odot \frac{\mathbf{x}}{\text{RMS}(\mathbf{x})}, \quad \text{RMS}(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{d}\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i^2 + \epsilon} \]

where \( \gamma \in \mathbb{R}^d \) is a learned scale parameter and \( \epsilon \) is a small constant for numerical stability.

Why drop the mean? Zhang and Sennrich (2019) hypothesised that the success of LayerNorm comes primarily from the scale invariance (dividing by the norm), not from re-centering. Empirically, RMSNorm matches LayerNorm on language modelling and translation tasks while being ~10–15% faster due to fewer operations.

RMSNorm is used in LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX, and most other modern open LLMs, always in the pre-norm configuration.

title: Context Window
summary: The maximum number of tokens a Transformer-based model can process in a single forward pass. It defines the model's "working memory" — all tokens within the window can attend to each other, but the model has no access to information beyo…

papers.citation: Architectural property of Transformer models; long-context research ongoing (2023–present).

sections.body:
The maximum number of tokens a Transformer-based model can process in a single forward pass. It defines the model's "working memory" — all tokens within the window can attend to each other, but the model has no access to information beyond it.

The context window is fundamentally limited by the \( O(n^2) \) cost of self-attention, where \( n \) is the sequence length. Both compute and memory scale quadratically, making very long contexts expensive.

ModelContext windowApprox. pages of text
GPT-3 (2020)2,048 tokens~3 pages
GPT-4 (2023)8K / 128K tokens~12 / ~200 pages
Claude 3.5 (2024)200K tokens~300 pages
Gemini 1.5 (2024)1M+ tokens~1,500 pages

Extending context: Techniques for longer contexts include:

  • RoPE scaling — interpolating or extrapolating rotary position embeddings beyond training length
  • FlashAttention — IO-aware exact attention that reduces memory from \( O(n^2) \) to \( O(n) \)
  • Sparse attention — attending to subsets of positions (Longformer, BigBird)
  • Long-context pretraining — gradually increasing context during training

A larger context window does not guarantee the model uses all of it effectively — "lost in the middle" (Liu et al., 2023) showed that models often struggle with information placed in the middle of long contexts.

title: Autoregression
summary: A generation strategy where each output token is conditioned on all previously generated tokens. The model decomposes the joint probability of a sequence into a product of conditional probabilities, generating one token at a time from le…

papers.citation: 2003 · Yoshua Bengio, Rejean Ducharme, Pascal Vincent, Christian Jauvin · A Neural Probabilistic Language Model
2018 · Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training

sections.body:
A generation strategy where each output token is conditioned on all previously generated tokens. The model decomposes the joint probability of a sequence into a product of conditional probabilities, generating one token at a time from left to right.

An autoregressive model factorises the sequence probability using the chain rule of probability:

\[ P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \]

where \( x_{<t} = (x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}) \) denotes all preceding tokens. At each step \( t \), a neural network \( f_\theta \) maps the prefix to a distribution over the vocabulary \( \mathcal{V} \):

\[ P(x_t = w \mid x_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(f_\theta(x_{<t}))_w \]

Training objective. The model is trained by maximising the log-likelihood of the training corpus, which is equivalent to minimising the cross-entropy loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \]

During training, teacher forcing provides the ground-truth prefix at every step, enabling full parallelisation across positions (every conditional can be computed simultaneously via causal masking). During inference, the model must consume its own previous outputs sequentially — a discrepancy known as exposure bias.

Decoding strategies control how tokens are selected from the predicted distribution:

StrategyMechanismTrade-off
Greedy\( \arg\max \) at each stepDeterministic but repetitive
Beam searchTrack top-\( k \) sequencesHigher likelihood, but often bland
Temperature samplingSample from \( \text{softmax}(z / T) \)\( T < 1 \): sharper; \( T > 1 \): more diverse
Top-\( k \)Sample from \( k \) most probable tokensTruncates tail, reduces incoherence
Nucleus (top-\( p \))Sample from smallest set with cumulative prob \( \geq p \)Adaptive vocabulary size per step

Autoregression is the generation paradigm used by all decoder-only LLMs (GPT, LLaMA, Claude). Its key advantage is exact, tractable likelihood computation, which provides a clean training signal. Its key limitation is sequential generation at inference time — each token depends on the previous, preventing parallelisation across output positions and creating a latency bottleneck proportional to sequence length.

Non-autoregressive alternatives (masked language models, diffusion models for text, speculative decoding) attempt to mitigate this by generating multiple tokens in parallel, but typically sacrifice quality or require iterative refinement to match autoregressive baselines.

title: Prompt
summary: The input text provided to a language model to condition its generation. The prompt defines the task, provides context, and steers the model's output — it is the primary interface through which users interact with LLMs.

papers.citation: 2019 · Radford et al. · Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
2020 · Brown et al. · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

sections.body:
The input text provided to a language model to condition its generation. The prompt defines the task, provides context, and steers the model's output — it is the primary interface through which users interact with LLMs.

In an autoregressive language model, the prompt is the initial token sequence \( x_1, \ldots, x_k \) that the model conditions on when generating the continuation \( x_{k+1}, x_{k+2}, \ldots \). Formally, the model computes:

\[ P(x_{k+1}, \ldots, x_T \mid x_1, \ldots, x_k) = \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \]

The prompt occupies positions in the context window (the model's maximum sequence length), so longer prompts leave less room for generation. The prompt is processed in a single forward pass (unlike generation, which is sequential), and its key-value representations are often cached (KV cache) to avoid recomputation during generation.

sections.title: Prompt taxonomy
sections.body:
TypeDescriptionExample
Direct instructionExplicit task description"Translate to French: ..."
Few-shot examplesInput-output demonstrations3 labelled sentiment examples + new input
Chain-of-thoughtElicit step-by-step reasoning"Let's think step by step..."
Structured templateRole-based format with special tokensSystem / user / assistant messages
Soft promptLearned continuous embeddings prepended to inputPrefix tuning (Li & Liang, 2021)

Prompt sensitivity. LLM outputs are often highly sensitive to prompt phrasing — semantically equivalent prompts can produce different results. This fragility motivates prompt engineering as a systematic discipline, as well as automated prompt optimisation methods like DSPy and APE.

Prompt injection is a security concern where adversarial user input overrides the intended system prompt instructions. Because prompts and user data share the same token stream, there is no architectural boundary between trusted instructions and untrusted input — a fundamental challenge for deployed LLM applications.

The observation that LLMs can perform diverse tasks given only an appropriate prompt — without any gradient updates — is one of the most surprising results in modern AI and the basis for in-context learning (Brown et al., 2020).

title: System Prompt
summary: A special prompt section, typically invisible to the end user, that defines the model's behaviour, persona, constraints, and instructions for a conversation. The system prompt is prepended to every interaction and takes precedence over u…

papers.citation: Formalised in OpenAI's Chat Completions API (2023) · Adopted by Claude, Gemini, and other chat APIs

sections.body:
A special prompt section, typically invisible to the end user, that defines the model's behaviour, persona, constraints, and instructions for a conversation. The system prompt is prepended to every interaction and takes precedence over user messages in well-designed models.

In a typical chat API, messages are structured with distinct roles:

RolePurposeWho controls it
systemSets behaviour, persona, constraintsDeveloper / application
userEnd-user inputEnd user
assistantModel's responseModel (generated)

The system prompt enables application-level control: a developer can instruct the model to behave as a specific agent ("You are a helpful customer support agent for Acme Corp. Only answer questions about our products.") without the user seeing or being able to easily override these instructions.

Implementation details. During instruction tuning, the model learns to give system messages higher priority than user messages. However, this is a soft constraint learned through training — not an architectural guarantee. The system prompt is tokenised and concatenated with the rest of the conversation, sharing the same attention mechanism. There is no hard separation at the model level between "trusted" system tokens and "untrusted" user tokens.

sections.title: Key properties and trade-offs
sections.body:
• Persists across the entire conversation (resent with every API call in stateless APIs)
  • Consumes context window tokens — an overly long system prompt reduces space for conversation history and user content
  • Used to implement safety guardrails, output formatting, tool use instructions, and persona
  • Some providers (e.g., Anthropic) distinguish a system parameter from the message array, enabling special positional handling

System prompt robustness is an active area of safety research. Jailbreak attacks attempt to override system prompt instructions through cleverly crafted user messages (e.g., "ignore previous instructions"). Defences include instruction hierarchy training (OpenAI, 2024), where the model learns to rank system-level instructions above user-level ones even when directly contradicted.

title: Prompting Format
summary: The structured template used to format messages into a token sequence that a chat model expects. Each model family has its own format defining how system, user, and assistant messages are delimited by special tokens.

papers.citation: Evolved with instruction-tuned models · ChatML introduced by OpenAI (2023) · Model-specific templates standardised by Hugging Face

sections.body:
The structured template used to format messages into a token sequence that a chat model expects. Each model family has its own format defining how system, user, and assistant messages are delimited by special tokens.

Chat models are fine-tuned on conversations formatted with specific special tokens. Using the wrong format degrades performance because the model sees out-of-distribution token patterns. The format tells the model who is speaking (system, user, assistant) and where turns begin and end.

sections.title: Example formats
sections.body:
ModelFormat sketch
ChatML (OpenAI)<|im_start|>system ...<|im_end|> <|im_start|>user ...<|im_end|>
LLaMA 2[INST] <<SYS>> ... <</SYS>> {user}[/INST]
LLaMA 3<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|> ...
Gemma<start_of_turn>user ...<end_of_turn> <start_of_turn>model ...

The special tokens (e.g., <|im_start|>, [INST]) are added to the tokenizer vocabulary during fine-tuning and are not present in the base model's pretraining data. They serve as unambiguous markers that cannot be confused with natural language content.

Automation. Hugging Face's tokenizer.apply_chat_template() reads a Jinja2 chat template stored in the tokenizer config and produces the correctly formatted token sequence for any supported model. This eliminates manual format handling and is the recommended approach for multi-model pipelines.

Turn-taking logic. After the user's message and the appropriate end-of-turn delimiter, the model knows it should generate an assistant response. Incorrect formatting can cause the model to continue generating user messages, produce incoherent output, or fail to stop generating (missing stop tokens).

Why formats differ. Each lab makes independent design choices during instruction tuning. There is no universal standard, though ChatML has become a common convention for OpenAI-compatible APIs. The proliferation of formats is a practical pain point when switching between models or building model-agnostic applications.

title: Few-Shot Prompting
summary: A prompting strategy where a small number of input-output examples are included in the prompt to demonstrate the desired task, allowing the model to perform the task on a new input without any parameter updates. Few-shot prompting is the…

papers.citation: 2020 · Tom Brown et al. · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

sections.body:
A prompting strategy where a small number of input-output examples are included in the prompt to demonstrate the desired task, allowing the model to perform the task on a new input without any parameter updates. Few-shot prompting is the canonical form of in-context learning.

A typical few-shot prompt has the structure:

Input: The food was amazing → Positive
Input: Terrible service, never again → Negative
Input: Pretty good but overpriced → ???

The model infers the pattern from the demonstrations and generalises to the new input. The "shot" count refers to the number of examples:

SettingExamplesNotes
Zero-shot0Task description only; relies on instruction-following ability
One-shot1Single demonstration; sufficient for simple format tasks
Few-shot2–~30Multiple demonstrations; upper bound limited by context window

Landmark result. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated that GPT-3's (175B) few-shot performance scales with model size, approaching fine-tuned baselines on many benchmarks without any gradient updates. On SuperGLUE, few-shot GPT-3 matched fine-tuned BERT-Large on several tasks — a paradigm shift from per-task fine-tuning to prompt-based interfaces.

Why does it work? The demonstrations effectively specify the task distribution, allowing the model to condition its generation on the implied input-output mapping. Mechanistic hypotheses include implicit Bayesian task inference (Xie et al., 2022) and transformer forward passes implementing mesa-gradient-descent on the in-context examples (von Oswald et al., 2023).

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• Example quality matters more than quantity — noisy or ambiguous examples hurt performance
  • Label balance affects results; skewed example distributions bias the model toward the majority label
  • Ordering effects: the order of examples significantly impacts performance (Lu et al., 2022); examples placed later in the prompt tend to be weighted more heavily due to recency bias
  • Format consistency: using a consistent delimiter and structure across examples improves reliability

Few-shot prompting is contrasted with fine-tuning, which updates model parameters on task-specific data. Fine-tuning generally achieves higher accuracy but requires labelled data, compute, and a separate model checkpoint per task. Few-shot prompting trades some accuracy for extreme flexibility — the same model serves arbitrary tasks.

title: In-Context Learning
summary: The ability of large language models to learn new tasks at inference time from examples or instructions provided in the prompt, without any gradient updates or fine-tuning. The model's parameters remain frozen — all "learning" happens th…

papers.citation: 2020 · Tom Brown et al. · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

sections.body:
The ability of large language models to learn new tasks at inference time from examples or instructions provided in the prompt, without any gradient updates or fine-tuning. The model's parameters remain frozen — all "learning" happens through the forward pass conditioned on the context.

In-context learning (ICL) was first demonstrated at scale by Brown et al. (2020) with GPT-3 (175B parameters). Given a prompt containing \( k \) demonstration pairs \( \{(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)\} \) followed by a query \( x_{k+1} \), the model produces:

\[ \hat{y}_{k+1} = \arg\max_y P_\theta(y \mid x_1, y_1, \ldots, x_k, y_k, x_{k+1}) \]

where \( \theta \) is fixed — no gradient update occurs. This distinguishes ICL from meta-learning methods that modify parameters at test time.

How does it work? This remains an active research question with several competing hypotheses:

HypothesisMechanismKey paper
Implicit Bayesian inferenceModel infers the latent task concept from examples and applies posterior predictive distributionXie et al. (2022)
Mesa-gradient descentTransformer forward passes implement implicit gradient descent steps on in-context examples; attention layers act as linear regression solversvon Oswald et al. (2023), Akyürek et al. (2023)
Task location / retrievalModel recognises the task from pretraining distribution and retrieves learned behaviourReynolds & McDonell (2021)
Induction headsSpecific attention head circuits (previous-token + induction heads) copy patterns from context to outputOlsson et al. (2022)

The mesa-gradient descent view is particularly striking: Garg et al. (2022) showed that transformers trained on random linear regression tasks learn to implement ridge regression in their forward pass, matching the Bayes-optimal estimator. For a linear model \( y = Wx + \varepsilon \), the transformer effectively computes:

\[ \hat{W} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i x_i^\top + \lambda I\right)^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i y_i^\top \]

purely through attention operations, with \( \lambda \) emerging implicitly.

Scaling behaviour. ICL ability emerges with scale — small models cannot do it effectively. Brown et al. showed that few-shot performance improves log-linearly with model size, with a clear gap between models below and above ~10B parameters. This makes ICL one of the canonical "emergent capabilities" of large language models.

sections.title: Surprising properties
sections.body:
• Label flipping: Min et al. (2022) showed that ICL performance is surprisingly robust to randomly flipped labels in demonstrations — suggesting the model partially relies on task format recognition rather than learning the input-output mapping from examples
  • Recency bias: Examples placed later in the prompt receive disproportionate weight, making example ordering matter significantly (Lu et al., 2022)
  • Task generality: ICL works across translation, classification, arithmetic, code generation, and reasoning — far beyond any single task distribution seen in training

ICL fundamentally changed the NLP paradigm from per-task fine-tuning (train a separate model for each task) to prompt-based universal interfaces (one model, many tasks). It also underpins the practical utility of modern LLM APIs, where users specify tasks through natural language rather than training data.

title: Prompt Engineering
summary: The practice of designing and iterating on prompts to elicit desired behaviour from language models. It encompasses the choice of instructions, examples, formatting, and reasoning strategies to maximise task performance without modifying…

papers.citation: 2023 · Zhou et al. · Large Language Models Are Human-Level Prompt Engineers

sections.body:
The practice of designing and iterating on prompts to elicit desired behaviour from language models. It encompasses the choice of instructions, examples, formatting, and reasoning strategies to maximise task performance without modifying model parameters.

Prompt engineering emerged as a discipline once practitioners discovered that LLM outputs are highly sensitive to the exact phrasing, structure, and content of prompts.
