A well-engineered prompt can close much of the gap between zero-shot and fine-tuned performance, making it a cost-effective alternative to model adaptation.

sections.title: Core techniques
sections.body:
TechniqueMechanismWhen to use
Few-shot promptingInclude \( k \) input-output demonstrationsFormat-sensitive tasks, classification, translation
Chain of thought (CoT)Elicit intermediate reasoning steps before the answerArithmetic, logic, multi-step reasoning
Self-consistencySample \( k \) CoT paths, majority-vote on the final answerTasks with verifiable answers (math, factual QA)
ReActInterleave reasoning traces with tool-use actions (search, calculator, code execution)Knowledge-intensive or multi-tool tasks
Role prompting"You are an expert in X" — activates domain-specific behaviourDomain-specific generation, persona control
Structured outputRequest JSON, XML, or markdown with schema examplesAPI integrations, data extraction

sections.title: General principles
sections.body:
• Specificity over generality: "Summarise this text in 3 bullet points, each under 20 words" outperforms "Summarise this text"
  • Show, don't tell: Providing output examples is more reliable than describing the desired format in prose
  • Decomposition: Break complex tasks into sub-tasks (e.g., extract entities first, then classify relationships) rather than asking for everything at once
  • Iterate empirically: Small wording changes can shift accuracy by 10–20 percentage points on benchmarks — prompt engineering is fundamentally an empirical discipline

Automated prompt optimisation. Manual prompt engineering is labour-intensive and does not scale. Several systems automate the process:

  • APE (Zhou et al., 2023): Uses an LLM to generate candidate prompts, scores them on a validation set, and selects the best-performing prompt
  • DSPy (Khattab et al., 2023): Treats prompts as programs with optimisable modules, compiling high-level task specifications into effective prompt chains
  • OPRO (Yang et al., 2023): Uses an LLM as the optimiser itself, iteratively refining prompts based on task performance feedback

Prompt engineering bridges the gap between the raw capability of a pretrained model and its task-specific performance. It is the primary skill required to use LLM APIs effectively, and its techniques (CoT, few-shot, ReAct) compose — combining multiple strategies often yields the best results.

title: Chain of Thought
summary: A prompting technique that encourages language models to generate intermediate reasoning steps before producing a final answer. Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic r…

papers.citation: 2022 · Jason Wei et al. · Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

sections.body:
A prompting technique that encourages language models to generate intermediate reasoning steps before producing a final answer. Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting dramatically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.

Standard prompting asks directly for the answer. CoT prompting elicits a reasoning trace:

Q: Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 cans of 3.
   How many does he have now?

Standard: 11

CoT: Roger started with 5 balls. He bought 2 cans
of 3 balls each, so 2 × 3 = 6 new balls.
5 + 6 = 11. The answer is 11.

CoT can be elicited in two main ways:

  • Few-shot CoT (Wei et al., 2022): Include examples with reasoning traces in the prompt — the model mimics the demonstrated reasoning pattern
  • Zero-shot CoT (Kojima et al., 2022): Simply append "Let's think step by step" — no demonstrations needed. This works because instruction-tuned models have learned to associate such phrases with step-by-step reasoning during training

Why does it work? Autoregressive models allocate a fixed amount of computation per token. For problems requiring multi-step reasoning (e.g., multi-digit arithmetic), a single token prediction is insufficient. By generating intermediate tokens, CoT effectively increases the model's computation budget — each reasoning step becomes a "scratchpad" that feeds information forward through the chain. Formally, without CoT the model must compute \( P(y \mid x) \) directly, while with CoT it computes:

\[ P(y \mid x) = \sum_{z} P(y \mid z, x) \cdot P(z \mid x) \]

where \( z \) represents the reasoning chain. The model approximates this by sampling a single chain \( z^* \) and computing \( P(y \mid z^*, x) \).

Emergent scaling behaviour. Wei et al. (2022) showed that CoT is an emergent capability that only helps models above approximately 100B parameters. Smaller models produce incoherent chains that do not improve (and can hurt) accuracy. This threshold effect is one of the most cited examples of emergent abilities in LLMs.

sections.title: Benchmark results (PaLM 540B with CoT)
sections.body:
BenchmarkStandard promptingCoT prompting
GSM8K (math)56.9%74.4%
SVAMP (math)79.0%86.6%
StrategyQA (commonsense)73.9%77.8%

sections.title: Extensions and variants
sections.body:
• Self-consistency: Sample multiple CoT chains, take majority vote on final answer — consistently improves over single-chain CoT
  • Tree of Thought: Explore branching reasoning paths with backtracking, enabling deliberate search
  • Least-to-most prompting: Decompose the problem into sub-problems, solve sequentially, each building on previous answers
  • Faithful CoT (Lyu et al., 2023): Generate symbolic reasoning (e.g., Python code) alongside natural language to ensure logical consistency

A common misconception is that CoT chains are always faithful to the model's internal reasoning. In practice, models can arrive at correct answers through unfaithful chains (plausible-looking but logically disconnected steps), making CoT an unreliable window into the model's actual computation.

title: Tree of Thought
summary: A reasoning framework that generalises chain-of-thought prompting by exploring multiple branching reasoning paths (a tree), evaluating intermediate states, and using search algorithms (BFS, DFS) to find the best solution. It enables deli…

papers.citation: 2023 · Shunyu Yao et al. · Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models

sections.body:
A reasoning framework that generalises chain-of-thought prompting by exploring multiple branching reasoning paths (a tree), evaluating intermediate states, and using search algorithms (BFS, DFS) to find the best solution. It enables deliberate planning and backtracking on complex problems.

While chain-of-thought follows a single linear reasoning path, Tree of Thought (ToT) treats problem-solving as a search over a tree where:

  • Each node is a partial solution (intermediate reasoning state)
  • Each edge is a reasoning step that extends the state
  • The model evaluates nodes using self-assessment ("Is this path promising?")
  • A search algorithm navigates the tree, pruning unpromising branches

Formal framework. At each reasoning step \( t \), the model generates a set of candidate "thoughts" \( \{z_t^{(1)}, \ldots, z_t^{(b)}\} \) where \( b \) is the branching factor. A value function \( V(s_t) \), implemented by prompting the same LLM, evaluates each resulting state \( s_t = (x, z_1, \ldots, z_t) \). The search algorithm selects which states to expand:

\[ s_{t+1}^* = \arg\max_{s_{t+1} \in \text{children}(s_t^*)} V(s_{t+1}) \]

The algorithm uses two components, both implemented via LLM prompting:

  1. Thought generation — propose \( b \) candidate next steps via sampling or independent prompts
  2. State evaluation — score each candidate on a scale (e.g., "sure / likely / impossible") or via majority vote across multiple evaluation prompts

sections.title: Search strategies
sections.body:
StrategyBehaviourBest for
BFSExpand all nodes at depth \( d \) before moving to \( d+1 \); prune bottom-ranked statesTasks with early-distinguishable good/bad paths (Game of 24)
DFSExplore one branch deeply; backtrack when value drops below thresholdTasks with deep solution paths (creative writing, crosswords)

Results. ToT dramatically outperforms standard CoT on tasks requiring exploration and backtracking:

  • Game of 24: 4% (CoT) → 74% (ToT) — the most striking result, because finding \( a \circ b \circ c \circ d = 24 \) often requires trying and abandoning dead-end orderings
  • Creative writing: ToT produces more coherent multi-paragraph stories by planning paragraph-level structure before generating prose
  • Crossword puzzles: DFS with backtracking solves significantly more clues than linear generation

Cost trade-off. ToT requires \( O(b \times d \times e) \) LLM calls per problem, where \( b \) is the branching factor, \( d \) is tree depth, and \( e \) is the number of evaluation calls per node. A typical Game of 24 solution uses ~100 LLM calls vs. 1 for standard prompting. This makes ToT orders of magnitude more expensive, limiting it to high-value problems where accuracy matters more than cost.

ToT connects LLM reasoning to classical AI search (A*, minimax), reintroducing deliberate planning into neural generation. Subsequent work (Besta et al., 2024, "Graph of Thought") generalises from trees to arbitrary DAGs, allowing thoughts to merge and form more flexible reasoning structures.

title: Self-Consistency
summary: A decoding strategy that samples multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths from a language model and selects the most frequent final answer by majority vote. It improves accuracy by marginalising over diverse reasoning traces rather than…

papers.citation: 2023 · Xuezhi Wang et al. · Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models

sections.body:
A decoding strategy that samples multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths from a language model and selects the most frequent final answer by majority vote. It improves accuracy by marginalising over diverse reasoning traces rather than relying on a single greedy path.

The core insight is that for problems with a definite correct answer, multiple independent reasoning attempts are more likely to converge on the right answer than on any particular wrong answer. Incorrect reasoning paths tend to produce diverse wrong answers, while correct paths converge.

sections.title: Algorithm
sections.body:
1. Given a prompt with CoT demonstrations and a query \( x \), sample \( k \) independent reasoning chains \( z_1, \ldots, z_k \) using temperature sampling (typically \( T \in [0.5, 1.0] \))
  2. Extract the final answer \( a_i \) from each chain \( z_i \)
  3. Return the plurality answer:
\[ \hat{a} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{1}[\text{answer}(z_i) = a] \]

This can be viewed as an approximation to marginalisation over reasoning paths:

\[ P(a \mid x) = \sum_{z} P(a \mid z, x) \cdot P(z \mid x) \approx \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{1}[\text{answer}(z_i) = a] \]

where the summation over all possible chains \( z \) is approximated by \( k \) samples from \( P(z \mid x) \).

sections.title: Comparison with alternative decoding strategies
sections.body:
MethodPathsSelectionRequires
Greedy CoT1 (greedy)Single answerNothing extra
Self-consistency\( k \) (sampled)Majority vote\( k \times \) compute
Best-of-\( k \) (BoN)\( k \) (sampled)Highest-scoring per a verifierTrained verifier / reward model
Universal self-consistency\( k \) (sampled)LLM selects most consistent answer from candidatesExtra LLM call for selection

Benchmark improvements (Wang et al., 2023, using Codex and PaLM):

BenchmarkGreedy CoTSelf-consistency (\( k=40 \))Gain
GSM8K56.5%74.4%+17.9pp
SVAMP78.7%86.6%+7.9pp
ARC-challenge85.2%88.7%+3.5pp

Diminishing returns. Performance improves rapidly with the first 5–10 samples, then saturates around \( k = 40 \). The optimal \( k \) depends on problem difficulty — harder problems benefit from more samples.

sections.title: Key properties
sections.body:
• Requires no additional training — purely a test-time strategy
  • Orthogonal to prompt improvements (CoT, few-shot) and composes with them
  • Only applicable when final answers are discrete and extractable (does not work for open-ended generation)
  • Directly trades compute for accuracy at inference time — an early example of "test-time compute scaling"

title: ReAct (Reason + Act)
summary: A prompting framework that interleaves reasoning traces with action steps (tool calls), allowing a language model to plan, act on external environments (search, code execution, APIs), observe results, and revise its reasoning. ReAct unif…

papers.citation: 2022 · Shunyu Yao, Jeffrey Zhao, Dian Yu, Nan Du, Izhak Shafran, Karthik Narasimhan, Yuan Cao · ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

sections.body:
A prompting framework that interleaves reasoning traces with action steps (tool calls), allowing a language model to plan, act on external environments (search, code execution, APIs), observe results, and revise its reasoning. ReAct unifies chain-of-thought reasoning with action-taking in a single prompt loop.

Standard chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting generates a reasoning trace but cannot interact with the world — it is prone to hallucination and cannot verify facts. Conversely, pure action-based agents (e.g., WebGPT) take actions without explicit reasoning, making their decisions opaque. ReAct combines both: the model alternates between thought, action, and observation steps in a structured loop.

Formally, at each time step \( t \), the model generates either a thought \( \hat{a}_t \) (internal reasoning, not sent to the environment) or an action \( a_t \) (sent to the environment, which returns observation \( o_t \)):

\[ c_t = \begin{cases} \text{Thought}(\hat{a}_t) & \text{reasoning step} \\ \text{Action}(a_t) \to o_t & \text{environment interaction} \end{cases} \]

The context for step \( t+1 \) is the concatenation of all prior steps: \( c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_t \).

sections.title: Example trace
sections.body:
Thought 1: I need to find when the Eiffel Tower was built.
Action 1:  Search["Eiffel Tower construction date"]
Obs 1:     Construction began January 1887, completed March 1889.
Thought 2: The answer is 1889.
Action 2:  Finish["1889"]

sections.title: Comparison with related prompting methods
sections.body:
MethodReasoningActionsGrounding
Standard promptingNoNoNone
Chain-of-thought (CoT)YesNoNone
Action-only (WebGPT)NoYesTool output
ReActYesYesTool output

On knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER), ReAct outperformed CoT alone by retrieving facts rather than hallucinating. On decision-making tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop), ReAct outperformed imitation learning baselines. The authors also showed that ReAct + CoT-self-consistency (using internal reasoning when external tools are unavailable) yields the best overall performance.

ReAct is the conceptual foundation for modern LLM agent frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, Claude's tool use). The key insight is that reasoning and acting are synergistic: reasoning helps the model plan which actions to take, while action results ground the reasoning in factual observations.

title: Function Calling
summary: A capability of language models to generate structured API calls (tool invocations) as part of their output, rather than only producing natural language. The model selects which function to call and fills in the arguments, enabling it to…

papers.citation: 2023 · OpenAI · Function Calling and Other API Updates
2023 · Schick et al. · Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools

sections.body:
A capability of language models to generate structured API calls (tool invocations) as part of their output, rather than only producing natural language. The model selects which function to call and fills in the arguments, enabling it to interact with external systems, databases, and services.

Function calling bridges the gap between language generation and programmatic action. In a typical flow:

  1. The developer provides tool definitions — each specifying a function name, natural-language description, and a JSON Schema for parameters — alongside the conversation context
  2. The model decides whether a tool call is needed (vs. a direct text response)
  3. If so, the model outputs a structured JSON object with the function name and arguments
  4. The host application executes the function and returns the result to the model
  5. The model incorporates the result into its final response

sections.title: Example
sections.body:
// Tool definition (simplified)
{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get current weather for a location",
  "parameters": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "location": {"type": "string"},
      "unit": {"enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"]}
    },
    "required": ["location"]
  }
}

// Model output
{"name": "get_weather",
 "arguments": {"location": "Paris", "unit": "celsius"}}

sections.title: Training approaches
sections.body:
ApproachDescription
SFT on tool-use tracesFine-tune on (query, tool call, result, response) sequences collected from humans or stronger models
Toolformer-style self-supervisionModel inserts API calls into training text where they improve perplexity (Schick et al., 2023)
RLHF with tool useReward model evaluates quality of tool selection and argument formatting

sections.title: Key design decisions
sections.body:
• Parallel vs. sequential calls: Some APIs allow the model to request multiple tool calls in a single turn
  • Forced vs. optional: The developer can force a specific tool call or let the model decide autonomously
  • Structured output: Function calling shares infrastructure with JSON mode / structured generation, often using constrained decoding to guarantee valid schemas

Function calling is the practical implementation of the ReAct paradigm and underlies modern AI agent systems. It transforms LLMs from text generators into orchestrators that can take verifiable actions in the real world.

title: Large Language Model (LLM)
summary: A language model with a very large number of parameters (typically billions), trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised learning. LLMs exhibit emergent capabilities — such as in-context learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, a…

papers.citation: 2020 · Tom Brown et al. · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3, popularising the term)

sections.body:
A language model with a very large number of parameters (typically billions), trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised learning. LLMs exhibit emergent capabilities — such as in-context learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, and instruction following — that are absent or weak in smaller models.

Modern LLMs are predominantly decoder-only Transformers trained with the causal language modelling objective:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}; \theta) \]

where \( x_t \) is the token at position \( t \), \( x_{1:t-1} \) denotes all preceding tokens, and \( \theta \) are the model parameters.

The training pipeline typically involves three stages:

  1. Pretraining: Self-supervised next-token prediction on trillions of tokens (web text, books, code). This stage consumes the vast majority of compute.
  2. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): Training on curated instruction-response pairs to teach the model to follow instructions and produce helpful responses.
  3. Alignment: RLHF, DPO, or constitutional AI methods to align behaviour with human preferences and safety requirements.
ModelParametersYearOrganisation
GPT-3175B2020OpenAI
PaLM540B2022Google
LLaMA 27B-70B2023Meta
Claude 3.5Undisclosed2024Anthropic

Scaling laws. Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that cross-entropy loss scales as a power law with compute \( C \), dataset size \( D \), and parameter count \( N \):

\[ L(N) \approx \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N}, \quad L(D) \approx \left(\frac{D_c}{D}\right)^{\alpha_D} \]

where \( \alpha_N \approx 0.076 \) and \( \alpha_D \approx 0.095 \) for Transformer language models. Hoffmann et al. (2022, "Chinchilla") refined this, showing that compute-optimal training requires scaling data and parameters roughly equally — many early LLMs were significantly under-trained relative to their parameter count.

Emergent capabilities — abilities that appear suddenly as model scale increases — include few-shot learning, arithmetic reasoning, code generation, and multilingual transfer. Wei et al. (2022) catalogued over 130 such capabilities, though subsequent work (Schaeffer et al., 2023) argued that "emergence" may partly be an artifact of nonlinear evaluation metrics rather than a true phase transition.

title: Pretraining
summary: The initial training phase where a model learns general representations from a large, unlabelled dataset using a self-supervised objective. For LLMs, pretraining typically means next-token prediction on trillions of tokens of text, produ…

papers.citation: 2010 · Dumitru Erhan, Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville, Pascal Vincent · Why Does Unsupervised Pre-training Help Deep Learning?
2018 · Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training
2019 · Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova · BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

sections.body:
The initial training phase where a model learns general representations from a large, unlabelled dataset using a self-supervised objective. For LLMs, pretraining typically means next-token prediction on trillions of tokens of text, producing a base model that captures language structure, world knowledge, and reasoning patterns.

The pretraining objective for decoder-only models is causal language modelling — predict the next token given all preceding tokens:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{pretrain}} = -\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{t-1}; \theta) \]

where \( T \) is the sequence length, \( x_t \) is the token at position \( t \), and \( \theta \) are the model parameters. For encoder models (BERT-style), the objective is masked language modelling (MLM) — predicting randomly masked tokens from bidirectional context:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}} = -\sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}} \log P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}}; \theta) \]

where \( \mathcal{M} \) is the set of masked positions and \( x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}} \) denotes the unmasked tokens.

sections.title: Key aspects of pretraining
sections.body:
AspectTypical practice
Data1-15 trillion tokens (web, books, code, papers)
Compute\( 10^{23} \)-\( 10^{25} \) FLOPs
DurationWeeks to months on thousands of GPUs
CostMillions of dollars
ResultBase model (not yet instruction-following)

Data curation is critical and involves several stages: deduplication (MinHash, exact substring matching), quality filtering (perplexity-based classifiers, heuristic rules), toxicity removal, and careful data mixing — the proportions of web text vs. books vs. code vs. scientific papers significantly affect downstream capabilities. For example, including ~5-10% code in pretraining data improves reasoning ability even on non-code tasks (Muennighoff et al., 2023).

Chinchilla scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that many early LLMs were significantly under-trained. The compute-optimal ratio is approximately 20 tokens per parameter — so a 7B model should be trained on ~140B tokens. This shifted the field toward training smaller models on more data (e.g., LLaMA 7B trained on 1T tokens).

The pretrained base model is a powerful but raw artifact: it can complete text fluently and has absorbed enormous factual knowledge, but it does not follow instructions or engage in dialogue. Subsequent SFT and alignment stages transform it into a useful assistant.

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
summary: A training stage that adapts a pretrained base model to follow instructions by training on curated (instruction, response) pairs. SFT transforms a raw text completion model into one that can engage in dialogue, follow formatting requirem…

papers.citation: 2022 · Ouyang et al. · Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)

sections.body:
A training stage that adapts a pretrained base model to follow instructions by training on curated (instruction, response) pairs. SFT transforms a raw text completion model into one that can engage in dialogue, follow formatting requirements, and perform specific tasks.

The SFT objective is standard cross-entropy, but applied only to the response tokens — the instruction tokens are present in the context but masked from the loss computation:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{SFT}} = -\sum_{t \in \mathcal{R}} \log P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}; \theta) \]

where \( \mathcal{R} \) is the set of token positions belonging to the response, and \( x_{1:t-1} \) includes both the instruction and the response tokens generated so far. This teaches the model what to generate given an instruction, without penalising it for the content of the instruction itself.

sections.title: Data for SFT
sections.body:
• Thousands to hundreds of thousands of high-quality (instruction, response) pairs
  • Sources: human-written, model-generated then human-filtered, or distilled from a stronger model (e.g., Alpaca distilled from GPT-4)
  • Diversity is essential: coding, mathematics, creative writing, analysis, multi-turn dialogue, tool use
StageData sizePurpose
PretrainingTrillions of tokensLearn language and knowledge
SFT10K-1M examplesLearn to follow instructions
Alignment (RLHF/DPO)10K-100K comparisonsAlign with human preferences

The LIMA insight. Zhou et al. (2023) demonstrated that as few as 1,000 carefully curated SFT examples can produce a surprisingly capable assistant, suggesting that SFT primarily teaches format and style — the knowledge was already acquired during pretraining. This "superficial alignment hypothesis" implies that the quality of SFT data matters far more than its quantity.

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• Learning rate: Typically 1e-5 to 2e-5, much lower than pretraining to avoid catastrophic forgetting
  • Epochs: Usually 2-5 epochs; more risks overfitting to the small dataset
  • Loss masking: Masking the instruction from the loss is standard but not universal — some practitioners include system prompts in the loss
  • Multi-turn format: SFT data must use the exact chat template (special tokens, role markers) that the model will use at inference

title: Finetuning
summary: The process of continuing to train a pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific or domain-specific dataset. Fine-tuning adapts the model's learned representations to new tasks while retaining the general knowledge acquired during pretr…

papers.citation: 2018 · Howard and Ruder · Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification
2019 · Devlin et al. · BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

sections.body:
The process of continuing to train a pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific or domain-specific dataset. Fine-tuning adapts the model's learned representations to new tasks while retaining the general knowledge acquired during pretraining — the foundation of modern transfer learning in deep learning.

Fine-tuning leverages transfer learning: instead of training from scratch (random initialisation), the pretrained weights provide a starting point that already captures useful features — language structure, world knowledge, visual features (in vision models), and reasoning patterns. The fine-tuning data adjusts these features for the target domain or task.

The update rule is standard gradient descent on the task-specific loss \( \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} \):

\[ \theta \leftarrow \theta_{\text{pretrained}} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta) \]

The critical difference from training from scratch is the initialisation: \( \theta_{\text{pretrained}} \) lies in a region of the loss landscape that generalises well, so only small adjustments are needed.

sections.title: Types of fine-tuning
sections.body:
TypeWhat changesData neededCompute
Full fine-tuneAll parametersMore dataHigh (all gradients + optimizer states)
PEFT (LoRA, adapters)Small subset / added paramsLess dataLow (only adapter gradients)
Feature extractionOnly new head layerMinimal dataVery low (base model frozen)

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• Learning rate: Much smaller than pretraining (typically 1e-5 to 5e-5 for LLMs) to avoid catastrophic forgetting — the phenomenon where new training overwrites previously learned representations
  • Epochs: Usually 1-5 epochs; more risks overfitting on the small dataset
  • Data quality: Quality matters far more than quantity; the LIMA paper showed 1,000 high-quality examples can suffice for SFT
  • Gradual unfreezing: Howard & Ruder (2018, ULMFiT) proposed unfreezing layers progressively from top to bottom, combined with discriminative learning rates (lower for earlier layers), which reduces catastrophic forgetting

Historical context. Fine-tuning was standard in computer vision (ImageNet-pretrained CNNs fine-tuned for downstream tasks) before being adopted in NLP. Howard & Ruder's ULMFiT (2018) demonstrated that language model pretraining + fine-tuning could match or exceed task-specific architectures, directly inspiring GPT and BERT. In the LLM era, "fine-tuning" typically refers to SFT on instruction data, but it also encompasses domain adaptation (e.g., medical, legal) and continued pretraining on domain-specific corpora.

title: Full Fine-Tune
summary: Fine-tuning that updates all of the model's parameters, as opposed to parameter-efficient methods that only modify a small subset. Full fine-tuning provides maximum expressiveness but requires storing and updating the complete model, mak…

papers.citation: 2020 · Rajbhandari et al. · ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models
2023 · Dettmers et al. · QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

sections.body:
Fine-tuning that updates all of the model's parameters, as opposed to parameter-efficient methods that only modify a small subset. Full fine-tuning provides maximum expressiveness but requires storing and updating the complete model, making it computationally expensive for large models.

In a full fine-tune, the gradient is computed for every parameter \( \theta_i \) in the model and all parameters are updated:

\[ \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) \]

For a model with \( N \) parameters, the memory footprint during training is dominated by four components:

ComponentSizeNotes
Model weights\( 2N \) bytes (fp16)The parameters themselves
Gradients\( 2N \) bytesOne gradient per parameter
Adam optimizer — first moment \( m \)\( 4N \) bytes (fp32)Running mean of gradients
Adam optimizer — second moment \( v \)\( 4N \) bytes (fp32)Running mean of squared gradients
Master weights (fp32 copy)\( 4N \) bytesFor mixed-precision training
Total\( \sim 16N \) bytese.g., ~112 GB for a 7B model

This excludes activation memory, which adds significantly during the forward pass (mitigated by gradient checkpointing).

sections.title: When to use full fine-tuning
sections.body:
• The target domain differs substantially from pretraining data (e.g., fine-tuning a general LLM on specialised scientific literature)
  • Maximum performance is required and compute budget allows it
  • Training a dedicated single-task model where serving multiple adapters is unnecessary

sections.title: When to prefer PEFT instead
sections.body:
• Limited GPU memory or multi-tenant serving (one base model + many LoRA adapters)
  • Small fine-tuning datasets where freezing most parameters acts as implicit regularisation
  • Rapid experimentation — PEFT trains much faster and adapter checkpoints are small (MBs vs. GBs)

Distributed training strategies make full fine-tuning feasible at scale: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) and DeepSpeed ZeRO shard model states (weights, gradients, optimizer states) across GPUs, reducing per-GPU memory to approximately \( 16N / G \) bytes for \( G \) GPUs. ZeRO Stage 3 shards all three components; Stage 2 shards gradients and optimizer states only.

title: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)
summary: A family of methods that fine-tune only a small number of additional or selected parameters while keeping the bulk of the pretrained model frozen. PEFT achieves performance comparable to full fine-tuning at a fraction of the memory and c…

papers.citation: 2019 · Houlsby et al. · Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for NLP
2021 · Hu et al. · LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
2021 · Li and Liang · Prefix-Tuning: Optimizing Continuous Prompts for Generation

sections.body:
A family of methods that fine-tune only a small number of additional or selected parameters while keeping the bulk of the pretrained model frozen. PEFT achieves performance comparable to full fine-tuning at a fraction of the memory and compute cost.

The core idea: a pretrained model with \( N \) parameters can be adapted to a new task by learning a small update \( \Delta\theta \) with \( |\Delta\theta| \ll N \). The rest of the model remains frozen, so only the adapter parameters require gradient computation and optimizer states.

MethodTrainable paramsMechanismInference overhead
LoRA~0.1-1% of \( N \)Low-rank decomposition \( \Delta W = BA \) of weight updatesZero (merge into base weights)
Adapters~1-5% of \( N \)Small bottleneck MLPs inserted after attention/FFN sublayersSmall (extra forward pass through adapter)
Prefix tuning~0.1% of \( N \)Learnable continuous prefix vectors prepended to keys and values at each layerSmall (extra KV entries)
Prompt tuning~0.01% of \( N \)Learnable soft prompt embeddings prepended to the inputNegligible

Why it works. Aghajanyan et al. (2021, "Intrinsic Dimensionality") showed that fine-tuning updates have a low intrinsic dimensionality — the weight changes during adaptation occupy a low-rank subspace, even when full fine-tuning is used. This means constraining updates to a small subspace loses little expressiveness.

sections.title: Key advantages
sections.body:
• Memory: Only adapter parameters and their optimizer states need GPU memory — for LoRA with rank 16 on a 7B model, this is ~100 MB vs. ~112 GB for full fine-tuning
  • Multi-task serving: One base model in memory + many lightweight adapters hot-swapped per request (systems like S-LoRA and Punica enable batched multi-adapter serving)
  • Regularisation: Freezing most parameters implicitly constrains the model, reducing overfitting on small datasets — PEFT often outperforms full fine-tuning when data is scarce
  • Storage: Each fine-tuned variant is a small delta (MBs instead of GBs), easy to version and distribute

LoRA has become the dominant PEFT method due to its simplicity, zero additional inference latency (adapters are merged into base weights after training), and consistently strong performance. QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) extends this by combining LoRA with 4-bit quantisation of the frozen base model, enabling fine-tuning of 65B-parameter models on a single 48 GB GPU.

title: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
summary: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that represents weight updates as a low-rank decomposition. Instead of updating a full weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \), LoRA learns two small matrices \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \tim…

papers.citation: 2021 · Edward Hu, Yelong Shen, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu, Yuanzhi Li, Shean Wang, Lu Wang, Weizhu Chen · LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

sections.body:
A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that represents weight updates as a low-rank decomposition. Instead of updating a full weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \), LoRA learns two small matrices \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) and \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k} \) with rank \( r \ll \min(d, k) \), so the update \( \Delta W = BA \) has only \( r(d + k) \) trainable parameters instead of \( dk \).

The forward pass with LoRA becomes:

\[ h = Wx + \Delta Wx = Wx + BAx \]

where \( W \) is frozen and only \( A, B \) receive gradients. A scaling factor \( \alpha / r \) controls the magnitude of the update:

\[ h = Wx + \frac{\alpha}{r} BAx \]

where \( \alpha \) is a constant hyperparameter (typically set equal to \( r \) or \( 2r \)). This scaling ensures the update magnitude remains stable as \( r \) varies.

Initialisation. \( A \) is initialised with random Gaussian values (\( \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) \)) and \( B \) with zeros, so \( \Delta W = BA = 0 \) at the start of training. This means the model begins from the exact pretrained weights, and the adaptation is learned incrementally.

PropertyValue
Trainable params (rank \( r=16 \), 7B model)~10-40M (0.1-0.5% of total)
Common target modules\( W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O \) projections; sometimes MLP layers
Typical rank \( r \)4-64 (higher for more complex tasks)
Inference overheadZero — merge \( W' = W + \frac{\alpha}{r}BA \) after training
Memory saving vs. full fine-tune~100x (only adapter params need optimizer states)

Theoretical motivation. Aghajanyan et al. (2021) showed that fine-tuning updates have a low intrinsic dimensionality — the weight changes during adaptation occupy a low-rank subspace even when all parameters are updated. LoRA directly parameterises this subspace, making the low-rank constraint explicit rather than emergent.

sections.title: Extensions
sections.body:
• QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023): Combines LoRA with 4-bit NormalFloat quantisation of the frozen base model, enabling fine-tuning of 65B models on a single 48 GB GPU
  • DoRA (Liu et al., 2024): Decomposes the update into magnitude and direction components, improving performance
  • LoRA+: Sets different learning rates for \( A \) and \( B \) matrices

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Parameter count. For a weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \), full fine-tuning requires \( dk \) trainable parameters. LoRA replaces this with \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) and \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k} \), giving \( r(d + k) \) parameters. The compression ratio is:

\[ \frac{r(d + k)}{dk} = r\left(\frac{1}{k} + \frac{1}{d}\right) \]

For a typical attention projection with \( d = k = 4096 \) and \( r = 16 \): ratio \( = 16 \times 2/4096 = 0.0078 \), i.e., 0.78% of the full parameter count.

title: LoRA Adapter
summary: The pair of low-rank matrices \( (A, B) \) that constitute a single LoRA module, attached to a specific weight matrix in the model. Multiple LoRA adapters can be trained for different tasks and hot-swapped at inference time without modif…

papers.citation: 2021 · Hu et al. · LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
2023 · Sheng et al. · S-LoRA: Serving Thousands of Concurrent LoRA Adapters

sections.body:
The pair of low-rank matrices \( (A, B) \) that constitute a single LoRA module, attached to a specific weight matrix in the model. Multiple LoRA adapters can be trained for different tasks and hot-swapped at inference time without modifying the base model, enabling efficient multi-task serving.

A LoRA adapter for a weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \) consists of:

  • \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) — the down-projection (maps from \( d \) dimensions to the low-rank bottleneck \( r \))
  • \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k} \) — the up-projection (maps back to the original \( k \) dimensions)

The modified forward pass is \( h = Wx + \frac{\alpha}{r}BAx \). A complete LoRA fine-tune attaches adapters to multiple weight matrices — typically the four attention projections \( W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O \) at every layer — so a "LoRA adapter" in practice refers to the collection of all \( (A, B) \) pairs for a given task.

sections.title: Serving strategies
sections.body:
StrategyMechanismTrade-off
Merge and unloadCompute \( W' = W + \frac{\alpha}{r}BA \) once, discard \( A, B \)Zero overhead but single-task only
Dynamic loadingKeep base model in memory; load/swap \( (A, B) \) pairs per requestMulti-tenant; small swap latency
Batched multi-adapterSystems like S-LoRA and Punica batch requests across different adapters on the same GPU using custom CUDA kernelsHigh throughput; engineering complexity

S-LoRA (Sheng et al., 2023) introduced a unified paging mechanism that stores adapters in a shared memory pool and uses custom CUDA kernels to apply different adapters to different requests within the same batch — enabling thousands of concurrent adapters on a single GPU.

sections.title: Practical details
sections.body:
• Adapter files are typically 10-100 MB (vs. GBs for the full model), making them easy to share, version, and distribute on platforms like Hugging Face Hub
  • The PEFT library (Hugging Face) provides a standard format: adapter weights are stored as safetensors with a JSON config specifying rank, alpha, and target modules
  • Adapter arithmetic is possible: adapters can be added, subtracted, or interpolated to combine capabilities or ablate specific behaviours

title: QLoRA (Quantized LoRA)
summary: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that combines 4-bit quantisation of the frozen base model with LoRA adapters trained in higher precision (BFloat16). QLoRA enables fine-tuning of 65B+ parameter models on a single 48GB GPU with ne…

papers.citation: 2023 · Tim Dettmers, Artidoro Pagnoni, Ari Holtzman, Luke Zettlemoyer · QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized Language Models

sections.body:
A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that combines 4-bit quantisation of the frozen base model with LoRA adapters trained in higher precision (BFloat16). QLoRA enables fine-tuning of 65B+ parameter models on a single 48GB GPU with negligible quality loss compared to full 16-bit fine-tuning.

QLoRA builds on LoRA's insight — that fine-tuning updates have low intrinsic rank — and adds aggressive quantisation of the frozen base weights to slash memory. It introduces three technical innovations:

  1. 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4): A quantisation data type that is information-theoretically optimal for normally-distributed values. Neural network weights are approximately Gaussian after pretraining, so NF4 places quantisation bins at the quantiles of \( \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \), ensuring each bin covers equal probability mass. This outperforms both uniform 4-bit quantisation and FP4.
  2. Double quantisation: Standard block-wise quantisation stores one FP32 scale factor per block of 64 weights, adding 0.5 bits/param overhead. Double quantisation quantises these scale factors themselves (from FP32 to FP8 with block size 256), reducing the overhead to ~0.127 bits/param — a net saving of ~0.37 bits/param across the whole model.
  3. Paged optimizers: Uses NVIDIA unified memory (CPU↔GPU automatic paging) to handle optimizer state memory spikes during gradient checkpointing. When a long sequence triggers a memory spike, optimizer states are evicted to CPU RAM and paged back in when needed.

sections.title: Forward pass
sections.body:
\[ h = W_{\text{BF16}}x + \frac{\alpha}{r}BAx \quad \text{where} \quad W_{\text{BF16}} = \text{dequant}(W_{\text{NF4}}) \]

where \( W_{\text{NF4}} \) are the frozen base weights stored in 4-bit NF4, dequantised to BF16 for each forward pass; \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) and \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k} \) are the LoRA adapter matrices in BF16; \( r \) is the adapter rank (typically 16–64); and \( \alpha \) is the LoRA scaling factor. Only \( A \) and \( B \) receive gradients — the base weights are never updated.

sections.title: Memory breakdown
sections.body:
MethodBase weightsMemory (65B)
Full fine-tune (FP16)16-bit + optimizer states~780 GB
LoRA (FP16 base)16-bit frozen~130 GB
QLoRA (NF4 base)4-bit frozen + BF16 adapters~33 GB

Dettmers et al. demonstrated that QLoRA matches 16-bit full fine-tuning on the MMLU benchmark across model scales (7B–65B). Their Guanaco model family (QLoRA-tuned LLaMA) achieved 99.3% of ChatGPT performance on the Vicuna benchmark. The practical impact was enormous: researchers could fine-tune the largest open models on consumer GPUs (a single A100 or even an RTX 3090 for smaller models), democratising access to LLM customisation that previously required clusters.

Connection to other methods: QLoRA is complementary to other efficiency techniques. It can be combined with FlashAttention for further memory savings during the forward pass, and with gradient checkpointing (which QLoRA's paged optimizers specifically support). The NF4 format also influenced subsequent quantisation research such as GPTQ and AWQ.

title: Base Model
summary: A pretrained language model before any supervised fine-tuning or alignment. The base model is trained solely on next-token prediction over a large corpus and produces raw text completions — it does not inherently follow instructions, eng…

papers.citation: 2022 · Ouyang et al. · Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
papers.note: Seminal reference for instruction-tuned base-to-assistant model transitions.

sections.body:
A pretrained language model before any supervised fine-tuning or alignment. The base model is trained solely on next-token prediction over a large corpus and produces raw text completions — it does not inherently follow instructions, engage in dialogue, or exhibit safety behaviours.

The base model is the product of the pretraining stage, where the model learns to minimise the autoregressive cross-entropy loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{pretrain}} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \]

This objective teaches the model the statistical structure of language — grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, code — but not how to be a helpful assistant. Given a prompt, a base model generates the most likely continuation according to the training distribution. Its behaviour is best understood as "autocomplete" rather than "question answering":

  • Given a question, it may continue with more questions (mimicking a quiz format from training data)
  • Given a code comment, it may write the corresponding function
  • Given a dialogue prefix, it may produce a plausible next turn — but also frequently breaks format

sections.title: Base model vs. instruction-tuned model
sections.body:
BehaviourBase modelInstruction-tuned
"What is the capital of France?"May continue with more quiz questions"The capital of France is Paris."
SafetyNo guardrailsTrained to refuse harmful requests
DialogueDoes not naturally take turnsFollows User/Assistant format
PromptingRequires few-shot or careful formattingWorks well with zero-shot instructions

The alignment pipeline transforms a base model into an assistant through several stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on instruction–response pairs, (2) reward model training on human preference data, and (3) reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or direct preference optimisation (DPO). Each stage is relatively cheap compared to pretraining — typically <1% of the pretraining compute.

The superficial alignment hypothesis (Zhou et al., 2023) argues that SFT and RLHF primarily teach the model a format — how to structure helpful, harmless responses — rather than adding new knowledge. Evidence: instruction-tuned models rarely outperform base models on knowledge benchmarks; a few thousand high-quality SFT examples suffice; and base models can be prompted to exhibit aligned behaviour with careful formatting (e.g., the Alpaca prompt template).

Base models are released by organisations as starting points for downstream customisation (e.g., LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, Gemma). They are also used directly for tasks where raw completion is desirable: perplexity evaluation, text infilling, data augmentation, and research into model internals (mechanistic interpretability studies typically use base models to avoid confounds from alignment training).

title: Open-Weight Model
summary: A model whose trained weights are publicly released, allowing anyone to download, run, fine-tune, and inspect the model. The term "open-weight" is more precise than "open-source," as most released models do not include training code, dat…

papers.citation: 2023 · Touvron et al. · LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
papers.note: Catalyzed the modern open-weight LLM ecosystem.

sections.body:
A model whose trained weights are publicly released, allowing anyone to download, run, fine-tune, and inspect the model. The term "open-weight" is more precise than "open-source," as most released models do not include training code, data, or fully permissive licences.

The distinction between "open-source" and "open-weight" became important as large language models were released with restrictions that violated the Open Source Initiative's definition. A truly open-source model would include weights, training code, data, and a permissive licence (OSI-approved). Most "open" model releases provide only the weights under a custom licence — hence "open-weight" is the accurate descriptor.

sections.title: The spectrum of model openness
sections.body:
LevelWhat's releasedExamplesLicence type
Closed / API-onlyNothing; access via APIGPT-4, Claude, GeminiTerms of service
Open-weight (restricted)Weights + custom licenceLLaMA 1 (non-commercial), GemmaCustom / community
Open-weight (permissive)Weights + permissive licenceLLaMA 3, Mistral, Qwen 2Meta licence, Apache 2.0
Open-weight + dataWeights + training data detailsOLMo (Dolma dataset)Apache 2.0
Fully open sourceWeights + code + data + OSI licencePythia, OLMoApache 2.0

Licence landscape: LLaMA 1 (Feb 2023) was leaked rather than openly released, but its weights spread rapidly, catalysing an ecosystem of fine-tunes (Alpaca, Vicuna). LLaMA 2 (Jul 2023) was officially released with a commercial licence that prohibited use by companies with >700M monthly active users. Mistral released its 7B model under Apache 2.0, establishing a more permissive precedent. The licence choice significantly affects downstream adoption: Apache 2.0 and MIT models see far more community fine-tuning and deployment than restricted-licence models.

sections.title: Why open-weight models matter for ML research
sections.body:
• Mechanistic interpretability: Researchers need weight access to study internal representations, attention patterns, and circuits
  • Fine-tuning and adaptation: QLoRA, LoRA, and full fine-tuning require weight access
  • Safety research: Red-teaming, alignment research, and jailbreak analysis require inspecting model internals
  • Reproducibility: API-only models can change without notice; local weights are immutable
  • Cost: Self-hosting eliminates per-token API fees at scale

Safety tension: Open weights are irreversible — once published, they cannot be recalled. This creates a dual-use concern: the same openness that enables safety research also enables misuse without guardrails. This debate shaped Meta's staged approach (LLaMA 1 restricted → LLaMA 3 more permissive) and informs ongoing policy discussions about model release practices.

title: Sampling (in Language Models)
summary: The process of selecting the next token from the model's predicted probability distribution during text generation. Different sampling strategies (greedy, top-k, top-p, temperature) trade off between diversity and quality, and their corr…

papers.citation: 2020 · Ari Holtzman, Jan Buys, Li Du, Maxwell Forbes, Yejin Choi · The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration

sections.body:
The process of selecting the next token from the model's predicted probability distribution during text generation. Different sampling strategies (greedy, top-k, top-p, temperature) trade off between diversity and quality, and their correct configuration is critical for practical LLM deployment.

At each autoregressive generation step \( t \), the model outputs logits \( z \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|} \) over the vocabulary \( V \). These are converted to probabilities via softmax with temperature \( T \):

\[ P(x_t = v \mid x_{<t}) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{v' \in V} \exp(z_{v'} / T)} \]

where \( T > 0 \) is the temperature parameter. As \( T \to 0 \), the distribution collapses to a point mass on the argmax (greedy decoding). As \( T \to \infty \), the distribution approaches uniform.

sections.title: Common sampling strategies
sections.body:
StrategyMechanismKey parameterDiversity
Greedy\( x_t = \arg\max_v P(v) \)—None
Beam searchMaintain top-\(B\) sequence hypotheses\( B \) (beam width)Low
TemperatureScale logits by \( 1/T \)\( T \in (0, \infty) \)Tunable
Top-kRestrict to \( k \) highest-prob tokens, renormalise\( k \) (e.g., 40–100)Moderate
Top-p (nucleus)Restrict to smallest set with cumulative prob \( \geq p \)\( p \) (e.g., 0.9–0.95)Adaptive

Typical inference pipeline: These strategies are composed sequentially: (1) compute logits, (2) apply temperature scaling, (3) apply top-k filtering, (4) apply top-p filtering within the remaining candidates, (5) apply repetition/frequency/presence penalties, (6) sample from the resulting distribution. The order matters — temperature before filtering changes how many tokens survive the cutoff.

Why greedy/beam search fail for open-ended generation: Holtzman et al. (2020) showed that human-written text has surprisingly high per-token perplexity — humans routinely choose tokens outside the top-1 or top-10. Maximisation-based decoding produces text that is unnaturally peaked and repetitive, falling into a "degeneration" loop. Nucleus sampling was proposed specifically to match the entropy profile of human language.

Practical guidance: For factual/structured tasks (classification, extraction, code with tests), low temperature or greedy decoding is preferred for reliability. For creative/conversational tasks, \( T \approx 0.7\text{–}1.0 \) with \( p \approx 0.9 \) is a common default. Setting both \( k \) and \( p \) provides a safety net: top-k prevents extreme tail sampling; top-p adapts the effective candidate set to confidence.

title: Greedy Decoding
summary: A deterministic decoding strategy that always selects the token with the highest probability at each generation step. It is the simplest decoding method but often produces repetitive, degenerate text because it never explores lower-proba…

papers.citation: 2020 · Holtzman et al. · The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration
papers.note: Analyzes degeneration behavior of deterministic decoding strategies such as greedy search.

sections.body:
A deterministic decoding strategy that always selects the token with the highest probability at each generation step. It is the simplest decoding method but often produces repetitive, degenerate text because it never explores lower-probability alternatives that may lead to globally better sequences.

At each step \( t \), greedy decoding selects:

\[ x_t = \arg\max_{v \in V} P(v \mid x_{<t}) \]

This is equivalent to sampling with temperature \( T \to 0 \). The full sequence is built left-to-right, committing irrevocably to each choice — there is no backtracking or reranking.

Why greedy decoding degenerates: Holtzman et al. (2020) showed that greedy (and beam search) decoding produces text with far lower per-token entropy than human writing. Human-written text regularly selects tokens outside the top-1 — we choose surprising words for emphasis, variety, and style. Maximisation-based decoding cannot reproduce this, leading to:

  • Repetition loops: The model gets stuck in self-reinforcing cycles ("The cat sat on the mat. The cat sat on the mat...") because repeating a phrase increases its conditional probability
  • Blandness: Always choosing the most likely token produces generic, hedging language ("It is important to note that...")
  • Global suboptimality: A locally suboptimal token at step \( t \) may unlock a much better continuation. Greedy decoding cannot discover this because it never explores alternatives. For example, choosing the second-most-likely word might lead to a more coherent paragraph

sections.title: Comparison with other decoding strategies
sections.body:
MethodExplores alternatives?Deterministic?Cost per step
GreedyNoYes\( O(|V|) \)
Beam search (\( B \) beams)Yes (top \( B \) prefixes)Yes\( O(B \cdot |V|) \)
Top-k samplingYes (stochastic, fixed \( k \))No\( O(|V|) \)
Top-p samplingYes (stochastic, adaptive)No\( O(|V| \log |V|) \)

When greedy decoding is appropriate: Despite its limitations for open-ended generation, greedy decoding remains the method of choice when (1) the task has a single correct answer (classification, entity extraction, arithmetic), (2) determinism is required for reproducibility, or (3) the output is verified by an external oracle (code generation with unit tests, where incorrect completions are discarded). Many LLM evaluation benchmarks use greedy decoding to eliminate sampling variance.

Relationship to beam search: Beam search generalises greedy decoding by maintaining the top \( B \) candidate sequences at each step. Greedy decoding is beam search with \( B = 1 \). However, beam search also suffers from degeneration for open-ended generation — it merely delays the problem by exploring a slightly wider frontier.

title: Top-k Sampling
summary: A sampling strategy that restricts the next-token choice to the \( k \) most probable tokens, then renormalises the distribution and samples from it. This prevents the model from selecting very low-probability (often nonsensical) tokens…

papers.citation: 2018 · Angela Fan, Mike Lewis, Yann Dauphin · Hierarchical Neural Story Generation

sections.body:
A sampling strategy that restricts the next-token choice to the \( k \) most probable tokens, then renormalises the distribution and samples from it. This prevents the model from selecting very low-probability (often nonsensical) tokens while preserving diversity within the top candidates.

At each step, the model produces a probability distribution \( P(v \mid x_{<t}) \) over the full vocabulary \( V \). Top-k sampling proceeds as follows:

  1. Sort tokens by probability in descending order
  2. Identify the set \( V_k \) of the \( k \) highest-probability tokens
  3. Set the probability of all tokens \( v \notin V_k \) to zero
  4. Renormalise the remaining probabilities to sum to 1
  5. Sample from the truncated distribution

Formally:

\[ P_{\text{top-k}}(v \mid x_{<t}) = \begin{cases} \frac{P(v \mid x_{<t})}{\sum_{v' \in V_k} P(v' \mid x_{<t})} & \text{if } v \in V_k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

where \( V_k = \{ v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k \} \) ordered such that \( P(v_1) \geq P(v_2) \geq \cdots \geq P(v_k) \).

The fixed-\( k \) problem: The key limitation of top-k sampling is that the optimal number of candidates varies dramatically across contexts. Consider two scenarios:

ContextDistribution shapeProblem with \( k = 40 \)
"The Eiffel Tower is in ___"Peaked (one dominant answer)Includes 39 implausible completions ("London", "Mars"...)
"I like to eat ___"Flat (many valid answers)Excludes many perfectly reasonable foods

This motivated top-p (nucleus) sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020), which adapts the candidate set size to the distribution's entropy — using fewer tokens when the model is confident and more when uncertain.

Practical usage: Common values range from \( k = 40 \) to \( k = 100 \). Top-k is almost always combined with temperature scaling (applied before the truncation) and often layered with top-p as a safety net: first truncate to \( k \) candidates, then apply nucleus filtering within those. Fan et al. (2018) introduced top-k for neural story generation, showing it produced more coherent and interesting stories than unrestricted sampling or beam search.

Computational cost: Top-k requires a partial sort or selection algorithm over \( |V| \) logits, which is \( O(|V|) \) with a selection algorithm (e.g., quickselect) — the same asymptotic cost as greedy decoding. This makes it negligibly more expensive than argmax in practice.

title: Top-p Sampling (Nucleus Sampling)
summary: A sampling strategy that selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold \( p \). Unlike top-k (which uses a fixed number of tokens), top-p adapts the candidate set to the shape of the distributio…

papers.citation: 2020 · Ari Holtzman, Jan Buys, Li Du, Maxwell Forbes, Yejin Choi · The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration

sections.body:
A sampling strategy that selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold \( p \). Unlike top-k (which uses a fixed number of tokens), top-p adapts the candidate set to the shape of the distribution — fewer tokens when the model is confident, more when uncertain.

Given the probability distribution \( P(v \mid x_{<t}) \) sorted in descending order, nucleus sampling finds the smallest set \( V_p \) such that:

\[ \sum_{v \in V_p} P(v \mid x_{<t}) \geq p \]

Then it zeroes out all tokens outside \( V_p \), renormalises, and samples. Formally:

\[ P_{\text{top-p}}(v \mid x_{<t}) = \begin{cases} \frac{P(v \mid x_{<t})}{\sum_{v' \in V_p} P(v' \mid x_{<t})} & \text{if } v \in V_p \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

sections.title: Adaptive behaviour — the key insight
sections.body:
ScenarioDistributionNucleus size (\( p = 0.9 \))Effect
Top token has 95% probPeaked1 tokenNear-greedy (model is confident)
Top 5 tokens cover 90%Moderate5 tokensFocused diversity
Top 200 tokens cover 90%Flat / uncertain200 tokensWide exploration

This adaptivity prevents two failure modes of fixed top-k: (1) including nonsensical low-probability tokens when the model is confident (a large \( k \) in a peaked distribution), and (2) excluding reasonable alternatives when the model is uncertain (a small \( k \) in a flat distribution).

Theoretical motivation: Holtzman et al. (2020) observed that the per-token probability of human-written text follows a wide range — sometimes the chosen word is the most likely, sometimes it is the 50th most likely. Maximisation-based decoding (greedy, beam search) produces text that occupies an unnaturally narrow band of high probability, which paradoxically makes it seem less natural. Nucleus sampling matches the entropy profile of human text by letting the effective candidate set breathe with the model's confidence.

Common configurations: \( p = 0.9 \) to \( 0.95 \) is the standard range. In production LLM APIs, top-p is typically combined with temperature scaling (applied first to reshape logits, then nucleus filtering truncates the tail). Some APIs also layer top-k as a hard ceiling before top-p as a safety net.

Computational cost: Top-p requires a full sort of the vocabulary probabilities to find the cumulative sum threshold, giving \( O(|V| \log |V|) \) per step — slightly more expensive than top-k's \( O(|V|) \) partial sort, but negligible relative to the forward pass cost of a large Transformer.

title: Frequency Penalty
summary: A decoding-time parameter that reduces the logit of a token proportionally to how many times it has already appeared in the generated text. It penalises repeated tokens more heavily the more often they occur, encouraging lexical diversit…

papers.citation: 2019 · Keskar et al. · CTRL: A Conditional Transformer Language Model for Controllable Generation

sections.body:
A decoding-time parameter that reduces the logit of a token proportionally to how many times it has already appeared in the generated text. It penalises repeated tokens more heavily the more often they occur, encouraging lexical diversity and reducing repetition loops.

The frequency penalty modifies the logit of each token \( v \) before the softmax is computed:

\[ z'_v = z_v - \alpha_{\text{freq}} \cdot \text{count}(v, x_{<t}) \]

where \( z_v \) is the raw logit for token \( v \), \( \text{count}(v, x_{<t}) \) is the number of times \( v \) has appeared in the generated sequence so far, and \( \alpha_{\text{freq}} \in [0, 2] \) is the penalty strength (0 = no penalty).

The penalty is linear in count, so a token used 5 times is penalised 5x as much as one used once:

Count of token \( v \)Logit adjustment (\( \alpha = 0.5 \))Effect on probability
0 (never seen)0Unchanged
1−0.5Mild reduction
5−2.5Significant reduction
10−5.0Near-elimination

Comparison with presence penalty: The distinction is crucial for choosing the right tool:

PropertyFrequency penaltyPresence penalty
Formula\( -\alpha \cdot \text{count}(v) \)\( -\alpha \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{count}(v) > 0] \)
Scales with repetitions?Yes (linearly)No (binary: 0 or 1)
Primary effectReduces word-level repetitionEncourages topic diversity
Best use caseAvoiding "the the the" in long textBrainstorming, listing diverse ideas

Practical considerations: Frequency penalty is applied to all tokens including function words ("the", "is", "and"), which appear frequently in natural text. Setting \( \alpha \) too high forces the model to avoid common words, producing stilted, unnatural prose. A value of \( 0.1\text{–}0.5 \) is typical for long-form generation. For shorter outputs, the penalty is less impactful because few tokens have accumulated high counts.

Related techniques: Frequency penalty is a simpler alternative to n-gram blocking (which hard-prevents any n-gram from repeating) and the repetition penalty of Keskar et al. (2019), which divides the logit by a factor \( \theta > 1 \) for previously seen tokens rather than subtracting. The multiplicative approach penalises high-probability tokens more than low-probability ones in absolute terms.

title: Presence Penalty
summary: A decoding-time parameter that applies a flat penalty to any token that has appeared at least once in the generated text, regardless of how many times it occurred. Unlike frequency penalty (which scales with count), presence penalty is b…

papers.citation: Popularised by the OpenAI API (2020s); conceptually related to Maximum Marginal Relevance (Carbonell & Goldstein, 1998) for diversity in retrieval.

sections.body:
A decoding-time parameter that applies a flat penalty to any token that has appeared at least once in the generated text, regardless of how many times it occurred. Unlike frequency penalty (which scales with count), presence penalty is binary — it encourages the model to introduce new vocabulary and topics rather than revisiting previously used tokens.

The presence penalty modifies the logit of token \( v \) before softmax:

\[ z'_v = z_v - \alpha_{\text{pres}} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{count}(v, x_{<t}) > 0] \]

where \( \alpha_{\text{pres}} \in [0, 2] \) is the penalty strength and \( \mathbb{1}[\cdot] \) is the indicator function. A token used once receives exactly the same penalty as one used 100 times — the penalty is about novelty, not frequency.

Concrete example: Suppose \( \alpha_{\text{pres}} = 1.0 \) and the model has generated a paragraph about cats. Every token that appeared ("cat", "fur", "the", "is") gets a logit reduction of 1.0, while never-seen tokens ("dog", "ocean", "quantum") retain their original logits. This creates a systematic bias toward unexplored vocabulary, pushing the model to shift topics or use synonyms.

sections.title: When to use each penalty
sections.body:
TaskRecommended penaltyRationale
Long-form article writingFrequency (low \( \alpha \))Reduce verbatim repetition while allowing reuse of topic words
Brainstorming / idea generationPresence (moderate \( \alpha \))Push model to explore new concepts each time
Creative writing with varied vocabularyBoth (low \( \alpha \) each)Reduce repetition + encourage novelty simultaneously
Factual Q&A / code generationNeither (or very low)Accuracy matters more than diversity; repetition of key terms is natural

Interaction with other decoding parameters: Presence penalty is applied to logits before softmax, so it interacts with temperature scaling. At low temperature (sharply peaked distribution), the penalty has a proportionally larger effect because even a small logit shift can change which token wins the argmax. At high temperature (flat distribution), the penalty's effect is diluted. Both frequency and presence penalties can be combined — they simply add their respective logit adjustments independently.

Limitation: Presence penalty penalises all previously seen tokens equally, including function words ("a", "the", "is") that must be reused for grammatical text. High values (\( \alpha > 1.0 \)) can produce ungrammatical output as the model contorts to avoid common words. This is the same issue as frequency penalty but more severe, since even a single use triggers the full penalty.

title: Hallucination
summary: The generation of text that is fluent and confident but factually incorrect, unsupported by the input, or internally inconsistent. Hallucinations are a fundamental challenge of LLMs because the training objective (next-token prediction)…

papers.citation: 2023 · Ji et al. · Survey of Hallucination in Natural Language Generation

sections.body: The generation of text that is fluent and confident but factually incorrect, unsupported by the input, or internally inconsistent. Hallucinations are a fundamental challenge of LLMs because the training objective (next-token prediction) rewards plausibility, not truthfulness — the model has no internal mechanism to distinguish what it "knows" from what it does not.

sections.title: Taxonomy of hallucination
sections.body:
TypeDefinitionExample
IntrinsicContradicts the provided source or contextA summary that attributes claims to a paper that never made them
ExtrinsicIntroduces claims that cannot be verified from the sourceAdding fabricated statistics or details not in the source document
FactualStates incorrect real-world facts with confidenceWrong dates, invented citations, non-existent papers, false historical claims

Ji et al. (2023) further distinguish faithfulness hallucination (output diverges from the source input) from factuality hallucination (output diverges from world knowledge), noting that the two require different evaluation approaches.

sections.title: Root causes
sections.body:
• Training objective mismatch: The cross-entropy loss \( \mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \log P_\theta(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \) rewards assigning high probability to training sequences, not factual accuracy. The model learns correlations, not truth conditions
  • No epistemic uncertainty: Standard LLMs have no mechanism to express "I don't know" — every vocabulary token always receives some probability mass, so the model is forced to generate something
  • Training data noise: The web corpus contains contradictions, outdated facts, and misinformation. The model absorbs all of it without a ground-truth filter
  • Exposure bias: During autoregressive generation, a single hallucinated token shifts the conditional distribution, making subsequent tokens condition on a false premise — errors compound
  • Sycophancy: RLHF-trained models learn that agreeing with the user earns higher reward, so they may fabricate supporting evidence for the user's stated belief

sections.title: Mitigation strategies
sections.body:
StrategyMechanismLimitation
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)Ground generation in retrieved documentsModel may still ignore or misinterpret retrieved context
Chain-of-thought promptingExplicit reasoning steps can catch inconsistenciesReasoning itself can be hallucinated ("faithful CoT" is not guaranteed)
Self-consistency decodingSample multiple outputs, take majority voteIncreases cost \( n \)-fold; systematic errors persist across samples
Calibration / abstention trainingTrain model to output "I don't know" when uncertainHard to calibrate; models may over- or under-abstain
Citation / attributionForce model to cite sources for each claimCitations themselves can be hallucinated

Evaluation metrics: Measuring hallucination is itself challenging. Common approaches include: (1) FActScore (Min et al., 2023) — decompose a generation into atomic facts and verify each against a knowledge source; (2) human evaluation with attribution judgments; (3) NLI-based detection — use a natural language inference model to check if claims are entailed by a reference. No metric is fully reliable, which makes benchmarking progress difficult.

Surprising finding: Larger models hallucinate differently, not necessarily less. Scaling improves factual recall but can increase confident hallucination — the model becomes more fluent at stating falsehoods convincingly. This has been called the "calibration-capability gap."

title: Misalignment
summary: A failure mode where a model's behaviour diverges from the intentions or values of its designers and users. A misaligned model may pursue unintended goals, produce harmful outputs, or optimise for proxy objectives that correlate poorly w…

papers.citation: 2019 · Stuart Russell · Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control
2019 · Evan Hubinger et al. · Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems
2022 · Richard Ngo, Lawrence Chan, Sören Mindermann · The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective

sections.body:
A failure mode where a model's behaviour diverges from the intentions or values of its designers and users. A misaligned model may pursue unintended goals, produce harmful outputs, or optimise for proxy objectives that correlate poorly with what humans actually want.

Misalignment is the central problem of AI safety. As models become more capable, the gap between what we specify (the objective function) and what we intend (the desired behaviour) becomes increasingly consequential. The problem was formalised by Stuart Russell as the value alignment problem: ensuring that an AI system's objectives match human values even in novel situations.

Misalignment can manifest at multiple levels:

LevelDescriptionExample
Objective misalignmentOptimising the wrong objectiveA chatbot that maximises engagement by being inflammatory
Behavioural misalignmentCorrect objective but undesired side effectsA coding assistant that writes working but insecure code
Goal misgeneralisationLearns a proxy that works in training but fails at deploymentA model that learns to be helpful only when being evaluated
Deceptive alignmentModel behaves well during oversight but pursues different goals when unmonitoredHypothesised in Hubinger et al. (2019) "Risks from Learned Optimization"

sections.title: Sources of misalignment in LLMs
sections.body:
• Specification problem: Difficulty of fully specifying human values in a loss function. The RLHF objective \( \max_\pi \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}, y \sim \pi(\cdot|x)}[r_\phi(x, y)] - \beta\, D_{\text{KL}}(\pi \| \pi_{\text{ref}}) \) optimises a learned reward model \( r_\phi \), which is itself an imperfect proxy for human preferences.
  • Reward hacking / Goodhart's Law: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." Models find shortcuts to maximise reward without achieving intended behaviour — e.g., producing verbose responses that score higher on reward models without being more helpful.
  • Distribution shift: Training-time alignment does not transfer to all deployment contexts. A model aligned via RLHF on English conversational data may behave differently on code, other languages, or adversarial prompts.
  • Sycophancy: Models learn to agree with users rather than be truthful, because human raters reward agreeable responses during RLHF training.

sections.title: Alignment techniques and their limitations
sections.body:
MethodApproachLimitation
RLHFTrain reward model from human preferences, then optimise policyReward model is a proxy; can be hacked
Constitutional AI (CAI)Model self-critiques against a set of principlesQuality depends on the constitution's completeness
DPODirect preference optimisation without separate reward modelStill relies on quality of preference data
Scalable oversightUse AI to help humans evaluate AI (recursive reward modelling, debate)Open research problem

The fundamental tension is that alignment difficulty scales with capability. More capable models can find more sophisticated ways to satisfy proxy objectives while violating intended goals — a phenomenon sometimes called the sharp left turn concern in safety research.

title: Bias (Fairness)
summary: Systematic and unfair favouring or disfavouring of particular groups in model predictions, arising from biases in training data, model design, or evaluation. In ML fairness, "bias" refers not to the statistical bias-variance trade-off bu…

papers.citation: 2016 · Bolukbasi et al. · Man is to Computer Programmer as Woman is to Homemaker?
2016 · Barocas and Selbst · Big Data's Disparate Impact

sections.body:
Systematic and unfair favouring or disfavouring of particular groups in model predictions, arising from biases in training data, model design, or evaluation. In ML fairness, "bias" refers not to the statistical bias-variance trade-off but to social biases that lead to discriminatory outcomes.

Fairness bias in machine learning became a major research area after a series of high-profile demonstrations that models — including word embeddings, image classifiers, and hiring tools — reproduce and amplify societal biases present in training data.

sections.title: Sources of bias
sections.body:
• Training data: Historical biases in text (e.g., associating certain professions with specific genders). Bolukbasi et al. (2016) showed word2vec encodes analogies like "man : computer programmer :: woman : homemaker."
  • Representation: Underrepresentation of minority groups in training corpora leads to worse performance for those groups (e.g., higher error rates in facial recognition for darker-skinned individuals, as shown by Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018).
  • Annotation: Human labellers' own biases reflected in ground-truth labels.
  • Amplification: Models can amplify small statistical patterns in the data into strong biases. If 60% of "cooking" images show women, a trained model may predict 80% women for cooking queries.

sections.title: Formal fairness criteria
sections.body:
MetricFormal DefinitionIntuition
Demographic parity\( P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=b) \)Equal positive prediction rates across groups
Equalized odds\( P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=b) \; \forall y \)Equal TPR and FPR across groups
Calibration\( P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=s, A=a) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=s, A=b) \; \forall s \)Same score means same probability regardless of group
Counterfactual fairness\( \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(U) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow b}(U) \)Prediction unchanged if sensitive attribute were counterfactually different

where \( A \) is the sensitive attribute (e.g., race, gender), \( Y \) is the true label, and \( \hat{Y} \) is the prediction.

Impossibility result: Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg et al. (2016) independently proved that except in degenerate cases, it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy demographic parity, equalized odds, and calibration. This means practitioners must make explicit value choices about which notion of fairness to prioritise — there is no universally "fair" classifier.

sections.title: Debiasing techniques
sections.body:
• Pre-processing: Rebalance or re-weight training data; remove sensitive attributes (though correlated features often leak information).
  • In-processing: Add fairness constraints to the loss function, e.g., adversarial debiasing where a discriminator tries to predict the sensitive attribute from model representations.
  • Post-processing: Adjust decision thresholds per group to equalise error rates (Hardt et al., 2016).
