For LLMs specifically, bias manifests in generated text (stereotypical associations, differential performance across languages/dialects) and is addressed through RLHF, Constitutional AI, and careful data curation — though elimination of all biases remains an open problem.

title: Explainability
summary: The degree to which a model's predictions can be understood by humans. Explainability methods aim to make black-box models (especially deep neural networks) more transparent by identifying which inputs, features, or internal computations…

papers.citation: 2016 · Marco Tulio Ribeiro, Sameer Singh, Carlos Guestrin · "Why Should I Trust You?": Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier
2017 · Scott M. Lundberg, Su-In Lee · A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions
2017 · Mukund Sundararajan, Ankur Taly, Qiqi Yan · Axiomatic Attribution for Deep Networks
2019 · Christoph Molnar · Interpretable Machine Learning

sections.body:
The degree to which a model's predictions can be understood by humans. Explainability methods aim to make black-box models (especially deep neural networks) more transparent by identifying which inputs, features, or internal computations drive a particular output.

As deep learning models grew in complexity and deployment stakes, explainability became both a research priority and a regulatory requirement (e.g., the EU's GDPR "right to explanation"). The field splits into methods that are interpretable by design versus post-hoc methods that explain opaque models after training.

sections.title: Categories of explainability methods
sections.body:
CategoryApproachExamples
Intrinsically interpretableModels transparent by designDecision trees, linear models, GAMs
Post-hoc (local)Explain individual predictionsLIME, SHAP, saliency maps, integrated gradients
Post-hoc (global)Explain overall model behaviourFeature importance, partial dependence plots, concept activation vectors
MechanisticReverse-engineer internal computationsProbing, activation patching, logit lens, sparse autoencoders

LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) explains a prediction by fitting a simple interpretable model \( g \) in the neighbourhood of an input \( x \):

\[ \xi(x) = \arg\min_{g \in G} \; \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g) \]

where \( f \) is the black-box model, \( \pi_x \) is a proximity kernel weighting perturbed samples near \( x \), and \( \Omega(g) \) penalises complexity of the explanation (e.g., number of non-zero features in a linear model).

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) assigns each feature a contribution based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory:

\[ \phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} \frac{|S|!\,(|N|-|S|-1)!}{|N|!} \left[ f(S \cup \{i\}) - f(S) \right] \]

where \( \phi_i \) is the contribution of feature \( i \), \( N \) is the set of all features, and \( S \) is a subset excluding \( i \). SHAP values are the unique additive feature attributions satisfying local accuracy, missingness, and consistency axioms.

Gradient-based methods for neural networks compute the gradient of the output with respect to the input \( \nabla_x f(x) \), highlighting which input dimensions most affect the prediction. Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al., 2017) accumulates gradients along a path from a baseline \( x' \) to the input:

\[ \text{IG}_i(x) = (x_i - x'_i) \int_{\alpha=0}^{1} \frac{\partial f(x' + \alpha(x - x'))}{\partial x_i} \, d\alpha \]

Explainability vs. interpretability: Some authors distinguish these: interpretability is the degree to which a human can understand the model's mechanism, while explainability refers to producing post-hoc explanations of an opaque model. An inherently interpretable model (e.g., a shallow decision tree) needs no separate explanation.

Mechanistic interpretability for Transformers: This frontier approach aims to reverse-engineer the internal computations of neural networks. Techniques include probing classifiers (testing what information is encoded in hidden states), the logit lens (projecting intermediate representations to vocabulary space), activation patching (causally testing which components matter for a prediction), and sparse autoencoders for decomposing activations into interpretable features (Bricken et al., 2023). This is particularly relevant for understanding how Transformers implement algorithms internally.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
summary: A framework that augments a language model's generation with relevant documents retrieved from an external knowledge base. The model conditions its response on both the user query and the retrieved passages, reducing hallucination and en…

papers.citation: 2020 · Patrick Lewis et al. · Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

sections.body:
A framework that augments a language model's generation with relevant documents retrieved from an external knowledge base. The model conditions its response on both the user query and the retrieved passages, reducing hallucination and enabling access to up-to-date or domain-specific information.

RAG was introduced by Lewis et al. (2020) at Facebook AI to address a fundamental limitation of parametric language models: their knowledge is frozen at training time and can be factually incorrect or outdated. By coupling a pre-trained retriever with a pre-trained generator, RAG creates a semi-parametric model that can access and cite external evidence.

sections.title: The RAG pipeline has three stages
sections.body:
1. Indexing: Documents are chunked, embedded into dense vectors using an encoder \( f_\theta \), and stored in a vector database (FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, etc.).
  2. Retrieval: Given a query \( q \), find the top-\( k \) most relevant document chunks via similarity search: \( \text{sim}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}) = \frac{f_\theta(q) \cdot f_\theta(d)}{\|f_\theta(q)\| \|f_\theta(d)\|} \)
  3. Generation: The retrieved chunks are prepended to the prompt as context; the LLM generates an answer grounded in them.

The original RAG paper defines two variants:

RAG-Sequence — uses the same retrieved document for the entire generated sequence:

\[ P_{\text{RAG-Seq}}(y \mid q) \approx \sum_{d \in \text{top-}k} P_\eta(d \mid q) \prod_{i=1}^{N} P_\theta(y_i \mid q, d, y_{1:i-1}) \]

RAG-Token — can use different documents for each generated token:

\[ P_{\text{RAG-Tok}}(y \mid q) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{d \in \text{top-}k} P_\eta(d \mid q) \, P_\theta(y_i \mid q, d, y_{1:i-1}) \]

where \( P_\eta(d \mid q) \) is the retriever's relevance score (using a bi-encoder like DPR) and \( P_\theta \) is the generator (originally BART).

ComponentOriginal RAGModern practice
RetrieverDPR (bi-encoder)Dense (E5, BGE), sparse (BM25), or hybrid
GeneratorBARTAny LLM (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, etc.)
Knowledge baseWikipediaAny document corpus
Re-rankerNoneCross-encoder re-ranking of retrieved chunks

Advantages over fine-tuning: RAG keeps knowledge external and updatable — no retraining needed when information changes. It also provides attribution (the retrieved sources can be cited), which is valuable for trust and verification.

sections.title: Challenges
sections.body:
• Retrieval quality: Irrelevant or contradictory documents degrade generation ("garbage in, garbage out").
  • Chunking strategy: Chunk size, overlap, and boundaries significantly affect what gets retrieved.
  • Lost in the middle: LLMs attend more to the beginning and end of the context window, sometimes ignoring relevant information placed in the middle of many retrieved passages (Liu et al., 2023).
  • Faithfulness: Models may still hallucinate or ignore retrieved evidence, especially when it conflicts with parametric knowledge.

title: Semantic Search
summary: A search method that matches queries and documents based on meaning (semantic similarity) rather than exact keyword overlap. Documents and queries are embedded into a shared vector space, and retrieval is performed via nearest-neighbor s…

papers.citation: Enabled by dense retrieval models · DPR: Karpukhin et al. (2020) · Sentence-BERT: Reimers & Gurevych (2019)

sections.body:
A search method that matches queries and documents based on meaning (semantic similarity) rather than exact keyword overlap. Documents and queries are embedded into a shared vector space, and retrieval is performed via nearest-neighbor search on these embeddings.

Traditional keyword-based search (e.g., TF-IDF, BM25) relies on lexical overlap: it finds documents containing the same terms as the query. This fails on synonyms ("car" vs. "automobile"), paraphrases, and cross-lingual queries. Semantic search, enabled by learned dense embeddings, overcomes this by operating in a continuous vector space where proximity corresponds to meaning.

sections.title: The semantic search pipeline
sections.body:
1. Encode documents and queries into dense vectors using a shared or dual encoder: \( \mathbf{d} = f_\theta(\text{doc}), \; \mathbf{q} = f_\theta(\text{query}) \)
  2. Index document vectors in a vector database (FAISS, ScaNN, HNSW) for efficient approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval
  3. Retrieve the \( k \) nearest documents by cosine similarity:
\[ \text{sim}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}) = \frac{\mathbf{q} \cdot \mathbf{d}}{\|\mathbf{q}\| \|\mathbf{d}\|} \]

Training embedding models: Dense retrieval models are trained using contrastive learning. Given a query \( q \), a positive document \( d^+ \), and negative documents \( \{d^-_j\} \), the model minimises:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\log \frac{e^{\text{sim}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}^+)/\tau}}{e^{\text{sim}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}^+)/\tau} + \sum_j e^{\text{sim}(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}^-_j)/\tau}} \]

where \( \tau \) is a temperature parameter. Hard negatives (documents that are lexically similar but semantically different) are crucial for training effective retrievers.

PropertyKeyword search (BM25)Semantic search
MatchingExact terms (lexical)Meaning / concepts (dense vectors)
Handles synonyms?NoYes
Handles typos?PoorlyReasonably well
Requires training?No (unsupervised)Yes (contrastive learning)
LatencyFast (inverted index)ANN adds overhead; sub-millisecond with HNSW

Embedding models: Sentence-BERT (2019) adapted BERT for efficient sentence-level embeddings via Siamese networks. Later models — E5, BGE, GTE, and OpenAI's text-embedding series — improved quality through larger training corpora and better negative mining strategies.

In practice, hybrid search (combining BM25 scores with semantic similarity via reciprocal rank fusion or learned score combination) often outperforms either method alone, as keyword matching catches exact matches that embeddings may miss. Semantic search is a core component of RAG pipelines, providing the retrieval stage that grounds LLM generation in external knowledge.

title: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Layer
summary: A neural network layer that contains multiple parallel "expert" sub-networks but activates only a small subset for each input token, selected by a learned router. This allows scaling model capacity (total parameters) without proportional…

papers.citation: 1991 · Robert A. Jacobs, Michael I. Jordan, Steven J. Nowlan, Geoffrey E. Hinton · Adaptive Mixtures of Local Experts
2017 · Noam Shazeer et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer
2022 · William Fedus, Barret Zoph, Noam Shazeer · Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models with Simple and Efficient Sparsity

sections.body:
A neural network layer that contains multiple parallel "expert" sub-networks but activates only a small subset for each input token, selected by a learned router. This allows scaling model capacity (total parameters) without proportionally increasing compute per token.

A sparse MoE layer replaces the standard FFN in a Transformer block:

\[ \text{MoE}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i(\mathbf{x}) \cdot E_i(\mathbf{x}) \]

where \( E_i \) are expert networks (typically small FFNs), \( g_i(\mathbf{x}) \) are routing weights from the router network, and only the top-\( k \) experts have non-zero \( g_i \) (sparsity).

The router computes:

\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = \text{TopK}(\text{softmax}(W_r \mathbf{x}), k) \]
ModelTotal paramsActive params/tokenExperts
Mixtral 8×7B47B~13B8 (top-2)
Switch Transformer1.6T~equivalent to T5-XXL2048 (top-1)
GPT-4 (rumoured)~1.8T~220B16 (top-2)

Key challenge: load balancing. Without regularisation, the router may collapse to always selecting the same few experts ("expert collapse"). An auxiliary load-balancing loss encourages uniform expert utilisation.

MoE models offer better performance per FLOP than dense models but require more memory (all experts must be stored) and are harder to train and serve due to communication overhead in distributed settings.

title: Router Network
summary: A small learned network within a Mixture-of-Experts layer that decides which expert(s) to activate for each input token. The router maps each token's hidden state to a probability distribution over experts, then selects the top-\( k \).

papers.citation: 2017 · Noam Shazeer et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer

sections.body:
A small learned network within a Mixture-of-Experts layer that decides which expert(s) to activate for each input token. The router maps each token's hidden state to a probability distribution over experts, then selects the top-\( k \).

The standard router is a single linear layer followed by softmax:

\[ p(\text{expert}_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}(W_r \mathbf{x} + b_r)_i \]

The top-\( k \) experts are selected, and their outputs are weighted by the (renormalised) routing probabilities:

\[ \text{output} = \sum_{i \in \text{TopK}} \frac{p_i}{\sum_{j \in \text{TopK}} p_j} \cdot E_i(\mathbf{x}) \]

sections.title: Routing challenges
sections.body:
• Load imbalance: The router may learn to send most tokens to a few "popular" experts, wasting capacity. An auxiliary loss penalises uneven expert utilisation.
  • Token dropping: When an expert's buffer is full, excess tokens may be dropped (Switch Transformer) or sent to a shared "overflow" expert.
  • Training instability: Router gradients can be noisy, especially with top-1 routing (hard selection). Techniques like expert-choice routing (Zhou et al., 2022) let experts choose tokens instead.
Routing strategy\( k \)Used in
Top-11Switch Transformer
Top-22Mixtral, GShard
Expert-choiceFixed capacity per expertExpert Choice (Zhou et al., 2022)

title: Expert Network
summary: One of the parallel sub-networks within a Mixture-of-Experts layer, each of which processes a subset of input tokens. In Transformer MoE models, each expert is typically a standard feed-forward network (FFN) with the same architecture bu…

papers.citation: 1991 · Robert A. Jacobs, Michael I. Jordan, Steven J. Nowlan, Geoffrey E. Hinton · Adaptive Mixtures of Local Experts
2017 · Noam Shazeer et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer

sections.body:
One of the parallel sub-networks within a Mixture-of-Experts layer, each of which processes a subset of input tokens. In Transformer MoE models, each expert is typically a standard feed-forward network (FFN) with the same architecture but independent weights.

Each expert \( E_i \) has the same structure as the position-wise FFN it replaces:

\[ E_i(\mathbf{x}) = W_2^{(i)}\,\sigma(W_1^{(i)}\mathbf{x} + b_1^{(i)}) + b_2^{(i)} \]

The experts share the same input and output dimensions but have independent weights, giving each the capacity to specialise in different aspects of the data.

Expert specialisation: Studies have shown that experts often develop interpretable specialisations, though the patterns vary:

  • Some experts specialise in syntactic patterns (punctuation, function words)
  • Others specialise in domains (code, scientific text, dialogue)
  • Some specialise in positional patterns (beginning vs. end of sequence)
  • Specialisation is often soft and overlapping rather than hard partitioning

The total parameter count of a MoE layer is \( N \times \text{params per expert} \), where \( N \) is the number of experts. Since only \( k \) experts are active per token, the compute per token is \( k/N \) of what a dense model with the same total parameters would require. This decoupling of parameter count from compute is the core advantage of MoE.

title: Top-k Routing
summary: The routing strategy in MoE models where each input token is sent to the \( k \) experts with the highest router scores. The outputs of the selected experts are combined using the router probabilities as weights. Most modern MoE models u…

papers.citation: 2017 · Shazeer et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks

sections.body:
The routing strategy in MoE models where each input token is sent to the \( k \) experts with the highest router scores. The outputs of the selected experts are combined using the router probabilities as weights. Most modern MoE models use top-1 or top-2 routing.

Given router logits \( \mathbf{h} = W_r \mathbf{x} \), top-k routing:

  1. Computes probabilities: \( \mathbf{p} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{h}) \)
  2. Selects indices: \( S = \text{TopK}(\mathbf{p}, k) \)
  3. Combines outputs: \( \text{output} = \sum_{i \in S} \tilde{p}_i \cdot E_i(\mathbf{x}) \) where \( \tilde{p}_i = p_i / \sum_{j \in S} p_j \)
\( k \)Trade-offExample models
1Minimum compute; harder to train (no gradient through unchosen experts)Switch Transformer
2Better quality, smoother gradients; 2× expert computeMixtral, GShard
HigherDiminishing returns; approaches dense model costRare in practice

A load-balancing auxiliary loss is critical with top-k routing to prevent expert collapse:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{balance}} = \alpha \cdot N \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i \]

where \( f_i \) is the fraction of tokens routed to expert \( i \) and \( P_i \) is the mean routing probability for expert \( i \). This loss is minimised when tokens are evenly distributed.

title: Load Balancing (MoE)
summary: The problem — and associated techniques — of ensuring that tokens are distributed roughly evenly across experts in a Mixture-of-Experts layer. Without explicit balancing, routers tend to collapse to using only a few experts, wasting mode…

papers.citation: 2017 · Shazeer et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks

sections.body: The problem — and associated techniques — of ensuring that tokens are distributed roughly evenly across experts in a Mixture-of-Experts layer. Without explicit balancing, routers tend to collapse to using only a few experts, wasting model capacity.

Why imbalance occurs: If one expert becomes slightly better early in training, the router sends more tokens to it, which generates more gradients for that expert, making it even better — a positive feedback loop leading to expert collapse (most tokens go to one or two experts).

sections.title: Balancing techniques
sections.body:
TechniqueMechanism
Auxiliary lossPenalise uneven token distribution via a differentiable loss term
Expert capacity factorHard limit on tokens per expert; overflow tokens dropped or rerouted
Noise injectionAdd noise to router logits during training for exploration
Expert-choice routingExperts choose their top-\( k \) tokens (guarantees uniform load)

The standard auxiliary loss from Shazeer et al.:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = \alpha \cdot N \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i \]

The coefficient \( \alpha \) is a hyperparameter (typically 0.01–0.1) that trades off load balance against task performance. Too high an \( \alpha \) forces uniform routing regardless of content, degrading quality.

Load balancing is also a systems challenge in distributed training: unbalanced routing causes some GPUs to be bottlenecked while others idle, reducing hardware utilisation.

title: Switch Transformer
summary: A simplified Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformer that routes each token to exactly one expert (top-1 routing), demonstrating that sparse expert models can be scaled to trillions of parameters while training faster than dense models of c…

papers.citation: 2022 · William Fedus, Barret Zoph, Noam Shazeer · Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion Parameter Models with Simple and Efficient Sparsity

sections.body:
A simplified Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformer that routes each token to exactly one expert (top-1 routing), demonstrating that sparse expert models can be scaled to trillions of parameters while training faster than dense models of comparable quality.

Standard MoE layers (Shazeer et al., 2017) route each token to the top-\( k \) experts (typically \( k=2 \)). The Switch Transformer's key simplification is top-1 routing: each token is dispatched to a single expert, cutting communication costs and simplifying the computation graph.

Routing mechanism. Given a token representation \( \mathbf{x} \), the router produces probabilities over \( N \) experts:

\[ p_i(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{e}_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{e}_j}} \]

where \( \mathbf{e}_i \) are learned expert embedding vectors. The token is sent to \( \text{argmax}_i \; p_i(\mathbf{x}) \), and the expert's output is multiplied by the router probability \( p_i(\mathbf{x}) \) to maintain differentiability.

Load-balancing loss. Without regularisation, the router collapses to sending all tokens to a few experts. The auxiliary loss encourages uniform utilisation:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{bal}} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i \]

where \( f_i \) is the fraction of tokens routed to expert \( i \), \( P_i \) is the mean router probability for expert \( i \) across the batch, and \( \alpha \) is a balancing coefficient (typically 0.01). Minimising \( \sum f_i \cdot P_i \) pushes toward uniform \( f_i = P_i = 1/N \).

Capacity factor. Each expert can process at most \( C \cdot n / N \) tokens per batch, where \( C \geq 1 \) is the capacity factor, \( n \) is the batch token count, and \( N \) is the number of experts. Overflow tokens skip the expert layer via the residual connection. In practice \( C = 1.0\text{--}1.5 \).

sections.title: Training stability techniques
sections.body:
• Selective precision: Router computation in FP32 for numerical stability; expert computation in BF16 for efficiency
  • Smaller initialisation: Expert weights initialised with reduced scale to avoid early instability
  • Dropout within experts: Higher dropout rates inside expert layers to compensate for sparse activation
ModelExpertsTotal paramsSpeedup over T5
Switch-Base1287B7x over T5-Base
Switch-C20481.6T4x over T5-XXL

The central finding is that scaling parameters via sparsity is more efficient than scaling compute via dense models. Switch-C achieved T5-XXL quality at a fraction of the training FLOPs. This paved the way for later MoE-based production models (e.g., Mixtral). The Switch Transformer replaces every other FFN layer in a standard Transformer with a Switch layer, leaving the attention layers dense.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Why the load-balancing loss encourages uniformity. The product \( f_i \cdot P_i \) is minimised when both are equal across experts. By the AM-GM inequality, for fixed \( \sum f_i = 1 \) and \( \sum P_i = 1 \), the sum \( \sum f_i P_i \) is minimised when \( f_i = P_i = 1/N \) for all \( i \). The factor \( N \) in front normalises the loss so it equals 1 at the uniform solution.

title: Model Merging
summary: The practice of combining the weights of multiple fine-tuned models into a single model without additional training. Model merging can combine the strengths of models specialised for different tasks (e.g., code, chat, reasoning) into one…

papers.citation: 2022 · Wortsman et al. · Model soups: averaging weights of multiple fine-tuned models improves accuracy without increasing inference time
2023 · Ilharco et al. · Editing Models with Task Arithmetic

sections.body:
The practice of combining the weights of multiple fine-tuned models into a single model without additional training. Model merging can combine the strengths of models specialised for different tasks (e.g., code, chat, reasoning) into one multitask model at zero extra training cost.

Given models \( \theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n \) all fine-tuned from the same base model \( \theta_0 \), merging combines them into \( \theta_{\text{merged}} \). The core methods, in rough order of sophistication:

MethodHow it worksLimitation
Linear (Model Soups)Weighted average: \( \theta = \sum_i \alpha_i \theta_i \)Cancellation of opposing updates
SLERPSpherical interpolation preserving weight normOnly 2 models at a time
Task ArithmeticAdd task vectors: \( \theta_0 + \sum_i \lambda_i (\theta_i - \theta_0) \)No interference handling
TIES-MergingResolve sign conflicts via majority vote, trim small valuesDeterministic trimming threshold
DARERandomly drop deltas, rescale to preserve expectationStochastic — variance across runs

Why merging works — linear mode connectivity. Fine-tuned models from the same base tend to lie in a connected low-loss region of parameter space. Interpolating between them stays within this basin, so the merged model performs well on all tasks. This was formalised by Neyshabur et al. (2020) and empirically demonstrated by Wortsman et al. (2022): averaging ~70 CLIP fine-tuned checkpoints improved both in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy.

sections.title: Practical considerations
sections.body:
• All models must share the same architecture and (typically) the same base initialisation
  • Merging in task-vector space \( (\theta_i - \theta_0) \) is generally better than merging raw weights, because it isolates the fine-tuning updates from the shared base
  • The scaling factors \( \lambda_i \) are critical hyperparameters, usually tuned on a validation set
  • Merging is free at inference time — unlike ensembling, the merged model has the same cost as a single model

Model merging has become a dominant strategy in the open-weight community. Tools like mergekit (by Charles Goddard) provide implementations of all major methods. Top models on the Open LLM Leaderboard are frequently merges of specialised fine-tunes.

title: Model Soups
summary: A model merging technique that averages the weights of multiple models fine-tuned from the same base with different hyperparameters. The resulting "soup" often outperforms any individual ingredient model, without additional training or i…

papers.citation: 2022 · Mitchell Wortsman, Gabriel Ilharco, Samir Yitzhak Gadre et al. · Model Soups: Averaging Weights of Multiple Fine-tuned Models Improves Accuracy without Increasing Inference Time

sections.body:
A model merging technique that averages the weights of multiple models fine-tuned from the same base with different hyperparameters. The resulting "soup" often outperforms any individual ingredient model, without additional training or increased inference cost.

The simplest version is uniform soup:

\[ \theta_{\text{soup}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i \]

A better variant is greedy soup: starting from the best individual model \( \theta_{\text{best}} \), iteratively consider each remaining model in order of descending validation accuracy. Add model \( \theta_k \) to the running average only if doing so improves validation performance; otherwise discard it. This typically selects 5--15 models out of dozens of candidates.

Key results (Wortsman et al., 2022, using CLIP ViT-B/32 fine-tuned on ImageNet):

  • Greedy soup improved ImageNet accuracy by ~0.5--1% over the best individual model
  • Out-of-distribution robustness (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-Sketch, ObjectNet) improved significantly — the soup lies closer to the centre of the low-loss basin, which correlates with flatter minima and better generalisation
  • Zero additional training or inference compute — identical cost to a single model

sections.title: Why it works — three complementary explanations
sections.body:
• Variance reduction: Averaging reduces prediction variance, similar to ensembling but in weight space rather than output space. Because the loss landscape is approximately convex in the basin around the shared initialisation, weight-space averaging yields a model near the centre of the basin
  • Linear mode connectivity: Models fine-tuned from the same base share a connected low-loss region; their convex combination also has low loss (Neyshabur et al., 2020)
  • Flat minima: The averaged model lands in a flatter region of the loss landscape, which is associated with better generalisation (Izmailov et al., SWA, 2018)

Model Soups can be seen as a precursor to the broader model merging ecosystem (task arithmetic, TIES, DARE). The key insight — that diverse fine-tuned checkpoints can be profitably combined — motivated subsequent work on merging models fine-tuned for different tasks, not just different hyperparameters.

title: SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)
summary: A method for interpolating between two sets of model weights along the surface of a hypersphere, preserving the magnitude (norm) of the weight vectors throughout the interpolation. SLERP is used in model merging as an alternative to simp…

papers.citation: 1985 · Shoemake · Animating Rotation with Quaternion Curves
papers.note: Original SLERP reference from SIGGRAPH 1985.

sections.body:
A method for interpolating between two sets of model weights along the surface of a hypersphere, preserving the magnitude (norm) of the weight vectors throughout the interpolation. SLERP is used in model merging as an alternative to simple linear interpolation.

For two vectors \( \mathbf{v}_0 \) and \( \mathbf{v}_1 \) with angle \( \Omega = \arccos(\hat{\mathbf{v}}_0 \cdot \hat{\mathbf{v}}_1) \) between them:

\[ \text{SLERP}(\mathbf{v}_0, \mathbf{v}_1; t) = \frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{v}_0 + \frac{\sin(t\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{v}_1 \]

where \( t \in [0, 1] \) controls the interpolation (\( t=0 \) gives \( \mathbf{v}_0 \), \( t=1 \) gives \( \mathbf{v}_1 \)).

sections.title: SLERP vs. linear interpolation (LERP)
sections.body:
PropertyLERPSLERP
PathStraight line (chord)Arc on the sphere
Norm at midpointReduced (dips below surface)Preserved (stays on surface)
Speed along pathNon-uniformConstant

In model merging, SLERP is applied layer-by-layer (or row-by-row) to the weight tensors of two models. It is particularly popular for merging two LLM checkpoints because preserving weight norms avoids the magnitude reduction that linear averaging can cause. However, SLERP only works for merging exactly two models; for three or more, methods like TIES or task arithmetic are used.

title: TIES-Merging
summary: A model merging method that resolves interference between task vectors by trimming small-magnitude parameters, resolving sign conflicts (via majority vote), and then averaging the remaining values. TIES addresses the performance degradat…

papers.citation: 2023 · Prateek Yadav et al. · TIES-Merging: Resolving Interference When Merging Models

sections.body:
A model merging method that resolves interference between task vectors by trimming small-magnitude parameters, resolving sign conflicts (via majority vote), and then averaging the remaining values. TIES addresses the performance degradation that occurs when naively averaging models with conflicting weight updates.

TIES-Merging (Trim, Elect Sign, Merge) operates on task vectors \( \tau_i = \theta_i - \theta_0 \), the difference between each fine-tuned model and the shared base model. The three steps:

  1. Trim: Set to zero all values in each \( \tau_i \) with magnitude below the top-\( k\% \) threshold. This removes noisy, low-impact updates.
  2. Elect Sign: For each parameter position, resolve sign conflicts by majority vote across models. If models disagree on the sign of a delta, choose the sign with the greater total magnitude.
  3. Merge: Average the trimmed task vectors (keeping only values that agree with the elected sign), then add back to the base: \( \theta_{\text{merged}} = \theta_0 + \lambda \cdot \bar{\tau} \)

Why standard averaging fails: When two fine-tuned models push a parameter in opposite directions, their updates cancel out during averaging, losing information from both. TIES resolves this by choosing one direction and zeroing the conflicting values.

MethodHandles sign conflicts?Trims noise?
Linear averageNo (cancellation)No
Task ArithmeticNoNo
TIES-MergingYes (majority vote)Yes (top-k trim)

title: DARE (Drop And REscale)
summary: A model merging preprocessing step that randomly drops (zeros out) a large fraction of each task vector's delta parameters, then rescales the remaining values to preserve the expected magnitude. DARE reduces interference between models b…

papers.citation: 2024 · Yu et al. · Language Models are Super Mario: Absorbing Abilities from Homologous Models as a Free Lunch

sections.body:
A model merging preprocessing step that randomly drops (zeros out) a large fraction of each task vector's delta parameters, then rescales the remaining values to preserve the expected magnitude. DARE reduces interference between models before merging, improving multi-task performance.

Given a task vector \( \tau = \theta - \theta_0 \), DARE applies:

\[ \tilde{\tau}_j = \begin{cases} \frac{\tau_j}{1 - p} & \text{with probability } 1-p \\ 0 & \text{with probability } p \end{cases} \]

where \( p \) is the drop rate (typically 0.9–0.99, i.e., dropping 90–99% of parameters). The rescaling by \( 1/(1-p) \) ensures \( \mathbb{E}[\tilde{\tau}_j] = \tau_j \), preserving the expected output.

This is analogous to dropout, but applied to the weight deltas rather than activations. The key insight is that fine-tuning changes are highly redundant — most individual parameter changes can be dropped without significantly affecting the model's behaviour.

MethodPreprocessingReduces interference?
Task ArithmeticNoneNo
TIES-MergingTrim + sign electionYes (deterministic)
DARERandom drop + rescaleYes (stochastic)

DARE can be combined with other merging methods (e.g., DARE + TIES, DARE + linear averaging). In practice, DARE with high drop rates (0.9+) works best for merging many models, as it dramatically reduces the chance of conflicting updates at any given parameter position.

title: Task Vector
summary: The difference between a fine-tuned model's weights and the base model's weights: \( \tau = \theta_{\text{ft}} - \theta_0 \). Task vectors encode the knowledge and skills learned during fine-tuning and can be arithmetically combined to t…

papers.citation: 2023 · Gabriel Ilharco et al. · Editing Models with Task Arithmetic

sections.body:
The difference between a fine-tuned model's weights and the base model's weights: \( \tau = \theta_{\text{ft}} - \theta_0 \). Task vectors encode the knowledge and skills learned during fine-tuning and can be arithmetically combined to transfer, compose, or negate capabilities.

Ilharco et al. (2023) showed that task vectors support intuitive arithmetic operations:

OperationFormulaEffect
Addition\( \theta_0 + \lambda\tau \)Apply a learned skill to the base model
Negation\( \theta_0 - \lambda\tau \)Remove a skill or behaviour
Composition\( \theta_0 + \lambda_1\tau_1 + \lambda_2\tau_2 \)Combine skills from multiple models
Analogy\( \theta_A + (\tau_B - \tau_A) \)Transfer a transformation from one domain to another

The scaling factor \( \lambda \in [0, 1] \) controls the strength of the task vector. Too large a \( \lambda \) degrades performance; the optimal value is typically found via grid search on a validation set.

Negation is particularly striking: subtracting a task vector fine-tuned on toxic text from a base model reduces the model's toxicity — the task vector effectively encodes "how to be toxic," and negating it removes that capability.

Task vectors are the foundation for TIES-Merging, DARE, and other advanced merging techniques. They work because fine-tuning updates occupy a low-dimensional subspace, making linear operations on them meaningful.

title: Model Compression
summary: A family of techniques that reduce the size, memory footprint, and computational cost of neural networks while preserving as much performance as possible. Compression enables deployment of large models on resource-constrained devices (mo…

papers.citation: Umbrella concept spanning decades: pruning (LeCun et al., 1990), distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), quantization (Jacob et al., 2018), lottery tickets (Frankle & Carlin, 2019).

sections.body:
A family of techniques that reduce the size, memory footprint, and computational cost of neural networks while preserving as much performance as possible. Compression enables deployment of large models on resource-constrained devices (mobile, edge) and reduces serving costs at scale.

The four principal compression techniques, each targeting a different axis of model cost:

TechniqueWhat it reducesMechanismTypical ratio
QuantizationBits per parameterLower-precision representation (FP16 → INT8 → INT4)2--4x memory
PruningNumber of parametersRemove unimportant weights or structures2--10x sparsity
Knowledge distillationArchitecture sizeTrain a small student to mimic a large teacher5--20x fewer params
Low-rank factorisationMatrix dimensionsDecompose \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \) into \( W \approx AB \) where \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times r}, B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times n}, r \ll \min(m,n) \)Variable

These techniques are frequently combined. A typical production pipeline: (1) distil a large teacher into a smaller student architecture, (2) quantize the student to INT8 or INT4, (3) optionally prune and fine-tune. Each stage compounds the compression.

The quality--compression trade-off is model- and task-dependent. Key empirical findings for LLMs:

  • INT8 quantization is nearly lossless for most models (Dettmers et al., LLM.int8(), 2022)
  • INT4 quantization (GPTQ, AWQ) achieves ~4x compression with 1--3% quality degradation on benchmarks
  • Distillation can transfer 80--90% of a large model's capability to a 10x smaller student (e.g., Phi-2 at 2.7B rivalling models 10x its size)
  • Structured pruning at 50% typically degrades quality more than equivalent-ratio quantization

Theoretical motivation — the lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carlin, 2019): dense networks contain sparse subnetworks ("winning tickets") that, when trained in isolation with the same initialisation, can match the full network's performance. This suggests that most parameters are redundant, justifying aggressive pruning. The stronger strong lottery ticket hypothesis claims that a sufficiently overparameterised random network already contains a subnetwork (without any training) that achieves good performance.

In the Transformer era, compression is essential: serving a 70B-parameter model in FP16 requires ~140 GB of memory, but INT4 quantization reduces this to ~35 GB — fitting on a single consumer GPU.

title: Knowledge Distillation
summary: A compression technique where a small "student" model is trained to reproduce the behaviour of a larger "teacher" model. The student learns from the teacher's soft probability distributions (which contain richer information than hard lab…

papers.citation: 2015 · Geoffrey Hinton, Oriol Vinyals, Jeff Dean · Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

sections.body:
A compression technique where a small "student" model is trained to reproduce the behaviour of a larger "teacher" model. The student learns from the teacher's soft probability distributions (which contain richer information than hard labels), enabling it to achieve performance closer to the teacher than training on ground truth alone.

The student is trained on a weighted combination of the standard hard-label loss and a distillation loss that matches the teacher's softened outputs:

\[ \mathcal{L} = (1-\alpha)\,\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(y, \hat{y}_s) \;+\; \alpha\,T^2\,D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\sigma\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{z}_t}{T}\right) \;\middle\|\; \sigma\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{z}_s}{T}\right)\right) \]

where:

  • \( \mathbf{z}_t, \mathbf{z}_s \) — teacher and student logits
  • \( T \) — temperature parameter (typically 2--20); higher \( T \) produces softer distributions that expose more inter-class structure
  • \( \sigma(\cdot / T) \) — softmax with temperature scaling
  • \( \alpha \) — interpolation weight between hard and soft losses (typically 0.5--0.9)
  • \( T^2 \) — compensates for the reduced gradient magnitude when logits are divided by \( T \); since \( \partial D_{\text{KL}} / \partial \mathbf{z}_s \) scales as \( 1/T^2 \), multiplying by \( T^2 \) restores the gradient to a comparable scale

Why soft targets help — "dark knowledge." A teacher's output like \([0.7, 0.2, 0.1]\) for classes [cat, dog, car] tells the student that cats look somewhat like dogs but not like cars. This inter-class similarity information is absent from the one-hot hard label \([1, 0, 0]\). Hinton called these implicit relationships "dark knowledge" because they are invisible in the labels but encoded in the teacher's outputs.

VariantWhat the student learns fromExample
Response-basedTeacher's output distribution (logits)Original KD (Hinton 2015)
Feature-basedTeacher's intermediate representationsFitNets (Romero et al., 2015)
Relation-basedRelationships between teacher's internal representations across samplesPKT (Passalis & Tefas, 2018)

Distillation in the LLM era. Knowledge distillation has become central to efficient LLM development:

  • Alpaca (2023): LLaMA-7B distilled from GPT-3.5 text-davinci-003 using 52K instruction-output pairs
  • Phi series (Microsoft): Small models (1.3B--2.7B) trained on curated data generated by larger teachers, achieving disproportionately strong performance
  • DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019): 40% smaller, 60% faster than BERT, retaining 97% of performance

The student can use a different architecture from the teacher — what matters is matching behaviour, not structure. For sequence models, distillation can also operate on intermediate token-level distributions or attention patterns, not just final outputs.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Why the \( T^2 \) factor is needed. The softmax with temperature is \( \sigma_i = e^{z_i/T} / \sum_j e^{z_j/T} \). Taking the gradient of the KL divergence with respect to a student logit \( z_s \):

\[ \frac{\partial D_{\text{KL}}}{\partial z_{s,i}} = \frac{1}{T}\left(\sigma\left(\frac{z_{s,i}}{T}\right) - \sigma\left(\frac{z_{t,i}}{T}\right)\right) \]

This gradient is \( O(1/T) \), so the loss itself scales as \( O(1/T^2) \) relative to the standard cross-entropy. Multiplying by \( T^2 \) restores the distillation gradient to a comparable magnitude as the hard-label gradient, keeping the two loss terms balanced regardless of \( T \).

title: Pruning
summary: A model compression technique that removes unnecessary weights or structures from a neural network to reduce its size and computational cost. Pruning exploits the observation that most parameters in over-parameterised networks contribute…

papers.citation: 1990 · LeCun, Denker, and Solla · Optimal Brain Damage
2023 · Frantar and Alistarh · SparseGPT: Massive Language Models Can be Accurately Pruned in One-Shot
2024 · Sun et al. · A Simple and Effective Pruning Approach for Large Language Models (Wanda)

sections.body: A model compression technique that removes unnecessary weights or structures from a neural network to reduce its size and computational cost. Pruning exploits the observation that most parameters in over-parameterised networks contribute little to performance and can be set to zero or removed entirely.

sections.title: Two main structural categories
sections.body:
TypeWhat's removedSpeedupTrade-off
UnstructuredIndividual weights → sparse matrixRequires sparse hardware/kernels (e.g., NVIDIA A100 structured sparsity)Can achieve 90%+ sparsity with minimal loss
StructuredEntire neurons, attention heads, layers, or channelsDirect speedup on standard hardware (smaller dense matrices)More quality loss at the same compression ratio

Pruning criteria determine which weights to remove. From simplest to most principled:

CriterionDecision ruleCost
MagnitudeRemove weights with smallest \( |w_{ij}| \)Negligible
Gradient-basedRemove weights with smallest \( |w_{ij} \cdot \nabla_{w_{ij}} \mathcal{L}| \)One backward pass
Second-order (OBD/OBS)Remove weights whose deletion least increases loss: \( \Delta\mathcal{L} \approx \frac{1}{2} \frac{w_{ij}^2}{[H^{-1}]_{ii}} \)Hessian computation
WandaScore = \( |w_{ij}| \cdot \|\mathbf{x}_j\|_2 \); prune lowest-scoringOne forward pass (calibration data)
SparseGPTIteratively prune + update remaining weights to compensate, using approximate inverse HessianOne forward pass + row-wise Hessian inverse

sections.title: Typical workflow — iterative pruning
sections.body:
1. Train the model to convergence
  2. Prune a fraction of weights (e.g., 20%)
  3. Fine-tune to recover lost accuracy
  4. Repeat steps 2--3 until target sparsity is reached

Iterative magnitude pruning can reach 90--95% sparsity on CNNs with minimal accuracy loss (Han et al., 2015). For LLMs, however, fine-tuning is prohibitively expensive, driving interest in one-shot methods like SparseGPT (Frantar & Alistarh, 2023) and Wanda (Sun et al., 2024) that prune without retraining.

The lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carlin, 2019) provides theoretical motivation: dense networks contain sparse subnetworks ("winning tickets") that, if trained from their original initialisation, match the full model's accuracy. This implies the dense model is overparameterised from the start and that the "right" sparse structure exists within it.

Connection to other compression methods: Pruning is complementary to quantization (prune then quantize for compounding savings) and can be viewed as the dual of knowledge distillation — pruning removes redundancy within a single model, while distillation transfers essential knowledge to a smaller architecture.

title: Structured Pruning
summary: A pruning approach that removes entire structural units — neurons, attention heads, channels, or layers — rather than individual weights. Structured pruning produces smaller dense models that run faster on standard hardware without requi…

papers.citation: 2019 · Michel et al. · Are Sixteen Heads Really Better than One?
2019 · Voita et al. · Analyzing Multi-Head Self-Attention: Specialized Heads Do the Heavy Lifting
2024 · Men et al. · ShortGPT layer pruning

sections.body:
A pruning approach that removes entire structural units — neurons, attention heads, channels, or layers — rather than individual weights. Structured pruning produces smaller dense models that run faster on standard hardware without requiring sparse matrix support.

Unlike unstructured pruning, which zeros out individual weights at arbitrary positions, structured pruning removes complete computational units. The resulting model is a standard dense model with smaller dimensions — no sparse kernels needed, and inference speedup is immediate on commodity GPUs.

sections.title: What can be pruned
sections.body:
StructureGranularityEffect
Neurons / channelsRemove rows/columns of weight matricesReduces width
Attention headsRemove entire heads from multi-head attentionReduces attention cost
LayersRemove entire Transformer blocksReduces depth
FFN intermediate dimShrink hidden dimension of MLP sub-layerReduces ~2/3 of layer params

Importance scoring. To decide which units to remove, a scoring function ranks each structure by its contribution to model quality. Common criteria include:

  • Magnitude-based: rank units by the \(\ell_1\) or \(\ell_2\) norm of their weight vectors, \(\| \mathbf{w}_i \|\). Cheap but ignores interactions between units.
  • Gradient / Taylor-based: estimate the increase in loss from removing unit \(i\) via a first-order Taylor expansion: \(\Delta \mathcal{L}_i \approx \left| \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_i} \cdot h_i \right|\), where \(h_i\) is the unit's output. This accounts for how much the unit is actually used during inference.
  • Fisher information: use \(I_i = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_i}\right)^2\right]\) to estimate sensitivity.

Advantage over unstructured pruning: The result is a smaller but regular dense model — standard matrix multiplications on GPUs with no special sparse kernels. This makes structured pruning practical for deployment on any hardware.

Disadvantage: Removing an entire unit is a coarser operation than zeroing individual weights. At the same compression ratio, structured pruning typically causes more quality degradation than unstructured pruning because the model has less flexibility in choosing what to discard.

LLM results. Michel et al. (2019) showed that in many Transformer models a large fraction of attention heads can be removed after training with minimal accuracy loss — in some cases only 20% of heads are truly important. Voita et al. (2019) confirmed this with an information-theoretic analysis. The "ShortGPT" approach (Men et al., 2024) showed that removing up to 25% of layers in some LLMs barely affects downstream metrics, suggesting significant redundancy in depth. After structured pruning, a brief fine-tuning phase (a few hundred steps) typically recovers most lost quality.

title: Unstructured Pruning
summary: A pruning approach that zeros out individual weights at arbitrary positions in the weight matrices, producing sparse matrices. Unstructured pruning achieves higher compression ratios than structured pruning at equivalent quality, but req…

papers.citation: 1990 · LeCun, Denker, and Solla · Optimal Brain Damage
2023 · Frantar and Alistarh · SparseGPT: Massive Language Models Can be Accurately Pruned in One-Shot

sections.body:
A pruning approach that zeros out individual weights at arbitrary positions in the weight matrices, producing sparse matrices. Unstructured pruning achieves higher compression ratios than structured pruning at equivalent quality, but requires sparse computation support for actual inference speedup.

The pruned weight matrix has entries set to zero based on an importance criterion:

\[ W'_{ij} = \begin{cases} W_{ij} & \text{if } |W_{ij}| > \tau \text{ (or other criterion)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

where \(\tau\) is a threshold, often set to achieve a target sparsity level \(s = \frac{\text{number of zeros}}{\text{total parameters}}\).

sections.title: Importance criteria
sections.body:
• Magnitude pruning (simplest): remove weights with smallest absolute value \(|W_{ij}|\). Assumes small weights contribute least.
  • Optimal Brain Damage (OBD) (LeCun et al., 1990): uses second-order information — approximates the loss increase from removing weight \(W_{ij}\) as \(\Delta \mathcal{L} \approx \frac{1}{2} H_{ii} W_{ij}^2\), where \(H_{ii}\) is the diagonal of the Hessian. Accounts for curvature, not just magnitude.
  • Optimal Brain Surgeon (OBS) (Hasselmo et al., 1993): extends OBD by using off-diagonal Hessian entries and adjusting remaining weights to compensate: \(\delta \mathbf{w} = -\frac{W_q}{[\mathbf{H}^{-1}]_{qq}} \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{e}_q\), yielding better results but at higher cost.
  • SparseGPT (Frantar & Alistarh, 2023): adapts OBS for LLMs by solving a layer-wise sparse regression problem efficiently, achieving 50–60% unstructured sparsity in one shot without retraining.

sections.title: Sparsity levels achievable
sections.body:
SparsityTypical Quality ImpactNotes
50%Negligible lossSafe for most models
80–90%Modest loss (recoverable with fine-tuning)Practical ceiling for many architectures
95%+Significant degradationRequires lottery-ticket-style techniques

sections.title: Hardware challenges
sections.body:
ChallengeIssue
GPU optimisationGPUs are optimised for dense matmul; random sparsity patterns cannot exploit SIMD parallelism
Irregular memory accessRandom sparsity patterns cause cache misses, negating theoretical FLOP savings
Storage overheadSparse formats (CSR, COO) require index arrays, partially offsetting compression gains

NVIDIA's Ampere and Hopper GPUs support 2:4 structured sparsity — exactly 2 zeros per group of 4 elements — a hardware-friendly compromise that achieves 2x speedup with dedicated Sparse Tensor Cores. This semi-structured pattern sits between fully unstructured and structured pruning, offering a practical middle ground for deployment.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

OBD loss approximation. Taylor-expand the loss around the current weights, assuming the model is at a local minimum (\(\nabla \mathcal{L} = 0\)):

\[ \Delta \mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w} + \delta\mathbf{w}) - \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) \approx \frac{1}{2}\, \delta\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{H}\, \delta\mathbf{w} \]

Setting a single weight \(W_q\) to zero means \(\delta w_q = -W_q\) and all other \(\delta w_i = 0\). Under the diagonal Hessian approximation:

\[ \Delta \mathcal{L}_q \approx \frac{1}{2} H_{qq} W_q^2 \]

Prune the weight with smallest \(\Delta \mathcal{L}_q\).

title: Quantization
summary: The process of reducing the numerical precision of model weights and/or activations (e.g., from 32-bit floating-point to 8-bit or 4-bit integers), decreasing model size and accelerating inference. Quantization is the most widely used LLM…

papers.citation: 2016 · Hubara et al. · Quantized Neural Networks
2023 · Frantar et al. · GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
2024 · Lin et al. · AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
2023 · Dettmers et al. · QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

sections.body: The process of reducing the numerical precision of model weights and/or activations (e.g., from 32-bit floating-point to 8-bit or 4-bit integers), decreasing model size and accelerating inference. Quantization is the most widely used LLM compression technique due to its simplicity, hardware support, and effectiveness.

Uniform quantization maps a floating-point value \( x \) to an integer grid:

\[ x_q = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{x - z_f}{s}\right), \quad \hat{x} = s \cdot x_q + z_f \]

where \( s = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^b - 1} \) is the scale factor, \( z_f \) is the zero point (ensuring real zero maps exactly to an integer), and \( b \) is the target bit width. The quantization error for any single value is bounded by \(\frac{s}{2}\).

Symmetric vs. asymmetric: symmetric quantization sets \(z_f = 0\) and maps \([-\alpha, \alpha]\) to \([-2^{b-1}, 2^{b-1}-1]\), simplifying computation. Asymmetric quantization uses a non-zero zero point to handle distributions not centered at zero (common for activations after ReLU).

PrecisionBits/paramSize (7B model)Quality
FP3232~28 GBBaseline
FP16 / BF1616~14 GBNear-lossless
INT88~7 GBMinimal loss
NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat)4~3.5 GBSmall loss
2-bit2~1.75 GBNoticeable degradation

sections.title: Two main approaches
sections.body:
• Post-training quantization (PTQ): Quantize a pre-trained model with no or minimal calibration data. Methods include GPTQ (second-order weight quantization), AWQ (activation-aware weight quantization), and GGUF (block-wise quantization used by llama.cpp). Fast but quality-limited at very low bit widths.
  • Quantization-aware training (QAT): Simulate quantization during training using fake quantize operations and the straight-through estimator for gradients. The model learns to be robust to quantization noise. Better quality but expensive — rarely used for full LLM training.

NF4 (NormalFloat4). Introduced by Dettmers et al. (2023) for QLoRA, NF4 is an information-theoretically optimal 4-bit data type for normally distributed weights. The 16 quantization levels are placed at quantiles of \(\mathcal{N}(0,1)\), ensuring each bin captures equal probability mass. This exploits the empirical observation that neural network weights are approximately Gaussian.

Block-wise quantization. Rather than using a single scale for an entire tensor, the weights are divided into blocks (e.g., 64 or 128 elements), each with its own scale factor. This reduces quantization error from outliers at the cost of storing one scale per block.

Quantization is essential for LLM deployment: a 70B model at FP16 requires ~140 GB VRAM (multiple expensive GPUs), but at 4-bit it fits on a single 48 GB GPU with modest quality loss (typically <0.5 perplexity points).

title: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)
summary: A quantization approach applied after training is complete, converting a pre-trained model's weights (and optionally activations) to lower precision without retraining. PTQ is fast and requires minimal data (a small calibration set) but…

papers.citation: 2023 · Frantar et al. · GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
2024 · Lin et al. · AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
2023 · Xiao et al. · SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models

sections.body:
A quantization approach applied after training is complete, converting a pre-trained model's weights (and optionally activations) to lower precision without retraining. PTQ is fast and requires minimal data (a small calibration set) but may degrade quality more than quantization-aware training at very low bit widths.

PTQ works by analysing the distribution of weights and activations on a calibration dataset to determine optimal quantization parameters. For uniform quantization with bit width \(b\):

\[ s = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^b - 1}, \quad z = \text{round}\!\left(-\frac{x_{\min}}{s}\right) \]

The goal is to minimise the layer-wise reconstruction error: for each layer with weight matrix \(\mathbf{W}\) and input activations \(\mathbf{X}\), find the quantized weights \(\hat{\mathbf{W}}\) that minimise:

\[ \min_{\hat{\mathbf{W}}} \left\| \mathbf{W}\mathbf{X} - \hat{\mathbf{W}}\mathbf{X} \right\|_2^2 \]

This layer-wise formulation makes PTQ tractable for large models — each layer is quantized independently using a small calibration set (typically 128–512 examples).

sections.title: Key PTQ methods for LLMs
sections.body:
MethodApproachBitsKey Idea
GPTQLayer-wise second-order quantization3–4Extends OBS: quantize one weight at a time, update remaining weights to compensate using Hessian \(\mathbf{H} = 2\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^\top\)
AWQActivation-aware weight quantization4Identifies salient weight channels (those multiplied by large activations) and scales them up before quantization to protect them
GGUF (llama.cpp)Block-wise quantization with mixed formats2–8Groups of 32–256 weights share one scale; supports many quant types (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_S, etc.)
SmoothQuantActivation-to-weight migrationW8A8Divides activation outliers by a per-channel factor \(s_j\) and multiplies weights by \(s_j\), smoothing the activation distribution for easier quantization

Weight-only vs. weight-and-activation quantization: Quantizing only weights (e.g., W4A16: 4-bit weights, 16-bit activations) is simpler because weight distributions are static and near-Gaussian. Activation quantization is harder because activations have dynamic ranges with outliers that vary per input — a few channels in Transformer models can have activations 100x larger than the median, causing severe quantization error if not handled (this is what SmoothQuant addresses).

Calibration. PTQ methods use a small calibration dataset (not for training, just to observe activation statistics). The calibration set should be representative of the deployment distribution. GPTQ uses ~128 sequences from C4 or WikiText; AWQ similarly. Results are not very sensitive to the specific calibration data.

PTQ dominates LLM quantization because retraining (QAT) is prohibitively expensive for models with tens of billions of parameters. A 70B model can be quantized to 4-bit in hours on a single GPU, with perplexity increase of only 0.1–0.5 points.

title: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
summary: A training approach that simulates the effect of quantization during the forward pass while maintaining full-precision weights for gradient computation. The model learns to be robust to quantization noise, resulting in better accuracy at…

papers.citation: 2018 · Benoit Jacob et al. · Quantization and Training of Neural Networks for Efficient Integer-Arithmetic-Only Inference

sections.body:
A training approach that simulates the effect of quantization during the forward pass while maintaining full-precision weights for gradient computation. The model learns to be robust to quantization noise, resulting in better accuracy at low bit widths compared to post-training quantization.

During the forward pass, weights pass through a fake quantize operation — quantize then immediately dequantize:

\[ \hat{W} = s \cdot \text{clamp}\!\left(\text{round}\!\left(\frac{W}{s}\right) + z,\; q_{\min},\; q_{\max}\right) - s \cdot z \]

The forward pass uses \( \hat{W} \) (which contains quantization noise), but the backward pass computes gradients with respect to the original full-precision \( W \) using the straight-through estimator (STE). The STE treats the non-differentiable rounding function as the identity for gradient purposes:

\[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} \approx \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{W}} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{q_{\min} \leq W/s \leq q_{\max}} \]

The indicator function clips gradients to zero for weights outside the quantization range, preventing the optimizer from pushing weights into regions that will be clamped anyway.

What the model learns during QAT: By experiencing quantization noise throughout training, the model shifts its weight distribution to be more quantization-friendly — weights cluster near quantization grid points, and the model learns to rely less on precise weight values. This is why QAT outperforms PTQ at low bit widths where quantization noise is large.

AspectPTQQAT
Training costNone (calibration only)Full or partial retraining
Quality at INT8Near-losslessNear-lossless
Quality at INT4Small lossMinimal loss
Quality at INT2Large degradationModerate degradation
When to useINT8+, or when retraining is too expensiveINT4 and below, or when maximum quality needed

QAT for LLMs. Full QAT is rarely applied to LLMs because the retraining cost is enormous. Instead, QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) offers an effective compromise: the base model is frozen in 4-bit NF4 precision, and only small LoRA adapter weights are trained in full precision. This combines the memory savings of quantization with the quality benefits of fine-tuning, making it possible to fine-tune a 65B model on a single 48 GB GPU. Strictly speaking QLoRA is quantized fine-tuning rather than pure QAT, but it captures the same principle of training while accounting for quantization effects.

Practical details: QAT typically requires 10–20% of the original training compute. The fake quantize operations are inserted at specific points in the computational graph (after each linear layer, and optionally after activations). Batch normalization folding must be handled carefully — the folded weights must be quantized as they will appear at inference time.

title: Preference-Based Alignment
summary: A class of methods that align language model behaviour with human preferences by training on pairwise comparisons (preferred vs. dispreferred responses) rather than absolute quality labels. This is the dominant paradigm for making LLMs h…

papers.citation: 2022 · Ouyang et al. · Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
2023 · Rafailov et al. · Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
2022 · Bai et al. · Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

sections.body:
A class of methods that align language model behaviour with human preferences by training on pairwise comparisons (preferred vs. dispreferred responses) rather than absolute quality labels. This is the dominant paradigm for making LLMs helpful, harmless, and honest after supervised fine-tuning.

The key insight behind preference-based alignment is that relative judgements are far more reliable than absolute ones: it is easier for a human annotator to say "response A is better than response B" than to assign a numerical quality score. All preference-based methods share a common data format — tuples \( (x, y_w, y_l) \) consisting of a prompt \(x\), a preferred (winning) response \(y_w\), and a dispreferred (losing) response \(y_l\).

The Bradley-Terry preference model. Most methods assume pairwise preferences follow the Bradley-Terry model, where the probability that response \(y_1\) is preferred over \(y_2\) depends on a latent reward function \(r^*(x, y)\):

\[ p(y_1 \succ y_2 \mid x) = \sigma\!\left(r^*(x, y_1) - r^*(x, y_2)\right) \]

where \(\sigma\) is the sigmoid function. This model underlies both RLHF reward model training and the DPO derivation.

sections.title: Two main approaches
sections.body:
ApproachPipelineObjectiveComplexity
RLHFTrain reward model \(R_\phi\) on preferences, then PPO to maximise \(R_\phi\)\(\max_\pi \mathbb{E}[R_\phi(x,y)] - \beta D_{\text{KL}}(\pi \| \pi_{\text{ref}})\)High — 3 models in memory
DPODirectly optimise policy from preference pairs, no reward model\(\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w|x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l|x)}\right)\right]\)Low — same as SFT

DPO derivation insight. Rafailov et al. showed that the optimal policy under the KL-constrained RLHF objective has a closed-form solution \(\pi^*(y|x) \propto \pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x) \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r^*(x,y)\right)\), which can be rearranged to express the reward in terms of the policy: \(r^*(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)} + C(x)\). Substituting this into the Bradley-Terry loss eliminates the reward model entirely, yielding the DPO loss above. This means DPO implicitly optimises the same objective as RLHF but without needing to train a separate reward model or run RL.

sections.title: Data collection pipeline
sections.body:
1. Sample \(k\) responses from the current policy (or a mix of policies) for each prompt
  2. Human annotators (or an AI judge) rank or compare response pairs
  3. Construct preference pairs \((x, y_w, y_l)\) from the rankings

The \(\beta\) parameter controls how far the aligned policy can drift from the reference policy. Low \(\beta\) allows large deviations (more alignment, risk of reward hacking); high \(\beta\) keeps the model close to its SFT baseline (conservative, may under-align). Typical values range from 0.1 to 0.5.

sections.title: Beyond RLHF and DPO
sections.body:
• IPO (Azar et al., 2023): replaces the sigmoid loss with a squared loss to avoid overfitting to deterministic preferences
  • KTO (Ethayarajh et al., 2024): uses only per-example thumbs-up/down labels rather than pairwise comparisons — easier to collect
  • RLAIF / Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022): uses a stronger AI model to generate preference labels, scaling data cheaply
  • Iterative DPO / online DPO: generates fresh responses from the current policy each round, preventing the staleness problem of offline preference data

Preference-based alignment sits after SFT in the standard LLM training pipeline (pretraining → SFT → alignment) and is responsible for many qualities that make modern assistants useful: precise instruction following, refusal of harmful requests, calibrated uncertainty, and balanced perspectives.

title: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)
summary: A method for aligning language models with human preferences by first training a reward model on human comparisons, then using reinforcement learning (PPO) to optimise the language model to maximise the learned reward while staying close…

papers.citation: 2022 · Long Ouyang et al. · Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback (InstructGPT)

sections.body:
A method for aligning language models with human preferences by first training a reward model on human comparisons, then using reinforcement learning (PPO) to optimise the language model to maximise the learned reward while staying close to the SFT policy. RLHF was the technique behind InstructGPT and ChatGPT.

The RLHF pipeline has three stages:

Stage 1 — Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): Fine-tune the base pretrained model on high-quality demonstration data (prompt, ideal response) to produce a policy \(\pi_{\text{SFT}}\) that can follow instructions. This model serves as the starting point and the KL anchor (reference policy).

Stage 2 — Reward model training: Collect pairwise human preferences: for each prompt \(x\), annotators compare two model-generated responses and label one as preferred (\(y_w\)) over the other (\(y_l\)). Train a reward model \(R_\phi(x, y)\) — typically the SFT model with a scalar head replacing the language modelling head — using the Bradley-Terry loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}_R = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(R_\phi(x, y_w) - R_\phi(x, y_l)\right)\right] \]

where \(\sigma\) is the sigmoid function. This loss maximises the probability that the reward model scores the preferred response higher.

Stage 3 — RL optimisation (PPO): Use Proximal Policy Optimization to maximise the reward while staying close to the reference policy:

\[ \max_\pi \;\mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D},\, y \sim \pi(\cdot|x)}\!\left[R_\phi(x, y)\right] - \beta\, D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(\pi(\cdot|x) \;\|\; \pi_{\text{SFT}}(\cdot|x)\right) \]

The KL divergence penalty is critical: without it, the policy collapses to degenerate outputs that exploit weaknesses in the reward model ("reward hacking"). In practice, the KL term is computed token-by-token: \(D_{\text{KL}} = \sum_t \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_t|x, y_{1:t-1})}{\pi_{\text{SFT}}(y_t|x, y_{1:t-1})}\).

sections.title: Models in memory during Stage 3
sections.body:
ComponentModelTrainable?Purpose
Policy \(\pi_\theta\)LLM being trainedYesGenerate responses
Value function \(V_\psi\)Critic (often shares backbone with policy)YesEstimate expected return for PPO advantage
Reward model \(R_\phi\)Frozen from Stage 2NoScore generated responses
Reference policy \(\pi_{\text{SFT}}\)Frozen copy of SFT modelNoKL anchor to prevent drift

Having up to four models in memory simultaneously (though the critic often shares the policy backbone) is the main practical difficulty of RLHF — it roughly doubles the GPU memory compared to SFT.

PPO specifics for language models. Each "episode" consists of generating a response \(y\) to a prompt \(x\). The reward is given only at the end of generation (sparse reward). The advantage is estimated using Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE). The PPO clipping objective prevents destructively large policy updates:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{PPO}} = \mathbb{E}_t\!\left[\min\!\left(\frac{\pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)}{\pi_{\text{old}}(a_t|s_t)} \hat{A}_t,\; \text{clip}\!\left(\frac{\pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)}{\pi_{\text{old}}(a_t|s_t)},\, 1-\epsilon,\, 1+\epsilon\right) \hat{A}_t\right)\right] \]

where \(\hat{A}_t\) is the GAE advantage estimate and \(\epsilon \approx 0.2\) is the clipping parameter.

sections.title: Known issues
sections.body:
• Reward hacking: the policy finds outputs scoring highly on \(R_\phi\) that are not genuinely good, exploiting distribution shift
  • Reward overoptimisation: Gao et al. (2023) showed that beyond a certain KL budget, further RL training decreases true quality while continuing to increase proxy reward
  • Training instability: PPO requires careful hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, KL coefficient \(\beta\), number of PPO epochs)

These difficulties motivated simpler alternatives like DPO, which collapses the reward model and RL stages into a single supervised loss.

title: Reward Model
summary: A model trained to predict human preferences by assigning a scalar score to (prompt, response) pairs. The reward model serves as a proxy for human judgement in RLHF, providing the training signal that guides the language model toward pre…

papers.citation: 2020 · Stiennon et al. · Learning to Summarize with Human Feedback
2023 · Gao et al. · Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization

sections.body: A model trained to predict human preferences by assigning a scalar score to (prompt, response) pairs. The reward model serves as a proxy for human judgement in RLHF, providing the training signal that guides the language model toward preferred behaviour.

Architecture. The reward model \(R_\phi(x, y)\) is typically a Transformer initialised from the SFT model checkpoint, with the language modelling head replaced by a linear projection to a single scalar. The input is the concatenation of prompt \(x\) and response \(y\); the scalar reward is read from the final token's hidden state:

\[ R_\phi(x, y) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{h}_T + b \]

where \(\mathbf{h}_T\) is the last-token hidden representation, \(\mathbf{w}\) and \(b\) are learnable parameters of the reward head.

Training objective. Given preference data \((x, y_w, y_l)\), the reward model is trained using the Bradley-Terry pairwise loss:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(R_\phi(x, y_w) - R_\phi(x, y_l)\right)\right] \]

This loss drives the reward model to assign higher scores to preferred responses. Note that only the difference in rewards matters — the absolute scale is arbitrary, and a constant can be added to all rewards without changing the loss. This is why reward models cannot be meaningfully compared across training runs.

Reward model accuracy. In practice, reward models achieve roughly 65–75% agreement with held-out human preferences (InstructGPT reported ~72%). Perfect agreement is impossible due to inherent annotator disagreement — inter-annotator agreement on preference tasks is typically 70–80%.

sections.title: Challenges
sections.body:
ChallengeDescriptionMitigation
Reward hackingPolicy exploits reward model weaknesses — generates outputs that score highly but are not genuinely goodKL penalty against reference policy; reward model ensembles
OveroptimisationGao et al. (2023) showed proxy reward can increase while true quality decreases beyond a KL threshold: \(R_{\text{true}} \approx \alpha \sqrt{D_{\text{KL}}} - \beta D_{\text{KL}}\)Careful KL budget; early stopping
Distribution shiftReward model trained on SFT-policy outputs must generalise to RL-policy outputs, which drift during trainingIterative reward model retraining; conservative KL
Annotation noiseHuman preferences are noisy (70–80% inter-annotator agreement), limiting ceiling accuracyMajority voting; better annotation guidelines

sections.title: Uses beyond RLHF training
sections.body:
• Best-of-N sampling (rejection sampling): generate \(N\) candidate responses and select the one with highest reward. This achieves quality improvements comparable to PPO with no training, but at \(N\times\) inference cost. Stiennon et al. showed best-of-64 nearly matches PPO quality.
  • Data filtering: score training data and discard low-reward examples before SFT.
  • Evaluation: use as an automatic evaluator for model quality (though subject to the same biases).

DPO and related methods bypass the explicit reward model by showing that the optimal reward can be recovered from the policy itself: \(r^*(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)} + C(x)\). However, explicit reward models remain useful for best-of-N sampling and as evaluators.

title: Self-Critique
summary: A technique where a language model evaluates and refines its own outputs by generating critiques of its initial response and then producing an improved version. Self-critique enables iterative refinement without external human feedback,…

papers.citation: 2022 · Bai et al. · Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
2023 · Madaan et al. · Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback
2023 · Shinn et al. · Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

sections.body: A technique where a language model evaluates and refines its own outputs by generating critiques of its initial response and then producing an improved version. Self-critique enables iterative refinement without external human feedback, leveraging the model's own capabilities as a judge.

The generate-critique-revise loop. Self-critique follows a structured multi-turn process:

  1. Generate: The model produces an initial response \(y_0\) to prompt \(x\)
  2. Critique: The model receives \((x, y_0)\) with an instruction like "Identify any errors, omissions, or areas for improvement" and generates a critique \(c\)
  3. Revise: The model receives \((x, y_0, c)\) and generates a revised response \(y_1\)
  4. Optionally iterate: \(y_1 \to c_1 \to y_2 \to \ldots\) for \(k\) rounds

This can be viewed as a form of iterated best response: each revision is conditioned on the critique of the previous attempt, creating an improving sequence. Empirically, most gains come in the first 1–2 rounds; further iterations show diminishing returns.

sections.title: Key methods using self-critique
sections.body:
MethodCritique mechanismUse of critique output
Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022)Critique against explicit constitutional principlesRevised responses become SFT training data; AI preferences become RL signal
Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023)Open-ended self-feedback on quality dimensionsDirectly serves the improved response to the user (inference-time only)
Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023)Verbal reinforcement — the model reflects on failed attemptsReflections stored in memory for future attempts at the same task
CriticGPT (OpenAI, 2024)Dedicated critic model trained to find bugs in codeAssists human reviewers in RLHF data collection

Self-critique for training data (Constitutional AI). In the supervised phase of Constitutional AI, self-critique is used at scale to generate improved training data. Starting from a potentially harmful response, the model critiques it against a constitutional principle (e.g., "Choose the response that is most respectful") and then revises. The revised responses form SFT training pairs. This is more scalable than collecting human demonstrations because the model generates both the critique and the revision.

Self-critique at inference time (Self-Refine). Madaan et al. showed that GPT-4 outputs improve by 5–20% across diverse tasks (dialogue, code, math reasoning) after just one round of self-feedback plus revision, with no additional training. The improvement is measured by task-specific metrics and is robust across domains. This makes self-critique a lightweight way to improve outputs without any model modification.

sections.title: Limitations and failure modes
sections.body:
• Sycophantic self-critique: models may generate superficially critical but vacuous feedback ("I should have been more thorough") that does not identify real errors
  • Blind spots: a model cannot reliably catch errors it is confident about — particularly factual hallucinations, since the same knowledge gap that caused the error prevents the model from recognising it during critique
  • Capability ceiling: Huang et al. (2024) showed that self-critique cannot reliably improve reasoning beyond the model's own capability level without external verification (e.g., a code interpreter or search engine)
  • Critique quality scales with model size: smaller models generate less useful critiques, so the technique is most effective with frontier models
