Self-critique relates to several broader ideas: chain-of-thought prompting (externalizing reasoning), ensemble methods (multiple passes improve quality), and RLHF (the model acts as its own annotator). It is increasingly used as a component in agentic systems where models must verify and correct their own outputs across multi-step tasks.

title: Constitutional AI
summary: An alignment method developed by Anthropic that uses a set of written principles (a "constitution") to guide model self-critique and revision, replacing much of the human feedback in RLHF with AI-generated feedback (RLAIF). The model lea…

papers.citation: 2022 · Yuntao Bai et al. (Anthropic) · Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

sections.body: An alignment method developed by Anthropic that uses a set of written principles (a "constitution") to guide model self-critique and revision, replacing much of the human feedback in RLHF with AI-generated feedback (RLAIF). The model learns to be helpful, harmless, and honest through two phases: supervised critique-and-revision, then RL against an AI preference model.

Motivation. Standard RLHF requires large volumes of human preference annotations, which are expensive and require annotators to evaluate harmful content. Constitutional AI reduces this burden by having the model itself generate feedback, guided by explicit principles that can be inspected, debated, and updated without recollecting data.

Phase 1 — Supervised critique and revision (SL-CAI). This phase generates improved training data through self-critique:

  1. Sample a potentially harmful prompt and generate a response (possibly by using a helpful-only model that does not refuse)
  2. Present the (prompt, response) pair to the model along with a randomly sampled constitutional principle
  3. The model critiques its response against the principle: "Identify specific ways in which the response is harmful, unethical, or unhelpful according to this principle"
  4. The model revises the response to address the critique
  5. Repeat with different principles for multiple rounds of critique-revision
  6. Fine-tune a new model on the final revised responses (SL-CAI model)

Phase 2 — Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF). This phase mirrors RLHF but replaces human annotators with the AI model:

  1. Generate pairs of responses from the SL-CAI model
  2. Ask a model (guided by constitutional principles) to choose the better response — this creates AI-labelled preference data
  3. Train a reward model \(R_\phi\) on these AI preferences using the standard Bradley-Terry loss: \[ \mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(R_\phi(x, y_w) - R_\phi(x, y_l)\right)\right] \]
  4. Run PPO against \(R_\phi\) with a KL penalty: \(\max_\pi \mathbb{E}[R_\phi(x,y)] - \beta D_{\text{KL}}(\pi \| \pi_{\text{SL-CAI}})\)

sections.title: Example constitutional principles
sections.body:
PrincipleTarget behaviour
"Choose the response that is least likely to be used for harmful purposes"Safety
"Choose the response that is most respectful of everyone's autonomy"Ethical respect
"Choose the response that is most honest and doesn't include false claims"Truthfulness
"Choose the response that best answers the question directly"Helpfulness

sections.title: Key advantages over standard RLHF
sections.body:
• Scalability: AI feedback is orders of magnitude cheaper than human annotations, enabling larger preference datasets
  • Transparency: the constitution is an explicit document that can be audited, versioned, and debated — unlike implicit preferences encoded in human annotations
  • Reduced red-teaming burden: humans do not need to write or evaluate harmful content, as the AI handles toxic text in the self-critique loop
  • Modularity: principles can be added, removed, or reweighted without recollecting preference data

Results. Bai et al. found that Constitutional AI models were simultaneously more helpful and more harmless than RLHF-only baselines — the two objectives are not purely in tension when principles guide alignment. The RLAIF preference model was nearly as accurate as one trained on human preferences, supporting the viability of AI-generated feedback.

Connections. Constitutional AI formalises the self-critique loop (see Self-Critique card) into a training pipeline. It introduced the RLAIF paradigm that has since been adopted more broadly — e.g., in synthetic data generation where stronger models label data for weaker ones. The principle-based approach also influenced later work on "system prompts" and "rules" that steer model behaviour at inference time.

title: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)
summary: An alignment method that optimises a language model directly from preference data without training a separate reward model or using reinforcement learning. DPO reformulates the RLHF objective into a simple classification-like loss on pre…

papers.citation: 2023 · Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell et al. · Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

sections.body:
An alignment method that optimises a language model directly from preference data without training a separate reward model or using reinforcement learning. DPO reformulates the RLHF objective into a simple classification-like loss on preference pairs, making it as easy to implement as supervised fine-tuning.

Standard RLHF involves three stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT), (2) reward model training on human preference pairs, and (3) RL optimisation (typically PPO) of the policy against the reward model with a KL penalty to stay close to the SFT model. DPO eliminates stages 2 and 3 by showing that the optimal policy under the KL-constrained reward maximisation objective has a closed-form relationship to the reward function.

The RLHF objective maximises:

\[ \max_{\pi_\theta} \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D},\, y \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot|x)}\!\left[r(x, y)\right] - \beta\, D_{\text{KL}}\!\left[\pi_\theta(y|x) \| \pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)\right] \]

The optimal policy satisfying this is:

\[ \pi^*(y|x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)}\, \pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x) \exp\!\left(\frac{r(x,y)}{\beta}\right) \]

Rearranging for the reward and substituting into the Bradley-Terry preference model \( P(y_w \succ y_l | x) = \sigma(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)) \) gives the DPO loss:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\!\left[\log \sigma\!\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w|x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l|x)}\right)\right] \]

where \( \pi_\theta \) is the policy being trained, \( \pi_{\text{ref}} \) is the frozen reference policy (typically the SFT model), \( \beta \) controls how far the policy can deviate from the reference (typically 0.1–0.5), \( y_w \) is the preferred response, and \( y_l \) is the dispreferred response.

The implicit reward recovered by DPO is:

\[ r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)} + \beta \log Z(x) \]

Since the partition function \( Z(x) \) cancels in the preference model, it never needs to be computed.

PropertyRLHF (PPO)DPO
Reward modelExplicit (separate model)Implicit (in the policy)
RL optimisationPPO (complex, unstable)None (standard gradient descent)
Models in memory4 (policy, ref, reward, value)2 (policy, reference)
HyperparametersMany (PPO clip, GAE \(\lambda\), etc.)Few (\(\beta\), learning rate)
ImplementationComplex (~1000 LoC)~10 lines of loss code

Gradient dynamics: The DPO gradient increases the relative probability of preferred responses and decreases dispreferred ones, weighted by how "wrong" the current model is. When the model already strongly prefers \( y_w \), the gradient is small — a natural curriculum effect.

sections.title: Variants and extensions
sections.body:
• IPO (Identity Preference Optimisation): replaces log-sigmoid with a squared loss to avoid overfitting to noisy preferences
  • KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimisation): works with only binary good/bad labels, not requiring paired comparisons
  • ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimisation): folds preference optimisation into SFT, eliminating the reference model entirely
  • SimPO: uses length-normalised log-probabilities as the implicit reward, removing the need for a reference model

Limitations: DPO can suffer from distribution shift — the policy trains on preference pairs generated by a different model. It also inherits any biases in the preference data and may overfit to surface patterns in preferred responses. Some work (e.g., online DPO / iterative DPO) addresses this by generating new responses with the current policy during training.

Derivations

Starting from the KL-constrained reward maximisation objective, the optimal policy is found by writing out the KL divergence and solving the variational problem:

\[ \pi^*(y|x) = \arg\max_\pi \; \mathbb{E}_\pi[r(x,y)] - \beta\, D_{\text{KL}}[\pi \| \pi_{\text{ref}}] \]

The Lagrangian gives:

\[ \pi^*(y|x) \propto \pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x) \exp\!\left(\frac{r(x,y)}{\beta}\right) \]

Solving for \( r \):

\[ r(x,y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y|x)} + \beta \log Z(x) \]

Substituting into Bradley-Terry \( P(y_w \succ y_l) = \sigma(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l)) \), the \( Z(x) \) terms cancel, and replacing \( \pi^* \) with the trainable \( \pi_\theta \) yields the DPO loss.

Python 
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def dpo_loss(pi_logps_w, pi_logps_l, ref_logps_w, ref_logps_l, beta=0.1):
    """Compute DPO loss given log-probs of preferred/dispreferred responses."""
    pi_logratios = pi_logps_w - pi_logps_l
    ref_logratios = ref_logps_w - ref_logps_l
    logits = beta * (pi_logratios - ref_logratios)
    return -F.logsigmoid(logits).mean()

title: Bradley-Terry Model
summary: A probabilistic model for pairwise comparisons that expresses the probability of one item being preferred over another as a function of their latent "strength" scores. It is the mathematical foundation of reward model training in RLHF an…

papers.citation: 1952 · Ralph Bradley, Milton Terry · Rank Analysis of Incomplete Block Designs: I. The Method of Paired Comparisons

sections.body:
A probabilistic model for pairwise comparisons that expresses the probability of one item being preferred over another as a function of their latent "strength" scores. It is the mathematical foundation of reward model training in RLHF and DPO, and underpins Elo ratings and the Chatbot Arena leaderboard.

Given two items \( i \) and \( j \) with latent strengths \( s_i \) and \( s_j \), the probability that \( i \) is preferred over \( j \) is:

\[ P(i \succ j) = \frac{e^{s_i}}{e^{s_i} + e^{s_j}} = \sigma(s_i - s_j) \]

where \( \sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z}) \) is the logistic sigmoid. This is equivalent to modelling the preference as a logistic regression on the score difference — only the difference \( s_i - s_j \) matters, so the scores are identifiable only up to a constant.

Maximum likelihood estimation: Given a dataset of \( N \) pairwise comparisons \( \{(w_k, l_k)\}_{k=1}^N \) where \( w_k \) beats \( l_k \), the log-likelihood is:

\[ \ell(\mathbf{s}) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \log \sigma(s_{w_k} - s_{l_k}) \]

This is concave in \( \mathbf{s} \), so gradient ascent or Newton's method finds the global optimum. The gradient for item \( i \) sums over all comparisons involving \( i \):

\[ \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial s_i} = \sum_{k:\, w_k = i} \left(1 - \sigma(s_i - s_{l_k})\right) - \sum_{k:\, l_k = i} \sigma(s_{w_k} - s_i) \]

In the RLHF context, the strength scores are parameterised by a neural network reward model \( R_\phi(x, y) \), and the preference probability becomes:

\[ P(y_w \succ y_l \mid x) = \sigma(R_\phi(x, y_w) - R_\phi(x, y_l)) \]

The reward model is trained to maximise the log-likelihood of observed preferences under this model. DPO goes further by substituting the closed-form optimal policy into the Bradley-Terry model, eliminating the need for an explicit reward model entirely.

ApplicationItems comparedStrength score
RLHF reward modellingLLM responsesReward \( R_\phi(x, y) \)
Chess / Elo ratingPlayersElo rating (with base-10 scaling)
Chatbot ArenaLLMsArena Elo score
Recommendation systemsProducts / contentLatent preference score

Connection to Elo: The Elo system is exactly the Bradley-Terry model with a base-10 logistic function and a conventional 400-point scale: \( P(A \text{ beats } B) = 1/(1 + 10^{(R_B - R_A)/400}) \). Setting \( s_i = R_i \cdot \ln 10 / 400 \) recovers the standard Bradley-Terry form.

sections.title: Assumptions and limitations
sections.body:
• Transitivity: If A > B and B > C, then A > C. Human preferences often violate this.
  • Independence of irrelevant alternatives: The relative ranking of A and B doesn't depend on C. Also commonly violated.
  • Single latent dimension: All variation is captured by one scalar per item. An LLM might be strong at coding but weak at creative writing — one score cannot capture this.

Extensions such as the Thurstone-Mosteller model (using a Gaussian rather than logistic link) and multi-dimensional Bradley-Terry models address some of these limitations.

Derivations

The Bradley-Terry model can be motivated from a random utility perspective. Suppose each item has a perceived quality \( U_i = s_i + \epsilon_i \) where \( \epsilon_i \) are i.i.d. Gumbel-distributed. Then:

\[ P(i \succ j) = P(U_i > U_j) = P(\epsilon_j - \epsilon_i < s_i - s_j) \]

Since the difference of two Gumbel random variables follows a logistic distribution:

\[ P(i \succ j) = \sigma(s_i - s_j) = \frac{e^{s_i}}{e^{s_i} + e^{s_j}} \]

This is the exact Bradley-Terry formula. The Gumbel assumption also connects to the Plackett-Luce model for ranking more than two items.

title: Pairwise Comparison
summary: An evaluation methodology where a human annotator (or model) is shown two items and asked which is better, rather than rating each item independently. Pairwise comparisons are more reliable than absolute ratings and are the standard data…

papers.citation: 1927 · L. L. Thurstone · A Law of Comparative Judgment
2022 · Ouyang et al. · Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback

sections.body:
An evaluation methodology where a human annotator (or model) is shown two items and asked which is better, rather than rating each item independently. Pairwise comparisons are more reliable than absolute ratings and are the standard data collection method for preference-based alignment of language models.

A pairwise comparison datum is a tuple \( (x, y_w, y_l) \): a prompt \( x \), the preferred (winning) response \( y_w \), and the dispreferred (losing) response \( y_l \). From a set of such tuples, a reward model or policy can be trained to reflect human preferences.

sections.title: Why pairwise comparisons are preferred over absolute ratings
sections.body:
• Lower cognitive load: "Which is better?" is easier and faster than "Rate this 1–10"
  • Higher inter-annotator agreement: Relative judgements are more consistent across raters. Cohen's kappa for pairwise comparisons is typically 0.6–0.8, compared with 0.3–0.5 for Likert scales in subjective tasks.
  • Robust to scale bias: Different annotators calibrate rating scales differently; pairwise comparisons avoid this entirely
  • Principled aggregation: Pairwise data naturally fits the Bradley-Terry model, enabling statistically grounded global rankings
Evaluation typeQuestion posedUsed inPros / Cons
Pairwise comparison"Which response is better?"RLHF, DPO, Chatbot ArenaHigh agreement; \(O(n^2)\) pairs for \(n\) items
Likert scale"Rate this 1–5"Survey-style annotationCheap; low agreement, scale bias
Best-of-N"Which of these N is best?"Multi-response evaluationEfficient; only identifies the winner
Ranked list"Order these by quality"Listwise evaluationInformation-rich; cognitively expensive

Scaling challenge: For \( n \) items, there are \( \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \) possible pairs. Comparing all pairs is impractical at scale, so efficient sampling strategies are used — the Chatbot Arena uses random matchmaking, while active-learning approaches like uncertainty sampling prioritise the most informative pairs.

From pairwise data to global rankings: Pairwise comparisons are aggregated into global orderings via the Bradley-Terry model, Elo ratings, or Thurstone-Case V scaling. These fit a latent strength score to each item that best explains the observed win/loss pattern.

Annotation quality: In the InstructGPT paper (Ouyang et al., 2022), annotators compared pairs of model outputs and achieved inter-annotator agreement of ~73%. Preference data is often noisy, motivating robust loss functions like IPO that are less sensitive to label noise.

title: Ranking
summary: The task of ordering a set of items by relevance, quality, or preference. In ML, ranking appears in information retrieval (ordering search results), recommendation systems, and LLM evaluation (ordering models by capability). Learning-to-…

papers.citation: 2009 · Tie-Yan Liu · Learning to Rank for Information Retrieval
2005 · Burges et al. · Learning to Rank using Gradient Descent

sections.body: The task of ordering a set of items by relevance, quality, or preference. In ML, ranking appears in information retrieval (ordering search results), recommendation systems, and LLM evaluation (ordering models by capability). Learning-to-rank methods train models to produce orderings that match human judgements.

sections.title: Learning-to-rank approaches
sections.body:
ApproachInputLoss functionExample
PointwiseIndividual items with relevance scoresRegression / classificationPRank, OC-SVM
PairwisePairs of items with preferenceCorrectly order each pairRankNet, LambdaRank
ListwiseEntire ranked listsDirectly optimise list-level metricsListNet, LambdaMART

RankNet loss (pairwise): Given predicted scores \( s_i, s_j \) for items where \( i \) should rank above \( j \):

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{RankNet}} = -\sum_{(i,j):\, y_i > y_j} \log \sigma(s_i - s_j) \]

This is exactly the Bradley-Terry log-likelihood applied to learned scores.

LambdaRank improves on RankNet by weighting each pair's gradient by the change in NDCG that swapping the pair would produce: \( |\Delta \text{NDCG}_{ij}| \). This directly optimises the non-differentiable ranking metric through gradient modification.

NDCG (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain) measures ranking quality with position-weighted relevance:

\[ \text{DCG}@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{2^{r_i} - 1}{\log_2(i+1)}, \quad \text{NDCG}@k = \frac{\text{DCG}@k}{\text{IDCG}@k} \]

where \( r_i \) is the relevance of the item at position \( i \) and IDCG is the DCG of the ideal ranking. NDCG ranges from 0 to 1.

MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) focuses on the first relevant result:

\[ \text{MRR} = \frac{1}{|Q|} \sum_{q=1}^{|Q|} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_q} \]
MAP (Mean Average Precision) averages precision at each relevant position across queries.

sections.title: Ranking in modern LLM systems
sections.body:
• Reward model training: Learning to rank responses by quality using pairwise preference data (Bradley-Terry)
  • RAG re-ranking: Cross-encoder re-rankers (e.g., Cohere Rerank, BGE) score query-document pairs and reorder initial BM25/embedding retrieval results
  • Model evaluation: Chatbot Arena ranks LLMs from crowd-sourced pairwise votes using Elo/Bradley-Terry
  • Best-of-N sampling: Generate N responses, rank by reward model score, return the top-ranked response

title: Elo Rating
summary: A rating system originally designed for chess that computes relative skill levels from pairwise match outcomes. In ML, Elo ratings are used to rank language models based on human preference votes, most notably in the LMSYS Chatbot Arena…

papers.citation: 1960 · Arpad Elo · The Rating of Chess Players, Past and Present
2023 · Zheng et al. · Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

sections.body:
A rating system originally designed for chess that computes relative skill levels from pairwise match outcomes. In ML, Elo ratings are used to rank language models based on human preference votes, most notably in the LMSYS Chatbot Arena leaderboard.

The expected score of player \( A \) against player \( B \):

\[ E_A = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(R_B - R_A)/400}} \]

where \( R_A \) and \( R_B \) are the current ratings. The base-10 exponential with the 400-point scale is a historical convention from chess — it means a 400-point advantage corresponds to a 10:1 expected win ratio.

After a match, ratings are updated:

\[ R_A' = R_A + K(S_A - E_A) \]

where \( S_A \) is the actual outcome (1 for win, 0.5 for draw, 0 for loss), \( E_A \) is the expected outcome, and \( K \) is the update magnitude (higher \( K \) means faster adaptation but more volatility). FIDE chess uses \( K = 40 \) for new players and \( K = 10 \) for established players.

Connection to Bradley-Terry: The Elo system is mathematically equivalent to the Bradley-Terry model with a base-10 logistic link. Setting \( s_i = R_i \cdot \ln 10 / 400 \) converts Elo ratings to Bradley-Terry strength parameters, giving the standard form \( P(i \succ j) = \sigma(s_i - s_j) \).

ApplicationPlayersMatch typeTypical K
FIDE ChessHuman playersGames10–40
Chatbot ArenaLLMsHuman preference votesMLE fit (not online)
Game AI (e.g., AlphaGo)AI agentsGame outcomesVaries

Online vs. batch estimation: The classical Elo update rule is an online algorithm — ratings change after each match. The Chatbot Arena instead uses batch maximum-likelihood estimation of Bradley-Terry scores on all votes simultaneously, which is more statistically efficient. They report "Elo" scores for familiarity, but the actual computation is batch MLE with bootstrap confidence intervals.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.body:
• Single latent dimension: Elo assumes one scalar captures all of a player's (or model's) ability. An LLM might excel at code but underperform at creative writing — one number cannot express this. The Chatbot Arena addresses this with category-specific Elo breakdowns.
  • Order dependence: Online Elo ratings depend on the sequence of matches; batch MLE avoids this.
  • Convergence: Requires many matches for stable estimates; bootstrap resampling provides confidence intervals.
  • Intransitivity: Like Bradley-Terry, Elo assumes transitive preferences, which can be violated in practice.
Derivations

The Elo expected score formula can be derived from the Bradley-Terry model by choosing a base-10 logistic function with scale parameter 400:

\[ P(A \succ B) = \frac{10^{R_A/400}}{10^{R_A/400} + 10^{R_B/400}} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(R_B - R_A)/400}} \]

The update rule \( R_A' = R_A + K(S_A - E_A) \) is a stochastic gradient ascent step on the Bradley-Terry log-likelihood with step size \( K \). The gradient of \( \log P(A \succ B) \) with respect to \( R_A \) is proportional to \( S_A - E_A \), the prediction error.

title: Pairwise Ranking
summary: A ranking approach that constructs a global ordering from individual pairwise preference judgements between items. Each comparison provides a local constraint ("A is better than B"), and these constraints are aggregated into a full ranki…

papers.citation: 1785 · Marquis de Condorcet · Essai sur l'application de l'analyse a la probabilite des decisions rendues a la pluralite des voix
2005 · Burges et al. · Learning to Rank using Gradient Descent

sections.body:
A ranking approach that constructs a global ordering from individual pairwise preference judgements between items. Each comparison provides a local constraint ("A is better than B"), and these constraints are aggregated into a full ranking using probabilistic models like Bradley-Terry or Elo-style updates.

Given \( n \) items, there are \( \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \) possible pairs. Comparing all pairs is often impractical, so systems sample a subset and rely on the aggregation model to infer the complete ranking.

Formal setup: For each observed pair \( (i, j) \), we record whether \( i \succ j \) or \( j \succ i \). The pairwise data can be represented as a tournament matrix \( M \) where \( M_{ij} \) counts how often \( i \) was preferred over \( j \).

sections.title: Aggregation methods
sections.body:
MethodHow it ranksProperties
Win-rateRank by fraction of comparisons wonSimple but ignores opponent strength
Bradley-Terry MLEFit latent strength scores via maximum likelihoodAccounts for opponent strength; statistically principled
Elo (online)Incremental updates after each matchEquivalent to BT; order-dependent
Copeland's methodCount opponents each item beats on aggregateCondorcet-inspired; simple, robust
PageRank on winsRandom-walk on the preference graphHandles dense tournament graphs well

Pairwise loss in learning-to-rank: RankNet (Burges et al., 2005) trains a scoring function \( f(x) \) by penalising incorrectly ordered pairs via the Bradley-Terry log-likelihood:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\sum_{(i,j): y_i > y_j} \log \sigma(f(x_i) - f(x_j)) \]

where \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid and \( f(x_i), f(x_j) \) are predicted relevance scores. LambdaRank extends this by weighting each pair's gradient by \( |\Delta\text{NDCG}| \).

sections.title: Applications in the LLM ecosystem
sections.body:
• Reward model training: Pairwise preference data \( (x, y_w, y_l) \) is the input to Bradley-Terry-based reward model training in RLHF
  • DPO: Directly optimises the policy on pairwise preferences without an explicit reward model
  • Model evaluation: The Chatbot Arena collects pairwise human votes and fits Bradley-Terry scores to rank LLMs
  • RAG re-ranking: Cross-encoder re-rankers implicitly perform pairwise scoring by evaluating query-document relevance

Condorcet's paradox: A notable result from social choice theory — pairwise majority preferences can be cyclic (A > B > C > A) even when each individual voter's preferences are transitive. The Bradley-Terry model avoids this by forcing a consistent global ordering through its parametric assumption, but this means it cannot represent genuinely cyclic preferences in the data.

title: Bootstrap Resampling
summary: A statistical method that estimates the sampling distribution of a statistic by repeatedly resampling with replacement from the observed data. It provides confidence intervals and standard errors without assuming a particular parametric…

papers.citation: 1979 · Bradley Efron · Bootstrap Methods: Another Look at the Jackknife

sections.body: A statistical method that estimates the sampling distribution of a statistic by repeatedly resampling with replacement from the observed data. It provides confidence intervals and standard errors without assuming a particular parametric distribution, making it widely used for uncertainty estimation in ML evaluation.

sections.title: The bootstrap procedure
sections.body:
1. From a dataset of \( n \) observations \( \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\} \), draw \( n \) samples with replacement to create a bootstrap sample \( \{x_1^*, \ldots, x_n^*\} \)
  2. Compute the statistic of interest \( \hat{\theta}^* \) (e.g., mean accuracy, Elo rating) on this resample
  3. Repeat \( B \) times (typically \( B = 1{,}000\text{–}10{,}000 \)) to obtain \( \{\hat{\theta}_1^*, \ldots, \hat{\theta}_B^*\} \)
  4. The empirical distribution of the \( B \) computed statistics approximates the sampling distribution of \( \hat{\theta} \)

sections.title: Confidence interval methods
sections.body:
MethodFormula (95% CI)Properties
Percentile\( [\hat{\theta}^*_{0.025},\; \hat{\theta}^*_{0.975}] \)Simple; can be biased if \(\hat{\theta}\) is biased
Basic (pivotal)\( [2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^*_{0.975},\; 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^*_{0.025}] \)Bias-corrected but not always narrower
BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated)Adjusts percentiles for bias and skewnessMost accurate; standard recommendation

Bootstrap standard error: The standard deviation of the bootstrap distribution estimates the standard error of \( \hat{\theta} \):

\[ \widehat{\text{SE}}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} (\hat{\theta}_b^* - \bar{\theta}^*)^2} \]

sections.title: Applications in ML
sections.body:
ApplicationWhat's bootstrappedWhy
Chatbot ArenaPairwise votes → Elo CIsNo closed-form CI for BT-MLE on sparse matchups
Model comparisonTest set accuracy → CI on differenceAssess if accuracy gap is statistically significant
Benchmark scoringPer-example scores → CI on aggregateReport reliability of composite metrics
Bagging (random forests)Training data → ensemble of modelsReduce variance by averaging bootstrap models

Connection to bagging: Bagging (Bootstrap AGGregatING) applies the bootstrap idea to model training — each base learner is trained on a different bootstrap sample, and predictions are averaged. Random forests are the most prominent example.

Key assumption and limitation: The bootstrap requires the observed sample to be representative of the population. It works for any statistic (median, ratio, correlation, Elo rating — not just the mean), but fails when the sample is too small or heavily biased. It also struggles with statistics that depend on extreme order statistics (e.g., the maximum), since resampling with replacement rarely produces new extremes.

Computational cost: The bootstrap is embarrassingly parallel — each resample can be computed independently. For \( B = 10{,}000 \) with an \( O(n) \) statistic, total cost is \( O(Bn) \).

Python 
import numpy as np

def bootstrap_ci(data, stat_fn=np.mean, B=10000, alpha=0.05):
    """Compute bootstrap percentile confidence interval."""
    n = len(data)
    boot_stats = np.array([
        stat_fn(data[np.random.randint(0, n, size=n)])
        for _ in range(B)
    ])
    lower = np.percentile(boot_stats, 100 * alpha / 2)
    upper = np.percentile(boot_stats, 100 * (1 - alpha / 2))
    return lower, upper

# Example: 95% CI for mean accuracy
accuracies = np.array([0.82, 0.85, 0.79, 0.88, 0.84, 0.81, 0.86])
lo, hi = bootstrap_ci(accuracies)
print(f"95% CI: [{lo:.3f}, {hi:.3f}]")

title: Confidence Interval
summary: A range of values that is likely to contain the true population parameter with a specified probability (e.g., 95%). In ML, confidence intervals quantify uncertainty in evaluation metrics, helping distinguish genuine performance differenc…

papers.citation: 1937 · Jerzy Neyman · Outline of a Theory of Statistical Estimation Based on the Classical Theory of Probability

sections.body: A range of values that is likely to contain the true population parameter with a specified probability (e.g., 95%). In ML, confidence intervals quantify uncertainty in evaluation metrics, helping distinguish genuine performance differences from statistical noise.

Correct interpretation: A 95% confidence interval means that if we repeated the experiment many times and computed the interval each time, 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter. The parameter is fixed; the interval is random. A single computed interval either contains the parameter or does not — saying "there is a 95% probability the true value is in this interval" is a common and incorrect Bayesian reinterpretation.

sections.title: Methods for computing CIs
sections.body:
MethodAssumptionsFormula / approachUse case
Normal (Wald)Large \( n \), approx. normal\( \hat{\theta} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}} \)Mean of continuous data
Bootstrap percentileMinimal; representative samplePercentiles of bootstrap distributionAny statistic (Elo, median, etc.)
Wilson intervalBinomial proportion\( \frac{\hat{p} + \frac{z^2}{2n} \pm z\sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n} + \frac{z^2}{4n^2}}}{1 + \frac{z^2}{n}} \)Accuracy on a test set
Clopper-PearsonBinomial, exactInverted binomial testConservative CI for proportions

Why Wilson over Wald for proportions: The Wald interval \( \hat{p} \pm z\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n} \) has poor coverage when \( \hat{p} \) is near 0 or 1 or when \( n \) is small. The Wilson interval is centred on a shrunken estimate and has much better coverage properties. For ML accuracy reporting on a test set of \( n \) examples, Wilson is the recommended choice.

sections.title: In ML evaluation
sections.body:
• "Model A: 85.2% accuracy (95% CI: 83.1–87.3)" is far more informative than reporting just "85.2%"
  • If two models' CIs overlap substantially, the difference may not be statistically significant — though overlapping CIs do not prove no difference (a paired test is needed)
  • The Chatbot Arena reports Elo ratings with bootstrap CIs (e.g., "1250 ± 15") to indicate ranking reliability
  • Benchmark leaderboards increasingly report CIs; without them, differences of <1% on benchmarks with ~1000 examples are likely noise

sections.title: CI width depends on
sections.body:
\[ \text{Width} \propto \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \]
  • Increasing sample size \( n \) shrinks the CI as \( 1/\sqrt{n} \) — quadrupling the test set halves the CI width
  • Higher confidence level (e.g., 99% vs. 95%) widens the CI (larger \( z \))
  • Higher variance \( \sigma^2 \) widens the CI

Bayesian alternative: Credible intervals give the posterior probability that the parameter lies in the interval, which is the interpretation most people intuitively want. A 95% credible interval means there is a 95% posterior probability the parameter is in the interval, conditional on the prior and data. Frequentist CIs and Bayesian credible intervals often numerically agree but differ fundamentally in interpretation.

title: Vision Language Model (VLM)
summary: A multimodal model that processes both images and text, enabling tasks like visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and reasoning about visual content. VLMs combine a vision encoder with a language model, bri…

papers.citation: 2022 · Alayrac et al. · Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning
2023 · Li et al. · BLIP-2: Bootstrapping Language-Image Pre-training with Frozen Image Encoders and Large Language Models
2023 · Liu et al. · Visual Instruction Tuning

sections.body:
A multimodal model that processes both images and text, enabling tasks like visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and reasoning about visual content. VLMs combine a vision encoder with a language model, bridging visual perception and linguistic reasoning.

The typical VLM architecture has three components:

  1. Vision encoder: Extracts visual features from the image, producing a sequence of patch embeddings. Typically a Vision Transformer (ViT) pretrained with contrastive learning (CLIP, SigLIP). An image is split into \( P \times P \) patches, linearly projected, and processed by transformer layers to produce \( N = (H/P) \times (W/P) \) visual tokens.
  2. Projection / adapter: Maps visual features into the language model's embedding space. Ranges from a simple linear projection (LLaVA) to an MLP, Q-Former with learned queries (BLIP-2), or cross-attention layers (Flamingo's Perceiver Resampler).
  3. Language model: A pretrained LLM that processes the interleaved visual and text tokens autoregressively. The visual tokens are treated as a prefix or interleaved within the text token sequence.

The forward pass can be written as:

\[ z_{\text{vis}} = \text{Proj}(\text{ViT}(I)), \quad z_{\text{txt}} = \text{Embed}(T) \] \[ h = \text{LLM}([\, z_{\text{vis}} \;;\; z_{\text{txt}} \,]) \]

where \( [\,;\,] \) denotes concatenation along the sequence dimension, \( I \) is the input image, and \( T \) is the text prompt.

ModelVision encoderLLMConnection methodYear
FlamingoNFNetChinchillaCross-attention (Perceiver)2022
BLIP-2ViT-G (EVA-CLIP)FlanT5 / OPTQ-Former (32 learned queries)2023
LLaVACLIP ViT-L/14LLaMA / VicunaLinear projection2023
LLaVA-1.5CLIP ViT-L/14@336Vicuna-13B2-layer MLP2023
InternVL 2.5InternViT-6BInternLM2Dynamic resolution + MLP2024

sections.title: Training stages
sections.body:
• Stage 1 — Alignment pretraining: Train only the projection layer on image-caption pairs (e.g., LAION, CC3M) to align visual and textual representations. The vision encoder and LLM are frozen. This is cheap (~hours on 8 GPUs).
  • Stage 2 — Visual instruction tuning: Fine-tune the projection and optionally the LLM on multimodal instruction-following data: visual QA, reasoning, OCR, chart understanding. This stage teaches the model to follow complex visual instructions.

Resolution and token efficiency: A 336x336 image with 14x14 patches produces 576 visual tokens — comparable to a paragraph of text. Higher-resolution approaches (dynamic tiling, multi-crop) improve detail recognition for tasks like document OCR but increase the token budget substantially. Some models (e.g., BLIP-2's Q-Former) compress visual information into a fixed number of tokens (32) regardless of resolution.

Capabilities and benchmarks: Modern VLMs can read text in images (OCR), understand charts and diagrams, solve visual math problems, describe scenes, and perform multi-step visual reasoning. Key benchmarks include VQAv2, TextVQA, DocVQA, MMMU (multi-discipline reasoning), and MathVista.

title: Cross-Attention
summary: An attention mechanism where queries come from one sequence and keys/values come from a different sequence, enabling a model to attend to information from an external source. Cross-attention is how encoder-decoder models condition the de…

papers.citation: 2014 · Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio · Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body:
An attention mechanism where queries come from one sequence and keys/values come from a different sequence, enabling a model to attend to information from an external source. Cross-attention is how encoder-decoder models condition the decoder on the encoder output, and how multimodal models ground generation in a different modality (e.g., text conditioned on images or audio).

In cross-attention, the query \( Q \) is projected from the target sequence (e.g., decoder hidden states) while \( K \) and \( V \) are projected from the source sequence (e.g., encoder output):

\[ Q = H_{\text{target}} W^Q, \quad K = H_{\text{source}} W^K, \quad V = H_{\text{source}} W^V \] \[ \text{CrossAttn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \]

where \( H_{\text{target}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \) and \( H_{\text{source}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d} \), producing an attention matrix of shape \( n \times m \). Each target position attends over all \( m \) source positions. The \( \sqrt{d_k} \) scaling prevents the dot products from growing large, which would push the softmax into saturated regions with vanishing gradients.

Attention typeQ sourceK, V sourceAttention shape
Self-attentionSame sequenceSame sequence\( n \times n \)
Cross-attentionSequence A (target)Sequence B (source)\( n \times m \)

In the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017): Each decoder layer contains three sub-layers: (1) masked self-attention over decoder tokens, (2) cross-attention where the decoder attends to the encoder output, and (3) a feed-forward network. The cross-attention layer is what allows the decoder to "see" the input sequence. The encoder output is computed once and reused as K, V for every decoder layer and every decoding step — this is computationally efficient.

sections.title: Applications across architectures
sections.body:
• Encoder-decoder Transformers: Decoder attends to encoder output for machine translation, summarisation (T5, BART, mBART, original Transformer)
  • Vision-language models: LLM attends to visual features from a vision encoder via cross-attention layers inserted between self-attention blocks (Flamingo's gated cross-attention, Perceiver Resampler)
  • Diffusion models: The U-Net's intermediate layers attend to text encoder embeddings via cross-attention for text-to-image generation (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2). The text embedding serves as K, V while the noisy image features serve as Q.
  • Speech models: The text decoder attends to audio encoder features (Whisper uses standard encoder-decoder cross-attention)

Cross-attention vs. concatenation: Decoder-only models (GPT, LLaMA, Claude) do not use cross-attention — they handle conditioning by concatenating context and input into a single sequence processed entirely by self-attention (sometimes called "in-context conditioning"). This architectural simplicity is one reason decoder-only models have become dominant for language tasks. However, cross-attention remains essential when the two modalities have fundamentally different structure (e.g., continuous image features vs. discrete text tokens) or when the source representation should be computed once and reused across many decoding steps.

Computational cost: Cross-attention costs \( O(n \cdot m \cdot d_k) \) per head, where \( n \) is the target length and \( m \) is the source length. In encoder-decoder models during autoregressive generation, the K, V projections of the encoder output can be cached (just like KV-caching in self-attention), so each decoding step only computes one new query vector.

title: Vision Encoder
summary: A neural network that converts a raw image into a sequence of dense feature vectors (or a single pooled vector) suitable for downstream reasoning. In vision-language models (VLMs), the vision encoder extracts visual representations that…

papers.citation: 2020 · Dosovitskiy et al. · An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
2021 · Radford et al. · Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision

sections.body:
A neural network that converts a raw image into a sequence of dense feature vectors (or a single pooled vector) suitable for downstream reasoning. In vision-language models (VLMs), the vision encoder extracts visual representations that are projected into the language model's embedding space as "visual tokens," enabling the LLM to reason about image content.

Early vision encoders were convolutional networks (ResNets, EfficientNets) whose final feature maps were flattened or pooled. Modern VLMs almost universally use Vision Transformers (ViT), introduced by Dosovitskiy et al. (2020), which treat an image as a sequence of patches — a direct analogy to tokens in NLP.

sections.title: ViT patch embedding pipeline
sections.body:
1. Patchify: Divide an image of size \( H \times W \times C \) into \( N = \frac{H}{P} \times \frac{W}{P} \) non-overlapping patches of size \( P \times P \times C \).
  2. Linear projection: Flatten each patch into a vector \( \mathbf{x}_i^{\text{patch}} \in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 C} \) and project: \[ \mathbf{z}_i^{0} = \mathbf{x}_i^{\text{patch}} \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{e}_i^{\text{pos}}, \quad \mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 C \times d} \] where \( d \) is the Transformer hidden dimension and \( \mathbf{e}_i^{\text{pos}} \) is a learned (or sinusoidal) positional embedding.
  3. Transformer encoding: Process the sequence \( [\mathbf{z}_{\text{CLS}}; \mathbf{z}_1^{0}; \ldots; \mathbf{z}_N^{0}] \) through \( L \) Transformer encoder layers with multi-head self-attention and FFN sub-layers.
  4. Output: Either the full sequence of \( N \) patch embeddings (used in VLMs to preserve spatial information) or the [CLS] token embedding (used for classification).

Positional encoding: Standard ViT uses learnable 1D positional embeddings. For variable-resolution inputs, 2D interpolation of positional embeddings is common. SigLIP and newer models often use RoPE-2D or NaViT-style packing to handle arbitrary aspect ratios without resizing.

EncoderTraining objectiveKey propertyUsed in
CLIP ViT-L/14Contrastive (image-text)Strong zero-shot transferLLaVA, OpenCLIP VLMs
SigLIPSigmoid contrastiveNo global softmax; scales to large batchesPaLI-X, Gemini-family
DINOv2Self-supervised (self-distillation)Excellent dense/local featuresDense prediction, retrieval
EVA-CLIPContrastive + MAE initImproved representation qualityInternVL, many open VLMs
ConvNeXtSupervised / CLIPCNN; no patch artifactsSome hybrid architectures

Integration with LLMs: The vision encoder is typically frozen during VLM training — its representations were learned during large-scale pretraining (e.g., CLIP's 400M image-text pairs). A lightweight projection module (linear layer in LLaVA-1.0, MLP in LLaVA-1.5, Q-Former in BLIP-2, Perceiver resampler in Flamingo) maps the encoder's output dimension to the LLM's embedding dimension. The choice of projector architecture controls the trade-off between visual token count, computational cost, and information preservation.

Resolution and token count: A ViT-L/14 at 224×224 produces 256 patch tokens. Increasing resolution to 336×336 yields 576 tokens. Some VLMs (LLaVA-NeXT, InternVL-1.5) use dynamic resolution, tiling the image into multiple crops processed independently, which can produce 1000+ visual tokens — placing pressure on the LLM's context window and motivating token compression techniques.

title: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training)
summary: A model that learns to align images and text in a shared embedding space using contrastive learning on 400 million image-text pairs scraped from the internet. CLIP enables zero-shot image classification, text-to-image retrieval, and serv…

papers.citation: 2021 · Alec Radford et al. (OpenAI) · Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision

sections.body:
A model that learns to align images and text in a shared embedding space using contrastive learning on 400 million image-text pairs scraped from the internet. CLIP enables zero-shot image classification, text-to-image retrieval, and serves as the vision backbone for many VLMs and text-to-image generation models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2).

CLIP was a landmark result from OpenAI demonstrating that natural language supervision at scale produces visual representations that transfer broadly — rivaling or exceeding task-specific supervised models on many benchmarks without any fine-tuning.

Architecture: Two parallel encoders trained jointly:

  • Image encoder: ViT-L/14 (or ResNet-50/101 in early variants) mapping images to \( \mathbb{R}^d \)
  • Text encoder: 12-layer Transformer (63M parameters) mapping tokenized text to \( \mathbb{R}^d \)

Both outputs are L2-normalized, so cosine similarity reduces to a dot product.

Contrastive loss: Given a batch of \( N \) image-text pairs, the \( N \) correct pairs should have high cosine similarity while the \( N^2 - N \) incorrect pairings should have low similarity. The symmetric InfoNCE loss is:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\!\left[\log \frac{\exp\!\bigl(\tau \cdot \cos(I_i, T_i)\bigr)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} \exp\!\bigl(\tau \cdot \cos(I_i, T_j)\bigr)} + \log \frac{\exp\!\bigl(\tau \cdot \cos(T_i, I_i)\bigr)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} \exp\!\bigl(\tau \cdot \cos(T_i, I_j)\bigr)}\right] \]

where \( \tau \) is a learned temperature parameter (initialized to 0.07, learned via log-parameterization to ensure positivity). The first term is image-to-text classification; the second is text-to-image. Large batch sizes (32,768 in the original paper) are critical because they provide more negative pairs per step.

Zero-shot classification: To classify an image into \( K \) classes without any training:

  1. Construct text prompts: "a photo of a {class}" for each class
  2. Encode the image and all \( K \) prompts
  3. Predict class \( \hat{k} = \arg\max_k \cos(I, T_k) \)

Prompt engineering matters significantly — "a photo of a {class}, a type of pet" outperforms bare class names by several points on domain-specific datasets.

Downstream useHow CLIP is usedWhat's frozen
VLMs (LLaVA, etc.)Vision encoder (frozen feature extractor)Image encoder
Stable DiffusionText encoder for conditioning the U-NetText encoder
DALL-E 2Both encoders; diffusion prior maps text→image embeddingsBoth encoders
Image search / retrievalEmbed queries and images into shared spaceBoth encoders

Limitations: CLIP struggles with fine-grained spatial reasoning ("the red cube is to the left of the blue sphere"), counting, and systematic compositionality. Its web-scraped training data introduces societal biases. SigLIP (Zhai et al., 2023) replaced the softmax-based contrastive loss with a sigmoid per-pair loss, eliminating the need for cross-device negatives and scaling more efficiently to larger batch sizes.

title: Program-Aided Language Model
summary: A prompting framework where the language model generates executable code (typically Python) to solve reasoning tasks instead of producing the answer directly in natural language. An external interpreter executes the code, and the result…

papers.citation: 2023 · Luyu Gao et al. (Carnegie Mellon) · PAL: Program-aided Language Models

sections.body:
A prompting framework where the language model generates executable code (typically Python) to solve reasoning tasks instead of producing the answer directly in natural language. An external interpreter executes the code, and the result is returned as the final answer — offloading computation from the LLM to a reliable runtime.

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting lets LLMs show reasoning steps, but the LLM must also perform the arithmetic and logic itself — a task it does unreliably. PAL separates reasoning decomposition (which LLMs do well) from computation (which interpreters do perfectly).

sections.title: Example comparison
sections.body:
Q: Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 cans of 3 balls each.
   How many does he have now?

CoT: Roger starts with 5. He buys 2 × 3 = 6. Total = 5 + 6 = 11.
PAL:
  tennis_balls = 5
  bought = 2 * 3
  answer = tennis_balls + bought
  print(answer)  # → 11

The LLM generates the code; a sandboxed Python interpreter executes it. The prompt includes few-shot exemplars demonstrating the code-generation pattern.

sections.title: Benchmark results (Codex as backbone)
sections.body:
BenchmarkCoT accuracyPAL accuracy
GSM8K (math word problems)65.6%72.0%
SVAMP74.4%79.4%
Date Understanding67.1%76.2%

sections.title: Advantages over pure CoT
sections.body:
• Arithmetic accuracy: LLMs make frequent calculation errors on multi-step problems; code execution is exact
  • Complex logic: Loops, conditionals, and data structures are natural in code but awkward in prose
  • Verifiability: Generated code can be inspected, debugged, and unit-tested — natural language reasoning cannot
  • Composability: Code can call libraries (datetime, sympy) for operations beyond basic arithmetic

Limitations: PAL requires the LLM to be proficient at code generation — weaker models produce buggy code that fails silently or errors out. It also cannot handle tasks where reasoning is inherently linguistic (ethical dilemmas, subjective judgments). The method assumes the problem is well-specified enough to be programmed, which excludes open-ended or ambiguous questions.

MethodReasoning mediumExecutionStrength
Chain of ThoughtNatural languageNone (LLM internal)General reasoning
PALPython codeExternal interpreterPrecise computation
PoT (Program of Thoughts)Python codeExternal interpreterSimilar to PAL; concurrent work
ReAct + code toolsNL reasoning + code actionsExternal interpreterMulti-step tool use

PAL is the conceptual precursor to modern code interpreter tools (ChatGPT Code Interpreter, Claude's tool use) and to agentic frameworks where LLMs write and execute code as part of multi-step problem solving.

title: FlashAttention
summary: An IO-aware exact attention algorithm that computes standard scaled dot-product attention without materializing the full \( N \times N \) attention matrix in GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM). By exploiting the GPU memory hierarchy through…

papers.citation: 2022 · Tri Dao et al. (Stanford) · FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness

sections.body: An IO-aware exact attention algorithm that computes standard scaled dot-product attention without materializing the full \( N \times N \) attention matrix in GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM). By exploiting the GPU memory hierarchy through tiling and recomputation, FlashAttention achieves 2--4x wall-clock speedup and reduces memory from \( O(N^2) \) to \( O(N) \).

The IO bottleneck: Standard attention computes \( S = QK^\top / \sqrt{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N} \), writes \( S \) to HBM, reads it back to compute \( P = \text{softmax}(S) \), writes \( P \) to HBM, reads it back to compute \( O = PV \). For long sequences, the \( O(N^2) \) reads and writes to slow HBM dominate runtime — not the FLOPs themselves. This is the key insight of FlashAttention: attention is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound.

sections.title: Tiling algorithm
sections.body:
1. Divide \( Q \) into blocks of \( B_r \) rows, and \( K, V \) into blocks of \( B_c \) rows, sized to fit in GPU SRAM (on-chip, ~20 MB on A100 vs. 40–80 GB HBM)
  2. For each \( Q \)-block, iterate over all \( K, V \)-blocks: load them into SRAM, compute the block attention scores \( S_{ij} = Q_i K_j^\top / \sqrt{d} \)
  3. Use the online softmax trick to incrementally compute the softmax without needing all scores at once
  4. Accumulate the output block \( O_i \) in SRAM, then write the final result to HBM once

Online softmax trick: The standard softmax requires a global max and sum over all \( N \) keys. The online algorithm maintains running statistics \( m_i \) (max so far) and \( \ell_i \) (sum of exponentials so far), updating them as each new \( K \)-block arrives:

\[ m_i^{\text{new}} = \max(m_i, \max(S_{ij})), \quad \ell_i^{\text{new}} = e^{m_i - m_i^{\text{new}}} \ell_i + \sum_k e^{S_{ijk} - m_i^{\text{new}}} \]

The partial output is rescaled accordingly, ensuring the final result is numerically identical to standard softmax.

Recomputation in the backward pass: Instead of storing \( S \) and \( P \) (the \( O(N^2) \) matrices) for the backward pass, FlashAttention recomputes them from \( Q, K, V \) (which are \( O(Nd) \)) on the fly. This trades extra FLOPs for dramatically reduced memory — a favorable trade because the operation is memory-bound anyway.

PropertyStandard attentionFlashAttention
HBM memory\( O(N^2) \)\( O(N) \)
HBM IO complexity\( O(N^2 d + N^2) \)\( O(N^2 d^2 / M) \) where \( M \) = SRAM size
FLOPs\( O(N^2 d) \)\( O(N^2 d) \) — identical
ExactnessExactExact (bit-for-bit identical output)
Wall-clock speedBaseline2–4x faster (A100)

Impact: FlashAttention is not an approximation — it computes exactly the same result as standard attention via a more IO-efficient access pattern. It has become the default attention kernel in PyTorch (via torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention), Hugging Face Transformers, and most training frameworks. It is critical for training and serving models with long context windows (32K–1M+ tokens).

Successors: FlashAttention-2 (2023) improved parallelism across sequence length and reduced non-matmul FLOPs, achieving ~2x speedup over FA-1. FlashAttention-3 (2024) targets Hopper GPUs (H100), exploiting FP8 tensor cores and asynchronous warp-specialized pipelines.

title: Data Parallelism
summary: A distributed training strategy where the full model is replicated on each GPU and the training data batch is split across GPUs. Each GPU computes gradients on its data shard, gradients are synchronized (all-reduced) across GPUs, and eac…

papers.citation: 2012 · Dean et al. · Large Scale Distributed Deep Networks

sections.body: A distributed training strategy where the full model is replicated on each GPU and the training data batch is split across GPUs. Each GPU computes gradients on its data shard, gradients are synchronized (all-reduced) across GPUs, and each GPU updates its local copy of the weights identically. This is the simplest and most widely used form of parallel training.

sections.title: Workflow for each training step
sections.body:
1. Scatter data: Split a global batch of size \( B \) into \( B/N \) examples per GPU (where \( N \) = number of GPUs)
  2. Local compute: Each GPU runs forward and backward pass independently on its shard, computing local gradients \( g_i \)
  3. All-reduce: Synchronize gradients across GPUs: \( \bar{g} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i \)
  4. Update: Each GPU applies the same optimizer step with \( \bar{g} \), keeping weights identical

After the all-reduce, all GPUs have identical weights, maintaining mathematical equivalence to single-GPU training with the full batch \( B \). The effective learning rate and batch size semantics are preserved.

All-reduce implementation: The ring all-reduce algorithm communicates \( 2(N-1)/N \) times the gradient size — approximately \( 2 \times |\theta| \) bytes, independent of the number of GPUs. For a 7B model in FP16, this is ~28 GB per step. Communication and computation can be overlapped by starting the all-reduce for earlier layers while later layers are still computing gradients.

Gradient accumulation: When the desired global batch size exceeds GPU memory, each GPU performs \( K \) forward-backward passes on micro-batches of size \( B/(NK) \), accumulates gradients locally, and only all-reduces once every \( K \) steps. This simulates a larger effective batch without additional memory.

AspectData parallelism
Memory per GPUFull model + optimizer states + activations for \( B/N \) examples
Communication volume\( \approx 2 \times |\theta| \) per step (ring all-reduce)
Scaling efficiencyNear-linear up to ~256 GPUs; degrades as communication/compute ratio grows
Primary limitationEach GPU must hold the entire model + optimizer states
Throughput scaling\( \approx N \times \) single-GPU throughput (ideal)

Memory breakdown for a 70B model (FP16 + Adam): Parameters = 140 GB, Adam states (FP32 copies + first/second moments) = 420 GB, total ~560 GB per GPU — far exceeding any single GPU. This motivates FSDP / ZeRO, which shard the redundant optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs while preserving data-parallel semantics.

Scaling considerations: As the number of GPUs increases, the per-GPU batch size \( B/N \) shrinks. If it becomes too small, the GPU is underutilized (low arithmetic intensity). The solution is to increase the global batch size \( B \), but very large batches can degrade generalization (the "large batch training" problem), mitigated by learning rate warmup and LARS/LAMB optimizers.
Frameworks: PyTorch DistributedDataParallel (DDP), Horovod, DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 0, JAX pmap.

title: Model Parallelism
summary: A distributed training strategy where the model itself is split across multiple GPUs, with each GPU holding only a portion of the model's parameters. This enables training models that are too large to fit in a single GPU's memory — a nec…

papers.citation: 2020 · Shoeybi et al. · Megatron-LM: Training Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models Using Model Parallelism
2021 · Narayanan et al. · Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Training on GPU Clusters Using Megatron-LM

sections.body:
A distributed training strategy where the model itself is split across multiple GPUs, with each GPU holding only a portion of the model's parameters. This enables training models that are too large to fit in a single GPU's memory — a necessity for modern LLMs with tens or hundreds of billions of parameters.

Model parallelism contrasts with data parallelism, where the full model is replicated on every GPU. In model parallelism, each GPU holds a distinct slice of the model and processes the same data flowing through its portion. The key design decision is how to partition the model:

TypeWhat's splitCommunicationBest for
Tensor parallelismWeight matrices within layers (width)All-reduce within each layer (2 per Transformer layer)Intra-node (NVLink)
Pipeline parallelismGroups of layers (depth)Activations between adjacent stagesInter-node (InfiniBand)
Context parallelismSequence lengthKV exchange via ring attentionVery long sequences
Expert parallelismMoE experts across GPUsAll-to-all token routingMixture-of-Experts models

Why model parallelism is necessary — a concrete example: A 175B parameter model (GPT-3 scale) in FP16 requires 350 GB just for parameters. With Adam optimizer (FP32 copy + two moment buffers), total memory is \( \approx 350 + 3 \times 350 = 1400 \) GB. A single A100 has 80 GB — so at minimum 18 GPUs are needed just for model state, before accounting for activations.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.body:
• Enables larger models: The only way to train models beyond single-GPU memory
  • Communication overhead: Activations and gradients must be transferred between GPUs at every partition boundary — more partition points mean more communication
  • Implementation complexity: Correctly partitioning models, handling different communication patterns, and load-balancing across GPUs is significantly harder than data parallelism
  • Pipeline bubbles: Pipeline parallelism suffers from GPU idle time at batch boundaries (mitigated by micro-batching and interleaved schedules)

3D parallelism in practice: Large-scale training (e.g., Llama 3 405B on 16K GPUs) combines all three dimensions simultaneously:

  • Tensor parallelism (TP=8): within a single node's 8 GPUs (high-bandwidth NVLink)
  • Pipeline parallelism (PP=16): across nodes (lower-bandwidth InfiniBand)
  • Data parallelism (DP=128): across groups of TP×PP devices

Total GPUs = TP × PP × DP = 8 × 16 × 128 = 16,384. The parallelism dimensions are carefully chosen to match the communication bandwidth at each level of the network topology.

title: Pipeline Parallelism
summary: A form of model parallelism that partitions the model's layers into sequential stages across GPUs, with each GPU processing a different stage. Data flows through the pipeline as micro-batches, allowing multiple GPUs to be active simultan…

papers.citation: 2019 · Huang et al. · GPipe: Efficient Training of Giant Neural Networks using Pipeline Parallelism
2021 · Narayanan et al. · Efficient Large-Scale Language Model Training on GPU Clusters Using Megatron-LM

sections.body:
A form of model parallelism that partitions the model's layers into sequential stages across GPUs, with each GPU processing a different stage. Data flows through the pipeline as micro-batches, allowing multiple GPUs to be active simultaneously on different portions of the batch.

A model with \( L \) layers is divided into \( P \) stages across \( P \) GPUs. Each GPU holds layers \( [l_{\text{start}}, l_{\text{end}}] \) for its stage. A global batch is split into \( M \) micro-batches that flow through the pipeline sequentially.

The pipeline bubble problem: At the start of processing, GPU \( k \) must wait for GPUs \( 1, \ldots, k-1 \) to process the first micro-batch before it receives any input. Similarly, at the end, early-stage GPUs go idle while later stages finish. The bubble fraction is:

\[ \text{Bubble fraction} = \frac{P - 1}{M + P - 1} \]

where \( P \) is the number of pipeline stages and \( M \) is the number of micro-batches. With \( P = 16 \) stages and \( M = 64 \) micro-batches, the bubble is \( 15/79 \approx 19\% \) wasted compute. More micro-batches reduce the bubble but increase memory (must store activations for all in-flight micro-batches).

sections.title: Pipeline schedules
sections.body:
ScheduleDescriptionBubblePeak memory
GPipeAll-forward then all-backward\( (P-1)/(M+P-1) \)\( O(M) \) activations
1F1B (PipeDream-Flush)Interleave: 1 forward, 1 backward once pipeline fillsSame\( O(P) \) activations (much lower)
Interleaved 1F1BMultiple virtual stages per GPU; finer granularity\( (P-1)/(M \cdot V + P - 1) \) with \( V \) virtual stages\( O(P) \)
Zero-bubble (Qi et al., 2024)Overlaps backward-for-weights with forward of next micro-batchNear zeroSlightly higher

1F1B in detail: In the warm-up phase, each GPU performs forward passes on micro-batches 1 through \( P \) (filling the pipeline). Once the pipeline is full, each GPU alternates: perform one backward pass on the oldest in-flight micro-batch, then one forward pass on the next micro-batch. This limits the number of in-flight micro-batches to \( P \) (instead of \( M \) in GPipe), dramatically reducing activation memory.

Communication: Only activation tensors (and their gradients) are sent between adjacent pipeline stages. For a Transformer layer with hidden dimension \( d \) and micro-batch sequence length \( s \times b \), the activation size is \( \approx 2sbd \) bytes (FP16). This is much less than the all-reduce of full gradients required by data parallelism, making pipeline parallelism well-suited for inter-node communication where bandwidth is limited.

Load balancing: Stages should have roughly equal computation. In Transformers with identical layers this is straightforward — assign \( L/P \) layers per stage. For heterogeneous architectures (e.g., embedding layers, output heads), careful profiling is needed to avoid stragglers.

title: Tensor Parallelism
summary: A form of model parallelism that splits individual layers' weight matrices across multiple GPUs, with each GPU computing a portion of each layer's output. Tensor parallelism distributes the model's width rather than its depth, requiring…

papers.citation: 2020 · Mohammad Shoeybi et al. · Megatron-LM: Training Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models Using Model Parallelism

sections.body: A form of model parallelism that splits individual layers' weight matrices across multiple GPUs, with each GPU computing a portion of each layer's output. Tensor parallelism distributes the model's width rather than its depth, requiring high-bandwidth inter-GPU communication within each layer forward and backward pass.

Column-parallel linear layer: For a linear layer \( Y = XW + b \) where \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k} \), split \( W \) column-wise across \( N \) GPUs:

\[ W = [W_1 \mid W_2 \mid \cdots \mid W_N], \quad W_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k/N} \]

Each GPU \( i \) receives the full input \( X \) and computes its partial output \( Y_i = XW_i \in \mathbb{R}^{b \times k/N} \). The outputs are concatenated (all-gather) to form the full \( Y \). Crucially, if the next operation is element-wise (e.g., GeLU activation), it can be applied locally without communication.

Row-parallel linear layer: Split \( W \) row-wise: \( W = [W_1; W_2; \ldots; W_N] \), \( W_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d/N \times k} \). Each GPU holds a shard of the input \( X_i \) and computes \( Y_i = X_i W_i \). The partial outputs are summed via all-reduce: \( Y = \sum_i Y_i \).

Megatron-LM Transformer partitioning: The key insight is to pair column-parallel and row-parallel layers to minimize communication:

Sub-layerSplit strategyCommunication
MHA: QKV projection \( W_{QKV} \)Column-split (each GPU gets full heads)None (GeLU/attention applied locally)
MHA: Output projection \( W_O \)Row-splitAll-reduce (forward); identity (backward)
FFN: \( W_1 \) (up-projection)Column-splitNone (GeLU applied locally)
FFN: \( W_2 \) (down-projection)Row-splitAll-reduce (forward); identity (backward)

Each Transformer layer requires 2 all-reduce operations in the forward pass (one after attention output projection, one after FFN down-projection) and 2 in the backward pass — 4 total per layer. Each all-reduce communicates a tensor of shape \( (b, s, d) \), where \( b \) = micro-batch size, \( s \) = sequence length, \( d \) = hidden dimension.

Communication cost: For a ring all-reduce, the volume per operation is \( \approx 2 \cdot b \cdot s \cdot d \cdot \text{sizeof(dtype)} \). With 4 operations per layer and \( L \) layers, the total per step is \( \approx 8Lbsd \cdot \text{sizeof(dtype)} \). This is proportional to sequence length, making TP communication-intensive for long sequences.

Bandwidth requirements: TP demands high intra-layer communication bandwidth, making it practical only within a single node where GPUs are connected via NVLink (900 GB/s bidirectional on H100) or NVSwitch. Typical TP degree is 8 (one per GPU in a node). Cross-node TP is rarely used due to InfiniBand's ~50x lower bandwidth.

Sequence parallelism (Megatron-LM v3): The LayerNorm and dropout operations between TP regions operate on the full \( (b, s, d) \) tensor, redundantly across GPUs. Sequence parallelism splits these operations along the sequence dimension, replacing the all-reduce with all-gather (forward) + reduce-scatter (backward) — same communication volume but eliminating the memory redundancy.

title: Context Parallelism
summary: A parallelism strategy that splits the input sequence along the sequence-length dimension across multiple GPUs. Each GPU processes a contiguous chunk of the sequence, with communication required only during the attention computation (to…

papers.citation: 2023 · Hao Liu et al. (UC Berkeley) · Ring Attention with Blockwise Transformers for Near-Infinite Context

sections.body: A parallelism strategy that splits the input sequence along the sequence-length dimension across multiple GPUs. Each GPU processes a contiguous chunk of the sequence, with communication required only during the attention computation (to exchange key-value pairs). This enables training and inference on very long sequences that would otherwise exceed single-GPU memory.

Motivation: Self-attention memory scales as \( O(N^2) \) in sequence length \( N \), and activation memory scales as \( O(N) \) per layer. For a 1M-token context, even with FlashAttention (which reduces attention memory to \( O(N) \)), the total activation memory for a large model can exceed a single GPU's capacity. Context parallelism (CP) distributes this memory across GPUs.

Given a sequence of length \( N \) and \( P \) GPUs, each GPU holds a contiguous chunk of \( N/P \) tokens. For feedforward and normalization layers, each GPU operates independently on its local chunk — no communication needed. The challenge is self-attention, where each token must attend to all positions.

sections.title: Ring attention algorithm
sections.body:
1. Each GPU computes \( Q_i, K_i, V_i \) for its local chunk of \( N/P \) tokens
  2. In a ring topology, GPUs pass \( K, V \) blocks around:
    • Step 0: GPU \( i \) computes attention of \( Q_i \) against its local \( K_i, V_i \)
    • Step 1: GPU \( i \) receives \( K_{i+1}, V_{i+1} \) from the next GPU, computes partial attention, and passes \( K_i, V_i \) forward
    • After \( P-1 \) steps, each GPU has attended to all positions
  3. Partial attention outputs are accumulated using the online softmax trick (same as FlashAttention), maintaining numerical correctness

The key insight is that KV communication in step \( k \) can be overlapped with attention computation from step \( k-1 \), hiding most of the communication latency behind compute.

PropertyContext parallelismTensor parallelism
What's splitSequence length (\( N/P \) tokens per GPU)Model width (hidden dim \( d/P \))
Communication patternRing KV exchange (P2P, overlapped)All-reduce per layer (blocking)
Communication volume per layer\( 2(P-1) \cdot \frac{N}{P} \cdot d \cdot \text{sizeof} \)\( 2 \cdot N \cdot d \cdot \text{sizeof} \) (per all-reduce)
Memory per GPUActivations for \( N/P \) tokensParameters \( \propto 1/P \), activations unchanged
Primary benefitEnables longer sequencesEnables larger models

Causal attention optimization: For autoregressive models, tokens only attend to earlier positions. GPUs holding later chunks have fewer valid KV blocks to process, creating load imbalance. Striped attention (distributing tokens in a round-robin pattern rather than contiguous chunks) and zigzag scheduling address this by balancing computation across GPUs.

Relationship to other techniques: Context parallelism is complementary to FlashAttention (which optimizes single-GPU attention IO) and tensor parallelism (which splits model width). Llama 3.1 (Meta, 2024) used CP with 8K–128K context lengths, combining it with TP=8 and PP=16 for 405B-parameter training. DeepSeek-V2 similarly uses CP for its 128K context window.

title: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP)
summary: A memory-efficient variant of data parallelism that shards model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs instead of replicating them. Each GPU stores only \( 1/N \) of the model state, dramatically reducing per-GPU memory…

papers.citation: 2020 · Samyam Rajbhandari et al. (Microsoft) · ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models

sections.body:
A memory-efficient variant of data parallelism that shards model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs instead of replicating them. Each GPU stores only \( 1/N \) of the model state, dramatically reducing per-GPU memory while preserving the mathematical semantics of data-parallel training.

In standard data parallelism (DDP), each of \( N \) GPUs stores a full copy of parameters, gradients, and optimizer states — massive redundancy. FSDP (equivalent to DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 3) eliminates this by sharding everything:

Sharding levelWhat's shardedPer-GPU memory (vs. DDP)Equivalent
Stage 1Optimizer states only\( \Psi + \Psi + 12\Psi/N \) bytesZeRO-1
Stage 2+ Gradients\( \Psi + (2 + 12/N)\Psi \) bytesZeRO-2
Stage 3 / FSDP+ Parameters\( (2 + 2 + 12)\Psi/N = 16\Psi/N \) bytesZeRO-3

where \( \Psi \) = number of parameters. The \( 16\Psi \) total comes from: 2 bytes (FP16 parameters) + 2 bytes (FP16 gradients) + 4 bytes (FP32 master copy) + 4 bytes (FP32 first moment) + 4 bytes (FP32 second moment) for Adam. With \( N = 64 \) GPUs, Stage 3 reduces per-GPU memory by 64x.

sections.title: FSDP workflow per layer
sections.body:
1. All-gather parameters: Before computing a layer's forward pass, temporarily reconstruct the full parameter tensor by gathering shards from all GPUs
  2. Forward pass: Compute with the full parameters, then discard the non-local shards (freeing memory)
  3. All-gather again for backward: Re-gather full parameters for gradient computation
  4. Reduce-scatter gradients: Each GPU receives the gradient shard corresponding to its parameter shard
  5. Local optimizer step: Each GPU updates only its \( 1/N \) shard of parameters

Communication cost: FSDP requires 3x the communication of DDP:

  • DDP: 1 all-reduce per step \( = 2\Psi \) bytes (ring all-reduce)
  • FSDP: 2 all-gathers (forward + backward) + 1 reduce-scatter \( = 3 \times 2\Psi/N \times N \approx 6\Psi \) bytes

This 3x overhead is acceptable because (a) communication overlaps with computation via layer-by-layer scheduling, and (b) the memory savings enable larger batch sizes per GPU, improving compute utilization.

Concrete example: A 70B model with Adam in mixed precision: \( 16 \times 70 \times 10^9 = 1120 \) GB total model state. With 64 GPUs: DDP needs 1120 GB per GPU (impossible); FSDP Stage 3 needs ~17.5 GB per GPU, leaving ample room for activations.

sections.title: FSDP sharding strategies
sections.body:
• FULL_SHARD: Parameters, gradients, and optimizer states all sharded (Stage 3)
  • SHARD_GRAD_OP: Only gradients and optimizer states sharded; parameters stay after forward (Stage 2 — faster, more memory)
  • HYBRID_SHARD: Full-shard within a node, replicate across nodes — reduces inter-node communication while maintaining intra-node memory efficiency
Implementations: PyTorch FSDP (torch.distributed.fsdp), DeepSpeed ZeRO (Stages 1–3), PyTorch FSDP2 (torch.distributed 2.x, improved composability with tensor parallelism and compile).

title: Model Sharding
summary: The practice of splitting a model's parameters (and associated optimizer states and gradients) across multiple devices so that no single device needs to hold the entire model in memory. Sharding is the core mechanism behind FSDP and Deep…

papers.citation: 2020 · Rajbhandari, Rasley, Raste, He · ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models

sections.body:
The practice of splitting a model's parameters (and associated optimizer states and gradients) across multiple devices so that no single device needs to hold the entire model in memory. Sharding is the core mechanism behind FSDP and DeepSpeed ZeRO, enabling training of models with hundreds of billions of parameters.

A single parameter in mixed-precision Adam training requires ~16 bytes of state: 2 bytes for the FP16 weight, 2 bytes for the FP16 gradient, 4 bytes for the FP32 master weight, 4 bytes for the first moment \( m \), and 4 bytes for the second moment \( v \). For a 70B-parameter model, this totals ~1.12 TB — far exceeding single-GPU memory.

sections.title: Sharding strategies (DeepSpeed ZeRO stages)
sections.body:
StageWhat is shardedMemory per GPUCommunication
ZeRO-1Optimizer states only\( 4\Psi + 12\Psi/N \) bytesSame as data parallelism
ZeRO-2Optimizer states + gradients\( 2\Psi + 14\Psi/N \) bytesReduce-scatter for gradients
ZeRO-3 / FSDPOptimizer states + gradients + parameters\( 16\Psi/N \) bytesAll-gather before forward/backward; reduce-scatter for gradients

where \( \Psi \) is the number of parameters and \( N \) is the number of GPUs.

Communication pattern for FSDP (ZeRO-3): Before each layer's forward pass, an all-gather collects the full parameters from all ranks. After the backward pass, a reduce-scatter distributes averaged gradients back to each rank's shard. The key insight is that each GPU only needs the full parameters transiently during computation — they can be discarded immediately after use.

sections.title: Sharding vs. other parallelism strategies
sections.body:
• Data parallelism: Each GPU holds a full model copy — sharding generalises this by partitioning the model state
  • Tensor parallelism: Splits individual matrix operations (e.g., splitting \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 4d} \) column-wise across GPUs), requires intra-node high-bandwidth communication
  • Pipeline parallelism: Assigns different layers to different GPUs, introduces bubble overhead

Sharding preserves mathematical equivalence with single-GPU training — it is purely a memory optimisation. In practice, large-scale training combines all three: FSDP across nodes, tensor parallelism within a node, and pipeline parallelism across node groups.

title: Floating-Point Operations (FLOPs)
summary: A measure of the computational cost of a neural network, counting the number of floating-point multiply-add operations required for a forward (and optionally backward) pass. FLOPs are the standard unit for comparing model efficiency, est…

papers.citation: 2020 · Kaplan et al. · Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models

sections.body: A measure of the computational cost of a neural network, counting the number of floating-point multiply-add operations required for a forward (and optionally backward) pass. FLOPs are the standard unit for comparing model efficiency, estimating training compute budgets, and formulating scaling laws.

Conventions: "FLOPs" (capital S) refers to the total number of operations; "FLOP/s" refers to operations per second (hardware throughput). A fused multiply-add (FMA) is typically counted as 2 FLOPs (one multiply, one add), though some sources count it as 1.

sections.title: FLOP counting for common operations
sections.body:
OperationFLOPs
Matrix multiply \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}, B \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n} \)\( 2mkn \)
Self-attention (QKV projections + output)\( 8d^2 T \) per layer (where \( d \) = hidden dim, \( T \) = seq length)
Attention scores \( QK^\top \) + softmax + weighted sum\( 4dT^2 \) per layer
FFN (two linear layers, \( d \to 4d \to d \))\( 16d^2 T \) per layer

Transformer FLOP estimates: For a model with \( N \) parameters processing \( T \) tokens:

  • Forward pass: \( \approx 2NT \) FLOPs (each parameter participates in one multiply-add per token)
  • Backward pass: \( \approx 4NT \) FLOPs (approximately 2x forward)
  • Full training step: \( \approx 6NT \) FLOPs per token

For a complete training run on \( D \) tokens:

\[ C \approx 6ND \text{ FLOPs} \]
