sections.title: Concrete examples
sections.body:
ModelParamsTraining tokensApprox. FLOPs
GPT-3175B300B\( 3.1 \times 10^{23} \)
LLaMA 2 70B70B2T\( 8.4 \times 10^{23} \)
LLaMA 3 405B405B15T\( 3.8 \times 10^{25} \)

Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU): The ratio of observed training throughput to theoretical peak FLOP/s of the hardware. MFU of 40–60% is typical for large training runs; the gap comes from memory bandwidth, communication overhead, and pipeline bubbles. MFU is a key efficiency metric for distributed training systems.

Connection to scaling laws: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that cross-entropy loss decreases as a power law in compute \( C \): \( L(C) \propto C^{-\alpha} \), with \( \alpha \approx 0.05 \). Hoffmann et al. (2022, "Chinchilla") refined this to show that for a fixed compute budget \( C \), the optimal allocation is roughly \( N \propto C^{0.5} \) and \( D \propto C^{0.5} \) — model size and data should scale equally.

title: Mixed Precision Training
summary: A training technique that uses lower-precision floating-point formats (FP16 or BF16) for most computations while maintaining a master copy of weights in FP32 for numerical stability. Mixed precision roughly halves memory usage and double…

papers.citation: 2018 · Micikevicius, Narang, Alben, Diamos et al. · Mixed Precision Training

sections.body: A training technique that uses lower-precision floating-point formats (FP16 or BF16) for most computations while maintaining a master copy of weights in FP32 for numerical stability. Mixed precision roughly halves memory usage and doubles throughput on modern GPUs equipped with tensor cores.

sections.title: The mixed precision workflow
sections.body:
1. Maintain master weights in FP32
  2. Cast weights to FP16/BF16 for the forward and backward pass
  3. Compute gradients in FP16/BF16
  4. Loss scaling: Multiply the loss by a scale factor \( S \) before backpropagation to shift small gradient values into FP16's representable range, then divide gradients by \( S \) before the optimizer step
  5. Update master weights in FP32 — small gradient updates (e.g., \( \eta \cdot g \approx 10^{-7} \)) would vanish in FP16 where the smallest representable positive value is \( \approx 6 \times 10^{-8} \)

Why loss scaling is needed for FP16: FP16 has only a 5-bit exponent, giving a dynamic range of \( [6 \times 10^{-8}, 6.5 \times 10^{4}] \). Gradient values frequently fall below \( 10^{-5} \) during training. Without scaling, these underflow to zero. Dynamic loss scaling starts with a large \( S \) (e.g., \( 2^{16} \)) and halves it whenever an overflow (inf/NaN) is detected, then doubles it periodically when training is stable.

FormatBitsExponent / MantissaRangeLoss scaling needed?
FP32328 / 23\( \pm 3.4 \times 10^{38} \)No
FP16165 / 10\( \pm 6.5 \times 10^{4} \)Yes
BF16168 / 7\( \pm 3.4 \times 10^{38} \)Generally no
FP8 (E4M3)84 / 3\( \pm 448 \)Yes (per-tensor scaling)

BF16 vs. FP16: BF16 shares FP32's 8-bit exponent, giving the same dynamic range. This eliminates the need for loss scaling, simplifying the training loop. The trade-off is reduced mantissa precision (7 bits vs. 10), meaning BF16 has ~2 fewer decimal digits of precision. In practice, this rarely matters for training because stochastic gradient noise dominates precision errors. Most modern LLM training (GPT-4, LLaMA, etc.) uses BF16.

Which operations stay in FP32: Certain operations are numerically sensitive and are kept in full precision even in mixed-precision training: softmax, layer normalization, loss computation, and the weight update itself. This selective casting is handled automatically by frameworks like PyTorch AMP (torch.cuda.amp).

Performance gains: NVIDIA tensor cores execute FP16/BF16 matrix multiplications at 2x the throughput of FP32 (e.g., A100: 312 TFLOP/s for BF16 vs. 156 TFLOP/s for FP32). Memory bandwidth is also halved, reducing both compute and I/O bottlenecks. Combined with reduced memory footprint (enabling larger batch sizes), mixed precision is essentially free performance.

title: Long-Context Pretraining
summary: A training strategy that extends a pretrained model's effective context length by continuing training on progressively longer sequences, typically as a separate phase after the bulk of pretraining at a shorter context. This enables model…

papers.citation: 2024 · Dubey et al. · The Llama 3 Herd of Models
2023 · Chen et al. · Efficient Context Window Extension of Large Language Models

sections.body:
A training strategy that extends a pretrained model's effective context length by continuing training on progressively longer sequences, typically as a separate phase after the bulk of pretraining at a shorter context. This enables models to attend to and reason over very long documents without retraining from scratch.

Most LLMs are pretrained at a relatively short context (4K–8K tokens) because attention cost scales as \( O(T^2 d) \) and memory scales as \( O(T^2) \) per layer, making long sequences expensive. Long-context pretraining extends this efficiently in a later phase:

  1. Short-context pretraining: Train on the majority of tokens (e.g., 95–99%) at 4K–8K context
  2. Long-context extension: Continue training on a smaller dataset with gradually increasing context length (e.g., 8K → 32K → 128K), adjusting positional encoding parameters at each stage

RoPE frequency scaling is the key technical enabler. RoPE encodes position via rotation angles \( \theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d} \). To extend beyond the trained length \( L \), the base frequency is increased:

\[ \theta_i' = \left(\beta \cdot 10000\right)^{-2i/d} \]

where \( \beta > 1 \) stretches the wavelengths so positions beyond \( L \) map to rotation angles the model has seen during training. YaRN (Peng et al., 2023) refines this with per-dimension scaling and a temperature correction factor:

\[ s(r_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } r_i < \alpha \\ \frac{r_i - \alpha}{\beta - \alpha} & \text{if } \alpha \leq r_i \leq \beta \\ 0 & \text{if } r_i > \beta \end{cases} \]

where \( r_i \) is the ratio of the target length to the wavelength at dimension \( i \), and \( s \) interpolates between no scaling and full interpolation.

ModelBase contextExtended contextMethod
LLaMA 38K128KGradual increase + RoPE base freq scaling
Gemini 1.5—1M+Ring attention + undisclosed scaling
Yi4K200KContinued pretraining + NTK-aware scaling
Code Llama4K100KPosition interpolation (Chen et al., 2023)

sections.title: Infrastructure requirements
sections.body:
• Context parallelism: Long sequences are split across GPUs, with each GPU computing attention over its chunk and exchanging KV states (ring attention)
  • FlashAttention: Tiled computation avoids materialising the full \( T \times T \) attention matrix, reducing memory from \( O(T^2) \) to \( O(T) \)
  • Data curation: Long-context training requires genuine long documents (books, code repos, legal filings); padding short documents wastes compute and can degrade quality

Evaluation: Long-context models are typically evaluated with needle-in-a-haystack tests and long-document QA, though these simple retrieval tasks may not capture actual reasoning capability over long contexts.

title: Needle in a Haystack
summary: A benchmark that evaluates a language model's ability to retrieve a specific piece of information (the "needle") embedded at various positions within a long context (the "haystack"). It tests whether models can effectively utilise their…

papers.citation: 2023 · Greg Kamradt · Needle in a Haystack test (original eval popularization)
2023 · Liu et al. · Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts

sections.body: A benchmark that evaluates a language model's ability to retrieve a specific piece of information (the "needle") embedded at various positions within a long context (the "haystack"). It tests whether models can effectively utilise their full context window or whether performance degrades for information at certain positions.

sections.title: Test procedure
sections.body:
1. Construct a long context by concatenating irrelevant "filler" text (essays, documents, repeated passages)
  2. Insert a specific, easily verified fact — e.g., "The best thing to do in San Francisco is eat a sandwich at Dolores Park on a sunny day" — at a controlled position (beginning, 25%, 50%, 75%, end)
  3. Ask the model a question whose answer requires retrieving the inserted fact
  4. Sweep over both total context length (e.g., 1K to 128K tokens) and needle position to produce a 2D heatmap of retrieval accuracy

sections.title: Interpreting the heatmap
sections.body:
PatternInterpretationCause
Uniform greenFull context window is functionalGood position encoding + training
Red/low accuracy in the middle"Lost in the middle" effectRecency + primacy bias in attention
Red at long lengthsContext window not functional at claimed lengthInsufficient long-context training or positional encoding failure
Degradation at extreme startAttention sink interferenceFirst tokens absorb disproportionate attention mass

"Lost in the middle" (Liu et al., 2023): Many models exhibit a U-shaped retrieval curve — performing well on information near the beginning and end of the context but poorly in the middle. This was shown across 10+ models and context lengths from 4K–128K. The effect is hypothesised to stem from the training data distribution (most relevant information tends to appear at the start of documents) and from the recency bias inherent in causal attention.

sections.title: Limitations of the benchmark
sections.body:
• Single-fact retrieval is simplistic. Real-world long-context tasks require multi-hop reasoning, aggregation, and synthesis across scattered information — not just finding one needle
  • No reasoning component. A model can "pass" needle-in-a-haystack while failing at summarising or comparing information spread across the context
  • Filler text quality matters. Random filler is easier than semantically distracting text that competes with the needle

More rigorous long-context benchmarks like RULER (Hsieh et al., 2024) and SCROLLS test multi-needle retrieval, variable-tracking, and long-document QA to address these gaps.

title: Positional Encoding
summary: A mechanism that injects sequence-order information into a Transformer's input representations. Self-attention is inherently permutation-equivariant — without positional encoding, "the cat sat on the mat" and "mat the on sat cat the" wou…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body: A mechanism that injects sequence-order information into a Transformer's input representations. Self-attention is inherently permutation-equivariant — without positional encoding, "the cat sat on the mat" and "mat the on sat cat the" would produce identical representations.

Why position information is needed: The self-attention operation \( \text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V \) treats its inputs as a set, not a sequence. Any permutation of the input tokens produces the same permutation of the output — attention has no built-in notion of "before" or "after." Positional encoding breaks this symmetry.

sections.title: Major approaches
sections.body:
TypeMechanismModelsLength generalisation
Absolute (sinusoidal)Fixed sinusoidal functions added to embeddingsOriginal TransformerLimited in practice
Absolute (learned)Learnable embedding \( E_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{L_{\max} \times d} \)GPT-2, BERTNone (hard cap at \( L_{\max} \))
Relative (ALiBi)Linear bias \( -m|i-j| \) on attention logitsBLOOM, MPTGood (no learned params)
Rotary (RoPE)Rotate Q, K by position-dependent anglesLLaMA, Mistral, QwenGood (with frequency scaling)

Original sinusoidal encoding (Vaswani et al., 2017):

\[ PE(pos, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \quad PE(pos, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \]

Each dimension oscillates at a different frequency, from wavelength \( 2\pi \) (dimension 0) to \( 2\pi \cdot 10000 \) (last dimension). The sinusoidal form was chosen because \( PE(pos + k) \) can be written as a linear transformation of \( PE(pos) \), theoretically allowing the model to learn to attend by relative position.

RoPE (Su et al., 2021) has become the dominant approach in modern LLMs. It applies a rotation to query and key vectors:

\[ \tilde{q}_m = R_\Theta^{(m)} q_m, \quad \tilde{k}_n = R_\Theta^{(n)} k_n \]

where \( R_\Theta^{(m)} \) is a block-diagonal rotation matrix with angles \( m\theta_i \). The attention score then depends only on relative position:

\[ \tilde{q}_m^\top \tilde{k}_n = q_m^\top R_\Theta^{(n-m)} k_n \]

RoPE's advantages: encodes relative position without additive biases, is compatible with linear attention and KV caching, and supports context length extension through base frequency scaling (NTK-aware, YaRN).

Historical progression: The field has moved from absolute to relative encodings as the need for length generalisation became clear with the rise of long-context applications. Nearly all LLMs since 2023 use RoPE.

title: Absolute Position Encoding
summary: A positional encoding scheme that assigns a unique, fixed vector to each absolute position in the sequence and adds it to the token embedding. Each position has the same representation regardless of the surrounding context or other tokens.

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body: A positional encoding scheme that assigns a unique, fixed vector to each absolute position in the sequence and adds it to the token embedding. Each position has the same representation regardless of the surrounding context or other tokens.

Sinusoidal (fixed) variant — used in the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017):

\[ PE(pos, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right), \quad PE(pos, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d}}\right) \]

where \( pos \) is the token position, \( i \) is the dimension index, and \( d \) is the embedding dimension. Each dimension oscillates at a different frequency:

  • Dimension 0: wavelength \( 2\pi \approx 6.3 \) positions
  • Dimension \( d-1 \): wavelength \( 2\pi \cdot 10000 \approx 62{,}832 \) positions

This creates a unique binary-like "fingerprint" for each position. The key mathematical property is that \( PE(pos + k) \) is a linear function of \( PE(pos) \) — specifically, there exists a matrix \( M_k \) (independent of \( pos \)) such that \( PE(pos+k) = M_k \cdot PE(pos) \). This means the model can learn to attend by relative position using linear projections, even though the encoding is absolute.

Learned variant — used in BERT and GPT-2:

A trainable embedding matrix \( E_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{L_{\max} \times d} \), where each row is a learned vector for that position. The model input becomes:

\[ h_0^{(t)} = E_{\text{token}}[x_t] + E_{\text{pos}}[t] \]
VariantParametersProsCons
Sinusoidal0No learnable parameters; theoretical extrapolation via linear transform propertyFixed; extrapolation degrades in practice beyond ~2x training length
Learned\( L_{\max} \times d \)Flexible; can capture complex positional patterns; slightly better performance at trained lengthsHard limit at \( L_{\max} \); zero extrapolation; adds parameters

Why absolute encodings have been superseded: Both variants conflate absolute identity with relative distance. The model must learn to extract relative information (\( |i - j| \)) from absolute signals — an indirect and wasteful computation. Relative methods (RoPE, ALiBi) encode relative position directly into the attention mechanism, leading to better length generalisation and simpler reasoning about token relationships. Since 2022, virtually all new LLMs use RoPE or ALiBi rather than absolute encodings.

Vaswani et al. (2017) found no significant performance difference between sinusoidal and learned absolute encodings, and chose sinusoidal for its potential extrapolation properties.

title: Relative Position Encoding
summary: A family of positional encoding methods that represent the distance between tokens rather than their absolute positions. By encoding relative offsets \( (i - j) \) directly into the attention computation, these methods generalise better…

papers.citation: 2018 · Shaw, Uszkoreit, Vaswani · Self-Attention with Relative Position Representations
2021 · Su et al. · RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding
2022 · Press et al. · Train Short, Test Long: Attention with Linear Biases Enables Input Length Extrapolation

sections.body:
A family of positional encoding methods that represent the distance between tokens rather than their absolute positions. By encoding relative offsets \( (i - j) \) directly into the attention computation, these methods generalise better to sequence lengths unseen during training and are more naturally aligned with the pairwise nature of attention.

Absolute encodings add a fixed vector per position to the input. Relative encodings instead modify the attention scores to be a function of the distance between query position \( i \) and key position \( j \). The core insight: language semantics depend on relative word order ("the dog bit the man" vs. "the man bit the dog"), not absolute position indices.

sections.title: Major approaches
sections.body:
MethodHow relative position entersParametersUsed in
Shaw et al. (2018)Learnable bias vectors \( a_{i-j}^K, a_{i-j}^V \) added to keys/values, clipped to max distance \( k \)\( 2k \times d_k \)Music Transformer, early T5
T5 relative biasLearnable scalar bias \( b(i-j) \) added to attention logits, bucketed logarithmically for long distances\( h \times n_{\text{buckets}} \)T5, FLAN-T5
ALiBi (Press et al., 2022)Fixed linear penalty: \( \text{score}(i,j) = q_i^\top k_j - m \cdot |i - j| \), where \( m \) is a head-specific slope0BLOOM, MPT
RoPE (Su et al., 2021)Rotate Q, K vectors by position-dependent angle; dot product depends only on \( i - j \)0LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX

RoPE in detail: Each pair of dimensions \( (2i, 2i+1) \) in Q and K is rotated by an angle \( m\theta_i \) at position \( m \), where \( \theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d} \):

\[ R_\theta^{(m)} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos m\theta_1 & -\sin m\theta_1 & & \\ \sin m\theta_1 & \cos m\theta_1 & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & & \cos m\theta_{d/2} & -\sin m\theta_{d/2} \\ & & & \sin m\theta_{d/2} & \cos m\theta_{d/2} \end{pmatrix} \]

The attention score becomes:

\[ (R_\theta^{(m)} q)^\top (R_\theta^{(n)} k) = q^\top R_\theta^{(n-m)} k \]

This depends only on the relative distance \( n - m \), not the absolute positions. RoPE has become dominant because it: (1) adds no parameters, (2) is compatible with KV caching (each key is rotated once at its position), (3) supports length extension via frequency scaling (NTK-aware, YaRN), and (4) preserves the linear algebra of attention without additive biases.

ALiBi takes an even simpler approach — a fixed linear decay on attention scores that implicitly creates a recency bias. The slopes \( m \) are set geometrically across heads (e.g., \( 2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \ldots, 2^{-H} \) for \( H \) heads), giving different heads different "attention spans." ALiBi generalises well to longer contexts but has been largely superseded by RoPE in practice.

title: Token ID
summary: The integer index that uniquely identifies a token within a tokenizer's vocabulary. Token IDs are the actual numerical inputs to a neural language model — text is first split into subword tokens and then mapped to integer IDs, which are…

papers.citation: 2016 · Sennrich et al. · Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units

sections.body: The integer index that uniquely identifies a token within a tokenizer's vocabulary. Token IDs are the actual numerical inputs to a neural language model — text is first split into subword tokens and then mapped to integer IDs, which are used to look up dense embedding vectors.

sections.title: The full text-to-representation pipeline
sections.body:
1. Raw text: "Hello world"
  2. Tokenization: Split into subword units → ["Hello", " world"]
  3. Vocabulary lookup: Map each token to its integer ID → [15496, 995]
  4. Embedding lookup: Index into the embedding matrix \( E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \) to get dense vectors → \( [\mathbf{e}_{15496}, \mathbf{e}_{995}] \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times d} \)
  5. Add positional encoding and pass to the model

The embedding lookup is mathematically equivalent to multiplying a one-hot vector \( \mathbf{x} \in \{0,1\}^{|V|} \) by the embedding matrix: \( \mathbf{e} = E^\top \mathbf{x} \). In practice, this is implemented as an efficient table lookup (nn.Embedding) rather than a full matrix multiply.

Special token IDs: Every tokenizer reserves specific IDs for control tokens:

TokenPurposeGPT-2 ID
<|endoftext|>End of sequence / document separator50256
[PAD]Padding for batched sequencesVaries by model
[BOS] / [EOS]Beginning / end of sequenceModel-specific

Token IDs are tokenizer-specific: The same text produces completely different IDs under different tokenizers because each tokenizer has its own learned vocabulary and merge rules. This is why a model's tokenizer must always be distributed alongside its weights — they are tightly coupled. You cannot substitute one tokenizer for another without retraining the embedding layer.

Weight tying: Many models (GPT-2, LLaMA, T5) share the embedding matrix between the input embedding layer and the output projection layer (the "language model head"). The output logits for vocabulary position \( v \) are computed as \( z_v = \mathbf{h}^\top \mathbf{e}_v \), where \( \mathbf{h} \) is the final hidden state and \( \mathbf{e}_v \) is the embedding for token \( v \). This reduces parameters by \( |V| \times d \) and acts as a regulariser by constraining the input and output representations to be consistent.

title: Vocabulary Size
summary: The total number of unique tokens in a tokenizer's vocabulary, denoted \( |V| \). Vocabulary size determines the dimensions of the embedding and output projection matrices and governs a fundamental trade-off between sequence compression…

papers.citation: 2019 · Radford et al. · Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
papers.note: Describes GPT-2's byte-level BPE setup and a 50,257-token vocabulary.

papers.citation: 2023 · Touvron et al. · LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
papers.note: Documents LLaMA tokenizer and vocabulary design choices.

sections.body: The total number of unique tokens in a tokenizer's vocabulary, denoted \( |V| \). Vocabulary size determines the dimensions of the embedding and output projection matrices and governs a fundamental trade-off between sequence compression (fewer tokens per text with larger \( |V| \)) and model size (more embedding parameters).

sections.title: How vocabulary size affects the model
sections.body:
• Embedding parameters: The input embedding matrix is \( E \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \). With weight tying (shared input/output embeddings), this is \( |V| \times d \) parameters; without tying, it doubles to \( 2|V| \times d \).
  • Softmax cost: At each generation step, the model computes \( |V| \) logits: \( z = W_{\text{out}} \mathbf{h} \), where \( W_{\text{out}} \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \). For large vocabularies, this becomes a significant fraction of per-token compute.
  • Sequence length: Larger \( |V| \) means each token covers more text on average, reducing sequence length and thus reducing the quadratic attention cost \( O(T^2) \). This is one of the main motivations for larger vocabularies in modern models.
Model\( |V| \)TokenizerEmbedding params (\( d \))
GPT-250,257BPE38.6M (\( d=768 \))
LLaMA 132,000SentencePiece BPE131M (\( d=4096 \))
LLaMA 3128,256tiktoken BPE1.05B (\( d=8192 \))
Gemini256,000SentencePiece—

sections.title: The vocabulary size trade-off in detail
sections.body:
Larger \( |V| \)Smaller \( |V| \)
Shorter token sequences → faster inference (less attention compute)Longer sequences → more attention compute per text
Better coverage of multilingual text and rare termsPoor multilingual efficiency (e.g., GPT-2 tokenizer fragments Chinese into bytes)
Larger embedding matrix (can be >1B params)Smaller embedding table; each token trained on more data
Sparser gradient updates per token (rare tokens trained infrequently)Denser updates but more compositional burden on the model

Multilingual motivation: The trend toward larger vocabularies (32K → 128K → 256K) is largely driven by multilingual efficiency. Older English-centric tokenizers required 3–5x more tokens to represent non-Latin scripts (Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Arabic), wasting context window and increasing inference cost for non-English users. LLaMA 3's 4x vocabulary increase over LLaMA 1 specifically aimed to improve multilingual token efficiency.

Practical consideration — vocabulary size and compute-optimal training: Embedding parameters are "cheap" in FLOPs because they involve only a table lookup (no matrix multiplication). However, they consume memory and can be undertrained: in a corpus of \( D \) tokens with uniform distribution, each token appears \( D/|V| \) times. For \( |V| = 128K \) and \( D = 15T \), each token is seen ~117M times — sufficient for learning, but rare tokens (bottom 10% by frequency) may see far fewer examples.

title: Subword Tokenization
summary: A tokenization strategy that splits text into units between characters and full words — common words remain whole tokens, while rare words are broken into smaller subword pieces. This provides an open vocabulary (any text can be encoded)…

papers.citation: 1994 · Gage · A New Algorithm for Data Compression
2016 · Sennrich et al. · Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units
2018 · Kudo and Richardson · SentencePiece: A Simple and Language Independent Subword Tokenizer and Detokenizer for Neural Text Processing

sections.body: A tokenization strategy that splits text into units between characters and full words — common words remain whole tokens, while rare words are broken into smaller subword pieces. This provides an open vocabulary (any text can be encoded) while keeping sequences reasonably short.

sections.title: Main algorithms
sections.body:
AlgorithmApproachUsed by
Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)Iteratively merge most frequent byte pairsGPT-2/3/4, LLaMA 3
WordPieceMerge pairs that maximise likelihoodBERT
Unigram (SentencePiece)Start large, prune low-probability tokensLLaMA 1/2, T5, XLNet

sections.title: Example (BPE)
sections.body:
"unhappiness" → ["un", "happiness"]  (common prefix + common word)
"transformative" → ["transform", "ative"]
"qwerty" → ["q", "wer", "ty"]  (rare word split finely)

sections.title: Why subword?
sections.body:
• Word-level tokenization cannot handle out-of-vocabulary words and requires huge vocabularies for morphologically rich languages
  • Character-level produces very long sequences (expensive attention) and loses morphological information
  • Subword is the sweet spot: open vocabulary, reasonable sequence length, captures morphological structure

BPE training starts with individual bytes (or characters) as the vocabulary, then repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair until the desired vocabulary size is reached. The merge order defines the tokenization rules. WordPiece is mathematically distinct: it chooses the merge that maximises the likelihood of the training data, effectively maximising \( \Delta = \log P(xy) - \log P(x) - \log P(y) \).

title: Special Tokens
summary: Reserved tokens in a tokenizer's vocabulary that carry structural or control meaning rather than representing natural language text. Special tokens delimit sequences, mark boundaries between roles in chat, and signal the start/end of gen…

papers.citation: 2019 · Devlin et al. · BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
papers.note: Established common special-token conventions like [CLS] and [SEP].

papers.citation: 2022 · Ouyang et al. · Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
papers.note: Helped standardize modern assistant-format special tokens in instruction tuning.

sections.body:
Reserved tokens in a tokenizer's vocabulary that carry structural or control meaning rather than representing natural language text. Special tokens delimit sequences, mark boundaries between roles in chat, and signal the start/end of generation.

TokenPurposeExample
BOS (beginning of sequence)Marks the start of a sequence<s>, <|begin_of_text|>
EOS (end of sequence)Signals generation should stop</s>, <|endoftext|>
PAD (padding)Fills shorter sequences in a batch to equal length[PAD]
SEP (separator)Separates two segments (e.g., premise/hypothesis)[SEP]
CLS (classification)Aggregate representation for classification (BERT)[CLS]
Role markersDelimit system/user/assistant turns in chat<|im_start|>, [INST]

Special tokens are typically excluded from normal tokenization — they cannot appear in regular text and are inserted programmatically. They have dedicated token IDs (often at the beginning or end of the vocabulary).

Practical importance: Using the wrong special tokens or placing them incorrectly causes severe performance degradation. For example, ChatML formats inputs like so before tokenisation:

<|im_start|>system
You are a helpful assistant.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
Hello!<|im_end|>

A chat model expecting these delimiters will behave erratically if given raw text without them. This is why tokenizer.apply_chat_template() exists — to handle special token insertion correctly.

title: Padding Token
summary: A special token used to fill shorter sequences in a batch to match the length of the longest sequence, enabling efficient batched computation on GPUs (which require uniform tensor dimensions). Padding tokens are masked out during attenti…

papers.citation: Standard practice in batched neural network training and inference.

sections.body:
A special token used to fill shorter sequences in a batch to match the length of the longest sequence, enabling efficient batched computation on GPUs (which require uniform tensor dimensions). Padding tokens are masked out during attention and loss computation so they don't affect the model's behaviour.

Given a batch with sequences of varying length:

Sequence 1: [The, cat, sat]           → [The, cat, sat, PAD, PAD]
Sequence 2: [Hello, world, how, are, you] → [Hello, world, how, are, you]

The padding token is excluded from computation via two masks:

  • Attention mask: Set to negative infinity (or a very large negative number) for padding positions before the softmax step in the attention equation: \[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V \] where \( M_{ij} = -\infty \) if position \( j \) is a PAD token. This prevents other tokens from attending to padding since \( \exp(-\infty) = 0 \).
  • Loss mask: Exclude padding positions from the cross-entropy loss so the model isn't penalised for failing to "predict" padding tokens during training.

sections.title: Left vs. right padding
sections.body:
ConventionUsed forReason
Right paddingTraining (encoder models)Standard convention
Left paddingBatched generation (decoder models)All sequences end at the same position, simplifying autoregressive generation by aligning the "next token" predictions

Some decoder-only models (GPT-2) were not trained with a padding token and require one to be added manually for batched inference. A common practice is to reuse the EOS token as the pad token.

title: BOS Token (Beginning of Sequence)
summary: A special token prepended to the input sequence to signal the start of a new text or conversation. It provides a consistent initial context for the model and, in autoregressive architectures, serves as the seed token from which generatio…

papers.citation: Standard practice in sequence models; formalised with BERT's [CLS] (Devlin et al., 2019) and adopted across modern LLMs.

sections.body: A special token prepended to the input sequence to signal the start of a new text or conversation. It provides a consistent initial context for the model and, in autoregressive architectures, serves as the seed token from which generation begins.

Why BOS matters mechanically. In an autoregressive language model, the probability of a sequence is factored as:

\[ P(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = P(x_1 \mid \text{BOS}) \prod_{t=2}^{n} P(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}) \]

Without BOS, the model must predict the first token \(x_1\) with zero context — effectively sampling from a marginal distribution over the vocabulary. The BOS token gives the model a fixed, learned anchor so that \( P(x_1 \mid \text{BOS}) \) is well-calibrated. The BOS embedding is learned during pretraining and encodes a prior over likely first tokens.

sections.title: Common BOS tokens across model families
sections.body:
ModelBOS tokenToken IDNotes
LLaMA 1/2<s>1Always prepended by tokenizer
LLaMA 3<|begin_of_text|>128000Separate from chat turn markers
GPT-2/3None—No BOS; first user token is the start
BERT[CLS]101Functions as BOS; its output used for classification

Interaction with chat templates. In chat-tuned models the BOS token is distinct from role delimiters. For example, LLaMA 3's chat format is:

<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>

You are a helpful assistant.<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

Hello!<|eot_id|>

The BOS appears exactly once at the very start; role markers handle turn boundaries. Omitting BOS or doubling it causes degraded output because the model encounters an out-of-distribution positional pattern at position 0.

Common pitfall: Some tokenizers add BOS automatically (add_bos_token=True), while others do not. When concatenating tokenized segments manually, accidentally including two BOS tokens or zero BOS tokens is a frequent source of bugs.

title: EOS Token (End of Sequence)
summary: A special token that signals the end of a generated sequence. During autoregressive generation, the model emitting the EOS token is the standard stopping criterion — generation halts when EOS is produced (or when a maximum length is reac…

papers.citation: Standard practice in sequence generation; part of all modern tokenizers.

sections.body: A special token that signals the end of a generated sequence. During autoregressive generation, the model emitting the EOS token is the standard stopping criterion — generation halts when EOS is produced (or when a maximum length is reached).

Mechanics of EOS in generation. At each decoding step, the model produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary including EOS. Generation stops when:

\[ \arg\max_v P(v \mid x_{1:t-1}) = \text{EOS} \quad \text{(greedy)} \]

or when EOS is sampled from the distribution (stochastic decoding). With beam search, a beam is marked as complete when it generates EOS; remaining beams continue until all are complete or a length limit is hit.

ModelEOS tokenNotes
GPT-2<|endoftext|>Also used as BOS and PAD (single special token)
LLaMA 1/2</s>Standard XML-style end tag
LLaMA 3 (chat)<|eot_id|>End-of-turn; distinct from end-of-text
BERT[SEP]Marks segment boundaries, not generation stopping

sections.title: Roles of the EOS token
sections.body:
• Stopping criterion: Generation terminates when EOS is sampled.
  • Document boundary in pretraining: EOS separates documents in the training corpus, teaching the model to reset its attention context across unrelated texts. Without this, the model would learn spurious dependencies between adjacent documents.
  • Turn delimiter in chat: In instruction-tuned models, EOS (or a specialised end-of-turn token) marks the end of each assistant response, enabling multi-turn conversation.

EOS vs. end-of-turn. Modern chat models distinguish between end-of-turn (conversation continues with the next speaker) and end-of-sequence (conversation is fully over). LLaMA 3 uses <|eot_id|> for end-of-turn and <|end_of_text|> for end-of-sequence. Confusing these causes the model to either halt prematurely (treating a turn boundary as conversation end) or ramble indefinitely (never seeing a stop signal).

title: Attention Mechanism
summary: A neural network component that computes a weighted sum over a set of values, where the weights are dynamically determined by the compatibility between a query and corresponding keys. Attention allows models to focus on the most relevant…

papers.citation: 2014 · Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio · Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

sections.body: A neural network component that computes a weighted sum over a set of values, where the weights are dynamically determined by the compatibility between a query and corresponding keys. Attention allows models to focus on the most relevant parts of the input, replacing fixed-size bottlenecks with flexible, content-dependent information routing.

Scaled Dot-Product Attention: In modern Transformers, attention is computed via matrix multiplication:

\[ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V \]
  • Q (Queries): The vectors asking for information (what the current token is looking for, shape \(N imes d_k\)).
  • K (Keys): The vectors serving as identity tags for incoming information (what each token holds, shape \(M imes d_k\)).
  • V (Values): The actual content or features to be aggregated, usually the same underlying vectors as the keys (shape \(M imes d_v\)).

The \( \sqrt{d_k} \) scaling factor prevents the dot-products from growing too large and pushing the softmax function into regions with extremely small gradients (the vanishing gradient problem).

sections.title: Historical evolution
sections.body:
YearMilestoneContribution
2014Bahdanau attentionAdditive attention for seq2seq, eliminating the context vector bottleneck
2015Luong attentionMultiplicative (dot-product) attention that is simpler and faster
2017Self-attention (Transformer)Attention as the sole sequence routing mechanism, removing recurrence
2022+FlashAttention, GQAHardware-aware efficiency optimisations for scale and caching

Before attention, seq2seq models compressed the entire input into a fixed-size vector via an RNN encoding. By replacing this with attention — letting the decoder directly look up states across the entire sequence sequence — performance bounded exponentially. Self-attention allowed this same operation to be applied in parallel across the sequence, shifting the field irreversibly to Transformers.

title: Encoder-Decoder Architecture
summary: A neural network architecture with two components: an encoder that processes the input into a contextual representation, and a decoder that generates the output conditioned on this representation. Originally designed for sequence-to-sequ…

papers.citation: 2014 · Sutskever et al. · Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need
2020 · Raffel et al. · Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer

sections.body:
A neural network architecture with two components: an encoder that processes the input into a contextual representation, and a decoder that generates the output conditioned on this representation. Originally designed for sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation, it remains important for tasks with distinct input and output sequences.

The information flow fundamentally addresses mapping a single input space sequence to an arbitrary output space sequence:

  1. Encoder: Processes the full input sequence bidirectionally, producing a tensor of contextualized representations \( H_{ ext{enc}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes d} \).
  2. Decoder: Generates the output autoregressively token-by-token. At each decoding step, Cross-Attention ensures the generated token queries \( H_{ ext{enc}} \).

In the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture:

  • Encoder: Uses bidirectional self-attention (each token accesses all others without masking, enabling deep context).
  • Decoder: Uses causal self-attention (tokens only attend to prior tokens) followed by cross-attention. In cross-attention, \( Q \) comes from the decoder's current state, while \( K, V \) come from the encoder's output \( H_{ ext{enc}} \).
ArchitectureModelsBest for
Encoder-decoderT5, BART, mBART, Flan-T5, WhisperTranslation, summarisation, ASR
Encoder-onlyBERT, RoBERTaClassification, NLU
Decoder-onlyGPT, LLaMA, ClaudeGeneral generation, chat

T5's insight: Raffel et al. (2020) demonstrated that virtually any NLP task can be framed as a text-to-text problem (input text → output text), solidifying the encoder-decoder as a universal architecture. Encoder-decoders retain an advantage for tasks where the entire input is given fully before generation begins (translation, ASR), as the encoder natively exploits full bidirectional context compared to decoder-only prompt causality.

title: Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)
summary: A computational framework for mapping an input sequence of data to an output sequence of potentially different length. Seq2seq models orchestrate an encoder to read the input and a decoder to generate the output iteratively, revolutioniz…

papers.citation: 2014 · Ilya Sutskever, Oriol Vinyals, Quoc Le · Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks

sections.body:
A computational framework for mapping an input sequence of data to an output sequence of potentially different length. Seq2seq models orchestrate an encoder to read the input and a decoder to generate the output iteratively, revolutionizing machine translation, summarisation, and speech recognition applications.

The original RNN-based seq2seq model (Sutskever et al., 2014) operated using two separate LSTMs:

  1. Encoder LSTM: Reads the input sequence iteratively and compresses its entirety into a fixed-length context vector \( \mathbf{c} \) (usually the final hidden state).
  2. Decoder LSTM: Iteratively generates an output token, conditioned strictly on \( \mathbf{c} \), its previous hidden state \( s_{t-1} \), and the prior predicted token \( y_{t-1} \).

The mathematical objective is to factorize the conditional probability of the target sequence:

\[ P(y_1, \ldots, y_m \mid x_1, \ldots, x_n) = \prod_{t=1}^{m} P(y_t \mid \mathbf{c}, y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}) \]

where the decoder updates its hidden state via \( s_t = ext{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, [y_{t-1}; \mathbf{c}]) \).

The Context Bottleneck. The fixed-length vector \( \mathbf{c} \) was a severe architectural bottleneck — all nuanced semantic syntactical information of sentences of arbitrary length had to be crushed into a single narrow vector. Attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) elegantly solved this by discarding the single bottleneck \( \mathbf{c} \) entirely, allowing the decoder to conditionally look up encoder states across the entire sequence at any \( t \). The Transformer (2017) formalized attention as the sole foundation and replaced the recurrent RNN steps entirely.

TaskInputOutput
TranslationEnglish sentenceFrench sentence
SummarisationDocumentSummary
Speech recognitionAudio featuresTranscript
Text-to-text (T5)Any NLP task descriptorTask result

title: Distributed Representation
summary: A representation paradigm where a distinct concept is encoded by an overlapping pattern of activation across many abstract continuous dimensions (neurons), and simultaneously each specific dimension participates in representing many diff…

papers.citation: 1986 · Geoffrey Hinton · Distributed Representations (in Parallel Distributed Processing, Rumelhart & McClelland)

sections.body:
A representation paradigm where a distinct concept is encoded by an overlapping pattern of activation across many abstract continuous dimensions (neurons), and simultaneously each specific dimension participates in representing many different concepts. This breaks starkly from local representation methods where each concept triggers exactly one unique dimension.

In a distributed embedding space, a concept like "cat" is mapped to a dense, continuous vector such as \( [0.2, -0.5, 0.8, 0.1, \ldots] \). Every dimension holistically blends to capture its semantic nuance.

PropertyLocal (one-hot)Distributed (dense)
Dimensionality\( |V| \) dimensions (vocabulary scale)\( d \ll |V| \) (e.g., 768 fixed dimensions)
Similarity MetricOrthogonal; equidistant everywhereCosine similarity mathematically captures meaning
GeneralisationBrittle; strictly rigid symbolsFluid; conceptually shares traits and features
CapacityLinearly 1 concept per dimExponentially \( O(2^d) \) distinct active patterns

Mathematically, the proximity between two learned vector representations evaluates their conceptual alignment. We use cosine similarity to measure this geometric angle:

\[ ext{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = rac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\| \|\mathbf{v}\|} \]

The profound power of distributed parameterizations lies in compositional algebraic generalisation. Models learn specific conceptual dimensions (e.g. royalty, gender) natively. A famous vector arithmetic proof is Word2Vec capturing semantic compositions linearly in space:

\[ \mathbf{v}_{ ext{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{ ext{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{ ext{woman}} pprox \mathbf{v}_{ ext{queen}} \]

Hinton (1986) originally presented distributed representations as the underlying computational principle of human connectionism. It fuels the hidden states, continuous embeddings, and attention algorithms of all deep learning architectures today. Modern mechanistic interpretability hypotheses (Superposition) argue neural networks encode radically more abstract variables than mathematically strictly possible by creatively overlapping codes into these distributed dimensions.

title: Representation Learning
summary: A critical subfield of machine learning dedicated to designing architectures that automatically discover and parse useful features (representations) from raw chaotic unstructured data. This replaces fragile hand-engineered feature pipeli…

papers.citation: 2013 · Yoshua Bengio, Aaron Courville, Pascal Vincent · Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives

sections.body:
A critical subfield of machine learning dedicated to designing architectures that automatically discover and parse useful features (representations) from raw chaotic unstructured data. This replaces fragile hand-engineered feature pipelines with end-to-end differentiable learning, enabling networks to build layered, hierarchical generalizations natively.

Before Deep Learning, traditional algorithms necessitated domain experts explicitly handcrafting features (e.g., SIFT filter banks for computer vision, TF-IDF histograms for document modeling). Representation learning bypasses this using backpropagation:

sections.title: Raw input \(\mathbf{x}\) \(\rightarrow\) Learned mapped features \(f_\theta(\mathbf{x})\) \(\rightarrow\) Task Output \(g_\phi(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}))\)
sections.body: The explicit objective is for \( f_ heta \) to project complicated, linearly intertwined raw data into a continuous latent space \( \mathcal{Z} \) where semantically similar items are mathematically proximate, meaning the simpler downstream classification layer \( g_\phi \) can succeed linearly.

sections.title: Categorization of methods
sections.body:
MethodSignalExamples
SupervisedExplicit Task labelsImageNet pretraining extracting intermediate filter weights
Self-supervisedImplicit masking tasks in dataMasked language modelling (BERT), autoregressive prediction (GPT)
ContrastiveSemantic distance pairsCLIP, SimCLR embeddings
GenerativeLatent reconstructionVariational Autoencoders, Diffusion

Modern representation techniques often exploit sophisticated mathematical losses directly manipulating the latent space geometry. For instance, contrastive frameworks utilize the InfoNCE Loss to attract positive pairings \( z_i, z_i^+ \) while repelling irrelevant pairings:

\[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_i^+)/\tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)} \]

The overarching paradigm of training foundation models on exabytes of data and fine-tuning cleanly verifies the philosophy that pre-training produces a universally applicable representation topology \( f_\theta \), which requires only minute task-specific adaptation later.

title: Latent Space
summary: The lower-dimensional space learned by a model where data points are represented as dense continuous vectors capturing their essential semantic features. "Latent" means hidden — these representations are not directly observed but are mat…

papers.citation: Foundational concept in generative modelling and representation learning.

sections.body:
The lower-dimensional space learned by a model where data points are represented as dense continuous vectors capturing their essential semantic features. "Latent" means hidden — these representations are not directly observed but are mathematically inferred by the model as an intermediate topological representation between input and output manifolds.

Mathematically, an encoder maps an input \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} \) into a latent vector \( \mathbf{z} \in \mathcal{Z} \), discovering the underlying data manifold.

Latent spaces arise in many architectures:

ModelLatent space mechanismDimensionality
AutoencoderDeterministic Bottleneck layer \( \mathbf{z} = f(\mathbf{x}) \)Chosen bottleneck size
Variational Autoencoder (VAE)Gaussian-distributed bottleneck \( \mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \)Chosen bottleneck size
Stable Diffusion (LDM)Compressed frozen VAE image representation64×64×4 (for 512×512 images)
LLM hidden statesToken representations at each layer\( d_{ ext{model}} \) (e.g., 4096)

sections.title: Properties of good latent spaces
sections.body:
• Smoothness: Nearby points represent similar inputs. Mathematical interpolations \( \mathbf{z}_{new} = (1 - lpha)\mathbf{z}_1 + lpha\mathbf{z}_2 \) produce semantically meaningful intermediate outputs (e.g., morphing a face to smiling).
  • Disentanglement: Independent factors of variation in the data (like lighting, shape, rotation) mathematically map to independent orthogonal dimensions in \(\mathcal{Z}\).
  • Completeness: Every sampled continuous point in the assumed space region decodes to a valid, coherent real-world output.

Latent spaces make generative models capable of novel creation: by sampling or taking a "latent space walk" in \( \mathcal{Z} \), we generate new data points unseen during training that conform to the target distribution's structure.

title: Embedding Space
summary: The continuous vector space in which discrete entities (words, tokens, images) are mapped and represented as dense vectors. The geometry of the embedding space structurally encodes semantic relationships: contextually similar items are s…

papers.citation: Concept from Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) and distributed representations (Hinton, 1986).

sections.body:
The continuous vector space in which discrete entities (words, tokens, images) are mapped and represented as dense vectors. The geometry of the embedding space structurally encodes semantic relationships: contextually similar items are situated physically close together, and trajectory directions in the space correspond to abstract concepts.

An embedding space is created by an embedding function \( f: \mathcal{X} o \mathbb{R}^d \) that projects discrete distinct inputs into a dense continuous space. This underlying embedding matrix \( E \) is essentially a learned lookup table.

sections.title: Notable mathematical properties
sections.body:
• Semantic similarity: Mapped via dot-products or angles. \( \cos( ext{embed}( ext{"king"}), ext{embed}( ext{"queen"})) \) converges to nearly \( 1.0 \).
  • Analogical reasoning: Compositional generalizations via geometry: \( ext{embed}( ext{"king"}) - ext{embed}( ext{"man"}) + ext{embed}( ext{"woman"}) pprox ext{embed}( ext{"queen"}) \) (Word2Vec)
  • Clustering: Concepts in the same semantic category implicitly group into tight high-dimensional mathematical spheres.
Embedding typeTarget EntityTypical \( d \) dimension
Word embeddings (Word2Vec)Whole words100–300
Token embeddings (LLMs)Subword tokens (post-BPE)768–8192
Sentence embeddings (Sentence-BERT)Full sentences/paragraphs384–1536
Joint Image/Text embeddings (CLIP)Images & Captions512–768

While "embedding space" and "latent space" overlap, the distinction is usually topological location: embedding space refers specifically to the mapped input representation layer bridging discrete symbols to vectors, whereas latent space more broadly describes internal hidden representations or bottlenecks elsewhere in the architecture.

Derivations

For Word2Vec's Skip-Gram formulation, the objective aligns the embedding space such that a target word \( w_c \) predicts its context word \( w_o \). The probability of a context word is given by a softmax over the embedding dot products:

\[ P(w_o | w_c) = rac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_o}^ op \mathbf{v}_{w_c})}{\sum_{w \in V} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^ op \mathbf{v}_{w_c})} \]

Maximizing this log-likelihood pushes the dot product (similarity) of frequently co-occurring words close together in embedding space.

title: Nearest Neighbor Search
summary: The problem of efficiently finding the top-\(k\) data points in a collection that are structurally closest to a given query point, measured by a distance metric like cosine similarity, inner product, or \(L_2\) Euclidean distance. In mac…

papers.citation: 2017 · Johnson et al. · Billion-scale similarity search with GPUs
papers.note: Introduces FAISS for efficient ANN search.

papers.citation: 2018 · Malkov and Yashunin · Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs

sections.body:
The problem of efficiently finding the top-\(k\) data points in a collection that are structurally closest to a given query point, measured by a distance metric like cosine similarity, inner product, or \(L_2\) Euclidean distance. In machine learning, nearest neighbor search empowers the retrieval engines behind semantic search, RAG, and large-scale recommendation systems.

Given a query vector \( \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^d \) and a dataset \( X = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\} \), the goal is to evaluate the similarity function \( s(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{x}_i) \) efficiently.

Exact search (k-NN) mathematically requires computing all \( N \) distances — costing \( O(Nd) \) time per query. For billions of parameters, this linear scan is prohibitively slow. Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) methods trade a tiny fractional accuracy penalty (recall) for logarithmic speed optimizations:

MethodLogical ApproachEcosystem/Library
IVF (Inverted File)Voronoi cluster vectors (k-means); search only nearby cell partitionsFAISS
HNSWHierarchical Navigable Small World graph (skip-list layered graphs)Hnswlib, FAISS
PQ (Product Quantization)Split vectors into chunks and compress values to look-up tablesFAISS
LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing)Design hash functions where similar vectors collide in the same bucketVarious

Modern ANN methods achieve immense sub-millisecond querying over billions of dense vectors with \( \ge 95\% \) recall (fraction of true accurate nearest neighbors found).

These algorithms inherently bypass the curse of dimensionality constraints found in traditional exact structures (like KD-Trees), which break down entirely past 20-30 dimensions and degrade asymptotically to brute-force \(O(Nd)\).

title: Vector Database
summary: A specialized database infrastructure optimised for persistently storing, indexing, and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings. Vector databases serve as the structural retrieval component of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pip…

papers.citation: Emerged as a distinct product category in 2022–2023, driven comprehensively by RAG pipelines.

sections.body:
A specialized database infrastructure optimised for persistently storing, indexing, and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings. Vector databases serve as the structural retrieval component of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines, enabling immensely scalable approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search across billions of embeddings.

A production vector database guarantees:

  • Vector indexing: Utilizing heavily parallelized ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF-PQ) for sub-millisecond \( O(\log N) \) similarity querying
  • Hybrid Metadata filtering: Efficiently combining vector geometry similarity with exact boolean attribute filters ("find semantic match AND date > 2024"). This is notoriously hard to optimize mathematically.
  • CRUD logic: Dynamically mutating, inserting, and deleting vectors without totally rebuilding the graph index.
  • Persistence and sharding: Hardware scaling, failover distributed across multinode environments.
Database EcosystemArchitecture TypeKey Feature
FAISSCore Library (in-memory)GPU-accelerated, highly optimised barebones math
PineconeManaged Cloud ServiceEnterprise tier serverless deployment
WeaviateOpen-source DatabaseAuto built-in vectorisation abstractions, GraphQL
ChromaOpen-source DatabaseLightweight SQLite-like footprint, great for AI prototyping
pgvectorCore PostgreSQL ExtensionACID vector search nestled inside existing SQL workflows

Unlike traditional relational databases indexing exact scalar structured data via B-trees, or full-text elastic databases indexing tokenized JSON, vector databases mandate traversing geometric topology in non-intuitive high-dimensional floating-point spaces.

title: BM25
summary: A statistical probabilistic ranking function used in information retrieval that scores documents heavily by term frequency and inverse document frequency, while strictly implementing saturation bounds and document length normalization. B…

papers.citation: 1994 · Stephen Robertson, Karen Spärck Jones · Okapi BM25 (TREC experiments)

sections.body:
A statistical probabilistic ranking function used in information retrieval that scores documents heavily by term frequency and inverse document frequency, while strictly implementing saturation bounds and document length normalization. BM25 serves as the indisputable baseline for sparse lexical search and uniquely complements neural dense retrieval methods.

The total BM25 (Best Matching 25) score for a tokenized query \( Q = \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\} \) matching against a target document \( D \) evaluates mathematically to:

\[ \text{BM25}(D, Q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{IDF}(q_i) \cdot \frac{f(q_i, D) \cdot (k_1 + 1)}{f(q_i, D) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|D|}{\text{avgdl}}\right)} \]

where the explicit components govern critical constraints:

  • \( f(q_i, D) \) = Term frequency (raw count of term \( q_i \) in document \( D \)).
  • \( |D| \) and \( \text{avgdl} \) = Document length and average corpus document length.
  • Term Saturation \(k_1\) (\(\approx\) 1.2): Ensures the score increases asymptotically sublinearly. The 15th matching occurrence of the word "apple" yields vanishingly less marginal value than the 1st, unlike naive TF-IDF.
  • Length Normalization \(b\) (\(\approx\) 0.75): Prevents artificially inflating scores simply because a document happens to be immensely longer than the corpus average.
  • Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): Penalizes commonly flooded stop-words (like "the") while radically rewarding rare tokens: \( \text{IDF}(q_i) = \log \frac{N - n(q_i) + 0.5}{n(q_i) + 0.5} \)

In modern RAG pipelines, hybrid search implementations fuse BM25 sparse results with Neural Dense Retrieval (calculating reciprocal rank fusion). BM25 flawlessly captures exact keyword and acronym overlaps that ambiguous semantic embeddings occasionally gloss over.

title: Sparse Retrieval
summary: Information retrieval models that formally represent queries and documents as immensely sparse, high-dimensional vectors spanning the exact size of the vocabulary space, where nearly all entries evaluate to zero. Algorithmic matching hea…

papers.citation: Classic information retrieval paradigm; learned sparse methods: SPLADE (Formal et al., 2021).

sections.body:
Information retrieval models that formally represent queries and documents as immensely sparse, high-dimensional vectors spanning the exact size of the vocabulary space, where nearly all entries evaluate to zero. Algorithmic matching heavily relies on precise lexical overlap occurring exclusively on non-zero dimensions.

In sparse lexical retrieval, every possible mathematical dimension literally corresponds one-to-one with a vocabulary term text index. The vector value reflects that term's extracted lexical consequence (e.g., BM25 metric or TF-IDF weights). The retrieval overlap dot-product evaluates as:

\[ s(q, d) = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} w_{q, i} \cdot w_{d, i} \]

where \( w_{q, i} \) and \( w_{d, i} \) are the strictly matched non-zero weights for token \(i\).

ConstraintSparse lexical retrievalDense neural retrieval
Algebraic SizeVocabulary-sized (e.g., \(250,000\)-D), 99% ZerosCompressed dense geometry (e.g., \(768\)-D)
Matching MechanicsExact character/token overlapCosine similarity in latent space
Vocabulary MismatchFails entirely on synonym abstractionsCaptured dynamically by training
Training CostZero (deterministic formulas)Expensive contrastive deep learning
Storage / ComputeLightning fast Inverted Index HashmapsComplex HNSW ANN graph navigations

Learned sparse retrieval models (like SPLADE or DeepImpact) bridge the gap. They employ transformer neural networks to predict term importance scores explicitly mapping back into a sparse vocabulary constraint framework. These uniquely expand the query intelligently (activating related non-present terms) resolving the synonym penalty while operating on wicked-fast traditional indexing hashes.

title: Dense Retrieval
summary: An advanced neural information retrieval paradigm that functionally encodes chaotic queries and extensive documents as low-dimensional, continuously dense vectors, retrieving results via rapid nearest neighbor geometry search inside an e…

papers.citation: 2020 · Vladimir Karpukhin et al. · Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering

sections.body:
An advanced neural information retrieval paradigm that functionally encodes chaotic queries and extensive documents as low-dimensional, continuously dense vectors, retrieving results via rapid nearest neighbor geometry search inside an embedding space. Distinctively, dense retrieval effortlessly captures semantic similarity, bridging answers even entirely lacking lexical keyword overlap.

The standard architectural backbone is the bi-encoder (or dual-encoder framework):

  • Query Transformer Encoder: \( \mathbf{q} = E_q( ext{query}) \in \mathbb{R}^d \)
  • Document Transformer Encoder: \( \mathbf{d} = E_d( ext{document}) \in \mathbb{R}^d \)
  • Relevance Scoring Kernel: Typically Cosine Similarity \( \cos(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{d}) \) or exact Dot Product \( s = \mathbf{q}^ op \mathbf{d} \)

Critically, the entire corpus of documents is safely pre-computed offline and mathematically frozen into a vector database (like FAISS / Pinecone) via ANN graphs. At runtime, the live LLM only executes a single rapid forward pass to embed \( \mathbf{q} \), ensuring sub-millisecond retrieval latency over millions of nodes.

Training Mechanics: The optimization mathematically hinges upon Contrastive Representation Learning applied over (query \( \mathbf{q} \), relevant doc \( \mathbf{d}^+ \), irrelevant doc \( \mathbf{d}^- \)) tuples. The InfoNCE loss maximizes similarity specifically on the positive pair whilst aggressively penalizing in-batch negatives:

\[ \mathcal{L} = -\log rac{\exp(\mathbf{q}^ op \mathbf{d}^+ / au)}{\exp(\mathbf{q}^ op \mathbf{d}^+ / au) + \sum_{j} \exp(\mathbf{q}^ op \mathbf{d}_j^- / au)} \]

Engineering robust dense retrievers is notoriously sensitive to Hard Negative Mining: injecting difficult negative documents into the batch that visually overlap the query but lack semantic relevance to force the gradient to learn nuance.

ModelArchitecture Strategy
DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval)Two segregated BERT encoders (one mapped to queries, one to passages)
E5, BGE, OpenAI `text-embedding-3`Unified Single Encoder utilizing explicit task-instruction prefixes
ColBERTLate token-level interaction matching (circumvents pooling bottleneck)

title: Grounding
summary: The imperative property ensuring a generative model's synthesized outputs are anchored algorithmically to verifiable external sources (retrieved documents, valid databases, real-world sensory observations) rather than generated dangerous…

papers.citation: 1990 · Harnad · The Symbol Grounding Problem
papers.note: Classic cognitive-science statement of grounding.

papers.citation: 2020 · Lewis et al. · Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
papers.note: Widely used grounding mechanism in modern LLM systems.

sections.body: The imperative property ensuring a generative model's synthesized outputs are anchored algorithmically to verifiable external sources (retrieved documents, valid databases, real-world sensory observations) rather than generated dangerously independent from internalized parameters. Grounded architectures structurally mitigate hallucination phenomena and mathematically bridge truthfulness.

sections.title: Explicit computational approaches to grounding
sections.body:
Engineering MethodMechanism of Grounding
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented)Inject deterministic factual vector search documents sequentially into the prompt layer
Explicit Attribution CheckersForce the model to formally emit inline `[citation x]` references mapping identically to context tokens
Tool Use / Function CallingSubcontract verifiable determinism to API calculators, SQL graphs, or live Python regex
Multi-Modal Spatial GroundingMandate bounding-box coordinates for text linking explicitly in VLMs (Visual Language Models)

Grounding vs. Factuality divergence: A beautifully grounded response expertly cites passages and adheres ruthlessly to its supplied prompt — but remains factually wrong if the source context was originally a delusion. Grounding represents structural validation of the logical pipeline (faithfulness), whereas Factuality inherently demands an assertion matches objective global truth.

Evaluation of Generative Grounding frequently devolves into measuring geometric Attribution: evaluating quantitatively if a specific logical subset proposition \(p_i\) naturally decomposes identically to source paragraph \(\mathcal{S}\).

title: Factuality
summary: The analytical metric evaluating the degree to which a generative model's explicit outputs formally align with objective real-world truth. Crucially, factuality is algorithmically distinct from grounding (citing data architectures) and f…

papers.citation: 2022 · Lin et al. · TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
2023 · Min et al. · FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision

sections.body: The analytical metric evaluating the degree to which a generative model's explicit outputs formally align with objective real-world truth. Crucially, factuality is algorithmically distinct from grounding (citing data architectures) and faithfulness (adhering strictly to provided constraints): a response may be impeccably grounded to an external prompt but still fail factuality if the prompt data was corrupt.

sections.title: Formal Taxonomy of Accuracy Terminology
sections.body:
ConstraintOperational DefinitionCanonical Failure State
FactualityEmitted claim strictly observes real-world objective truth"Paris is currently the formal capital of Germany."
FaithfulnessOutput constraints perfectly subset the injected context dataFabricating external paper conclusions not present in the RAG prompt span
GroundingGeneration maps specifically to trackable architecture or APIsSynthesizing a highly plausible but fundamentally untraceable percentage statistic

sections.title: Why next-token LLMs inherently hallucinate
sections.body:
• Pre-training mathematically maximizes pure cross-entropy likelihood optimization (\(P(x_{n} | x_{1:n-1})\)), not objective truth. They are stochastic simulators of plausible likelihood, generating text statistically designed merely to resemble truth.
  • The Information Funnel: Information past static cutoff weights is lost entirely.
  • Long-Tail Density: Rare facts constitute minimal statistical weight during pre-training stochastic descent mapping, inevitably yielding chaotic inference.

Contemporary Evaluation Frameworks: Benchmarks like TruthfulQA test zero-shot capabilities to avoid deeply embedded human misconceptions. FActScore (Min et al., 2023) analytically parses long-form streams into discrete atomic claim propositions and audits them computationally against Wiki API parameters.

title: Faithfulness
summary: The crucial constraint that a model's generated sequence remains rigorously consistent with, and mathematically supported solely by, the provided input parameter context. A faithful RAG abstraction only executes claims provably contained…

papers.citation: Formal knowledge synthesized around summarisation boundaries
2020 · Maynez et al. · On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

sections.body:
The crucial constraint that a model's generated sequence remains rigorously consistent with, and mathematically supported solely by, the provided input parameter context. A faithful RAG abstraction only executes claims provably contained within the retrieved vector passages; hallucinating external (even if objectively true) information violates faithfulness.

Faithfulness metrics strictly evaluate and penalize conditional generation tasks, demanding algorithms operate formally as constrained transfer functions rather than unbridled creative engines:

Constraint TaskFormal Faithfulness Condition
Abstractive SummarizationTarget sentence representations must entirely subset the input domain graph
RAGInferred claims must explicitly route back to the retrieved vector chunks
Data-to-TextOutput sequence must mirror JSON/tabular inputs with zero syntactic hallucinations
Machine TranslationCross-lingual topological preservation of source semantics

Intrinsic hallucination serves as the direct mathematical antithesis — generating sequences explicitly contradicting or fabricating additions entirely detached from the \( \mathbf{x} \) prompt context.

sections.title: Programmatic Measurement
sections.body:
• NLI Architectures (Natural Language Inference): Evaluate using specific RoBERTa/DeBERTa models to classify logically whether Output \( \mathcal{O} \) is statistically Entailed by Source \( \mathcal{S} \).
  • QAGS (Question Generation & Answering): Force model to dynamically generate queries from its output \( \mathcal{O} \), answer them blindly utilizing \(\mathcal{S}\), and measure divergence.
  • LLM-as-a-Judge: Employ stronger parameter variants (GPT-4) with systematic prompts instructing zero-tolerance contradiction checking.

title: Calibration
summary: The alignment between a model's predicted probabilities and actual outcomes. A well-calibrated model that predicts 70% probability for an event should be correct about 70% of the time. Poor calibration means the model is overconfident or…

papers.citation: 2017 · Guo et al. · On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
1999 · Platt · Probabilistic Outputs for Support Vector Machines and Comparisons to Regularized Likelihood Methods
2005 · Zadrozny and Elkan · Transforming Classifier Scores into Accurate Multiclass Probability Estimates

sections.body:
The alignment between a model's predicted probabilities and actual outcomes. A well-calibrated model that predicts 70% probability for an event should be correct about 70% of the time. Poor calibration means the model is overconfident or underconfident in its predictions.

Formally, a model is perfectly calibrated if:

\[ P(Y = y \mid \hat{P} = p) = p \quad \forall p \in [0, 1] \]

This is visualised with a reliability diagram: bin predictions by predicted probability, plot the actual accuracy in each bin. A perfectly calibrated model lies on the diagonal.

sections.title: Calibration metrics
sections.body:
• Expected Calibration Error (ECE): Weighted average of |accuracy − confidence| across bins
  • Brier score: Mean squared error of predicted probabilities
\[ \text{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{n_b}{N} |\text{acc}(b) - \text{conf}(b)| \]
IssueSymptomFix
OverconfidencePredicts 95% confidence but correct 70% of timeTemperature scaling, label smoothing
UnderconfidencePredicts 60% confidence but correct 90% of timeLess regularisation

Modern deep networks are typically overconfident — they assign very high probabilities to their predictions even when wrong (Guo et al., 2017). For LLMs, calibration relates to the model's ability to express appropriate uncertainty: a well-calibrated LLM should be uncertain about things it doesn't know.

Python 
import numpy as np

def expected_calibration_error(y_true, y_prob, n_bins=10):
    bin_boundaries = np.linspace(0, 1, n_bins + 1)
    ece = 0.0
    for i in range(n_bins):
        bin_mask = (y_prob >= bin_boundaries[i]) & (y_prob < bin_boundaries[i+1])
        if not np.any(bin_mask): continue
        bin_acc = np.mean(y_true[bin_mask])
        bin_conf = np.mean(y_prob[bin_mask])
        ece += np.sum(bin_mask) / len(y_true) * np.abs(bin_acc - bin_conf)
    return ece

title: Temperature Scaling
summary: A simple post-hoc calibration method that divides the model's logits by a single learned scalar \( T \) (temperature) before softmax. Temperature scaling does not change the model's predictions (argmax is preserved) but adjusts the confi…

papers.citation: 2017 · Chuan Guo, Geoff Pleiss, Yu Sun, Kilian Weinberger · On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks

sections.body:
A simple post-hoc calibration method that divides the model's logits by a single learned scalar \( T \) (temperature) before softmax. Temperature scaling does not change the model's predictions (argmax is preserved) but adjusts the confidence of the predicted probabilities.

\[ \hat{p}_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)} \]

The temperature \( T \) is optimised on a validation set to minimise the negative log-likelihood (or ECE).

\( T \)Effect on probabilitiesWhen used
\( T < 1 \)Sharper (more confident)Model is underconfident
\( T = 1 \)UnchangedAlready calibrated
\( T > 1 \)Flatter (less confident)Model is overconfident (common)

Guo et al. (2017) showed that temperature scaling — despite being a single-parameter method — matches or outperforms more complex calibration methods (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) for modern neural networks. The reason: the logits of modern networks are well-ordered (correct class has highest logit) but poorly scaled.

In the LLM context, "temperature" is used differently — as a sampling parameter that controls generation diversity. The concept is the same (scaling logits before softmax), but the purpose differs: calibration aims for accurate probabilities, while sampling temperature aims for desirable generation behaviour.

Python 
import torch.nn.functional as F

def temperature_scale(logits, temperature):
    # Logits shape: (batch_size, num_classes)
    # Temperature: scalar > 0
    return F.softmax(logits / temperature, dim=-1)

title: Batch Normalization
summary: A normalisation technique that standardises the activations of each layer across the batch dimension, using per-batch statistics during training and running averages during inference. Batch normalisation accelerates training, stabilises…

papers.citation: 2015 · Sergey Ioffe, Christian Szegedy · Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

sections.body:
A normalisation technique that standardises the activations of each layer across the batch dimension, using per-batch statistics during training and running averages during inference. Batch normalisation accelerates training, stabilises gradients, and allows higher learning rates.

For a mini-batch \( \mathcal{B} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_m\} \) at a given layer:

\[ \hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_\mathcal{B}}{\sqrt{\sigma_\mathcal{B}^2 + \epsilon}}, \quad y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta \]

where \( \mu_\mathcal{B} \) and \( \sigma_\mathcal{B}^2 \) are the batch mean and variance, and \( \gamma, \beta \) are learned scale and shift parameters that restore the representation's capacity.

NormalisationNormalises overDominant in
Batch normBatch dimensionCNNs (ResNet, etc.)
Layer normFeature dimensionTransformers
RMSNormFeature dimension (no mean)Modern LLMs

sections.title: Why batch norm is not used in Transformers
sections.body:
• Variable sequence lengths make batch statistics unreliable
  • Autoregressive generation processes one token at a time — no batch to normalise over
  • Layer norm (normalising over the feature dimension) is independent of batch size and works with any sequence length

Batch norm was transformative for CNN training (enabling much deeper networks and higher learning rates) but has been entirely superseded by layer norm and RMSNorm in the Transformer era.

Python 
import torch

def batch_norm(x, gamma, beta, eps=1e-5):
    # x shape: (batch, features)
    mean = x.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
    var = x.var(dim=0, unbiased=False, keepdim=True)
    x_hat = (x - mean) / torch.sqrt(var + eps)
    return gamma * x_hat + beta

title: Layer Normalization
summary: A normalisation technique that standardises activations across the feature dimension for each individual example, independent of other examples in the batch. Layer norm is the standard normalisation in Transformers because it works with…

papers.citation: 2016 · Jimmy Lei Ba, Jamie Ryan Kiros, Geoffrey Hinton · Layer Normalization

sections.body:
A normalisation technique that standardises activations across the feature dimension for each individual example, independent of other examples in the batch. Layer norm is the standard normalisation in Transformers because it works with variable sequence lengths and single-example inference.

For a hidden state \( \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \):

\[ \text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{x}) = \gamma \odot \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta \]

where \( \mu = \frac{1}{d}\sum_i x_i \), \( \sigma^2 = \frac{1}{d}\sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2 \), and \( \gamma, \beta \in \mathbb{R}^d \) are learnable parameters.

sections.title: Key difference from batch norm
sections.body:
PropertyBatch normLayer norm
Statistics computed overBatch dimensionFeature dimension
Depends on batch size?YesNo
Works with batch size 1?Poorly (needs running stats)Yes
Train/inference gap?Yes (batch vs. running stats)No

In the original Transformer, layer norm is applied after each sub-layer (post-norm): \( \text{LN}(x + \text{SubLayer}(x)) \). Modern models use pre-norm: \( x + \text{SubLayer}(\text{LN}(x)) \), which improves training stability by ensuring the residual path carries unnormalised signals.

Most modern LLMs have replaced LayerNorm with RMSNorm, which drops the mean subtraction for slightly better efficiency with comparable performance.

Python 
import torch

def layer_norm(x, gamma, beta, eps=1e-5):
    # x shape: (batch, seq_len, features)
    mean = x.mean(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    var = x.var(dim=-1, unbiased=False, keepdim=True)
    x_hat = (x - mean) / torch.sqrt(var + eps)
    return gamma * x_hat + beta

title: Gradient Clipping
