Highly capable, generalizing optimizers tend to exploit any structural gap between the specified objective $R_{\text{proxy}}$ and the intended objective $R_{\text{true}}$.

The Alignment Taxonomy:

  • Outer Alignment: Does the training objective accurately capture the target behavior? (Is the reward function correct? E.g., stopping an AI from optimizing for "clicking 'like'" instead of "user satisfaction".)
  • Inner Alignment: Does the trained model robustly optimize the specified training objective, or has it developed an emergent, surrogate objective (mesa-optimization) that only coincidentally aligns during the training distribution?

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Inner and Outer alignment failure modes happen when the underlying objectives decouple out-of-distribution.
Current approaches: RLHF (learning a reward model from human preferences), Constitutional AI (AI self-critique using principles), and Debate (systems checking each other for scalable oversight).

title: Interpretability
summary: The study of understanding what a machine learning model has learned and why it produces specific outputs, making internal representations and decisions transparent to humans.

papers.citation: 2017 · Doshi-Velez & Kim · Towards A Rigorous Science of Interpretable Machine Learning

sections.body:
The study of understanding what a machine learning model has learned and why it produces specific outputs, making internal representations and decisions transparent to humans.

Interpretability (often conflated with explainability) is critical for debugging edge cases, identifying inherent biases, building trust for deployment, and verifying alignment with human values. The core difficulty is the tension between faithfulness (does the explanation actually represent the exact structural calculation?) and comprehensibility (can a human understand the explanation?).

Hierarchy of Interpretability:

Level Approach Methods
Behavioral Treat model as a black box. Evaluate inputs vs. outputs. Benchmarks, Adversarial Evals
Attribution Attribute the output to specific input features. SHAP, LIME, Saliency Maps
Representation Extract concepts encoded within hidden layer activations. Linear Probes, Logit Lenses
Mechanistic Reverse-engineer the exact causal circuitry to algorithms. Sparse Autoencoders, Activation Patching

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Post-hoc methods trade faithfulness for human comprehensibility. Mechanistic methods seek to bridge the gap.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: A rigorous subfield of interpretability treating neural networks as phenomena to be reverse-engineered into understandable algorithms by identifying specific features, circuits, and computational motifs inside the weights.

papers.citation: 2022 · Olah et al. · In-context Learning and Induction Heads

sections.body:
A rigorous subfield of interpretability treating neural networks as phenomena to be reverse-engineered into understandable algorithms by identifying specific features, circuits, and computational motifs inside the weights.

Mechanistic interpretability (mech interp) approaches models like compiled binary code. The goal is to decompile the raw weights and activations into causal, human-understandable sub-graphs (circuits). This aims to transition AI alignment from behavioral testing to scientific guarantee.

Core Primitives:

  • Features: The fundamental units of meaning. They are represented as directions in the high-dimensional activation space, not necessarily as single neurons (due to superposition).
  • Circuits: Computable sub-networks connecting features. For instance, an Induction Head circuit implements in-context copying by noticing a previous sequence `[A][B]` and predicting `[B]` when seeing `[A]` again.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Induction circuit mechanically explains few-shot learning by tracing causal attention hooks backward.

Standard Pipeline: Researchers use Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle superimposed activations into monosemantic (interpretable) features. Then, they use Activation Patching to causally verify that clamping those features influences the final prediction reliably.

title: Attention Visualization
summary: The practice of rendering the self-attention weight matrices of a Transformer as heatmaps or bipartite graphs to inspect which input tokens the model "looks at" when computing contextual embeddings.

papers.citation: 2019 · Jain & Wallace · Attention is not Explanation

sections.body:
The practice of rendering the self-attention weight matrices of a Transformer as heatmaps or bipartite graphs to inspect which input tokens the model "looks at" when computing contextual embeddings.

In a standard attention mechanism, the scalar weight $A_{ij}$ indicating how heavily token $i$ attends to token $j$ is computed as:

\[ A_{ij} = \frac{\exp\left((Q_i \cdot K_j) / \sqrt{d_k}\right)}{\sum_{m} \exp\left((Q_i \cdot K_m) / \sqrt{d_k}\right)} \]

where $Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k}$ are the query and key vectors. Because these weights sum to $1$ ($\sum_j A_{ij} = 1$), they are naturally visualized as a probability mass flowing between sequence positions.

Frequent Motifs:

  • Local / Diagonal: Tying a token deeply to its immediate neighbors.
  • Vertical Stripes / BOS Sinks: Excess attention "dumped" onto harmless tokens (like [CLS], punctuation, or <s>). This occurs when the head has no useful information to aggregate but must sum its weights to $1$ to satisfy the Softmax.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A causal attention lower-triangular mask typical in decoder-only generative LLMs.

Critical Limitation: Attention is Not Explanation. In multi-layer Transformers, attention weights combine iteratively with dense MLP layers via residual streams. High attention to a token does not intrinsically prove causal importance for the output, as the token's vector might contain features mixed in from other positions dynamically. Activation patching is preferred for causal attribution.

title: Probing (Neural Networks)
summary: A diagnostic technique used to determine whether specific information (e.g., part-of-speech, semantic relations) is encoded within a neural network's internal representations by training a simple, supervised classifier over its frozen hi…

papers.citation: 2019 · Hewitt & Liang · Designing and Interpreting Probes with Control Tasks

sections.body:
A diagnostic technique used to determine whether specific information (e.g., part-of-speech, semantic relations) is encoded within a neural network's internal representations by training a simple, supervised classifier over its frozen hidden states.

If a simple linear classifier can accurately predict a property $y$ from a deep layer's hidden state $h_L$, it acts as compelling evidence that the network has autonomously encoded that feature space in a linearly separable manner during unsupervised pretraining.

Formally, a linear probe minimizes:

\[ \mathcal{L} = \text{CrossEntropy}(y, \text{softmax}(W_{\text{probe}} h_L + b)) \]

where the base model weights yielding $h_L$ are entirely frozen.

The Selectivity Problem:

Hewitt & Liang raised a methodological vulnerability: if one uses a high-capacity probe (like an MLP), the probe might just learn the mapping entirely on its own, memorizing arbitrary patterns rather than identifying structures fundamentally organized by the base model.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A probe extracts semantic tokens from intermediate layers strictly via lightweight, constrained regression.

To assure rigorousness, researchers evaluate Selectivity: the performance gap between a probe predicting true linguistic labels versus a probe predicting completely randomized labels (a "control task"). High selectivity proves the probe is merely reading structure the network independently authored, not imposing its own capability.

title: Logit Lens
summary: An interpretability technique that applies the final unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden layers of a Transformer, converting those intermediate states into next-token predictions to trace how model confidence evolves through the ne…

papers.citation: 2020 · nostalgebraist · interpreting GPT: the logit lens (Blog Post)

sections.body:
An interpretability technique that applies the final unembedding matrix to intermediate hidden layers of a Transformer, converting those intermediate states into next-token predictions to trace how model confidence evolves through the network depth.

The Logit Lens relies on a structural quirk of Transformers: because of the residual stream, every layer's output lives in the same consistent $d$-dimensional embedding space. Therefore, the exact same word unembedding matrix $W_U \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$ used at the very end of the model can technically be multiplied against the residual stream at any arbitrary layer $\ell$.

For an intermediate representation $h_\ell$, the projected vocabulary probabilities are:

\[ \mathbf{p}_\ell = \text{softmax}(W_U h_\ell) \]

Key Observations:

  • Gradual Refinement: The correct output token typically rises in probability steadily across middle layers, rather than abruptly snapping at the end.
  • Crystallization: Sometimes the model "makes up its mind" confidently by layer $L/2$ and the remaining layers simply act as identity pass-throughs.
  • Early Bigram Tracking: The first few layers often naively predict the most common bigram continuation (e.g., seeing "Harry", predicting "Potter" immediately), which deeper layers then refine or override based on complex context.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Logit Lens taps into the residual stream progressively, projecting latent states back into vocabulary space.

The Tuned Lens is an upgrade specifying that early layers operate in a slightly shifted coordinate space; instead of raw $W_U$, an optimal affine translation $W_\ell h_\ell + b_\ell$ is fit algorithmically per layer to yield more calibrated early predictions.

title: Activation Analysis
summary: The family of interpretability techniques that analyze the intermediate dynamic outputs (activations) of neurons or layers during a forward pass, rather than just the static weights.

papers.citation: 2017 · Olah et al. · Feature Visualization

sections.body:
The family of interpretability techniques that analyze the intermediate dynamic outputs (activations) of neurons or layers during a forward pass, rather than just the static weights.

Because weights interact in profoundly non-linear, multifaceted ways, reverse-engineering a neural network from its weights alone is essentially impossible without tracing data flow. Activation analysis treats the firing patterns on specific inputs as the primary object of study.

Core Techniques:

  • Feature Visualization / Maximization: Finding the specific synthetic input (using gradient ascent on the input pixels/tokens) that maximally fires a specific internal neuron to deduce its semantic role (e.g., identifying a "curve detector" in a CNN).
  • Activation Patching / Causal Tracing: The gold standard for causal effect. You freeze the network, pass a corrupted input (e.g., "The Eiffel Tower is in Rome"), and selectively "patch in" specific clean activations (from "The Eiffel Tower is in Paris") to isolate the exact circuit where factual recall occurs.
  • Activation Steering: Computing a mean differential activation vector between two behaviors (e.g., polite vs. toxic outputs) and forcefully injecting that vector into the hidden stream during inference to steer behavior without retraining.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Activation Patching systematically swaps individual layer states to isolate the precise causal locus of a behavior.

title: Sparse Autoencoder (Mechanistic Interpretability)
summary: An auxiliary neural network containing a wide bottleneck subjected to an L1 sparsity penalty, trained ad-hoc on a larger model's internal activations to disentangle superimposed features into monosemantic axes.

papers.citation: 2023 · Cunningham et al. · Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features

sections.body:
An auxiliary neural network containing a wide bottleneck subjected to an L1 sparsity penalty, trained ad-hoc on a larger model's internal activations to disentangle superimposed features into monosemantic axes.

Neural networks suffer from polysemanticity (single neurons firing for multiple unrelated distinct concepts due to superposition). Trying to interpret an individual dense neuron is mathematically incoherent. A Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) solves this by expanding the underlying $d$-dimensional activation $x$ into a much larger $m$-dimensional space ($m \gg d$) where features can align cleanly with individual axes.

SAE Architecture:

An Encoder projects $x$ into the wide bottleneck, and a Decoder reconstructs $\hat{x}$ back into the original space. The critical component is the $L_1$ penalty forcing the bottleneck representation $f(x)$ to be profoundly sparse (mostly zeros).

\[ f(x) = \text{ReLU}(W_{\text{enc}} x + b_{\text{enc}}) \] \[ \hat{x} = W_{\text{dec}} f(x) + b_{\text{dec}} \]

The total loss heavily heavily penalizes the absolute sum of the activations:

\[ \mathcal{L} = \underbrace{\|x - \hat{x}\|_2^2}_{\text{Reconstruction Error}} + \lambda \underbrace{\|f(x)\|_1}_{\text{Sparsity Penalty}} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The $L_1$ penalty forces the SAE to isolate singular semantic concepts in the massive $f(x)$ layer.

Anthropic successfully scaled this to extract 34 million monosemantic parameters from Claude 3 Sonnet, perfectly isolating highly abstract concepts (e.g. "code bugs", "transit infrastructure", "golden gate bridge") inside the features of $f(x)$.

title: Superposition (Neural Networks)
summary: The mathematical phenomenon where a neural network deliberately represents more independent features than it has available dimensions, by embedding features as nearly-orthogonal directions in activation space.

papers.citation: 2022 · Elhage et al. · Toy Models of Superposition

sections.body:
The mathematical phenomenon where a neural network deliberately represents more independent features than it has available dimensions, by embedding features as nearly-orthogonal directions in activation space.

Elhage et al. definitively proved that polysemanticity (neurons firing for multiple mixed concepts) is not an accident of SGD, but a deeply logical compression strategy. If a model needs to track 1,000 distinct semantic features but only has 100 hidden dimensions, it cannot assign one feature per neuron locally.

If features are inherently dense (they all show up at once), compression is impossible. However, semantic features are strictly sparse (the feature "President" and the feature "Lava" rarely occur in the precise same semantic context). Because they are sparse, the network can pack them into high-dimensional space as directions rather than dimensions.

Driven by the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma, high-dimensional spaces boast exponentially vast quantities of "almost orthogonal" vectors. The model superimposes features along these sheer vectors, accepting a minuscule amount of mathematical interference in exchange for vast capacity.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A toy model demonstrating Polysemanticity: extracting Feature 1 inevitably excites fractions of Feature 2 and 5.

title: Foundation Model
summary: A massive, heavily capitalized machine learning architecture trained entirely organically via self-supervision on vast amounts of uncurated data, designed intentionally to be adaptable to tens of thousands of downstream narrow tasks.

papers.citation: 2021 · Bommasani et al. · On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

sections.body:
A massive, heavily capitalized machine learning architecture trained entirely organically via self-supervision on vast amounts of uncurated data, designed intentionally to be adaptable to tens of thousands of downstream narrow tasks.

Coined by the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI) in 2021, the term "Foundation Model" codified the paradigm shift away from task-specific architectures. A singular Foundation Model (like GPT-4, Gemini, or Llama) sits dynamically at the base level of the supply chain, while thousands of software products perform cheap finetuning, prompting, or LoRA adaptations on top of it to resolve localized business tasks.

Core Characteristics:

  • Self-Supervision + Scale: Trained on entire internet scrapes using simple intrinsic heuristics (next-token prediction, objective masking), utilizing tens of thousands of GPUs.
  • Emergence: Exhibiting capabilities entirely absent in smaller variants of identical mathematical architecture (e.g. few-shot analogical reasoning, chain-of-thought logic).
  • Homogenization: Because fine-tuning a base model destroys task-specific architectures economically, the entire AI industry homogenizes around a handful of Foundation Models. Consequently, an exploit, bias, or flaw instantiated in a Foundation Model cascades globally across all derived end-products.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A single monolithic Foundation Model branching recursively via adaptation into narrow utility agents.

title: Scaling Laws
summary: Empirical power-law relationships between a language model's performance (measured by cross-entropy loss) and three core factors: model size (number of parameters \( N \)), dataset size (number of tokens \( D \)), and compute budget (FLO…

papers.citation: 2020 · Kaplan et al. · Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models

sections.body: Empirical power-law relationships between a language model's performance (measured by cross-entropy loss) and three core factors: model size (number of parameters \( N \)), dataset size (number of tokens \( D \)), and compute budget (FLOPs \( C \)).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Log-log plot of test loss vs. compute demonstrating a linear trend, indicative of a power law.

Kaplan et al. (2020) at OpenAI discovered that language model loss follows remarkably smooth power laws over several orders of magnitude:

\[ L(N) \propto N^{-\alpha_N}, \quad L(D) \propto D^{-\alpha_D}, \quad L(C) \propto C^{-\alpha_C} \]

sections.title: Key Findings and The Chinchilla Revision
sections.body:
• Kaplan et al. (2020): Argued that larger models are asymptotically more sample-efficient. Suggested allocating most compute to increasing model size rather than dataset size.
  • Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022): Revised the optimal allocation. They demonstrated that model size and data should be scaled equally. To train a model optimally for a given compute budget, you need about 20 tokens per parameter. This implied existing models like GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens) were massively undertrained on data.

Limitations: Scaling laws accurately predict the cross-entropy loss, but they do not predictably translate to downstream task performance or zero-shot abilities, which often exhibit sudden "emergent" improvements.

sections.title: Derivations
sections.body:
Derivations

Chinchilla optimal allocation: For a compute budget \( C \) (in FLOPs), the optimal model size \( N_{\text{opt}} \) and token count \( D_{\text{opt}} \) scale as:

\[ N_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{a}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{b} \]

where empirical fits yield \( a \approx 0.50 \) and \( b \approx 0.50 \). Since compute requirements for Transformer forward-backward passes are roughly \( C \approx 6ND \), we distribute the budget equally between \( N \) and \( D \).

title: What are emergent capabilities in large language models?
summary: Abilities that are absent or perform at random chance in smaller models, but abruptly appear once a model reaches a critical scale (in parameters or training compute), seemingly jumping discontinuously rather than improving smoothly.

papers.citation: 2022 · Wei et al. · Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models

sections.body: Abilities that are absent or perform at random chance in smaller models, but abruptly appear once a model reaches a critical scale (in parameters or training compute), seemingly jumping discontinuously rather than improving smoothly.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Accuracy on a specific task remains at chance until a threshold scale is reached, where it rapidly improves.

Wei et al. (2022) documented this phenomenon across numerous tasks. While the next-token prediction loss follows smooth power-law scaling, downstream task performance (like 3-digit addition or chain-of-thought reasoning) does not.

sections.title: Notable Emergent Capabilities
sections.body:
• Arithmetic and Logic: Solving multi-step math problems or unscrambling words.
  • Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Reasoning: The ability to benefit from step-by-step reasoning prompts only appears in LLMs larger than ~60B parameters; smaller models' performance degrades when forced to use CoT.

sections.title: The "Mirage" Debate
sections.body: Schaeffer et al. (2023) argued that emergence might be a mirage caused by the observer's choice of metric. Non-linear, discontinuous metrics like "Exact String Match" or "Multiple-Choice Accuracy" require a model to be perfectly correct to score anything. If you change the metric to a continuous measure like Token-Level Brier Score or Log-Likelihood, the models' partial progress becomes visible, and improvements scale smoothly and predictably rather than abruptly.

title: Zero-Shot Learning
summary: The ability of a model to perform a target task using only a natural-language instruction detailing the task, without having been explicitly fine-tuned on the task and without receiving any demonstration examples in its context window.

papers.citation: 2020 · Brown et al. · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

sections.body: The ability of a model to perform a target task using only a natural-language instruction detailing the task, without having been explicitly fine-tuned on the task and without receiving any demonstration examples in its context window.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Zero-shot generalization: solely relying on the instruction to produce the output.

Zero-shot learning tests a model's intrinsic ability to generalize its pre-trained knowledge to novel formatting and logic requirements. It heavily relies on the model having implicitly gathered similar data patterns during massive unsupervised pre-training.

sections.title: Comparisons of In-Context Learning
sections.body:
Setup Examples Given Context Usage
Zero-Shot \( k = 0 \) Instruction + Input query
One-Shot \( k = 1 \) Instruction + 1 Example + Input
Few-Shot \( k \ge 2 \) Instruction + Multiple Examples + Input

Enhancement via Instruction Tuning: Because raw base-LLMs are trained solely for next-token prediction on web text, their raw zero-shot ability is weak (they naturally want to simply continue the document). **Instruction Tuning (SFT) / FLAN** fine-tunes the model explicitly on a dataset of instruction-response pairs to drastically boost its zero-shot interaction capabilities.

Vision Domain Distinction: In Computer Vision, Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL) has a slightly different meaning: classifying unseen test classes by leveraging side-information or semantic embeddings (like text descriptions or attributes) mapping image features to a shared latent space.

title: One-Shot Learning
summary: The ability of an algorithm or model to learn information about a task or object from a **single demonstration example** (or a very small handful), mimicking human cognitive abilities to rapidly acquire new concepts.

papers.citation: 2015 · Koch et al. · Siamese Neural Networks for One-shot Image Recognition

sections.body:
The ability of an algorithm or model to learn information about a task or object from a **single demonstration example** (or a very small handful), mimicking human cognitive abilities to rapidly acquire new concepts.

In modern ML, "One-Shot" takes distinct forms depending on the domain:

1. Natural Language Processing (LLMs):

Providing exactly one input-output pair in the text prompt to condition the model's generation format via in-context learning.

Instruction: Classify sentiment.
Example: "I loved the food!" -> Positive
Input: "The service was awful." ->

2. Computer Vision / Meta-Learning:

Learning a classifier robust enough to categorize novel classes given just one image per new class. Traditional models (like deep CNNs) severely overfit on a single example, so One-Shot Learning relies on Metric Learning or Meta-Learning architectures:

  • Siamese Networks: Train two identical feature extractors to output a distance metric \( D(x_1, x_2) \). To classify a new "1-shot" image, measure its distance to the single support example.
  • Prototypical Networks: Encode the single support example into an embedding space; new queries are classified based on nearest-neighbor distance to the single prototype.
  • MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning): Train the network's initialization such that a single gradient descent step on one example effectively adapts the weights to the new class without destroying generalization.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Siamese architecture learning representation for one-shot computer vision inference.

title: Transfer Learning
summary: The core technique of taking knowledge learned by a model on a large, generic source dataset/task and adapting (transferring) that learned representation to perform well on a smaller, more specific target task.

papers.citation: 2014 · Yosinski et al. · How Transferable are Features in Deep Neural Networks?

sections.body: The core technique of taking knowledge learned by a model on a large, generic source dataset/task and adapting (transferring) that learned representation to perform well on a smaller, more specific target task.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Transferring representations to sidestep the requirement for massive target-domain datasets.

Rather than randomly initializing weights \( \theta \) and training from scratch, we initialize the model with weights \( \theta_{src} \) pre-trained on a massive source domain (like ImageNet or the entire web). This enables deep networks to converge faster, generalize better, and avoid overfitting when target data is scarce.

sections.title: Modes of Transfer
sections.body:
Method Mechanism
Linear Probing (Feature Extraction) Freeze the entire feature backbone. Swap out the final head and train only the new linear classifier head.
Full Fine-Tuning Initialize with pre-trained weights, then update all weights end-to-end on the target task.
Parameter-Efficient (PEFT) Freeze the backbone but inject small learned modules (like LoRA adapters). Huge memory savings.
Why it works (Vision): Lower layers in CNNs behave like generic Gabor filters (detecting edges, color blobs) applicable to any visual task. Higher layers capture task-specific semantic parts.

Negative Transfer: Occurs when the source domain is so intrinsically unrelated or contradictory to the target domain that initializing with pre-trained weights actually degrades target performance worse than training from scratch.

title: Data Augmentation
summary: Artificially expanding the size and diversity of a training dataset by applying label-preserving programmatic transformations to the existing data points, acting as a strong implicit regularizer.

papers.citation: Data Augmentation

sections.body: Artificially expanding the size and diversity of a training dataset by applying label-preserving programmatic transformations to the existing data points, acting as a strong implicit regularizer.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Creating varied training instances forces the model to learn invariant representations.

By preventing the network from seeing the exact same pixel values twice, data augmentation forces the model to learn fundamental concepts (e.g., shape of a cat) rather than memorizing high-frequency noise or exact pixel coordinates, massively reducing overfitting.

sections.title: Advanced Augmentations
sections.body:
• Mixup: Creating purely synthetic examples and softly labeled targets by interpolating between two unrelated images. \( \tilde{x} = \lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2 \) and \( \tilde{y} = \lambda y_1 + (1-\lambda)y_2 \).
  • CutMix: Pasting a bounding box patch of Image B onto Image A, mixing labels proportionally to the box area.
  • AutoAugment / RandAugment: Automating the search for the optimal sequence and magnitude of augmentations (e.g., shear, scale, contrast).

Modality Differences: Augmentation is universally essential in Computer Vision and Audio (time stretching, pitch shifting, noise injection). It is fundamentally more difficult in NLP because changing a single word (e.g., synonym replacement or dropping words) can completely destroy or invert the semantic meaning of a sentence.

title: Active Learning
summary: A semi-supervised framework where the learning algorithm is allowed to interactively interrogate a human oracle, selectively asking it to label only the most profoundly informative data points from a large pool of unlabeled data.

papers.citation: Active Learning

sections.body:
A semi-supervised framework where the learning algorithm is allowed to interactively interrogate a human oracle, selectively asking it to label only the most profoundly informative data points from a large pool of unlabeled data.

In domains where obtaining unannotated data is cheap (e.g., scraping medical scans, autonomous driving footage) but expert human labeling is extremely expensive, Active Learning aims to achieve maximum model accuracy utilizing the absolute minimum number of labels.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The Active Learning feedback loop querying human annotation for the most ambiguous samples.

sections.title: Querying Strategies
sections.body:
• Uncertainty Sampling: The most common approach. The model asks for labels on samples where its predictive confidence is the lowest. Measuring proximity to a decision boundary, or calculating entropy: \( H(X) = - \sum p_i \log p_i \).
  • Query-By-Committee (QBC): Maintain an ensemble of models. The strategy queries data points where the variance or disagreement among the ensemble members' predictions is highest.
  • Expected Model Change: Query the instance that would impart the largest magnitude gradient update to the current model parameters.
  • Core-set / Diversity Sampling: Query samples across highly segregated multi-dimensional clusters to map the entire space uniformly, avoiding redundant dense regions.

title: Classification Head
summary: An interchangeable classification layer (typically a fully connected Linear network + Softmax) affixed to the very top outputs of a frozen pre-trained feature backbone network.

papers.citation: Classification Head

sections.body:
An interchangeable classification layer (typically a fully connected Linear network + Softmax) affixed to the very top outputs of a frozen pre-trained feature backbone network.

Deep Learning networks conceptually isolate into a Feature Extractor Backbone (which processes raw input \( X \) into a dense representation vector \( h \)) and the Task Head (which maps \( h \) into human-interpretable logits).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: The classification head receives embedding outputs to yield specific predictions.

Mechanism: Given backbone representation vector \( h \in \mathbb{R}^d \), the head compresses it to the exact number of classes \( C \):

\[ z = W \cdot h + b, \quad \hat{y} = \text{softmax}(z) \]

where \( W \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times d} \).

sections.title: Standard Implementations
sections.body:
• Vision Models (ResNet, ViT): Often perform Global Average Pooling on the final convolutional features to obtain a 1D vector \( h \), then attach the classification head.
  • NLP Models (BERT): An explicit `[CLS]` (Classification) token is placed at the very start of the sequence. The model forces the entire sentence's contextual interpretation into the final hidden representation of this `[CLS]` token. The linear head connects exclusively to this token's dimension.

title: Softmax Head
summary: The specialized final unembedding layer combined with a softmax activation attached to an Auto-Regressive Language Model specifically mapped to output a bounded probability distribution over the entire vocabulary.

papers.citation: Softmax Head

sections.body:
The specialized final unembedding layer combined with a softmax activation attached to an Auto-Regressive Language Model specifically mapped to output a bounded probability distribution over the entire vocabulary.

Often utilized interchangeably with "Language Modeling Head", it serves the precise role of decoding the neural network's internal dense hidden state space dimension \( d \) back into discrete word/token identifiers.

sections.title: Mathematical Formulation
sections.body:
For a final hidden state vector \( h \) and vocabulary \( V \):

\[ P(w_i \mid \text{context}) = \text{Softmax}(W_{U} \cdot h) = \frac{\exp(e_i^T h / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{|V|} \exp(e_j^T h / \tau)} \]

where \( W_{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{|V| \times d} \) is the unembedding matrix, \( e_i \) represents the transpose row of the specific token, and \( \tau \) is decoding temperature.

sections.title: Critical Engineering Specifics
sections.body:
• Weight Tying: To drastically cull parameter bloat, many models (GPT-2, canonical Transformers) force the Softmax Unembedding matrix \( W_U \) to be identically transposed to the original Input Embedding Matrix \( W_E \). I.e., \( W_U = W_E^T \).
  • Massive Computational Bottleneck: Executing this multiplication across an enormous token vocabulary (e.g. 100,000+ words) forces the LM head parameters to become a massive constraint memory-wise.
  • Logit Lens Interpretability: Because \( W_U \) acts as a clean mapping from embedding space to human text, interpretability researchers will "trick" the model by projecting middle-layer activations directly through the LM head to analyze what the model believes the next token is halfway through execution.

title: Beam Search
summary: A deterministic text generation decoding heuristic that maintains a set width of the top-\( k \) most probable overall sequences at each generating step rather than aggressively locking into just the singular most probable next word (gre…

papers.citation: Beam Search

sections.body: A deterministic text generation decoding heuristic that maintains a set width of the top-\( k \) most probable overall sequences at each generating step rather than aggressively locking into just the singular most probable next word (greedy search).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Beam Search with Beam Width \( k = 2 \). Lower probability paths are consistently pruned to manage combinatorics.

In greedy decoding, a model picks the best token, but sometimes a slightly suboptimal word early on mathematically allows for an overwhelmingly superior sequence of words later on. Beam Search circumvents this blind spot by simultaneously tracking multiple paths.

At time step \( t \):

  1. Expand all \( k \) current sequence hypotheses against the entire vocabulary.
  2. Calculate cumulative sequence log-probabilities: \( \text{Score}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^t \log P(w_i \mid w_{1..i-1}) \).
  3. Strictly prune and discard all sequences down to only the highest scoring \( k \) paths.

Length Penalty Problem: Because log-probabilities are continuously negative, longer sequences organically suffer a heavy score reduction purely for being long. To combat this, Beam Search almost always includes a normalization factor \( \alpha \) to penalize shortness, dividing the score by the total token length.

While standard for Machine Translation and rigidly structured NLG tasks, Beam Search is almost entirely abandoned in modern generative conversational LLMs in favor of stochastic nucleus sampling (Top-P) because Beam Search notoriously produces very safe, bland, and repetitive text loops devoid of human-like diverse novelty.

title: Repetition Penalty
summary: A decoding-time heuristic modification algebraically applied to token logits that penalizes the probability of generating tokens that have already appeared in the current generation or prompt, mitigating the severe autoregressive failure…

papers.citation: 2019 · Keskar et al. · CTRL: A Conditional Transformer Language Model for Controllable Generation

sections.body:
A decoding-time heuristic modification algebraically applied to token logits that penalizes the probability of generating tokens that have already appeared in the current generation or prompt, mitigating the severe autoregressive failure mode of degenerate repetition loops.

Standard language models frequently fall into cyclic repetition (e.g., "I don't know, I don't know, I don't know..."). This occurs because predicting a token naturally increases the likelihood of predicting context-adjacent tokens, creating a runaway positive feedback loop once a phrase repeats even once.

Implementation (Keskar et al., 2019): Before the softmax distribution is calculated, the raw logit \( z_i \) of every token \( i \) that is already present in the generated sequence is penalized by a multiplicative factor \( \theta > 1 \) (typically 1.1 to 1.3):

\[ z_i' = \begin{cases} z_i / \theta & \text{if } z_i > 0 \text{ and } i \in \text{generated} \\ z_i \cdot \theta & \text{if } z_i < 0 \text{ and } i \in \text{generated} \\ z_i & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Because "dog" was in the context, its positive logit is divided by \( \theta \). A negative logit like "cat" (if it were in context) would be multiplied. Unseen tokens like "park" are untouched.

sections.title: Variations
sections.body:
Method Equation
Frequency Penalty \( z_i' = z_i - \alpha \cdot \text{count}(i) \) — penalty scales up the more it's repeated.
Presence Penalty \( z_i' = z_i - \beta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{count}(i) > 0] \) — constant penalty just for existing.

Tuning is delicate. High repetition penalties severely degrade human-like syntax because structural words (the, a, is) are naturally meant to be repeated heavily.

title: Encoder (Transformer)
summary: The half of the original Transformer architecture dedicated definitively to understanding : it processes the entirety of an input sequence bidirectionally in parallel using unmasked multi-head self-attention to generate deeply contextual…

papers.citation: 2017 · Vaswani et al. · Attention Is All You Need

sections.body: The half of the original Transformer architecture dedicated definitively to understanding: it processes the entirety of an input sequence bidirectionally in parallel using unmasked multi-head self-attention to generate deeply contextualized vector representations of every token.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: A single standard Transformer Encoder block layout. There is NO causal masking.

Inside the Encoder, the Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA) allows every single token to query and attend to every other token in the sequence simultaneously. The attention matrix is completely dense/unmasked. Because of this, it can flawlessly resolve forward and backward dependencies like pronouns or grammar structures.

sections.title: Encoder-Only Models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa)
sections.body:
By discarding the Decoder half entirely, you create an architecture strictly meant for analyzing text and outputting classification/regression labels. BERT trains this encoder via Masked Language Modeling (MLM)—randomly hiding tokens and forcing the bidirectional encoder to predict them based on surrounding forward and backward context.

Encoders are overwhelmingly superior to decoders at tasks that require reading comprehension, like Sentiment Analysis, Named Entity Recognition, or producing dense text embeddings (sentence-transformers).

title: Decoder (Transformer)
summary: The component of a Transformer architecture responsible for generative sequence modeling . It enforces autoregressive generation by using causal (masked) self-attention to strictly prevent tokens from looking forward into the future.

papers.citation: Decoder (Transformer)

sections.body: The component of a Transformer architecture responsible for generative sequence modeling. It enforces autoregressive generation by using causal (masked) self-attention to strictly prevent tokens from looking forward into the future.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Left: Modern Decoder-only block (GPT family). Right: Original Sequence-to-Sequence Decoder block containing the Cross-Attention mechanism.

sections.title: Mechanism of Causal Masking
sections.body:
To prevent cheating during training (predicting word \( t \) by just looking at word \( t+1 \)), an upper-triangular matrix of \( -\infty \) is added to the pre-softmax attention query-key dot products. This forces token \( i \) to only attend to tokens \( j \le i \).

\[ \text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V \]

where \( M_{ij} = -\infty \) for \( j > i \).

sections.title: Variations
sections.body:
• Encoder-Decoder Decoders (T5, BART): The decoder features an extra Cross-Attention layer right after the Masked Self-Attention. It uses its own hidden states as Queries (Q) but pulls Keys (K) and Values (V) from the final output of the isolated Encoder block. Ideal for Translation/Summarization.
  • Decoder-Only (GPT, Llama, Claude): The entire model is just decoders. Prompt instructions are simply placed at the beginning of the sequence. It's vastly easier to scale and optimize via KV-Caching, leading to its total dominance in the LLM era.

title: Neural Language Model
summary: An autoregressive model that estimates the probability distribution over a sequence of words using Neural Networks, resolving the curse of dimensionality found in naive statistical N-Gram models by leveraging dense, continuous vector emb…

papers.citation: 2003 · Bengio et al. · A Neural Probabilistic Language Model

sections.body:
An autoregressive model that estimates the probability distribution over a sequence of words using Neural Networks, resolving the curse of dimensionality found in naive statistical N-Gram models by leveraging dense, continuous vector embeddings.

Before 2003, statistical Language Models simply counted word occurrence frequencies in text (N-Grams) and calculated Markov probabilities. If an exact 4-word phrase was never seen in training (which happens constantly due to the combinatorial explosion of language), its probability defaulted to zero.

Bengio's 2003 breakthrough introduced the Neural Probabilistic Language Model. By mapping every word vocabulary id \( w_i \) to a dense continuous vector (Word Embedding) via a lookup matrix \( C(w_i) \), similar words (like "dog" and "cat") are plotted close together in semantic space. Thus, if the network learned that "the dog is running", it will mathematically generalize to assign high probability to "the cat is running" even if it never saw that phrase during training.

sections.title: The Fundamental Objective
sections.body:
A Neural Language Model calculates the joint probability of a sequence as the product of conditional probabilities. The network is trained with Cross-Entropy Loss to predict the very next token:

\[ P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^T P(w_t \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{t-1}; \theta) \]
Architecture Timeline Context Mechanism
Feedforward NN (2003) Fixed-width sliding window. Context \( n \) is hardcoded.
RNN / LSTM (2010-2015) Hidden state recurrently carried forward. Theoretically infinite context, but gradients vanish.
Transformer (2017+) Self-attention directly queries any historical token without sequential decay. Massive parallelization.

title: Semantic Space
summary: A continuous, high-dimensional mathematical vector space where human concepts (words, sentences, or images) are mapped as points such that geometric relationships like spatial proximity and directional vectors directly correspond to sema…

papers.citation: 2013 · Mikolov et al. · Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space

sections.body: A continuous, high-dimensional mathematical vector space where human concepts (words, sentences, or images) are mapped as points such that geometric relationships like spatial proximity and directional vectors directly correspond to semantic meaning and relationships.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Translating the "gender" vector from "King" geometrically lands on "Queen". Spatial parallelism encodes semantic logic.

Semantic spaces computationally realize the Distributional Hypothesis of linguistics (Firth, 1957): "You shall know a word by the company it keeps." Because similar terms appear in similar textual contexts across the internet, embedding networks assign them similar coordinates.

sections.title: Mathematical Properties
sections.body:
• Cosine Similarity: Meaning similarity is measured via the angle between vectors (Cosine Similarity) rather than Euclidean distance to mitigate frequency sizing effects.
  • Compositional Algebra: The space enables linear arithmetic. Most famously from Mikolov's Word2Vec: \( \vec{V}_{\text{King}} - \vec{V}_{\text{Man}} + \vec{V}_{\text{Woman}} \approx \vec{V}_{\text{Queen}} \)
  • Cross-Lingual & Cross-Modal: Encoders like CLIP map images and text into the exact same unified semantic space to perform Zero-Shot classification and image retrieval.

Static vs. Contextual Spaces: FastText and Word2Vec are Static; the word "bank" occupies exactly one coordinate. Transformer embeddings (BERT, GPT) are Contextual; the output vector for "bank" dynamically moves through the semantic space based on the surrounding sentence (river vs. financial).

title: Information Retrieval
summary: The academic and engineering discipline of searching for information in a document, or searching for documents themselves within a massive unstructured repository in order to satisfy a specific user query or information need.

papers.citation: Information Retrieval

sections.body:
The academic and engineering discipline of searching for information in a document, or searching for documents themselves within a massive unstructured repository in order to satisfy a specific user query or information need.

While IR is decades old, it has recently become the foundational pillar of the RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) ecosystem for LLMs, allowing models to sidestep hallucination by verifying dynamic facts against a search database mid-generation.

sections.title: The Two Dominant Paradigms
sections.body:
Method Mechanism Pros & Cons
Sparse Retrieval (Lexical) Term-frequency statistics like BM25 and TF-IDF against an Inverted Index. Extremely fast. Interpretable. Matches exact jargon/IDs. Terrible at synonyms or semantic intent.
Dense Retrieval (Semantic) Passing queries and documents through an Embedding Model (e.g., Bi-Encoder) and performing Approximate Nearest Neighbor via Cosine Similarity in Semantic Space. Understands profound context and synonyms. Slow. Fails on exact arbitrary alphanumeric keyword lookups.
Hybrid Search: State-of-the-Art systems use both algorithms parallelly, merging their sorted ranking lists mathematically utilizing Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).

sections.title: Evaluation Metrics
sections.body:
• Precision@k: The percentage of the retrieved top-\k documents that are actually relevant.
  • Recall@k: The percentage of total relevant documents existing in the database that successfully appeared in the top-\k.
  • NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain): A metric assigning heavy value to putting the highly relevant results at the very top (Rank 1/2) as opposed to Rank 9/10, modeling human attention span.

title: Logit Adjustment
summary: A suite of techniques executed immediately prior to the Softmax function that add or subtract specific algebraic biases directly to the raw output logits, enabling post-hoc control, debiasing, or constraint enforcement without necessitat…

papers.citation: 2021 · Menon et al. · Long-Tail Learning via Logit Adjustment

sections.body:
A suite of techniques executed immediately prior to the Softmax function that add or subtract specific algebraic biases directly to the raw output logits, enabling post-hoc control, debiasing, or constraint enforcement without necessitating any model retraining.

The beauty of the neural network logit space \( z_i \) is that it is fundamentally additive. Intervening directly on logits is a fast, computationally negligible, interpretation-friendly method to override standard behavior.

sections.title: 1. Correcting Long-Tail Class Imbalance (Menon et al., 2021)
sections.body:
Standard training forces a neural network to wildly over-predict the majority class. If you know the prior training distribution probabilities \( \pi_y \) for every class \( y \), you can subtract the log-prior from the logit at evaluation time to construct a Bayes-Optimal balanced classifier algebraically:

\[ z_y' = z_y - \tau \log \pi_y \]

Rare classes possess tiny negative logarithms, making \( - \log \pi_y \) a massive positive value that pushes up the raw logit score, saving the class.

sections.title: 2. Autoregressive Constrained Decoding
sections.body:
To mathematically force an LLM to output valid JSON or adhere to a specific grammar, parsers (like Outlines/Guidance) run a finite state machine algorithm mid-generation. If generating a specific alphabetical token violates the schema, the platform simply overwrites that token's logit:

\[ z_{\text{invalid}} = -\infty \]

Post-softmax, the probability is precisely \( 0.0 \).

sections.title: 3. Advanced Inference Controls
sections.body:
• Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG): Very common in Diffusion models (Stable Diffusion) but extending to text. Subtracts unconditional logits from conditional logits \( (Z_{cond} - Z_{uncond}) \cdot W \) to force the generator to amplify whatever uniquely defines the prompt.
  • Contrastive Decoding: Subtracting a "small dumb model's" logits from a "large smart model's" logits to force the large model to output only sophisticated, high-quality tokens.
  • Repetition Penalties: Dividing or subtracting generated token logits via simple heuristics.

title: Softmax Normalization
summary: The standard algebraic function used as the final layer in classification neural networks, responsible for mapping a vector of arbitrarily scaled real-valued numbers (logits) into a mathematically valid, normalized probability distribution.

papers.citation: Softmax Normalization

sections.body:
The standard algebraic function used as the final layer in classification neural networks, responsible for mapping a vector of arbitrarily scaled real-valued numbers (logits) into a mathematically valid, normalized probability distribution.

Given a vector of raw logits \( z \) output from the last layer, the Softmax function for the \( i \)-th class is:

\[ \sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \]

By exponentially amplifying \( e^{z} \), it ensures all values are strictly positive. The division by the denominator sum guarantees the entire vector adds up to precisely \( 1.0 \).

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Softmax heavily amplifies the highest value due to exponential operations.

sections.title: Translation Invariance Trick (Numerical Stability)
sections.body: Exponentiating standard model logits regularly causes \( e^{300} \) `float32` Overflow (Infinity/NaN errors) during code implementation. Because Softmax divides the exponent by the algebraic sum array, subtracting a uniform constant from all \( z \) values identically preserves the output probabilities. Frameworks like PyTorch execute a log-sum-exp trick: subtract the maximum logit \( \max(z) \) from every input prior to exponentiation.

sections.title: Temperature Scaling
sections.body:
Dividing all logits by a temperature scalar \( \tau \) before softmax modifies distribution shape:

\[ \sigma(z_i; \tau) = \frac{e^{z_i / \tau}}{\sum_j e^{z_j / \tau}} \]
  • High \( \tau \) (\( > 1 \)): Flattens the distribution towards uniform entropy. Extensively used in Knowledge Distillation arrays (Hinton).
  • Low \( \tau \) (\( < 1 \)): Artificially sharpens the dominant highest values. Approaching \( \tau = 0.0 \) renders Softmax to a strict `argmax` one-hot configuration.

title: Over-Parameterization
summary: The mathematical paradigm defining modern Deep Learning where a model houses dramatically more trainable parameters than the total number of data training points it sees, yet contradicts classical statistical theory by demonstrating elit…

papers.citation: 2019 · Belkin et al. · Reconciling modern machine-learning practice and the bias-variance trade-off

sections.body: The mathematical paradigm defining modern Deep Learning where a model houses dramatically more trainable parameters than the total number of data training points it sees, yet contradicts classical statistical theory by demonstrating elite generalization rather than catastrophic overfitting.

sections.title: Illustration
sections.body: Belkin's "Double Descent" curve showing test error decreasing as networks grow vastly beyond classical limits.

Classical statistical theory posits the Bias-Variance Trade-off: simpler algorithms underfit (bias), complex algorithms eventually fit exactly through noise (overfit/variance), forming a U-shape curve.

However, Belkin et al. (2019) quantified the Double Descent phenomenon. Once a neural network reaches the "Interpolation Threshold" (possessing just enough parameters to blindly memorize and perfectly fit 100% of training data exactly), adding even *more* parameters causes Test Error to ironically plummet a second time.

sections.title: Reasons for Generalization in Over-Parameterization
sections.body:
• Implicit Regularization: Gradient Descent implicitly prefers the widest, smoothest, and most minimal-norm solutions among the theoretically infinite configurations that yield zero training loss.
  • Lottery Ticket Hypothesis: The bigger the network randomly initialized, the far higher probability it contains a smaller "sub-network" perfectly positioned to rapidly optimize the task logic gradient.
  • Smooth Loss Landscaping: Huge parameter dimensions provide optimizer routes to navigate around gradient dead-ends, reducing local minima traps.

title: Domain-Specific Pretraining
summary: The secondary phase executed immediately following general internet pre-training, where a Language Model is trained autoregressively on a gigantic specialized corpus (e.g. Biomedical Data) utilizing self-supervised loss, prior to supervi…

papers.citation: 2020 · Gururangan et al. · Don't Stop Pretraining: Adapt Language Models to Domains and Tasks

sections.body:
The secondary phase executed immediately following general internet pre-training, where a Language Model is trained autoregressively on a gigantic specialized corpus (e.g. Biomedical Data) utilizing self-supervised loss, prior to supervised instruction tuning.

Also recognized under the acronym **DAPT** (Domain-Adaptive PreTraining). While standard generic models like baseline Llama 3 comprehend general English syntax perfectly, they mathematically drown trying to reason through esoteric hyper-academic syntax, advanced Python syntax trees, or clinical genomic abbreviations.

sections.title: The 3-Step LLM Training Lifecycle
sections.body:
Phase Data Type Goal
1. General Pre-Training 1-20 Trillion Tokens (CommonCrawl Web) Language syntax, grammar, general facts.
2. Domain Pre-Training 500 Billion Tokens (PubMed / GitHub / LexisNexis) Injecting niche lexicon, professional syntax structures, and deep context alignment.
3. Fine-Tuning (SFT / RLHF) 10k - 100k curated Q&A Pairs Instruction following, formatting, and safety guardrails.

sections.title: Mechanisms of Improvement
sections.body:
• Vocabulary Shifting: Distributional meanings shift violently across domains. The word "positive" is uplifting in a generic dataset, but highly concerning in medical records. DAPT aligns semantic vector spaces to the target industry's expectation.
  • Tokenizer Expansion: Often engineers deliberately inject hundreds of new niche acronyms/tokens to the tokenizer during DAPT so the network avoids violently shattering terminology into individual meaningless subwords.

Notable historic examples of this progression include extracting CodeLlama heavily from Llama 2 via DAPT on parsed codebases, and establishing BioBERT / SciBERT / LegalBERT.
